topics.title: System Memory Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Memory Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**System Memory Management** in Linux is the kernel subsystem responsible for tracking, allocating, deallocating, and controlling the computer's primary memory (RAM) resources. It provides a uniform abstraction over **physical memory (RAM)** and **secondary storage (Swap/Hard Disk)** so that every running process believes it has its own private, contiguous, and isolated address space — a mechanism known as **Virtual Memory**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, memory management encompasses five pillars:
1. **Virtual Address Space abstraction**
2. **Paging and Page Table management**
3. **Demand Paging and Page Fault handling**
4. **Swapping between RAM and Disk**
5. **Kernel-level memory allocators (Buddy & Slab)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A student must understand *how Linux maps virtual addresses to physical addresses*, the role of the **MMU (Memory Management Unit)**, **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)**, and the difference between **logical, physical, and virtual addresses**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Post Office Box System"**

Imagine the **Post Office of a Large City**:
- Every citizen (a *process*) is given a **P.O. Box number** (a *virtual address*).
- The citizen never knows *which physical shelf* in the warehouse stores their letters.
- A **postal clerk** (the *MMU* in the CPU) maintains a **register** (the *page table*) that maps "P.O. Box 42" to "Shelf B-13, Row 7".
- When the warehouse gets full, the clerk **moves old letters to a remote godown** (the *Swap space* on disk) and writes a note: "P.O. Box 42 is now at the remote godown".
- A **fast lookup notebook** kept at the counter (the *TLB*) speeds up the common cases.

This is exactly how Linux memory management works:
- The process sees a **Virtual Address**.
- The **MMU + Page Table** translate it to a **Physical Address in RAM**.
- If the page is not in RAM, a **Page Fault** occurs and the kernel fetches it from **Swap**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

| Term | Standard Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Page Size (Linux default)** | Smallest unit of memory managed by the MMU | **4 KB** (can be 2 MB or 1 GB HugePages) |
| **Virtual Address Space (per 64-bit process)** | Range of addresses a process can use | **2⁶⁴ bytes** (practically 47-bit = 128 TB on x86_64) |
| **Physical RAM** | Actual hardware memory | Typically **4 GB – 128 GB** in modern systems |
| **TLB Size** | Cache for recent virtual-to-physical translations | **64 – 1536 entries** (CPU dependent) |
| **TLB Lookup Time** | Time to consult the TLB | **~1 ns** |
| **Memory Access Time** | Time to read RAM | **~100 ns** |
| **Disk Access Time (Swap)** | Time to read from swap device | **~1 – 10 ms (1,000,000 – 10,000,000 ns)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **~10,000× speed gap** between RAM and Disk is the *single most important reason* memory management exists — to keep the working set in RAM as much as possible.

---

## 1.4 Address Translation Hierarchy (Overview)

The CPU uses three address types:

$$
\text{Logical Address} \xrightarrow{\text{Segment Unit (optional)}} \text{Virtual Address} \xrightarrow{\text{MMU + Page Table}} \text{Physical Address}
$$

Linux typically operates in **flat (paging-only) mode** on 64-bit systems, so logical ≈ virtual. Each virtual address is split into:

$$
\text{Virtual Address} = \underbrace{[\text{PML4}][\text{PDPT}][\text{PD}][\text{PT}]}_{\text{Indices into 4-level page table}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{[\text{Offset}]}_{\text{Position within page (12 bits = 4 KB)}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Linux Memory Management — Step-by-Step

### Pillar 1: Virtual Address Space Abstraction
- **Why?** Process isolation, security, and the illusion of infinite memory.
- **How?** Each process is given a private address space (typically `0x0000000000000000` to `0x00007FFFFFFFFFFF` for user space on x86_64, totaling **128 TB**).
- Kernel space occupies the upper half (`0xFFFF800000000000` to `0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF`).
- The kernel's `task_struct` stores `mm_struct` describing the memory layout.

### Pillar 2: Paging (Demand-Free Allocation)
- Memory is divided into **fixed-size pages** (default **4 KB** in Linux).
- Physical RAM is divided into **page frames** of the same size.
- The **Page Table** (multi-level in x86_64) maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers.
- A **Page Table Entry (PTE)** contains bits like: `Present`, `Read/Write`, `User/Supervisor`, `Accessed`, `Dirty`, and the **PFN (Page Frame Number)**.

### Pillar 3: Demand Paging & Page Fault Handling
Linux uses **pure demand paging**: no page is loaded into RAM until the process actually touches it.

**Page Fault Service Flow:**
1. Process accesses a virtual address whose PTE has `Present = 0`.
2. CPU raises a **Page Fault Exception (#PF, vector 14)**.
3. Kernel's page-fault handler `handle_mm_fault()` runs.
4. Kernel checks if the address is valid → if not, sends **SIGSEGV** (Segmentation Fault).
5. Allocates a free frame from the **Buddy System**.
6. If frame is available: I/O schedule reads page from swap/file → updates PTE → resumes process.
7. If no free frame: invoke a **Page Replacement Algorithm**, evict a victim page, write it to swap if dirty.

### Pillar 4: Swapping
- When RAM is full, inactive pages are moved to a dedicated **swap partition or swap file** on disk.
- Linux prefers **Swapout of anonymous pages** (heap, stack) and **page-cache eviction** for file-backed pages.
- The **swappiness** kernel parameter (0–100, default **60**) controls the bias toward swap.

### Pillar 5: Kernel Memory Allocators

| Allocator | Purpose | Granularity |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| **Buddy System** | Allocates and coalesces pages in power-of-2 blocks | 1 page, 2, 4, 8, … up to 2¹¹ pages (8 MB) |
| **Slab Allocator** (SLUB in modern Linux) | Allocates kernel objects of fixed size (e.g., `task_struct`, `inode`) | Object-level (caches of common sizes) |
| **kmalloc()** | General-purpose kernel memory | Byte-level (uses slab internally) |
| **vmalloc()** | Allocates virtually contiguous (but not physically contiguous) memory | Page-level for large buffers |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these formulas — they appear in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** questions every KTU exam cycle.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Number of pages in a process** | $N_{\text{pages}} = \lceil \dfrac{\text{Process Size}}{\text{Page Size}} \rceil$ | Process size divided by page size, rounded up. |
| 2 | **Number of frames in RAM** | $N_{\text{frames}} = \dfrac{\text{Physical RAM (bytes)}}{\text{Page Size (bytes)}}$ | Total physical frames available. |
| 3 | **Internal Fragmentation** | $I_f = \text{Page Size} - (\text{Process Size} \bmod \text{Page Size})$ | Wasted space in the last page (only if not 0). |
| 4 | **External Fragmentation** | $E_f = \text{Unallocated} - \text{Maximum allocatable block}$ | Applies to non-paging schemes. |
| 5 | **Page Table Size (single-level)** | $S_{\text{PT}} = N_{\text{pages}} \times \text{PTE size}$ | For n-level paging: divide by $4^n$ per level. |
| 6 | **Effective Access Time (no TLB)** | $EAT = (1-p) \times T_m + p \times T_{pf}$ | $p$ = page fault rate, $T_m$ = memory time, $T_{pf}$ = page fault service time. |
| 7 | **Effective Access Time (with TLB)** | $EAT = (1-p)\,[(1-q)\,T_{tlb} + q\,(T_{tlb}+T_m)] + p\,T_{pf}$ | $q$ = TLB miss rate. |
| 8 | **TLB Reach** | $\text{TLB Reach} = \text{TLB Size} \times \text{Page Size}$ | Memory accessible without a TLB miss. |
| 9 | **Page Fault Rate** | $p = \dfrac{\text{Number of Page Faults}}{\text{Total Memory References}}$ | Ratio between 0 and 1. |
| 10 | **Hit Ratio** | $H = 1 - p$ | Fraction of accesses served from RAM. |
| 11 | **Hit Ratio with TLB** | $H = (1-q) + q \times (1-p)$ | Combines TLB and page-table hits. |
| 12 | **Buddy System Block Size** | $B_k = 2^k$ pages, $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 10$ | Standard order list. |
| 13 | **Virtual Address Split (n-level)** | $\text{VA} = p_1 \, p_2 \, \dots \, p_n \, \vert \, d$ | $p_i$ indexes the $i$-th table; $d$ = offset. |
| 14 | **Optimal Page Replacement Misses (lower bound)** | $\text{OPT misses} \ge \text{Num. distinct pages in future reference string when buffer empty}$ | Used in Belady's optimal algorithm. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** Use `\vert` for absolute-value bars and **logical-OR** symbols to avoid breaking markdown tables. The vertical pipe `|` is reserved strictly for table column separation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Servers (AWS / Azure):** Memory over-commit via swap allows renting more virtual memory than physically available.
- **Mobile Devices (Android):** `kswapd` and `lmkd` kill background apps to free RAM.
- **Embedded Linux (Raspberry Pi):** ZRAM and ZSWAP compress memory instead of swapping to disk, reducing SSD wear.
- **Databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL):** Tuned `vm.swappiness` and huge pages dramatically improve performance.
- **Container Runtimes (Docker):** `cgroups` enforce memory limits per container via the same kernel memory management primitives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Access Time (EAT) Without TLB

This is the **most frequently asked derivation in KTU ESE Module 1** and accounts for **5–7 marks** in Part B.

**Setup:** A memory reference takes $T_m$ nanoseconds if the page is in RAM (no page fault). If a page fault occurs, additional time $T_s$ is spent on swap I/O, plus the normal $T_m$ to read the data once it's in RAM.

**Step 1 — Define the two outcomes of a memory reference:**
- With probability $(1 - p)$ → page is in RAM (a **page hit**), cost = $T_m$.
- With probability $p$ → page fault, cost = $T_s + T_m$.

**Step 2 — Write the weighted-average formula:**

$$
EAT = (1 - p) \cdot T_m + p \cdot (T_s + T_m)
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the expression:**

$$
EAT = T_m - p \cdot T_m + p \cdot T_s + p \cdot T_m
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $-p \cdot T_m$ with $+p \cdot T_m$:**

$$
EAT = T_m + p \cdot T_s
$$

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form (KTU textbook answer):**

$$
\boxed{\,EAT = T_m + p \cdot T_s\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the question explicitly says "page fault service time $T_{pf}$ includes the memory access itself", use the form:
> $$EAT = (1 - p)\,T_m + p\,T_{pf}$$
> Read the wording carefully in the KTU paper.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Access Time With TLB (Full Form)

**Setup:** Let $q$ = TLB miss rate, $T_{tlb}$ = TLB lookup time, $T_m$ = memory access time, $T_{pf}$ = page fault service time, $p$ = page fault rate.

**Step 1 — Branch on TLB outcome:**
- TLB hit (probability $1 - q$): cost = $T_{tlb} + T_m$.
- TLB miss, page hit in RAM (probability $q \cdot (1 - p)$): cost = $T_{tlb} + T_m + T_m = T_{tlb} + 2 T_m$.
- TLB miss + page fault (probability $q \cdot p$): cost = $T_{tlb} + T_m + T_{pf}$.

**Step 2 — Aggregate Expected Time:**

$$
EAT = (1-q)\,(T_{tlb} + T_m) + q\,(1-p)\,(T_{tlb} + 2T_m) + q\,p\,(T_{tlb} + T_m + T_{pf})
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the three terms:**

$$
EAT = T_{tlb} + T_m - q T_{tlb} - q T_m + q T_{tlb} + 2 q T_m - 2 q p T_m + q p T_{tlb} + q p T_m + q p T_{pf}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the $-q T_{tlb}$ and $+q T_{tlb}$ pair:**

$$
EAT = T_{tlb} + T_m + q T_m - 2 q p T_m + q p T_{tlb} + q p T_m + q p T_{pf}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine the $q p T_m$ terms: $-2q p T_m + q p T_m = -q p T_m$:**

$$
EAT = T_{tlb} + T_m + q T_m - q p T_m + q p T_{tlb} + q p T_{pf}
$$

**Step 6 — Factor the $q p$ group and simplify:**

$$
EAT = T_{tlb} + T_m + q T_m + q p (T_{tlb} + T_{pf} - T_m)
$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed KTU answer (most common form):**

$$
\boxed{\,EAT = (1-p)\,(1-q)\,T_{tlb} + (1-p)\,q\,(T_{tlb}+T_m) + p\,(T_{tlb} + T_m + T_{pf})\,}
$$

This is the form you should reproduce verbatim in the exam for full marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Page Table Size for 2-Level Paging

**Setup:** Logical address space = $2^{32}$ bytes, Page size = $2^{12}$ bytes, PTE size = $4$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Number of pages (entries in outer page table):**

$$
N_{\text{outer}} = \frac{2^{32}}{2^{12}} = 2^{20}
$$

**Step 2 — Each inner page table must fit in one page. Entries per inner table:**

$$
N_{\text{inner}} = \frac{\text{Page Size}}{\text{PTE Size}} = \frac{2^{12}}{2^2} = 2^{10}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of inner page tables required:**

$$
N_{\text{tables}} = \frac{2^{20}}{2^{10}} = 2^{10}
$$

**Step 4 — Total memory consumed by the page table:**

$$
S_{\text{PT}} = N_{\text{tables}} \times \text{Page Size} = 2^{10} \times 2^{12} = 2^{22} = 4 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification (alternative computation):**

$$
S_{\text{PT}} = N_{\text{outer}} \times \text{PTE Size} = 2^{20} \times 4 \text{ B} = 4 \text{ MB} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Page Replacement Algorithms (Belady's Anomaly Check)

Consider the reference string of length 12 with **3 frames**: `7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3`

| Step | Ref | FIFO Fault? | LRU Fault? | Optimal Fault? |
|------|-----|-------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | 7 | **Fault** | **Fault** | **Fault** |
| 2 | 0 | **Fault** | **Fault** | **Fault** |
| 3 | 1 | **Fault** | **Fault** | **Fault** |
| 4 | 2 | **Fault** (evict 7) | **Fault** (evict 7) | **Fault** (evict 7) |
| 5 | 0 | Hit | Hit | Hit |
| 6 | 3 | **Fault** (evict 0) | **Fault** (evict 0) | **Fault** (evict 0) |
| 7 | 0 | **Fault** (evict 1) | **Fault** (evict 1) | Hit |
| 8 | 4 | **Fault** (evict 2) | **Fault** (evict 2) | **Fault** (evict 2) |
| 9 | 2 | **Fault** (evict 3) | Hit | Hit |
| 10 | 3 | Hit | **Fault** (evict 4) | Hit |
| 11 | 0 | **Fault** (evict 0) | **Fault** (evict 0) | Hit |
| 12 | 3 | Hit | Hit | Hit |
| **Total Page Faults** | | **9** | **9** | **6** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Belady's Anomaly (also called the FIFO Anomaly):** Increasing the number of page frames can *increase* the number of page faults for FIFO. Test it by running the same string with **4 frames** — FIFO gives **10 faults**, even though it has more memory! LRU and OPT are **stack algorithms** and are immune to this.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linux Virtual-to-Physical Address Translation (x86_64, 4-Level Paging)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Process Issues Virtual Address 48 bit] --> S1
    S1[SPLIT INTO FIVE FIELDS] --> A[PML4 INDEX 9 bit]
    S1 --> B[PDPT INDEX 9 bit]
    S1 --> C[PD INDEX 9 bit]
    S1 --> D[PT INDEX 9 bit]
    S1 --> E[OFFSET 12 bit]
    A --> TLB{TLB HIT}
    TLB -- YES --> PA1[Return Physical Frame Number plus OFFSET]
    TLB -- NO --> CR3[CR3 REGISTER points to PML4 base]
    CR3 --> L1[PML4 TABLE]
    L1 --> L2[PDPT TABLE]
    L2 --> L3[PD TABLE]
    L3 --> L4[PT TABLE]
    L4 --> PTE[PTE contains PFN plus FLAG BITS]
    PTE --> PA1
    PA1 --> MMU[MMU SENDS 52 bit PHYSICAL ADDRESS TO RAM]
    MMU --> RAM[PHYSICAL RAM RETURNS DATA]
    PA1 --> TLBL[Update TLB with new translation]
```

---

## 4.2 Linux Memory Subsystem — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USERSPACE
        APP1[User Process 1]
        APP2[User Process 2]
        SHELL[Shell and Tools]
    end
    subgraph KERNELSPACE
        subgraph MM[MEMORY MANAGEMENT SUBSYSTEM]
            VM[Virtual Memory Manager]
            PF[Page Fault Handler handle_mm_fault]
            SWAP[Swap Subsystem]
            PG[Page Allocator Buddy System]
            SLAB[Slab Allocator SLUB]
        end
        subgraph PROC[PROCESS SUBSYSTEM]
            TS[task_struct]
            MM2[mm_struct per process]
        end
    end
    subgraph HARDWARE
        MMU_HW[MMU and TLB]
        RAM_HW[Physical RAM DIMM]
        DISK_HW[Swap Device SSD or HDD]
    end
    APP1 --> MM2
    APP2 --> MM2
    SHELL --> MM2
    MM2 --> VM
    VM --> PF
    VM --> SWAP
    VM --> PG
    VM --> SLAB
    PF --> MMU_HW
    PG --> RAM_HW
    SWAP --> DISK_HW
    MMU_HW --> RAM_HW
    TS --> MM2
```

---

## 4.3 Page Fault Service Routine — Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process accesses Virtual Address] --> B{TLB Hit}
    B -- YES --> X[Return data from Physical RAM]
    B -- NO --> C[Consult Page Table]
    C --> D{Present bit equals 1}
    D -- YES --> Y[Load PTE into TLB and return data]
    D -- NO --> E[CPU raises Page Fault Exception 14]
    E --> F{Address legally mapped}
    F -- NO --> G[Send SIGSEGV and kill process]
    F -- YES --> H[Allocate free frame from Buddy System]
    H --> I{Free frame available}
    I -- NO --> J[Invoke page replacement algorithm]
    J --> K{Victim page is dirty}
    K -- YES --> L[Write victim to swap]
    K -- NO --> M[Discard victim]
    L --> N[Schedule disk I/O to load required page]
    M --> N
    I -- YES --> N
    N --> O[Update PTE with new PFN set Present equals 1]
    O --> P[Invalidate old TLB entry]
    P --> Q[Resume process at faulting instruction]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

### Q1. Differentiate between logical, physical, and virtual addresses. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Logical Address | Physical Address | Virtual Address |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Generated by** | CPU / Process | Memory hardware | CPU via MMU |
| **Visible to** | Programmer / compiler | Hardware only | Process (via OS) |
| **Uniqueness** | Same in all processes | Globally unique | Same in all processes |
| **Translation needed?** | No (flat mode) | N/A | Yes → physical via page table |

In a flat-memory 64-bit OS like Linux, **logical address = virtual address**; the MMU translates the virtual address to the physical address at runtime.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q2. What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)? Why is it needed? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

- **Definition:** The TLB is a small, fully-associative hardware cache inside the **MMU** that stores recent **virtual-to-physical page translations** (key = virtual page number, value = PTE).
- **Why needed:** Walking a multi-level page table on every memory access costs **2–4 extra RAM accesses**, which would slow down the CPU by ~70%. The TLB reduces this to a single comparison in ~1 ns for the vast majority of accesses (hit rate > 99%).
- **TLB Reach** = TLB entries × Page size, e.g., 64 entries × 4 KB = **256 KB** of directly accessible memory.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the concept of **demand paging** with a neat diagram. How does the OS handle a page fault? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (2 marks):** Demand paging loads pages into RAM only when they are first accessed, not at process start. This keeps RAM usage minimal and allows the system to run programs larger than physical memory.

**2. Mechanism (3 marks):** The `Present` bit in each PTE is initially **0**. When a process accesses such a page:
- CPU raises **trap #14 (Page Fault)**.
- Kernel's `handle_mm_fault()` runs.
- Validates the address via the VMA (`vm_area_struct`) list.
- Allocates a frame (Buddy System) or evicts a victim (replacement algorithm).
- Issues disk I/O to read the page from swap or executable file.
- Updates the PTE (`Present = 1`, sets PFN).
- Invalidates any stale TLB entry.
- Restarts the faulting instruction.

**3. Pure-demand vs. Prepaging (2 marks):** Linux uses pure demand paging (no anticipatory loading). Prepaging (e.g., Windows, madvise(MADV_WILLNEED)) loads a small window ahead, reducing I/O bursts.

---

**(b)** Consider a system with a **page fault service time of 8 ms**, **memory access time of 100 ns**, and a **page fault rate of 1 in 1000 references**. Calculate the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. What happens if you double the RAM (assume fault rate drops to 1 in 10,000)? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_m = 100$ ns
- $T_s = 8$ ms $= 8 \times 10^6$ ns
- $p_1 = \dfrac{1}{1000} = 10^{-3}$ (Case 1)
- $p_2 = \dfrac{1}{10{,}000} = 10^{-4}$ (Case 2)

**Case 1 (1 in 1000 fault rate):**

$$
EAT_1 = T_m + p_1 \cdot T_s = 100 + 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^6
$$

$$
EAT_1 = 100 + 8000 = 8100 \text{ ns} \approx 8.1 \,\mu\text{s}
$$

The system is **81× slower** than ideal RAM.

**Case 2 (1 in 10,000 fault rate — after doubling RAM):**

$$
EAT_2 = 100 + 10^{-4} \times 8 \times 10^6 = 100 + 800 = 900 \text{ ns}
$$

**Conclusion:** Doubling RAM reduces EAT by **9×** (from 8100 ns to 900 ns), demonstrating why memory upgrades yield dramatic performance gains for page-fault-bound workloads.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the **Buddy System** memory allocation algorithm with an example of allocating and deallocating 3 pages from an 8-page block. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (3 marks):** The Buddy System keeps free pages in **power-of-2 lists** (orders 0–10). A request of $2^k$ pages is satisfied by splitting a larger block repeatedly until the right size is obtained. Two buddies always coalesce when both are free.

**Example (4 marks):** Request = 3 pages (rounded up to order $k=2$, i.e., **4 pages**).
- Start: one block of 8 pages (order 3).
- **Split** the 8-block into two 4-blocks → return one 4-block to the user, keep one 4-block in the order-2 free list.
- When the 4-block is **freed**, check its buddy (the other 4-block). If buddy is free, **coalesce** back into an 8-block.

**Properties:** Allocation = $O(\log N)$ splits; coalescing = $O(\log N)$ checks. Disadvantage: **internal fragmentation** up to 50% of the requested size.

---

**(b)** What is **Belady's Anomaly**? Prove with the reference string `1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5` that FIFO suffers from it. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Belady's Anomaly is the counter-intuitive phenomenon where **increasing the number of page frames can increase the number of page faults** for certain replacement algorithms, notably **FIFO**.

**Proof by Page Fault Count (5 marks):**

| Frames = 3 (FIFO) | Frames = 4 (FIFO) |
|---|---|
| Faults after 1, 2, 3 = 3 | Faults after 1, 2, 3, 4 = 4 |
| 4 → **Fault** (evict 1) | 1 → Hit |
| 1 → **Fault** (evict 2) | 2 → Hit |
| 2 → **Fault** (evict 3) | 5 → **Fault** (evict 1) |
| 5 → **Fault** (evict 4) | 1 → **Fault** (evict 2) |
| 1 → **Fault** (evict 1) | 2 → **Fault** (evict 3) |
| 2 → **Fault** (evict 2) | 3 → **Fault** (evict 4) |
| 3 → Hit | 4 → **Fault** (evict 5) |
| 4 → **Fault** (evict 3) | 5 → **Fault** (evict 1) |
| 5 → **Fault** (evict 4) | |
| **Total = 9 faults** | **Total = 10 faults** |

**Conclusion:** With **4 frames, FIFO incurs MORE faults (10) than with 3 frames (9)**, despite having more memory. This proves Belady's Anomaly. Algorithms that are **stack algorithms** (LRU, Optimal) are immune to this anomaly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Memory Management questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing **ns, μs, and ms** in EAT calculations. Always convert $T_s$ to the same unit as $T_m$ **before** substituting. (Loss: up to 2 marks)
> 2. **Formula Selection:** Using $EAT = T_m + p \cdot T_s$ when the question says "page fault service time **includes** the memory access" — should be $EAT = (1-p)T_m + p\,T_{pf}$. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting Offset Bits:** Computing page table size as $2^{20}$ entries × 4 B = 4 MB **but** forgetting to note that the *outer* page table is also page-aligned, costing an extra page in some architectures. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Belady's Anomaly Setup:** Drawing the FIFO queue state with **the wrong eviction order** (e.g., evicting the most recently loaded instead of the oldest). Use a strict FIFO pointer — oldest out. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 5. **Missing `vm.swappiness` discussion:** For Linux-specific questions, mentioning only algorithms without connecting to `swappiness`, `/proc/meminfo`, or `kswapd` loses the Linux-specific marks. (Loss: 2–3 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, System Memory Management**

- **Three address types:** Logical, Virtual, Physical — translated by MMU.
- **Page size** default = **4 KB**; x86_64 uses **4-level paging** (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT).
- **TLB** is a hardware cache for translations; **TLB Reach** = entries × page size.
- **Demand paging** loads pages on first access; page fault → trap #14 → `handle_mm_fault()`.
- **EAT (no TLB):** $EAT = T_m + p \cdot T_s$ — memorize this derivation.
- **EAT (with TLB):** $EAT = (1-p)(1-q) T_{tlb} + (1-p)q (T_{tlb}+T_m) + p(T_{tlb}+T_m+T_{pf})$.
- **Page replacement algorithms:** FIFO (suffers Belady's Anomaly), LRU (stack algorithm, no anomaly), Optimal (theoretical lower bound).
- **Buddy System:** power-of-2 splits/coalesces, $O(\log N)$, up to 50% internal fragmentation.
- **Slab Allocator (SLUB):** used for fixed-size kernel objects like `task_struct`.
- **Linux tuning knobs:** `vm.swappiness` (default 60), `/proc/sys/vm/page-cluster`, `vm.overcommit_memory`.
- **Commands to inspect memory:**
  - `free -h` — RAM + swap usage
  - `cat /proc/meminfo` — detailed breakdown
  - `top` / `htop` — per-process memory
  - `vmstat 1` — virtual memory statistics every 1 s
  - `pmap <pid>` — process memory map
  - `swapon --show` — active swap devices
- **Belady's Anomaly:** more frames can yield more FIFO faults; LRU and OPT are stack algorithms and immune.
- **Fragmentation:** Paging eliminates external fragmentation but introduces internal fragmentation in the last page.
- **HugePages** (2 MB / 1 GB) reduce TLB pressure for large-memory workloads (databases, HPC).

> **End of Module 1 — System Memory Management Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Program Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Program Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Software Program Management — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Software Program Management (SPM)** is the disciplined, systematic orchestration of all activities, tools, artifacts, and processes required to plan, build, version, package, distribute, and maintain a software system throughout its lifecycle. Within the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PEEVT412 – Linux and Scripting*, the term specifically refers to the **Linux-native toolchain** used by engineers to convert source code into reliable, reproducible, distributable software products.

The discipline spans five functional pillars as defined by the KTU Module 1 outcome statements:

1. **Source Code Organization** — Directory layout, naming conventions, modular decomposition.
2. **Version Control & Configuration Management** — Tracking change history across contributors.
3. **Build Automation** — Compiling, linking, and assembling source into executables via deterministic rules.
4. **Dependency & Package Management** — Resolving external libraries and producing distributable artifacts.
5. **Release Engineering** — Tagging, packaging (`.deb`, `.rpm`, `.tar.gz`), and deploying software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Outcome (CO1):** *The student should be able to describe the architecture of a Linux-based software project, apply version control operations, and automate builds using Makefiles and shell scripts.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Construction Site" Model

Imagine you are the project manager of a **30-story apartment complex**:

| Construction Site Element | Software Program Management Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Architectural blueprint | Source code + project structure |
| Site supervisor's daily logbook | **Git commit history** (who changed what, when, why) |
| Pre-fabricated components (doors, windows) | Reusable libraries (libc, libm, glib) |
| Master construction schedule | **Makefile** (rules defining order of operations) |
| Inventory warehouse with receipt system | **Package manager** (apt, yum, dnf, pacman) |
| Final handover document with warranty | Tagged release + changelog |
| CCTV + access control | Configuration management + access permissions |

The foreman does not manufacture cement from scratch each morning — he orders a known, versioned batch. Likewise, a Linux developer does not rewrite `libc`; she declares a **dependency** and the package manager fetches a known, versioned build. This is the essence of **Software Program Management**: *reproducibility through declarative orchestration*.

> [!NOTE]
> Reproducibility is the **single most important property** of a managed software program. Given the same source code and the same build instructions, a competent engineer anywhere on Earth must be able to produce a byte-identical binary.

### 1.3 The Five Pillars — A Visual Overview

```
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │          SOFTWARE PROGRAM MANAGEMENT (SPM)             │
   └────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘
                │
   ┌────────────┼────────────────┬──────────────┬──────────┐
   │            │                │              │          │
   ▼            ▼                ▼              ▼          ▼
 SOURCE      VERSION         BUILD         PACKAGE      RELEASE
 ORGANIZE     CONTROL        AUTOMATE      MANAGE       ENGINEER
   │            │                │              │          │
   ▼            ▼                ▼              ▼          ▼
 tree       git, svn        make, cmake    apt, dnf     tar, deb
 find       branches        Makefile       yum, rpm     git tag
 layout     merges          rules          pip, npm     semver
```

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary-Aligned)

- **Artifact** — Any file produced by the build (object file, binary, tarball).
- **Repository (Repo)** — The on-disk database holding source files and their full history.
- **Commit** — An immutable snapshot of the working tree, identified by a SHA hash.
- **Tag** — A human-readable label (e.g., `v1.2.0`) attached to a specific commit.
- **Target** — A named output in a Makefile (e.g., `all:`, `app:`).
- **Dependency** — An external component (library, header, tool) required to build.
- **Distribution** — A packaged, installable collection of artifacts (`*.deb`, `*.rpm`).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Software Program Management — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation

The discipline of Software Program Management rests on three foundational theorems from classical software engineering and Unix philosophy:

**Theorem 1 — The Reproducibility Principle (Raymond, 1999):**
> *Given identical inputs and an identical toolchain, the build process must produce identical outputs.*

This is operationalised in Linux through **declarative build specifications** (Makefiles, `CMakeLists.txt`, `configure.ac`). Every input is hashed, every command is logged, and every output is deterministic.

**Theorem 2 — The Composability Principle (McIlroy, 1978 — Unix Pipes):**
> *Write programs that do one thing well and work together.*

This is operationalised through **modular source trees**, **shared libraries** (`.so`), and **package inter-dependency graphs**.

**Theorem 3 — The Traceability Principle (Fowler, 2001):**
> *Every change to the system must be attributable, auditable, and reversible.*

This is operationalised through **version control systems** (Git, Mercurial) where every modification is bound to a unique cryptographic identity.

## 2.2 The Build Pipeline — Five Logical Phases

1. **Source Phase** — `.c`, `.cpp`, `.py`, `.sh` files in a structured tree.
2. **Preprocessing Phase** — Macro expansion, header inclusion (`gcc -E`).
3. **Compilation Phase** — Source → object files (`.o`); syntax → assembly → machine code.
4. **Linking Phase** — Object files + libraries → executable (`ld`, `gcc -o`).
5. **Packaging Phase** — Executable + metadata → `.deb` / `.rpm` / `.tar.gz`.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> While Software Program Management is not a heavily numerical topic, the following equations govern the discipline and frequently appear in KTU derivations and short-answer questions.

### 2.3.1 Build Time Equation

For a project with $n$ source files and an incremental build where $k$ files have been modified, the rebuild time $T$ is:

$$T_{\text{build}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{c,i} \cdot \mathbb{1}_{i \in M} + T_{\text{link}}$$

where:
- $t_{c,i}$ = compile time of file $i$ (seconds)
- $\mathbb{1}_{i \in M}$ = indicator function (1 if file $i$ is in the modified set $M$, else 0)
- $T_{\text{link}}$ = static link time (seconds)

### 2.3.2 SHA-1 Commit Identity (Git)

A Git commit's identity is the SHA-1 hash of its content object:

$$\text{SHA1} = H(\text{blob} \;\vert\; \text{tree} \;\vert\; \text{parent} \;\vert\; \text{author} \;\vert\; \text{committer} \;\vert\; \text{message})$$

yielding a 40-character hexadecimal digest used as the commit's globally unique identifier.

### 2.3.3 Semantic Versioning Equation

Given version string `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`:

$$V = 256^{2} \cdot \text{MAJOR} + 256 \cdot \text{MINOR} + \text{PATCH}$$

(used internally by `rpm` and many package managers for ordering).

### 2.3.4 Comprehensive SPM Cheat-Sheet Table

| Layer | Tool(s) | Key File / Command | Purpose | KTU Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Source Layout | `tree`, `find` | `Makefile`, `CMakeLists.txt` | Define project topology | **High** |
| Version Control | `git` | `.git/`, `git log`, `git diff` | Track change history | **Very High** |
| Branching | `git branch`, `git merge` | `main`, `develop`, `feature/*` | Parallel development | **High** |
| Build Engine | `make`, `gmake` | `Makefile` | Rule-based automation | **Very High** |
| Meta-Build | `autoconf`, `automake`, `cmake` | `configure.ac`, `Makefile.am` | Portability layer | **Medium** |
| Compiler | `gcc`, `g++`, `clang` | `gcc -c foo.c` | Source → object | **High** |
| Linker | `ld`, `gcc -o` | dynamic `.so`, static `.a` | Object → executable | **Medium** |
| Pkg Manager | `apt`, `dnf`, `yum`, `pacman` | `apt install <pkg>` | Install / resolve deps | **Very High** |
| Build Dep | `build-essential`, `gcc`, `make` | `apt build-dep` | Fetch compile-time deps | **High** |
| Packaging | `dpkg-deb`, `rpmbuild` | `.deb`, `.rpm` | Distribution format | **High** |
| Archiving | `tar`, `gzip`, `xz` | `tar -czvf` | Source/binary tarball | **Medium** |
| Release | `git tag`, `git archive` | `v1.0.0` | Mark stable release | **Medium** |
| Doc Gen | `man`, `doxygen`, `pandoc` | `README.md`, `man/` | User documentation | **Low–Medium** |
| Test | `make check`, `ctest`, `pytest` | `test/` | Validation | **Medium** |
| CI Hook | `cron`, `systemd` timer | `git hook` | Automated trigger | **Low** |

### 2.3.5 The Make Dependency Graph (Why It Matters)

A Makefile encodes a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ = set of **targets** (files to be produced)
- $E$ = set of **edges** (prerequisite relationships)

If file $A$ has prerequisites $B$ and $C$, the edge is $B \rightarrow A$ and $C \rightarrow A$. Make uses **topological sort** to schedule compilation, only rebuilding a target when its modification time $m$ is older than any prerequisite's:

$$\text{rebuild}(A) \iff \max_{p \in \text{pre}(A)} m_p > m_A$$

This single inequality is the **entire theoretical engine** of incremental builds. It is why changing one `.c` file in a 10,000-file project rebuilds in seconds rather than hours.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in the Real World

| Industry Sector | SPM Application |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems** | Cross-compilation toolchains, reproducible firmware images |
| **Cloud / DevOps** | CI/CD pipelines (Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions) |
| **Cybersecurity** | Auditable build chains, signed packages (GPG, Sigstore) |
| **AI/ML** | Reproducible model training environments (containers) |
| **Telecommunications** | Carrier-grade Linux package management (CGL) |
| **Aerospace** | DO-178C traceability via SCM + build records |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all six sectors above, **failure of Software Program Management** translates directly into safety, financial, or legal risk. The KTU curriculum treats SPM as foundational precisely because the cost of mismanagement is asymmetric — cheap to prevent, catastrophic to repair.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Software Program Management — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked examples are **examination-grade**. Each step is expanded with reasoning comments. Multi-line equations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` blocks with a blank line above and below every display equation.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example A — Makefile Dependency Resolution

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A C project `student_app` consists of three source files: `main.c`, `io.c`, and `helpers.c`. The header file `helpers.h` is included by both `io.c` and `main.c`. Write the minimum Makefile that builds the executable and explain, step-by-step, what happens when the user edits only `helpers.h` and runs `make`.

### Step 1 — Identify the Build Graph

The dependency graph is:

$$\text{main.o} \rightarrow \text{student\_app}, \quad \text{io.o} \rightarrow \text{student\_app}, \quad \text{helpers.o} \rightarrow \text{student\_app}$$
$$\text{main.c}, \text{helpers.h} \rightarrow \text{main.o}, \quad \text{io.c}, \text{helpers.h} \rightarrow \text{io.o}, \quad \text{helpers.c}, \text{helpers.h} \rightarrow \text{helpers.o}$$

In graph form: $G = (V, E)$ with $V = \{\texttt{main.o}, \texttt{io.o}, \texttt{helpers.o}, \texttt{student\_app}\}$ and $E$ defined by the arrows above.

### Step 2 — Write the Minimal Makefile

```make
CC      = gcc
CFLAGS  = -Wall -O2
OBJ     = main.o io.o helpers.o
TARGET  = student_app

all: $(TARGET)

$(TARGET): $(OBJ)
	$(CC) -o $@ $^

main.o: main.c helpers.h
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c main.c

io.o: io.c helpers.h
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c io.c

helpers.o: helpers.c helpers.h
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c helpers.c

clean:
	rm -f $(OBJ) $(TARGET)
```

### Step 3 — Trace the Build When Only `helpers.h` is Modified

We initialise the modification timestamps (in **epoch seconds** for clarity) as:

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\texttt{helpers.h}} &= 1000 \\
m_{\texttt{main.o}}    &= 500 \\
m_{\texttt{io.o}}      &= 500 \\
m_{\texttt{helpers.o}} &= 500 \\
m_{\texttt{student\_app}} &= 400
\end{aligned}$$

**Action:** User runs `touch helpers.h` → $m_{\texttt{helpers.h}}$ becomes $1001$.

**Make's Traversal (Depth-First, Reverse-Topological):**

1. Make examines `$(TARGET) = student_app` (target $A$).
   Its prerequisites are `main.o, io.o, helpers.o` (set $P_A$).
   Condition: rebuild only if $\max_{p \in P_A} m_p > m_A$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\max(m_{\texttt{main.o}}, m_{\texttt{io.o}}, m_{\texttt{helpers.o}}) &= \max(500, 500, 500) = 500 \\
m_{\texttt{student\_app}} &= 400 \\
500 > 400 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{link required.}
\end{aligned}$$

2. Make recurses into `main.o`. Prereqs are `main.c, helpers.h`.

$$\begin{aligned}
\max(m_{\texttt{main.c}}, m_{\texttt{helpers.h}}) &= \max(600, 1001) = 1001 \\
m_{\texttt{main.o}} &= 500 \\
1001 > 500 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{recompile main.c.}
\end{aligned}$$

3. Make recurses into `io.o`. Prereqs are `io.c, helpers.h`.

$$\begin{aligned}
\max(m_{\texttt{io.c}}, m_{\texttt{helpers.h}}) &= \max(600, 1001) = 1001 \\
m_{\texttt{io.o}} &= 500 \\
1001 > 500 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{recompile io.c.}
\end{aligned}$$

4. Make recurses into `helpers.o`. Prereqs are `helpers.c, helpers.h`.

$$\begin{aligned}
\max(m_{\texttt{helpers.c}}, m_{\texttt{helpers.h}}) &= \max(600, 1001) = 1001 \\
m_{\texttt{helpers.o}} &= 500 \\
1001 > 500 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{recompile helpers.c.}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Resulting Command Sequence

$$\text{make} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{gcc -Wall -O2 -c main.c} \\
\text{gcc -Wall -O2 -c io.c} \\
\text{gcc -Wall -O2 -c helpers.c} \\
\text{gcc -o student\_app main.o io.o helpers.o}
\end{cases}$$

Four commands executed — **a full incremental rebuild** triggered by a single header edit. This demonstrates the **transitive closure of header dependencies** and is the central reason Make remains dominant in C/C++ workflows.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B — Git Object Identity & Commit Hash

**Problem Statement:**
A developer creates a new file `calc.py` containing the function `add(a,b)`. Explain how Git computes the commit hash and what the resulting `40`-character identifier represents.

### Step 1 — Form the Blob

Git first stores the file content as a **blob object**:

$$\text{blob} = \text{"blob "} + \text{len(content)} + \text{``\textbackslash 0''} + \text{content}$$

The SHA-1 of this concatenated byte string is computed:

$$\text{hash}_{\text{blob}} = \text{SHA1}(\text{blob})$$

### Step 2 — Construct the Tree

The tree object encodes the directory structure, referencing the blob by its hash:

$$\text{tree} = \text{"tree "} + \text{len(tree\_content)} + \text{``\textbackslash 0''} + \text{tree\_content}$$

where `tree_content` lists entries of the form `<mode> <name> <hash>\0`.

### Step 3 — Construct the Commit

For the **first commit** (no parent):

$$\text{commit} = \text{"commit "} + \text{len(body)} + \text{``\textbackslash 0''} + \text{body}$$

where

$$\text{body} = \text{"tree "} + \text{hash}_{\text{tree}} + \text{``\textbackslash n''} + \text{"author ...\textbackslash n"} + \text{"committer ...\textbackslash n"} + \text{``\textbackslash n''} + \text{message}$$

The final commit hash is:

$$\text{hash}_{\text{commit}} = \text{SHA1}(\text{commit})$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

The resulting 40-character hex string is **content-addressed**: any modification — even a single byte in the source — produces a **completely different hash**. This is the cryptographic foundation that makes Git history **tamper-evident**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example C — Package Dependency Resolution

**Problem Statement:** `pkg A` depends on `B (>= 2.0)` and `C`. `pkg B (2.1)` depends on `D`. `pkg C` depends on `D (>= 1.5)`. The repository has `B 2.1`, `C 1.0`, and `D 1.7`. Determine the install plan.

### Step 1 — Build the Dependency Graph

Vertices: $\{A, B, C, D\}$. Edges: $B \rightarrow A$, $C \rightarrow A$, $D \rightarrow B$, $D \rightarrow C$.

### Step 2 — Apply Constraints Topologically

- $A$ requires $B \geq 2.0$ → candidate $B\_{2.1}$ ✓
- $A$ requires $C$ (any) → candidate $C\_{1.0}$ ✓
- $B\_{2.1}$ requires $D$ → candidate $D\_{1.7}$ ✓
- $C\_{1.0}$ requires $D \geq 1.5$ → candidate $D\_{1.7}$ ✓ (single $D$ shared)

### Step 3 — Install Order (Reverse Topological)

$$\boxed{\text{Install: } D_{1.7} \;\rightarrow\; B_{2.1} \;\rightarrow\; C_{1.0} \;\rightarrow\; A}$$

This is exactly what `apt-get install A` computes and prints on the screen as the "The following additional packages will be installed" notice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conflict detection** occurs when no candidate satisfies all constraints simultaneously. KTU theory questions often test this with negative examples.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Software Program Management — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid** syntax, with strictly alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `nodeA1`, `phaseB`) and clean uppercase labels. No reserved keywords are used as node names.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Five-Pillar SPM Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    SPMSYS["SOFTWARE PROGRAM MANAGEMENT SYSTEM"]
    
    SPMSYS --> P1["PILLAR 1 SOURCE ORGANIZATION"]
    SPMSYS --> P2["PILLAR 2 VERSION CONTROL"]
    SPMSYS --> P3["PILLAR 3 BUILD AUTOMATION"]
    SPMSYS --> P4["PILLAR 4 PACKAGE MANAGEMENT"]
    SPMSYS --> P5["PILLAR 5 RELEASE ENGINEERING"]
    
    P1 --> P1A["DIRECTORY TREE"]
    P1 --> P1B["NAMING CONVENTIONS"]
    P1 --> P1C["MODULAR DECOMPOSITION"]
    
    P2 --> P2A["GIT REPOSITORY"]
    P2 --> P2B["BRANCH MODEL"]
    P2 --> P2C["COMMIT HISTORY"]
    P2 --> P2D["TAGS AND RELEASES"]
    
    P3 --> P3A["MAKEFILE RULES"]
    P3 --> P3B["COMPILER INVOCATIONS"]
    P3 --> P3C["LINKER STAGE"]
    P3 --> P3D["AUTOTOOLS AND CMAKE"]
    
    P4 --> P4A["APT OR DNF"]
    P4 --> P4B["DEPENDENCY RESOLUTION"]
    P4 --> P4C["BINARY PACKAGES"]
    
    P5 --> P5A["TARBALL GENERATION"]
    P5 --> P5B["CHANGELOG WRITING"]
    P5 --> P5C["SEMANTIC VERSIONING"]
    
    P1A --> P3A
    P2A --> P3A
    P3A --> P4C
    P4C --> P5A
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The Linux Build Pipeline (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SOURCE FILES\nmain.c io.c helpers.c\nhelpers.h"]
    
    B1["PREPROCESS\ngcc -E"]
    B2["COMPILE\ngcc -c"]
    B3["ASSEMBLE\nas"]
    B4["LINK\nld OR gcc -o"]
    B5["PACKAGE\ndpkg-deb OR rpmbuild"]
    B6["DISTRIBUTE\napt repo OR yum repo"]
    
    A --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6
    
    C1["PREPROCESSED .i"]
    C2["ASSEMBLY .s"]
    C3["OBJECT .o"]
    C4["EXECUTABLE a.out"]
    C5["PACKAGE .deb OR .rpm"]
    C6["REPOSITORY METADATA"]
    
    B1 -.produces.-> C1
    B2 -.produces.-> C2
    B3 -.produces.-> C3
    B4 -.produces.-> C4
    B5 -.produces.-> C5
    B6 -.produces.-> C6
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Git Branching & Release Workflow

```mermaid
gitGraph
    commit id: "INIT"
    commit id: "FEAT A"
    branch feature1
    checkout feature1
    commit id: "F1 WIP1"
    commit id: "F1 WIP2"
    checkout main
    commit id: "HOTFIX"
    checkout feature1
    commit id: "F1 DONE"
    checkout main
    merge feature1
    commit id: "RELEASE V1.0"
    commit id: "PATCH V1.0.1" tag: "v1.0.1"
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Makefile Dependency Graph (Worked Example 3.1)

```mermaid
graph TD
    TGT["TARGET student_app"]
    MAIN_O["main.o"]
    IO_O["io.o"]
    HELP_O["helpers.o"]
    MAIN_C["main.c"]
    IO_C["io.c"]
    HELP_C["helpers.c"]
    HELP_H["helpers.h"]
    CC["gcc -o LINKER"]
    
    TGT --> MAIN_O
    TGT --> IO_O
    TGT --> HELP_O
    TGT --> CC
    
    MAIN_O --> MAIN_C
    MAIN_O --> HELP_H
    IO_O --> IO_C
    IO_O --> HELP_H
    HELP_O --> HELP_C
    HELP_O --> HELP_H
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Package Dependency Resolution State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> REQRECV: USER REQUESTS PACKAGE A
    REQRECV: REQUEST RECEIVED
    REQRECV --> DEPSCAN: PARSE A REQUIRES
    DEPSCAN: SCAN DEPENDENCIES
    DEPSCAN --> VERCHECK: APPLY VERSION CONSTRAINTS
    VERCHECK: VERSION CHECK
    VERCHECK --> CONFLICT: NO CANDIDATE
    VERCHECK --> PLANNED: ALL CONSTRAINTS SATISFIED
    CONFLICT: CONFLICT DETECTED
    CONFLICT --> [*]: HALT WITH ERROR
    PLANNED: PLAN READY
    PLANNED --> INSTALL: TOPOLOGICAL SORT
    INSTALL: INSTALL IN ORDER
    INSTALL --> [*]: SUCCESS
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Software Program Management — Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Rapid Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define **Software Program Management**. List any four activities it encompasses in a Linux environment.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Software Program Management (SPM) is the systematic planning, organisation, versioning, building, packaging, and release of software using Linux-native tools and processes to ensure **reproducibility, traceability, and maintainability** of the software artifact throughout its lifecycle.

*(1 mark — definition)*

The four principal activities in a Linux environment are:
1. **Source Code Organisation** — Maintaining a logical directory tree with `src/`, `include/`, `lib/`, `test/`, and `docs/` subdirectories.
2. **Version Control** — Tracking every change using Git, including commits, branches, and tags.
3. **Build Automation** — Using `make` and `Makefile` rules to compile and link source deterministically.
4. **Package Management** — Distributing software as `.deb` (Debian/Ubuntu) or `.rpm` (Red Hat/Fedora) packages via `apt` or `dnf`.

*(2 marks — ½ mark per correct activity, well-explained)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Supplementary]**
What is a **Makefile**? Explain the three components of a Make rule with an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Makefile is a **declarative build specification file** consumed by the `make` utility to automate the compilation and linking of software.

*(1 mark)*

The three components of a Make rule are:

| Component | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Target** | File to be produced | `app.o` |
| **Prerequisites** | Input files the target depends on | `app.c app.h` |
| **Recipe** | Shell commands to produce the target | `gcc -c app.c` |

*(2 marks — 1 for listing, 1 for example)*

Example rule:
```make
app.o: app.c app.h
    gcc -Wall -c app.c -o app.o
```

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students answer **one full question** (either Q-A or Q-B), each carrying **14 marks** distributed across two sub-parts of **7 marks** each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Linux software build pipeline** from source code to a distributable package. List the tools used at each stage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A project `reportgen` consists of files `report.c`, `parser.c`, `parser.h`, and `Makefile`. The executable `reportgen` is built by linking `report.o` and `parser.o`. Write a complete `Makefile` for the project and explain what happens when only `parser.h` is modified. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[.c, .h] → PREPROCESS → [.i] → COMPILE → [.s] → ASSEMBLE → [.o]
        gcc -E              gcc -S             as
                          ↓
                       [.o files] → LINKER (ld) → EXECUTABLE
                          ↓
                       PACKAGER (dpkg-deb / rpmbuild) → [.deb / .rpm]
                          ↓
                       REPOSITORY (apt repo / yum repo)
```

**Stage-by-Stage Explanation (3 marks):**

1. **Preprocessing** — `gcc -E source.c -o source.i`. Expands `#include`, `#define`, and conditional compilation directives.
2. **Compilation** — `gcc -S source.i -o source.s`. Translates preprocessed C into assembly.
3. **Assembly** — `as source.s -o source.o`. Converts assembly into relocatable machine code (object file).
4. **Linking** — `gcc -o app main.o util.o -lm`. Resolves external symbols, combines multiple objects, links against libraries (`libm`, `libc`).
5. **Packaging** — `dpkg-deb --build reportgen_1.0` or `rpmbuild -ba reportgen.spec`. Bundles binary, metadata (name, version, dependencies, maintainer).
6. **Distribution** — Upload to APT/YUM repository; users install via `apt install reportgen` or `dnf install reportgen`.

**Tools Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Stage | Tool | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Preprocess | `gcc -E` / `cpp` | `.i` |
| Compile | `gcc -S` | `.s` |
| Assemble | `as` | `.o` |
| Link | `ld` / `gcc -o` | Executable |
| Package | `dpkg-deb` / `rpmbuild` | `.deb` / `.rpm` |
| Distribute | `apt` / `dnf` | Installed binary |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Makefile (4 marks):**
```make
CC      = gcc
CFLAGS  = -Wall -g -O2
OBJ     = report.o parser.o
TARGET  = reportgen

all: $(TARGET)

$(TARGET): $(OBJ)
	$(CC) -o $@ $^

report.o: report.c parser.h
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c report.c

parser.o: parser.c parser.h
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c parser.c

clean:
	rm -f $(OBJ) $(TARGET)

.PHONY: all clean
```

**Explanation of behaviour when only `parser.h` is modified (3 marks):**

Let modification times be:

$$m_{\texttt{parser.h}}=1000,\; m_{\texttt{parser.c}}=900,\; m_{\texttt{report.c}}=900,\; m_{\texttt{parser.o}}=800,\; m_{\texttt{report.o}}=800,\; m_{\texttt{reportgen}}=700$$

When `touch parser.h` is executed, $m_{\texttt{parser.h}} \to 1001$. Make traverses the DAG:

1. Target `reportgen` requires `report.o` and `parser.o`. Since both are newer than the binary ($800 > 700$), relinking is required.
2. Target `report.o` requires `report.c` and `parser.h`. Since $1001 > 800$, recompile `report.c`.
3. Target `parser.o` requires `parser.c` and `parser.h`. Since $1001 > 800$, recompile `parser.c`.

Resulting command sequence:
$$\text{gcc -Wall -g -O2 -c report.c} \;\rightarrow\; \text{gcc -Wall -g -O2 -c parser.c} \;\rightarrow\; \text{gcc -o reportgen report.o parser.o}$$

Both object files are recompiled because the header's transitive influence propagates to every translation unit that includes it.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Version Control Systems (VCS)**. Compare the **Centralised** and **Distributed** models with two examples each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the command line, demonstrate how you would:
   (i) Initialise a Git repository, create a file `app.py`, and commit it with the message *"Initial commit"*. *(3 marks)*
   (ii) Create a branch `feature-x`, make a change, and merge it back to `main`. *(2 marks)*
   (iii) Tag the latest commit as `v1.0.0` and push everything to a remote called `origin`. *(2 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A **Version Control System (VCS)** is a software tool that records changes to a file or set of files over time so that specific versions can be recalled later. It provides **history, branching, merging, and collaboration** capabilities.

*(2 marks — definition + importance)*

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Centralised VCS (CVCS) | Distributed VCS (DVCS) |
|---|---|---|
| Repository location | Single central server | Every clone has full history |
| Network dependency | Required for almost all operations | Most operations work offline |
| Failure mode | Server down = no work | Any clone can restore the server |
| Branching | Heavyweight, server-side | Lightweight, local |
| Examples | **Subversion (SVN)**, **CVS** | **Git**, **Mercurial (hg)** |
| Speed of common ops | Slower (network) | Very fast (local) |
| Collaboration model | Lock-modify-unlock or merge at server | Free-form local commits, push when ready |

**Examples:**

- **CVCS:** Apache Subversion (`svn`), Concurrent Versions System (`cvs`), Perforce (`p4`).
- **DVCS:** Git (`git`), Mercurial (`hg`), Bazaar (`bzr`).

*(1 mark — examples + concluding remark)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Initialise, create file, commit (3 marks):**
```bash
mkdir project
cd project
git init
echo 'print("Hello, KTU")' > app.py
git add app.py
git commit -m "Initial commit"
```

**Explanation:** `git init` creates a `.git/` subdirectory containing the object database and refs. `git add` stages the file's content as a **blob** in the staging area (index). `git commit` creates a **commit object** pointing to a tree referencing the blob, with the parent set to `HEAD~1` (or none for the first commit).

**(ii) Branch, modify, merge (2 marks):**
```bash
git checkout -b feature-x
echo '# new feature' >> app.py
git add app.py
git commit -m "Add feature x"
git checkout main
git merge feature-x
```

**Explanation:** `git checkout -b feature-x` creates and switches to a new branch pointer. After committing, switching back to `main` and executing `git merge` performs a **fast-forward merge** (since `main` had not advanced), moving the `main` pointer to the new commit.

**(iii) Tag and push (2 marks):**
```bash
git tag v1.0.0
git remote add origin git@github.com:user/project.git
git push -u origin main
git push origin v1.0.0
```

**Explanation:** `git tag v1.0.0` creates a lightweight tag pointing to the current `HEAD` commit. `git push -u origin main` publishes the main branch and sets upstream tracking. `git push origin v1.0.0` publishes the tag separately because tags are not pushed by default.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in SPM Questions (Syllabus Module 1):**
> 1. **Confusing `gcc` and `make`** — `gcc` is a *compiler driver*; `make` is a *build orchestrator*. They are **not interchangeable**. Students lose 1–2 marks for using "gcc" and "make" as synonyms.
> 2. **Forgetting the tab indentation** in Makefile recipes — Make requires a **literal TAB character** at the start of every recipe line. Spaces will produce a cryptic `missing separator` error. Examiners check this in lab exams.
> 3. **Mis-stating Git commit identity** — A commit's hash is computed from its **content + parent + author + message**, not just the file content. The blob is what hashes the file, but the commit's identity includes more.
> 4. **Confusing `.tar.gz` with a "package"** — A tarball is an **archive**, not a package. A package contains metadata (`.deb` has a `control` file; `.rpm` has a spec). Examiners deduct marks for this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the topological-order argument** in dependency resolution — Always explicitly state the install order (deepest dependency first).
> 6. **Ignoring `.PHONY` targets** — Forgetting `.PHONY: clean` causes `clean` to fail silently if a file named `clean` ever exists in the directory.
> 7. **Not specifying `-o` with `gcc`** — When asked to "write the command to compile `foo.c` into an executable", students must include `gcc -o myapp foo.c`; writing just `gcc foo.c` produces `a.out`, which is technically correct but loses a mark for not naming the output.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 1 — SPM)**

- **Definition:** Software Program Management = planning + versioning + building + packaging + releasing software on Linux.
- **Five Pillars:** Source Organisation, Version Control, Build Automation, Package Management, Release Engineering.
- **Makefile Rule Syntax:** `target: prereq1 prereq2 ... \n\t<tab>recipe`. Recipes **must** start with a tab.
- **Make's Core Logic:** Rebuild target $A$ iff $\max_{p \in \text{pre}(A)} m_p > m_A$.
- **Common Make Variables:** `$@` (target), `$^` (all prereqs), `$<` (first prereq), `$?` (newer prereqs).
- **Common Make Built-ins:** `CC`, `CXX`, `CFLAGS`, `LDFLAGS`, `OBJ`, `TARGET`.
- **GCC Pipeline:** `gcc -E` (preprocess) → `gcc -S` (compile) → `as` (assemble) → `ld` (link).
- **Git Init Workflow:** `git init` → `git add` → `git commit -m "msg"`.
- **Git Branching:** `git branch <name>`, `git checkout <name>`, `git checkout -b <name>`, `git merge <name>`.
- **Git Identity:** `git config --global user.name "X"`, `git config --global user.email "x@y.z"`.
- **Git Tagging:** `git tag v1.0.0` (lightweight), `git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "msg"` (annotated).
- **Package Formats:** `.deb` (Debian/Ubuntu via `apt`, `dpkg`), `.rpm` (Red Hat/Fedora via `dnf`, `yum`, `rpm`).
- **Key Package Commands:** `apt install <pkg>`, `apt search <kw>`, `apt show <pkg>`, `dpkg -i <file.deb>`, `rpm -ivh <file.rpm>`.
- **Build Dependencies:** `apt build-dep <pkg>` (Debian) or `dnf build-dep <pkg>` (Fedora).
- **Tarball:** `tar -czvf name.tar.gz dir/` (gzip), `tar -cjvf name.tar.bz2 dir/` (bzip2), `tar -cJvf name.tar.xz dir/` (xz).
- **Reproducibility Principle:** Same source + same toolchain → same output. This is **the** non-negotiable rule of SPM.
- **Reproducibility Threats to Avoid:** Unpinned dependencies, timestamps in binaries, non-deterministic compiler flags (`-O0` in release, debug symbols left in).
- **Semantic Versioning:** `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`. Increment MAJOR on incompatible API change, MINOR on backward-compatible feature, PATCH on backward-compatible bugfix.
- **Common Directory Layout:** `src/`, `include/`, `lib/`, `test/`, `docs/`, `Makefile`, `README.md`, `LICENSE`, `CHANGELOG.md`.
- **Golden Rule for KTU Lab Exam:** Always run `make clean` before submission to avoid submitting stale object files.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hardware Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hardware Management** in the Linux operating system refers to the comprehensive set of kernel subsystems, user-space utilities, virtual filesystems, and configuration frameworks responsible for **detection, identification, configuration, monitoring, and control of physical and virtual hardware devices** attached to the system. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEVT412 – Linux and Scripting), Hardware Management encompasses the Linux kernel module loader, the `/proc` and `/sys` virtual filesystems, the **udev** device manager, and the CLI toolchain (`lspci`, `lsusb`, `lshw`, `dmidecode`, `lsmod`, `modprobe`, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module 1 (Hardware Management)**
> The expected learning outcomes map to **CO1**: *"Understand the architecture of Linux OS and manage hardware devices using shell commands."* Students must be able to enumerate devices, load/unload kernel modules, inspect hardware through virtual filesystems, and interpret hardware addresses (IRQ, I/O ports, DMA).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your Linux computer is a **large commercial apartment building (the system)**, and every hardware component is a **tenant moving in**.

- The **kernel** is the building's security and management office.
- **Hardware detection** is the act of seeing a new tenant at the gate.
- **Device drivers** are the lease agreements — written rules that tell the building how to communicate with each tenant.
- **Kernel modules** are **plug-in lease templates** that can be loaded (`insmod`) when a new tenant arrives and unloaded (`rmmod`) when they leave, so the building doesn't have to keep every agreement open all the time.
- The **`/dev` directory** is the building's **apartment number directory** — every device gets a flat number (file node) so users can talk to it.
- The **`/proc` and `/sys` filesystems** are the **notice boards and CCTV monitors** that let you peek into the kernel's brain and watch live system vitals.
- **`udev`** is the **smart receptionist** that dynamically assigns apartment numbers (device nodes) and runs custom rules whenever a tenant checks in or out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Remember**
> - **IRQ (Interrupt Request)** — numbered hardware lines (typically **0–15** on legacy PIC, extended to **0–255** with APIC).
> - **I/O Port Address** — hexadecimal ranges in the **0x000 – 0xFFFF** space.
> - **DMA Channel** — Direct Memory Access channels, traditionally **0–7**.
> - **Major:Minor Numbers** — device file identifiers (e.g., `8:0` for `/dev/sda`).

### 1.3 The Linux Hardware Stack — Bird's-Eye View

Linux treats every piece of hardware as a **file**. This is the famous *"Everything is a file"* philosophy. To manage hardware, you must master three layers:

1. **Kernel Layer** – device drivers compiled into the kernel or loaded as **Loadable Kernel Modules (LKMs)**.
2. **Virtual Filesystem Layer** – `/proc`, `/sys`, `/dev` expose hardware data as readable text files.
3. **User-Space Layer** – CLI utilities and the `udev` daemon that act on behalf of the administrator.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Linux Kernel & Loadable Kernel Modules (LKMs)

The Linux kernel is **monolithic with modular support**. Core code (memory manager, scheduler, VFS) is always resident in RAM, but peripheral drivers can be inserted/removed at runtime as **.ko (Kernel Object) files** located in `/lib/modules/$(uname -r)/kernel/`.

**Why modularity?**
- **Smaller resident memory footprint** — unused drivers are not loaded.
- **Hot-swappable** — drivers can be updated without recompiling the kernel.
- **Fault isolation** — a buggy module crashes only itself, not the whole kernel.

#### 2.1.1 Module Lifecycle Commands (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

| Command | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `lsmod` | List currently loaded kernel modules | `lsmod | head` |
| `insmod` | Insert a specific `.ko` file (no dependency resolution) | `sudo insmod /path/to/mymod.ko` |
| `rmmod` | Remove a loaded module | `sudo rmmod mymod` |
| `modprobe` | Smart insert with **automatic dependency resolution** from `/lib/modules` | `sudo modprobe e1000e` |
| `modprobe -r` | Smart remove with dependents | `sudo modprobe -r e1000e` |
| `depmod` | Rebuild module dependency map (`modules.dep`) | `sudo depmod -a` |
| `modinfo` | Show metadata of a module | `modinfo nvidia` |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** `modprobe` vs `insmod` is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**. Remember: `modprobe` reads `/lib/modules/$(uname -r)/modules.dep`, resolves dependencies recursively, and forces the right module — `insmod` does **none** of this.

### 2.2 The /proc Filesystem — Process & Hardware Information

`/proc` is a **virtual (pseudo) filesystem** mounted at boot. It consumes **zero disk space**; files are generated on-the-fly by the kernel.

| Path | What it Exposes |
|---|---|
| `/proc/cpuinfo` | Per-CPU vendor, model, MHz, caches, flags |
| `/proc/meminfo` | Total/free/available RAM, swap, buffers, cache |
| `/proc/interrupts` | **IRQ** counts per CPU core |
| `/proc/ioports` | **I/O port ranges** used by each device |
| `/proc/dma` | Assigned **DMA channels** |
| `/proc/devices` | Character and block major numbers registered |
| `/proc/modules` | Same as `lsmod` output |
| `/proc/cmdline` | Boot arguments passed by GRUB |
| `/proc/partitions` | Disk and partition table |
| `/proc/version` | Kernel build string |
| `/proc/uptime` | System uptime in seconds (idle, total) |
| `/proc/loadavg` | 1/5/15-min load averages + running/total tasks |
| `/proc/PID/` | Per-process information for process ID `PID` |

### 2.3 The /sys Filesystem — sysfs (The Modern Hardware Tree)

`/sys` (sysfs) was introduced in kernel **2.6** to expose a **structured, object-oriented** view of devices, buses, drivers, and kernel subsystems. It is the backbone used by `udev`, `systemd`, and modern tools.

Top-level directories of `/sys`:

- `/sys/devices/` — canonical view of every device, organized by bus.
- `/sys/bus/` — physical and logical buses (`pci`, `usb`, `scsi`, `i2c`).
- `/sys/class/` — devices grouped by **function** (`net`, `block`, `input`, `tty`).
- `/sys/block/` — block devices.
- `/sys/module/` — every loadable kernel module.
- `/sys/firmware/` — firmware and ACPI data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two filesystems?** `/proc` is **process-centric** and human-readable; `/sys` is **device-centric**, hierarchical, and machine-friendly. The kernel developers moved hardware-related entries from `/proc` into `/sys` to clean up the namespace.

### 2.4 The udev Device Manager

`udev` is the **userspace daemon** that manages device nodes in `/dev` dynamically. When the kernel announces a device event via **netlink**, `udev` reads rules from `/etc/udev/rules.d/` and `/lib/udev/rules.d/` to:

1. Create/remove device nodes in `/dev`.
2. Set permissions and ownership.
3. Run scripts (e.g., auto-mount USB drives).
4. Trigger firmware loading.

**Rule file naming convention:** `NN-name.rules` where `NN` is the priority (lower runs first).
**Rule example:**

```
KERNEL=="sda1", SUBSYSTEM=="block", ACTION=="add", \
RUN+="/bin/mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/usb"
```

### 2.5 Hardware Detection Command Toolbox (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

| Command | Package | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `lspci` | `pciutils` | List all **PCI/PCIe** devices (-v, -vv, -t for tree) |
| `lsusb` | `usbutils` | List all **USB** devices (-v, -t for tree) |
| `lshw` | `lshw` | **Detailed** hardware report (CPU, RAM, buses, firmware) |
| `dmidecode` | `dmidecode` | Read **DMI/SMBIOS** tables (manufacturer, serial, BIOS) |
| `hdparm` | `hdparm` | Get/set SATA/IDE drive parameters |
| `smartctl` | `smartmontools` | **S.M.A.R.T.** disk health diagnostics |
| `inxi` | `inxi` | Friendly all-in-one hardware summary |
| `hwinfo` | `hwinfo` | Exhaustive probing (slower, more verbose than `lshw`) |
| `dmesg` | `util-linux` | Kernel **ring buffer** — boot logs, hotplug events |
| `uptime`, `free`, `top` | coreutils/procps | Live resource metrics |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although Hardware Management is more command-driven than equation-driven, the following memory, addressing, and I/O formulas are **examinable**.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Addressable I/O space (x86) | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ ports | ports | Range `0x0000`–`0xFFFF` |
| Legacy IRQ lines | 16 lines (PIC), 24 vectors per line | vectors | APIC extends to 24 IRQs |
| DMA channels (ISA) | 8 channels | channels | Channels 0–3 (8-bit), 5–7 (16-bit) |
| Memory page size (default) | $4\,\text{KiB} = 2^{12}$ bytes | bytes | Configurable (hugepages: 2 MiB, 1 GiB) |
| Total physical addressing (x86_64) | $2^{48}$ bytes = **256 TiB** | bytes | Virtual: $2^{57}$ bytes |
| Linux load average interpretation | $\text{LA}_n = \frac{\text{active R} + \text{D}}{\text{n CPUs}}$ | dimensionless | < 1.0 = healthy, > 1.0 = queued |
| Major number range | Dynamic: 234–254 (char), 254–255 (block) | integer | Older kernels had fixed assignments |
| Free RAM formula | $\text{Available} = \text{Free} + \text{Reclaimable} - \text{Reserved}$ | KiB/MiB | See `/proc/meminfo` field `MemAvailable` |
| Uptime parsing | `cat /proc/uptime` → `uptime idle` | seconds | First field is wall-clock uptime |
| CPU utilisation (sampled) | $\%CPU = 100 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\Delta \text{idle}}{\Delta t}\right)$ | % | Use `top`, `mpstat`, or `vmstat` |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Server Administration:** Use `lshw`, `dmidecode`, and `smartctl` to audit bare-metal hardware before deploying cloud workloads.
- **Embedded Linux:** Cross-compile loadable modules (`make modules ARCH=arm CROSS_COMPILE=arm-linux-gnueabihf-`) and `insmod` them on the target board.
- **IoT / Edge Devices:** `udev` rules trigger custom actions when sensors (USB, I²C) are plugged in.
- **DevOps & Automation:** `dmidecode` + `lshw` in provisioning scripts verify hardware SKUs for license binding.
- **Forensics:** `dmesg` and `/var/log/kern.log` reveal hardware tampering, bad sectors, and overheating events.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Reading CPU & Memory Information

**Problem (KTU Style — 3 marks):** Write a one-liner shell command pipeline to display the *number of physical CPU cores*, the *CPU model name*, and the *total RAM in MiB* on a Linux system.

**Model Solution:**

```bash
echo "Cores: $(nproc)"; \
grep -m1 'model name' /proc/cpuinfo | cut -d: -f2; \
awk '/MemTotal/ {printf "Total RAM: %d MiB\n", $2/1024}' /proc/meminfo
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. `nproc` → reads `/proc/cpuinfo` and prints the number of *online* processing units.
2. `grep -m1 'model name' /proc/cpuinfo` → fetches the first line containing the model name.
3. `cut -d: -f2` → splits on `:` and keeps the second field (the actual model string).
4. `awk '/MemTotal/'` → matches the `MemTotal:` line in `/proc/meminfo` (value is in **KiB**).
5. `printf "Total RAM: %d MiB\n", $2/1024` → converts KiB to MiB using:

$$
\text{MiB} = \frac{\text{KiB}}{1024}
$$

Output on a typical lab machine:
```
Cores: 4
Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-8250U CPU @ 1.60GHz
Total RAM: 7856 MiB
```

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Manual Module Load with Dependency Walk

**Problem (KTU Style — 7 marks):** Demonstrate how to load the `nfs` filesystem module along with its dependencies, then verify the loaded objects.

**Model Solution:**

```bash
# Step 1: Check current state
lsmod | grep nfs
# (no output → not loaded)

# Step 2: Attempt simple insertion
sudo modprobe nfs
# Internally modprobe runs:
#   - Reads /lib/modules/$(uname -r)/modules.dep
#   - Resolves dependencies: nfs -> nfs_acl -> lockd -> sunrpc
#   - Calls insmod in the right order

# Step 3: Verify
lsmod | grep -E 'nfs|lockd|sunrpc'
# Expected output (order may vary):
# nfs                   290816  0
# nfs_acl                16384  1 nfs
# lockd                  98304  1 nfs
# sunrpc                364544  4 nfs,lockd,nfs_acl,...
```

**Analytical Matrix — Module Load Order:**

| Step | Module Loaded | Depends On | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `sunrpc` | (nothing) | RPC transport layer; foundation for NFS |
| 2 | `lockd` | `sunrpc` | File-locking daemon |
| 3 | `nfs_acl` | `sunrpc`, `lockd` | NFS Access Control Lists |
| 4 | `nfs` | All above | Actual NFS client filesystem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The numbers under each module name (e.g., `290816`) denote **module size in bytes**, and the second column shows **reference count** — how many other modules/components are using it. You cannot `rmmod` a module with a non-zero reference count.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Writing a udev Rule for Automatic USB Backup

**Problem (KTU Style — 7 marks):** Write a `udev` rule that runs a backup script every time a USB mass-storage device with vendor ID `0781` (SanDisk) is plugged in. Show the steps to reload udev rules and verify.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Create the action script** `/usr/local/bin/usb-backup.sh`:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Auto-backup script triggered by udev
LABEL=$(lsblk -no LABEL "$DEVNAME")
MOUNT=/mnt/usb
LOG=/var/log/usb-backup.log

mkdir -p "$MOUNT"
mount "$DEVNAME" "$MOUNT"
echo "$(date): Backed up to $MOUNT (label=$LABEL)" >> "$LOG"
rsync -a /home/student/Documents/ "$MOUNT"/backup/ >> "$LOG" 2>&1
umount "$MOUNT"
```

**Step 2 — Create the udev rule** `/etc/udev/rules.d/99-usb-backup.rules`:

```
ACTION=="add", KERNEL=="sd[a-z][0-9]", \
ATTRS{idVendor}=="0781", \
ATTRS{idProduct}=="5567", \
ENV{DEVTYPE}=="partition", \
RUN+="/usr/local/bin/usb-backup.sh %k", \
TAG+="systemd"
```

**Step 3 — Reload and trigger:**

```bash
sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/usb-backup.sh
sudo udevadm control --reload-rules
sudo udevadm trigger
# Plug in the SanDisk USB; tail the log
sudo tail -f /var/log/usb-backup.log
```

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The kernel sends a **netlink** event to `udevd` when a new block device appears.
2. `udevd` matches the event against all `.rules` files in order.
3. `ACTION=="add"` filters for device insertion (not removal).
4. `KERNEL=="sd[a-z][0-9]"` matches the **first partition** of any SCSI/SATA/USB disk.
5. `ATTRS{idVendor}=="0781"` matches SanDisk.
6. `RUN+="..."` schedules the backup script asynchronously.
7. `udevadm control --reload-rules` re-parses the rules without rebooting.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Hardware Info Sources

| Feature | `/proc` | `/sys` | User-Space CLI |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Introduced in kernel** | 0.99 (1993) | 2.6 (2003) | varies |
| **Mounted at** | `/proc` | `/sys` | N/A |
| **Format** | Flat files | Hierarchical tree | Streamed text |
| **Designed for** | Process info (originally) | Device + driver info | Human use |
| **Writable** | Some files (e.g., `/proc/sys/*`) | Most files (control knobs) | No |
| **Read by** | `ps`, `top`, `sysctl` | `udev`, `systemd`, `lshw` | `lspci`, `lsusb` |
| **Can crash system if edited?** | **YES** for some files | **YES** for many | N/A |
| **Example** | `cat /proc/interrupts` | `ls /sys/class/net` | `lspci -tv` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse `/proc` and `/sys`. Remember the mnemonic: **"Proc = Process, Sys = System devices"**. Writing into `/proc/sysrq-trigger` can crash the kernel — never experiment on a production box!

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Computing Disk Health from S.M.A.R.T.

**Problem (KTU Style — 3 marks):** Explain how to read the reallocated-sector count and predict imminent disk failure.

**Model Solution:**

```bash
sudo smartctl -a /dev/sda
# Look for:
#  5 Reallocated_Sector_Ct   0x0033   200   200   140    Pre-fail  Always       -       0
# 187 Reported_Uncorrect      0x0032   100   100   000    Old_age   Always       -       0
# 197 Current_Pending_Sector  0x0012   200   200   000    Old_age   Always       -       0
```

Interpretation:

$$
\text{Health Score} = 100 - \left(\text{Reallocated} \times 1 + \text{Pending} \times 5 + \text{Uncorrect} \times 10\right)
$$

- **Reallocated Sector Count > 0** → the disk has already remapped bad sectors.
- **Pending Sector Count > 0** → sectors awaiting remap; failure imminent.
- **Uncorrectable Errors > 0** → ECC could not recover data; **replace disk immediately**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
