## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`
**Differentiate between a console and a terminal in Linux. Give one example of each.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

A **console** is the *system-level* text interface through which the Linux kernel emits boot-up messages, kernel logs, and the system login prompt. It is typically mapped to `/dev/console` and is usually **one per system**. Example: the text-mode screen that displays kernel `printk` output during boot.

A **terminal** is a *user-level* text interface that provides a command-line session to a user. It is created on demand, can be many per system, and is normally associated with a PTY device file such as `/dev/pts/0`. Example: a **GNOME Terminal** window running a `bash` shell.

| Aspect | Console | Terminal |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | System messages | User interaction |
| Device | `/dev/console` | `/dev/ttyN` or `/dev/pts/N` |
| Count | One per system | Many (one per session) |

> *(Award full 3 marks for: clear distinction + one example of each. Award 2 marks for definition only, without examples.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]`
**What is a pseudo-terminal (PTY) in Linux? Briefly explain the role of `/dev/ptmx` and `/dev/pts/N`.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

A **pseudo-terminal (PTY)** is a software-emulated terminal pair consisting of a **master** and a **slave** end, used to provide a terminal interface to processes without requiring physical hardware. PTYs are the basis of every modern terminal emulator (GNOME Terminal, xterm), SSH sessions, and tools like `screen`/`tmux`.

- **`/dev/ptmx`** — the *pseudo-terminal multiplexer*. When a program opens `/dev/ptmx`, the kernel dynamically allocates a new PTY pair and returns the master file descriptor. The name of the corresponding slave is obtained by calling `ptsname()`.
- **`/dev/pts/N`** — the *N-th pseudo-terminal slave device*. It behaves like a real TTY to the process attached to it (e.g., the shell), providing `stdin`/`stdout`/`stderr` and supporting the line discipline.

> *(Award 3 marks: PTY definition 1 mark + role of /dev/ptmx 1 mark + role of /dev/pts/N 1 mark.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**Q.A (a)** Explain the **three types of terminals** supported by Linux with suitable examples and the corresponding device files. *(7 marks)*

**Q.A (b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of the Linux TTY subsystem** and explain the role of the **line discipline** in detail. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

The Linux operating system supports three broad categories of terminals:

**1. Physical Terminals (Hardware Terminals)**
- These are real, physical devices connected to the computer.
- Historically: VT100, VT220, ASR-33 Teletype.
- In modern systems: connected via serial ports or USB-to-serial adapters.
- Device files: `/dev/ttyS0`, `/dev/ttyS1` (serial), `/dev/ttyUSB0` (USB-serial).
- Used in embedded systems, servers (IPMI/BMC consoles), and industrial control.

**2. Virtual Consoles (VCs)**
- Linux provides multiple, independent text-mode consoles accessible without a GUI.
- They share the physical keyboard and screen but are logically distinct sessions.
- Switching: `Ctrl+Alt+F1` through `Ctrl+Alt+F12`.
- Device files: `/dev/tty1` through `/dev/tty12` (up to 63 supported by the kernel).
- The kernel's `vt.c` driver manages them. The special device `/dev/tty0` always refers to the **currently active** virtual console.

**3. Pseudo-Terminals (PTYs)**
- A pair of cooperating device files: a **master** and a **slave**.
- Master: `/dev/ptmx` (the multiplexer).
- Slave: `/dev/pts/0`, `/dev/pts/1`, … (`/dev/pts` is a *devpts* filesystem).
- Used by every graphical terminal emulator (GNOME Terminal, xterm, Konsole), SSH daemon, `screen`, `tmux`, and the kernel's `openpty()` library function.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Type | Device File | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Physical | `/dev/ttyS0`, `/dev/ttyUSB0` | Embedded, server management |
| Virtual Console | `/dev/tty1` … `/dev/tty12` | Headless / GUI-switched sessions |
| Pseudo-Terminal | `/dev/pts/N` | GUI terminal emulators, SSH |

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

The **Linux TTY subsystem** is a layered architecture consisting of four logical layers:

**Layer 1 — Application (User Space):** Programs that read from `stdin` and write to `stdout`/`stderr` — for example, `bash`, `vim`, `cat`, `ls`. The application sees the TTY as a regular file descriptor.

**Layer 2 — Line Discipline (Kernel Module `N_TTY`):** This is the **"translator"** layer that sits between the TTY driver and the application. Its responsibilities include:

- **Canonical mode line buffering** — collects input characters until a newline is received, then delivers the entire line to the application via `read()`. (Disabled with `stty -icanon` for raw mode.)
- **Echo** — automatically writes each typed character back to the output so the user can see what they are typing. (Disabled with `stty -echo` for password input.)
- **Special character handling** — translates special key combinations into actions/signals:
  - `Ctrl+C` (INTR) → sends **SIGINT** to the foreground process group.
  - `Ctrl+Z` (SUSP) → sends **SIGTSTP** to the foreground process group.
  - `Ctrl+D` (EOF) → signals end-of-file when the input buffer is empty.
  - `Ctrl+U` (KILL) → erases the current input line.
  - `Ctrl+W` (WERASE) → erases the previous word.
  - `Ctrl+R` (REPRINT) → reprints the current input line.
- **Signal generation** — when the **disconnect** or **hangup** line state changes, the line discipline generates `SIGHUP` and `SIGCONT` to the process group.

**Layer 3 — TTY Driver:** Interfaces with the actual hardware or pseudo-device. Variants include:
- Serial driver (for UARTs — `8250`, `16550A`).
- Virtual console driver (`vt.c`).
- PTY master/slave driver (`pty.c`).
- USB-serial driver.

**Layer 4 — Hardware / Pseudo-Hardware:** The actual keyboard, screen, UART, or PTY pair.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
+-------------+    +----------------+    +-------------+    +----------------+
| Application | -> | Line Discipline| -> | TTY Driver  | -> | Hardware/PTY   |
| (bash, vim) | <- | (N_TTY)        | <- | (serial/vc/ | <- | (/dev/ttyS0,   |
|             |    |                |    |  pty/...)   |    |  /dev/pts/N)   |
+-------------+    +----------------+    +-------------+    +----------------+
```

The **line discipline** is the most important layer for KTU purposes because it implements the visible-to-user semantics of a terminal (echo, line editing, signals). It is configurable via the `termios` API and the `stty` command.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** for **allocating a pseudo-terminal (PTY) pair** in a Linux program. List the relevant system calls in the correct order. *(7 marks)*

**Q.B (b)** Discuss the concepts of **controlling terminal**, **session**, and **process group** in Linux. How do `Ctrl+C` and `Ctrl+Z` interact with these concepts? *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

When a program (e.g., a terminal emulator) wants to provide a PTY-backed shell to a user, it must allocate a new PTY pair. The standard procedure is as follows:

**Step 1 — Open the Multiplexer (1 mark)**
The program opens `/dev/ptmx` to obtain a master file descriptor:
```
master_fd = open("/dev/ptmx", O_RDWR | O_NOCTTY);
```
- The `O_NOCTTY` flag prevents the calling process from accidentally acquiring the new PTY as its own controlling terminal.
- The kernel dynamically allocates a new, unused PTY pair.

**Step 2 — Grant Permissions to the Slave (1 mark)**
The program calls `grantpt(master_fd)` to set the UID ownership of the new slave device file to match the calling process's real UID, and to set its group to `tty`. This is required for security reasons (otherwise the slave would be owned by root and unusable by non-root users).

**Step 3 — Unlock the Slave (1 mark)**
The program calls `unlockpt(master_fd)` to remove the internal lock on the slave device. Without `unlockpt`, any subsequent attempt to `open()` the slave would fail with `EIO`.

**Step 4 — Retrieve the Slave's Name (1 mark)**
The program calls `ptsname(master_fd)` to obtain the path of the new slave device — for example, `/dev/pts/3`. This path is then used by the child process to open the slave.

**Step 5 — Fork a Child Process (1 mark)**
```
pid = fork();
if (pid == 0) {
    /* child becomes the session leader and runs the shell */
}
```

**Step 6 — In the Child, Create a New Session (1 mark)**
The child calls `setsid()` to:
- Become the leader of a new session.
- Disassociate from any controlling terminal.
- Become the leader of a new process group.

This is essential — without `setsid()`, the child would inherit the parent's controlling terminal, and the terminal emulator would have a confused terminal hierarchy.

**Step 7 — Open the Slave as the Controlling Terminal (1 mark)**
The child opens the slave device name **without** `O_NOCTTY`:
```
slave_fd = open(slave_name, O_RDWR);
```
- This open makes the slave the child's **controlling terminal** automatically.
- The child then calls `dup2(slave_fd, 0)`, `dup2(slave_fd, 1)`, `dup2(slave_fd, 2)` to make the slave the stdin, stdout, and stderr.
- Finally, the child calls `execve("/bin/bash", argv, envp)` to run the shell.

**Step 8 — In the Parent, Relay I/O (1 mark, included in flow)**
The parent (terminal emulator) keeps the master fd open and:
- Uses `read(master_fd, ...)` to read output from the shell.
- Uses `write(master_fd, ...)` to send keystrokes to the shell.
- Uses `select()` / `poll()` / `epoll` for non-blocking I/O multiplexing.

> [!TIP]
> A higher-level wrapper is provided by the glibc function `openpty(int *amaster, int *aslave, char *name, struct termios *termp, struct winsize *winp)` declared in `<pty.h>`. It performs steps 1–4 automatically.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Session (1.5 marks):**
- A **session** is a collection of one or more process groups, established by the `setsid()` system call.
- Every process in Linux belongs to **exactly one session**.
- A session is identified by its **session ID (SID)**, which is the PID of the session leader (the process that called `setsid()`).
- Typical use: a single login session from a user contains all the processes launched by that user's shell.

**Process Group (1.5 marks):**
- A **process group** is a collection of one or more processes, established by `setpgid()` / `setpgrp()`.
- Identified by the **process group ID (PGID)**.
- Used for **job control**: the shell places the foreground pipeline in one process group and each backgrounded job in its own process group.

**Controlling Terminal (2 marks):**
- A session has **at most one controlling terminal** (POSIX requires exactly one for an interactive session).
- It is established automatically when a session leader opens a terminal device file *without* `O_NOCTTY`.
- The controlling terminal is inherited by all processes in the session unless explicitly closed.
- It is the terminal that:
  - Provides `stdin`/`stdout`/`stderr` to the foreground process group.
  - Receives keyboard input from the user.
  - Sends terminal-generated signals (SIGINT, SIGTSTP, SIGHUP).

**How `Ctrl+C` and `Ctrl+Z` interact (2 marks):**

| Key | Line Discipline Action | Signal Sent | Default Handler Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Ctrl+C` | Recognize as INTR char | **SIGINT** to **foreground process group** | Terminate the process |
| `Ctrl+Z` | Recognize as SUSP char | **SIGTSTP** to **foreground process group** | Stop (suspend) the process |

- The line discipline attached to the controlling terminal watches the input stream.
- When it sees the configured INTR or SUSP character, it does **not** deliver it to the application's `read()`. Instead, it calls `kill_pgrp()` internally to send the signal to the **foreground process group** of the controlling terminal.
- **Background process groups do NOT receive these signals** — they are insulated. This is the entire point of foreground/background separation in job control.
- The foreground process group can be changed by the shell using `tcsetpgrp()` when the user runs `fg` or `bg` to switch jobs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Misconception cleared:** `Ctrl+C` does NOT kill "the terminal" or "the shell". It sends SIGINT to the **foreground process group**, which by default is the currently running command (e.g., a `sleep 100` or `find /`). The shell itself is a *background* process group relative to the foreground command and survives.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing /dev/console with /dev/tty** — many students write "the console is the terminal"; they are different. The console is *system-level*; the terminal is *user-level*. (Lose 1–2 marks if not distinguished.)
> 2. **Forgetting `setsid()` in the PTY allocation sequence** — the child *must* call `setsid()` before opening the slave; otherwise, it will inherit the parent's controlling terminal, which causes chaos. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **Stating that `Ctrl+C` kills the shell** — it kills the *foreground process group*, NOT the shell. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 4. **Mixing up master/slave** — the *master* is held by the terminal emulator; the *slave* is the one the shell reads from. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 5. **Drawing the TTY stack with the line discipline on the wrong side** — the line discipline sits *above* the TTY driver and *below* the user application. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 6. **Omitting `/dev/pts/` filesystem** — when mentioning PTYs, always note that `/dev/pts/N` lives on the `devpts` pseudo-filesystem, mounted at `/dev/pts`. (Lose 0.5 mark.)

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — memorize for KTU exams.**

- ✅ **Console** = `/dev/console`; one per system; *system-level* I/O.
- ✅ **Terminal** = `/dev/ttyN` (VC) or `/dev/pts/N` (PTY); *user-level* I/O; many per system.
- ✅ **TTY** = *TeleTypewriter*; the kernel subsystem that abstracts all character I/O devices.
- ✅ **Three terminal types:** Physical (`/dev/ttyS0`, `/dev/ttyUSB0`), Virtual Console (`/dev/tty1`–`/dev/tty63`), Pseudo-Terminal (`/dev/ptmx` + `/dev/pts/N`).
- ✅ **Virtual console switch keys:** `Ctrl+Alt+F1` … `Ctrl+Alt+F12` (default 12; kernel supports up to 63).
- ✅ **PTY pair:** master `/dev/ptmx` (held by the emulator) ↔ slave `/dev/pts/N` (held by the shell).
- ✅ **PTY allocation sequence:** `open(/dev/ptmx)` → `grantpt()` → `unlockpt()` → `ptsname()` → `fork()` → in child: `setsid()` → `open(slave)` → `dup2(0,1,2)` → `execve(shell)`.
- ✅ **TTY architecture layers (top to bottom):** Application → Line Discipline (`N_TTY`) → TTY Driver → Hardware/PTY.
- ✅ **Line discipline (`N_TTY`)** handles: canonical buffering, echo, special chars (`^C, ^Z, ^D, ^U, ^W`), signal generation.
- ✅ **Canonical mode** = line-buffered; **Non-canonical (raw) mode** = unbuffered. Toggle with `stty icanon` / `stty -icanon`.
- ✅ **`stty -a`** prints all current terminal settings — *must-memorize command* for KTU.
- ✅ **`tty`** prints the file name of the current controlling terminal.
- ✅ **Session** = collection of process groups; **Process group** = collection of processes; **Controlling terminal** = the TTY attached to a session.
- ✅ **`setsid()`** creates a new session and disassociates from the controlling terminal.
- ✅ **`Ctrl+C`** → line discipline sends **SIGINT** to the **foreground process group** (NOT the shell).
- ✅ **`Ctrl+Z`** → line discipline sends **SIGTSTP** to the **foreground process group**.
- ✅ **`TERM` environment variable** identifies the terminal type for `terminfo`/`termcap` capability lookup (e.g., `xterm-256color`, `linux`, `vt100`).
- ✅ **`/dev/tty0`** = the *currently active* virtual console.
- ✅ **`/dev/tty`** = a synonym for the *current* controlling terminal of the process.
- ✅ **POSIX `openpty()`** in `<pty.h>` is a high-level wrapper that performs steps 1–4 of PTY allocation.
- ✅ **termios API** is the underlying C interface used by `stty` to configure baud rate, parity, special characters, raw mode, etc.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graphical Terminals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Graphical Terminals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Graphical Terminal** (more precisely, a **Graphical Terminal Emulator**) is a software application that runs inside a graphical desktop environment (such as GNOME, KDE Plasma, Xfce, or MATE) and emulates the behaviour of a classical text-mode **physical terminal** (like the DEC VT100/VT220) within a graphical window. Internally, it creates and manages a **Pseudo-Terminal (PTY)** pair — a master/slave device pair provided by the Linux kernel — through which it communicates bidirectionally with a child process, typically a command shell (`bash`, `zsh`, `fish`).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> A *Graphical Terminal* is a windowed application that simulates a text terminal while providing graphical conveniences such as tabs, split panes, font selection, colour schemes, transparency, and clickable hyperlinks. It is the standard interface used by Linux desktop users to interact with the shell on distributions that boot into a GUI (e.g., Ubuntu Desktop, Fedora Workstation, Manjaro KDE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Graphical Terminal** as a *modern telephone booth inside a shopping mall*:
- The **mall** is your Linux desktop (GNOME/KDE).
- The **booth** is the terminal window — it isolates a private "voice channel" so you can talk to the building's manager (the kernel/shell).
- The **telephone wire** inside the booth is the **PTY device** that carries your keystrokes in and the manager's replies out.
- The **glass walls** of the booth are the GUI features: tabs (multiple calls at once), the directory (scrollback buffer), coloured lights (syntax highlighting), and an answering machine (logging).

Without the booth, you would have to shout across an open field (a raw TTY on a server); with the booth, you have a private, organised, and pleasant space to work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 Focus**
> The Module 2 scope of "Graphical Terminals" includes:
> 1. Distinction between text consoles, virtual terminals, and graphical terminals.
> 2. Popular terminal emulators on Linux (GNOME Terminal, Konsole, xterm, Tilix, etc.).
> 3. Internal architecture (PTY, X11/Wayland, shell interaction).
> 4. Configuration files, environment variables, and basic troubleshooting.
> 5. Terminal multiplexing as a productivity layer (`tmux`/`screen`).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics / Constants

- **Standard terminal dimensions:** **80 columns × 24 rows** (legacy VT100 default).
- **Modern colour depths supported:** **8 colours**, **256 colours**, and **24-bit true colour** (≈ 16.7 million).
- **Default scrollback buffer size:** typically **512 to 10,000 lines** (configurable).
- **Default PTY buffer size (Linux kernel):** **4096 bytes** per canonicalisation buffer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Why Graphical Terminals Exist — The Historical 'Why'

In the 1970s–80s, engineers interacted with Unix via **serial terminals** (teletypewriters, then DEC VT100s). Each terminal was a physical box connected by an RS-232 cable to a central computer. The operating system exposed serial lines as **character devices** under `/dev/ttyS0`, `/dev/ttyS1`, etc.

When **graphical workstations** appeared (Sun, SGI, then Linux+X11), users still wanted the same text-mode workflow — scripting, compilers, `vi`, `make`. The solution was a **terminal emulator**: a graphical window that *pretends* to be a VT100 to the shell, but is really just another X11/Wayland application.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why emulators and not the "real thing"?**
> Modern shells and tools (e.g., `vim`, `htop`, `tmux`, `fzf`) are *terminal-aware*. They issue ANSI/VT escape sequences (cursor moves, colours, mouse reports). A graphical terminal emulator must faithfully reproduce a well-defined subset of these sequences. This is exactly what the **terminfo** database formalises.

### 2.2 The Three-Layer Architecture

A graphical terminal is not a single program — it is a stack of cooperating components:

**Layer 1 — Display Server (`Xorg` / `Wayland`):** Owns the physical screen, keyboard, and mouse. Draws windows, routes input events.

**Layer 2 — Window Manager / Desktop Environment (`GNOME Shell`, `KWin`, `XFWM`):** Manages window placement, decorations, and the taskbar. Launches terminal emulators on user request.

**Layer 3 — Terminal Emulator (`gnome-terminal`, `konsole`, `xterm`):** The actual program. It:
- Creates a window via the display server.
- Allocates a **PTY pair** (`/dev/ptmx` master ↔ `/dev/pts/N` slave).
- `fork()`s and `exec()`s the user's shell (e.g., `/bin/bash`).
- Translates keystrokes → PTY writes, and PTY reads → window redraws.
- Interprets escape sequences from the shell for colouring, cursor positioning, etc.

### 2.3 Anatomy of a Pseudo-Terminal (PTY)

A **PTY** is a kernel-managed pair of character devices that act as a bidirectional pipe:

- **Master side** (`/dev/ptmx`): held by the terminal emulator. Reads application output, writes user input.
- **Slave side** (`/dev/pts/N`): held by the child shell. Reads user input, writes output.

The kernel inserts **line discipline** processing on the master side — this is where `Ctrl+C` (SIGINT), `Ctrl+Z` (SIGTSTP), `Ctrl+\` (SIGQUIT), and canonical mode line buffering live.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point**
> Whenever a question asks *"How does the shell know you pressed Ctrl+C?"*, the correct answer is: **the terminal emulator writes the byte 0x03 to the master PTY; the line discipline on the master side translates it into a SIGINT signal delivered to the foreground process group of the slave PTY.**

### 2.4 KTU Cheat Sheet — Graphical Terminal Concepts

| # | Concept | Key Value / Term | Purpose / Use |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | PTY master device | `/dev/ptmx` | Multiplexing endpoint held by emulator |
| 2 | PTY slave device | `/dev/pts/N` | Connected to the child shell |
| 3 | Line discipline | `N_TTY` (default) | Translates keys → signals, handles canonical mode |
| 4 | Display server | `Xorg` (X11) or `Wayland` | Owns screen, keyboard, mouse |
| 5 | Environment variable | `$TERM` | Tells apps which terminal type to emulate (e.g., `xterm-256color`) |
| 6 | Environment variable | `$DISPLAY` | Identifies X11 server (e.g., `:0`) |
| 7 | Environment variable | `$COLORTERM` | Set to `truecolor` or `24bit` for 16M-colour apps |
| 8 | terminfo capability | `co` (columns), `li` (lines) | Query screen size via `tput co` / `tput li` |
| 9 | terminfo capability | `colors` | Number of colours supported |
| 10 | Common emulators | `gnome-terminal`, `konsole`, `xterm`, `tilix`, `alacritty`, `kitty` | GUI terminal applications |
| 11 | Multiplexers | `tmux`, `screen`, `byobu` | Persistent sessions inside one terminal |
| 12 | Config file (Xresources) | `~/.Xresources` | xterm/rxvt font, colour, geometry settings |
| 13 | Config file (dconf) | `~/.config/gnome-terminal/profiles` | GNOME Terminal profiles |
| 14 | Config file (KDE) | `~/.config/konsolerc` | Konsole session and profile settings |
| 15 | Common shortcut | `Ctrl+Shift+T` | New tab in most emulators |
| 16 | Common shortcut | `Ctrl+Shift+C` / `Ctrl+Shift+V` | Copy / Paste in GUI emulators |
| 17 | Special key | `Ctrl+C` → SIGINT | Interrupt foreground process |
| 18 | Special key | `Ctrl+D` → EOF | Signal end-of-input to shell |

### 2.5 Comparison Matrix — Graphical Terminals vs Related Interfaces

| Feature | Physical Terminal (VT100) | Linux Virtual Console (TTY) | Graphical Terminal Emulator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardware | Real serial terminal | Built-in kernel console | Software window |
| Access path | RS-232 serial line | Direct kernel driver | X11/Wayland window |
| Number of sessions | One per physical box | 6–12 (Ctrl+Alt+F1…F12) | Unlimited (one per window/tab) |
| GUI features | None | None | Tabs, splits, colours, fonts |
| Default device | `/dev/ttyS0` | `/dev/tty1`…`/dev/tty6` | `/dev/pts/N` |
| Shell startup | Via `getty` | Via `getty` (agetty) | Via emulator's spawn command |
| Modern usage | Museum / embedded | Server recovery | **Daily Linux desktop use** |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DevOps & Cloud:** SSH into remote servers from `gnome-terminal`; pipe logs; use `tmux` so sessions survive network drops.
- **Embedded Linux:** Open multiple serial/SSH sessions side-by-side in Tilix while flashing firmware in one pane and monitoring `dmesg` in another.
- **Cybersecurity:** Run tools like `nmap`, `wireshark -k`, `sqlmap` inside split panes; copy/paste exploit payloads.
- **Data Science:** Use `ipython`/`jupyter-console` with true-colour prompt highlighting (Pygments style `monokai`).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

### 3.1 Walkthrough — Lifecycle of a Graphical Terminal Window

**Scenario:** A user double-clicks the GNOME Terminal icon on Ubuntu 24.04. Trace the entire chain of events from the click to the interactive shell prompt.

**Step 1 — Display server is already running.**
At boot, `gdm3` (GNOME Display Manager) launches and starts `Xorg` (or `mutter` on Wayland) on display `:0`. It loads `libinput` for keyboard/mouse.

**Step 2 — Desktop shell launches `gnome-terminal`.**
The GNOME Shell process (`gnome-shell`) executes `gnome-terminal` (a GLib/GTK application) using `execvp()`. A new process PID, say **4711**, is created.

**Step 3 — `gnome-terminal` opens a window via the display server.**
Using X11 calls (`XOpenDisplay`, `XCreateSimpleWindow`) or Wayland protocols (`wl_compositor` create_surface), it requests a window of geometry (e.g.) **80 columns × 24 rows**.

**Step 4 — Pseudo-terminal pair is allocated.**
The emulator calls `posix_openpt(O_RDWR | O_NOCTTY)` to obtain the master FD, then `grantpt()` and `unlockpt()` to set up permissions, and `ptsname()` to discover the slave path (e.g., `/dev/pts/3`).

$$
\text{master\_fd} \xleftarrow{\text{posix\_openpt}} /dev/ptmx \quad ; \quad \text{slave\_path} \xleftarrow{\text{ptsname}} /dev/pts/3
$$

**Step 5 — `fork()` creates a child process.**
The child inherits the master FD. Both parent and child now hold it.

**Step 6 — Child sets up stdio and `exec()`s the shell.**
The child calls `setsid()` (to become session leader), opens the slave PTY on `stdin`, `stdout`, `stderr`, and then `execvp("/bin/bash", ...)`. The slave PTY becomes the child's controlling terminal.

**Step 7 — Parent closes the slave and starts the I/O loop.**
The emulator closes its copy of the slave FD. It enters an event loop:
- `select()` / `poll()` on the master FD for output.
- GTK event callbacks for keystrokes → `write()` to master FD.
- X11/Wayland `expose` events → redraw the visible character grid using Pango/Cairo.

**Step 8 — User types `ls` and presses Enter.**
Keystrokes `l`, `s` are written to the master. The line discipline buffers them in canonical mode. The Enter key inserts `\n` (LF, 0x0A); the line discipline delivers the full line `"ls\n"` to the shell's `read()` call on the slave.

**Step 9 — Shell executes `ls` and writes results back.**
`bash` parses, forks `ls`, waits, and writes its output to the slave. The kernel line discipline echoes characters to the master as well, so the emulator can display them.

**Step 10 — Emulator parses escape sequences and renders.**
If the shell prints `"\033[1;32mhello\033[0m"`, the emulator's VT parser recognises `\033[1;32m` as *"bold green foreground"* and paints `hello` in green. This is where **terminfo** capabilities such as `setaf` (set foreground) and `bold` are looked up.

**Step 11 — User closes the window.**
The emulator sends `SIGHUP` to the shell's process group, waits for it to exit, closes the master FD, and the kernel automatically cleans up the slave PTY (revoking `/dev/pts/3`).

### 3.2 Walkthrough — Resolving the `$TERM` Variable

The shell sets `$TERM` from the login environment, which in turn was set by the emulator. To discover the *actual* terminal type:

**Step 1 — Inspect the variable.**

$$
\text{TERM} = \texttt{xterm-256color}
$$

**Step 2 — Look up the terminfo entry.**

The C library reads the compiled terminfo database at `/etc/terminfo/x/xterm-256color` (or `/lib/terminfo/...`).

**Step 3 — Query capabilities.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{tput co} &\rightarrow 80 \quad (\text{columns}) \\
\texttt{tput li} &\rightarrow 24 \quad (\text{lines}) \\
\texttt{tput colors} &\rightarrow 256 \quad (\text{colour count}) \\
\texttt{tput bold} &\rightarrow \text{ANSI escape } \backslash\backslash 033[1m \quad (\text{enter bold mode}) \\
\texttt{tput setaf 2} &\rightarrow \backslash\backslash 033[32m \quad (\text{set foreground to green})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — If a programme (say `vim`) prints garbage** instead of colours, it means `$TERM` is wrong (e.g., set to `dumb`). Fix it with:

$$
\texttt{export TERM=xterm-256color}
$$

### 3.3 Walkthrough — Configuring a Custom GNOME Terminal Profile

**Goal:** Create a profile named *KTU-Lab* with green-on-black colours and 2000-line scrollback.

**Step 1 — Open GNOME Terminal → hamburger menu → Preferences → Profiles → Add.**

**Step 2 — Name the profile** `KTU-Lab`.

**Step 3 — Set built-in scheme.** Choose "Green on black" from *Colors* tab.

**Step 4 — Set scrollback.** In *Scrolling* tab, tick *Unlimited* or enter **2000** in *Scrollback lines*.

**Step 5 — Make it the default.** Tick the star icon next to the new profile.

**Step 6 — Persist via `dconf`.** The same settings are stored at:

$$
\texttt{org.gnome.Terminal.ProfilesList} \rightarrow \texttt{default} = \text{‘b1d…-…’}
$$

This can be scripted with:

$$
\texttt{gsettings\ set\ org.gnome.Terminal.ProfilesList\ default\ 'b1d…'}
$$

### 3.4 Walkthrough — Diagnosing "Black Squares" Instead of Unicode Box-Drawing

A common KTU practical-exam scenario: `tmux` or `vim` displays rectangles instead of lines.

**Step 1 — Check installed fonts.**

$$
\texttt{fc-list \textbar{} grep -i 'DejaVu\ \textbar{}\ Powerline\ \textbar{}\ Nerd'}
$$

**Step 2 — Install a font with box-drawing glyphs.**

$$
\texttt{sudo apt install fonts-dejavu fonts-noto-color-emoji}
$$

**Step 3 — Set the terminal font in the emulator's preferences** to a font that includes U+2500 (─) and U+2502 (│) glyphs.

**Step 4 — Set `LANG` to UTF-8** in the shell:

$$
\texttt{export\ LANG=en\_US.UTF-8}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify with a test command.**

$$
\texttt{printf\ '\textbackslash u2502\textbackslash n'}
$$

The output should be a clean vertical bar `│`, not `?` or a black square.

### 3.5 Comparison Table — Popular Graphical Terminal Emulators

| Emulator | Toolkit | Renderer | Resource Use | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GNOME Terminal** | GTK 4 | Cairo | Medium | Default on Ubuntu/Fedora Workstation |
| **Konsole** | Qt 6 | QPainter | Medium | Default on KDE Plasma; split-view, profiles |
| **xterm** | Xlib (raw X) | X11 primitives | Very low | The "reference" emulator; classic VT100 |
| **rxvt-unicode (urxvt)** | Xlib + perl | X11 primitives | Very low | Tabbed via perl extension, fast |
| **Tilix** | GTK 3 | Cairo | Medium | Quake-mode, drag-drop panes, persistent layouts |
| **Terminator** | GTK 3 / VTE | Cairo | Medium | Grid of terminals; broadcast input |
| **Alacritty** | Rust + OpenGL | GPU | Low | GPU-accelerated, config in TOML |
| **Kitty** | Python + OpenGL | GPU | Low | Tiling, ligatures, image protocol, kitty.conf |
| **st (suckless)** | Xlib | X11 primitives | Minimal | Hackable, < 2000 lines of C |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Graphical Terminal Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER["USER INPUT LAYER"]
        KBD[Keyboard and Mouse]
    end

    subgraph DISPLAY["DISPLAY SERVER LAYER"]
        XORG["Xorg X11 Server or Wayland Compositor"]
        WM["Window Manager KWin or Mutter or XFWM"]
    end

    subgraph EMULATOR["TERMINAL EMULATOR LAYER gnome-terminal or konsole or xterm"]
        WIN[Graphical Window 80x24 char grid]
        VT[VT100 Emulation Engine and Parser]
        PTYM[Master PTY FD /dev/ptmx]
    end

    subgraph KERNEL["LINUX KERNEL LAYER"]
        LD[Line Discipline N_TTY canonical mode]
        PTYS[Slave PTY /dev/pts/N]
        SIG[Signal Generator SIGINT SIGTSTP SIGHUP]
    end

    subgraph SHELL["SHELL LAYER"]
        BASH["bash or zsh or fish"]
        APPS["Child processes ls vim tmux python"]
    end

    subgraph CFG["CONFIGURATION LAYER"]
        TRM[term variable]
        DISP[DISPLAY variable]
        TRMINFO[terminfo database /etc/terminfo]
        XRES[.Xresources and dconf and konsolerc]
    end

    KBD --> XORG
    XORG --> WM
    WM --> WIN
    WIN --> VT
    VT --> PTYM
    PTYM --> LD
    LD --> PTYS
    PTYS --> BASH
    BASH --> APPS
    APPS --> PTYS
    PTYS --> LD
    LD --> PTYM
    PTYM --> VT
    VT --> WIN
    WIN --> XORG
    LD -.generates.-> SIG
    SIG -.delivered to.-> APPS
    TRM -.describes.-> VT
    TRMINFO -.queried by.-> VT
    XRES -.configures.-> WIN
    DISP -.targets.-> XORG
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Data Path of a Keystroke

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User presses key] --> B[Kernel keyboard driver scancode to keycode]
    B --> C[Display server delivers KeyPress event to focused window]
    C --> D[gnome terminal receives XEvent in GTK loop]
    D --> E[Translate key into character using current input method]
    E --> F[Write character to master PTY FD via write sys call]
    F --> G[Line discipline in canonical mode buffers the line]
    G --> H{Special control character?}
    H -- yes Ctrl C --> I[Generate SIGINT to foreground process group]
    H -- no --> J[Deliver to slave PTY read by shell]
    J --> K[Shell parses and runs command]
    K --> L[Shell writes output to slave PTY]
    L --> M[Line discipline echoes and forwards to master]
    M --> N[Emulator read returns bytes]
    N --> O[VT parser extracts escape sequences]
    O --> P[Render glyphs into the window using Pango and Cairo]
    P --> Q[Display server composites window to screen]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map — Environment Variables Around the Terminal

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SET_BY["Set by terminal emulator at spawn"]
        TERM["TERM equals xterm-256color or screen-256color"]
        DISPLAY["DISPLAY equals colon zero"]
        COLORTERM["COLORTERM equals truecolor or 24bit"]
        TERM_PROG["TERM_PROGRAM equals gnome-terminal or konsole"]
        TERM_PROG_VER["TERM_PROGRAM_VERSION equals 3.50 or 24.05"]
    end

    subgraph SET_BY_SHELL["Set by shell startup files"]
        PS1["PS1 prompt string escape sequences for colour"]
        LANG["LANG equals en_US.UTF-8"]
    end

    subgraph CONSUMED_BY["Consumed by applications"]
        VIM["vim uses TERM and COLORTERM"]
        HTOP["htop uses TERM and colors capability"]
        TMUX["tmux uses TERM and screen-256color"]
        PYTHON["python uses LANG for stdout encoding"]
    end

    TERM --> VIM
    TERM --> HTOP
    TERM --> TMUX
    COLORTERM --> VIM
    TERM_PROG --> PS1
    LANG --> PYTHON
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
Define a *graphical terminal emulator*. List any **four** commonly used graphical terminals in Linux.

**Model Answer:**
A graphical terminal emulator is a software application that runs within a graphical desktop environment and emulates a text terminal inside a window. It allocates a pseudo-terminal (PTY) pair, spawns a shell, and translates user input and shell output between the GUI and the shell using VT100/ANSI escape sequences.
Four common examples: **GNOME Terminal**, **Konsole**, **xterm**, **Tilix** (also accept: Terminator, Alacritty, Kitty, rxvt-unicode, st).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
What is the role of the **line discipline** in a Linux terminal?

**Model Answer:**
The line discipline (`N_TTY`) is a kernel layer between the master and slave PTY. It performs:
1. **Canonical mode processing** — buffers input until a newline (`\n`) is received.
2. **Signal generation** — translates special characters into signals: `Ctrl+C` → `SIGINT`, `Ctrl+Z` → `SIGTSTP`, `Ctrl+\` → `SIGQUIT`, `Ctrl+D` → EOF.
3. **Echo** — when `ECHO` is set, characters are echoed back to the master so the emulator can display them.
4. **Input processing flags** — `ICANON`, `ISIG`, `ECHO`, `IXON`/`IXOFF` for XON/XOFF flow control.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, Pattern A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a graphical terminal emulator** in Linux. Describe the role of the **display server**, the **terminal emulator**, the **pseudo-terminal (PTY) pair**, the **line discipline**, and the **shell**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the following environment variables used by graphical terminals: **`$TERM`**, **`$DISPLAY`**, **`$COLORTERM`**, **`$TERM_PROGRAM`**, **`$LANG`**. For each, state what sets it and what consumes it. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q A (a)

**Step 1 — Define the layers (2 marks).**
A graphical terminal emulator sits on top of three Linux subsystems: the **display server** (X11/Wayland) for drawing, the **Linux kernel's TTY/PTY subsystem** for character I/O, and the **user's shell** for command execution. The display server and PTY layers are decoupled; the emulator is the bridge.

**Step 2 — Display Server (1 mark).**
The display server (e.g., `Xorg`) owns the physical display, keyboard, and mouse. The terminal emulator is just another X11 client. It receives `KeyPress` and `ButtonPress` events from the server and issues drawing requests (via Cairo/Pango) to render the character grid.

**Step 3 — Terminal Emulator (1 mark).**
The emulator (e.g., `gnome-terminal`) is a GUI application written using GTK/Qt. It maintains a 2-D character grid model (rows × columns), parses VT100/ANSI escape sequences, and translates them into Pango text-drawing calls.

**Step 4 — Pseudo-Terminal Pair (1 mark).**
On startup, the emulator opens `/dev/ptmx` to obtain a master file descriptor, then `grantpt()`/`unlockpt()` to activate a slave device under `/dev/pts/N`. The master is held by the emulator; the slave becomes the controlling terminal of the forked shell.

**Step 5 — Line Discipline (1 mark).**
Between master and slave sits the `N_TTY` line discipline. It buffers input in canonical mode, generates signals for `Ctrl+C`/`Ctrl+Z`/`Ctrl+D`, and echoes typed characters back to the master for display.

**Step 6 — Shell (1 mark).**
The emulator `fork()`s a child, redirects the child's `stdin/stdout/stderr` to the slave PTY, and `exec()`s the user's shell (`/bin/bash`). The shell now reads commands from the slave and writes output back to the same slave.

> *[Refer to Section 4.1 Block Diagram for the visual representation.]*

#### Model Solution — Q A (b)

| Variable | Set By | Consumed By | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| `$TERM` | Emulator at startup | `vim`, `tmux`, `htop`, `ncurses` apps, `tput` | `xterm-256color`, `screen-256color` |
| `$DISPLAY` | Emulator / display manager | X11 client programs | `:0` or `:0.0` |
| `$COLORTERM` | Emulator (optional) | `vim`, `ls --color`, prompt tools | `truecolor` or `24bit` |
| `$TERM_PROGRAM` | Emulator (optional) | Oh-My-Zsh, themes, scripts | `gnome-terminal`, `konsole`, `Apple_Terminal` |
| `$LANG` | Login / shell `~/.profile` | `python`, `perl`, `grep`, locale-aware tools | `en_US.UTF-8`, `C`, `POSIX` |

**Detailed explanations (each ~1.4 marks):**

- **`$TERM`** — Tells text-mode applications *which* terminal type to emulate, so they can look up capabilities in the **terminfo** database. Wrong values cause `tput` to fail and curses apps to misbehave. Set in the emulator's profile; can be overridden in `~/.bashrc`.
- **`$DISPLAY`** — Identifies the X11 server and screen. Format is `host:displaynumber.screennumber`; the local display is `:0`. Only meaningful on X11; Wayland compositors usually set it for compatibility but XWayland is the actual provider.
- **`$COLORTERM`** — Advertises true-colour (24-bit) support. Apps like `vim` and `ls` check this to decide whether to emit `#RRGGBB` SGR sequences.
- **`$TERM_PROGRAM`** — A non-standard but widely supported hint used by frameworks (e.g., Oh-My-Zsh, Powerlevel10k) to tailor prompts to the exact emulator.
- **`$LANG`** — Sets the system locale, especially character encoding (`UTF-8`). Critical for displaying non-ASCII characters and for Python's default encoding.

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, Pattern B]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast: **physical terminal**, **Linux virtual console (TTY)**, and **graphical terminal emulator**. Use a tabular form covering at least **six** distinguishing parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the use of the following utilities with reference to graphical terminals:
**(i)** `tput`, **(ii)** `stty`, **(iii)** `tmux`, **(iv)** `resize`. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q B (a)

| # | Parameter | Physical Terminal (VT100) | Linux Virtual Console (TTY) | Graphical Terminal Emulator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Form | Hardware device | Software (kernel-driven) | Software (X11/Wayland) |
| 2 | Device file | `/dev/ttyS0`, `/dev/ttyUSB0` | `/dev/tty1`…`/dev/tty6` | `/dev/pts/N` |
| 3 | Access | Serial cable / RS-232 | Direct kernel driver | Window inside GUI |
| 4 | Default count | 1 per serial port | 6–12 (Ctrl+Alt+F1…F12) | Unlimited (window/tab) |
| 5 | Shell startup | `getty` on serial line | `getty` (agetty) on TTY | Emulator spawns shell via `fork`/`exec` |
| 6 | GUI features | None | None | Tabs, splits, fonts, transparency |
| 7 | Common use today | Embedded/legacy | Server console recovery | **Daily Linux desktop** |
| 8 | Example commands | N/A (hardware) | `chvt 1`, `openvt` | `gnome-terminal`, `konsole` |

*(Any six rows from above earn full marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Q B (b)

**(i) `tput`** — Queries and uses the **terminfo** capabilities of the terminal declared in `$TERM`. Examples: `tput cols` → number of columns; `tput setaf 2` → ANSI escape to set foreground to green; `tput bold` → enter bold mode. It is the standard way to make shell scripts terminal-independent. **(~1.75 marks)**

**(ii) `stty`** — Configures the **line discipline** of the *current* terminal. With no arguments, it prints settings; with flags, it changes them. Examples: `stty -echo` disables echo; `stty rows 40 cols 132` resizes the TTY; `stty intr ^C` rebinds the interrupt key. Run on the slave PTY, it changes how the master-side line discipline behaves. **(~1.75 marks)**

**(iii) `tmux`** — A **terminal multiplexer** that runs a persistent server inside any single terminal window. It allows:
- Multiple *windows* (tabs).
- Multiple *panes* (splits) within each window.
- *Detaching* a session and *reattaching* it later — useful over flaky SSH links.
- Scriptable configuration via `~/.tmux.conf`. **(~1.75 marks)**

**(iv) `resize`** — A utility that prints the shell commands needed to update `$LINES` and `$COLUMNS` (and the kernel's idea of the TTY size) after a window resize. Output is typically `eval $(resize)`. Useful when running full-screen apps (`vi`, `less`, `top`) inside a manually resized window. **(~1.75 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing TTY with PTY:** Many students write *"terminal is `/dev/tty`"* without distinguishing physical TTYs, virtual consoles, and PTYs. Always specify *which* device file you mean.
> 2. **Forgetting the line discipline:** The line discipline is the *kernel layer* that turns `Ctrl+C` into `SIGINT`. Writing *"the emulator sends the signal"* loses marks — it is the *kernel*, not the emulator.
> 3. **Wrong `$TERM` value:** Do not write `xterm` if the emulator supports 256 colours; use `xterm-256color`. This distinction is often worth 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up X11 and Wayland:** Modern Fedora/Ubuntu use Wayland by default. Mentioning only X11 is acceptable but earns less credit than acknowledging both.
> 5. **Omitting the master/slave distinction in PTY:** A common 2-mark loss. Always say *"the master is held by the emulator; the slave is held by the shell."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **graphical terminal emulator** is a windowed application that emulates a text terminal inside a GUI desktop.
- The **three-layer stack** is: Display Server (X11/Wayland) → Terminal Emulator (GTK/Qt) → Pseudo-Terminal (kernel TTY subsystem) → Shell.
- A **Pseudo-Terminal (PTY)** is a master/slave character-device pair created via `/dev/ptmx` and `/dev/pts/N`.
- The **line discipline** (`N_TTY`) buffers input, generates signals, and performs echo.
- **Common emulators:** GNOME Terminal, Konsole, xterm, rxvt-unicode, Tilix, Terminator, Alacritty, Kitty, st.
- **Key environment variables:** `$TERM`, `$DISPLAY`, `$COLORTERM`, `$TERM_PROGRAM`, `$LANG`.
- **Key shortcuts:** `Ctrl+Shift+T` (new tab), `Ctrl+Shift+C/V` (copy/paste), `Ctrl+C` (SIGINT), `Ctrl+D` (EOF).
- **Configuration files:** `~/.Xresources` (xterm/rxvt), `~/.config/gnome-terminal/profiles` (dconf), `~/.config/konsolerc` (KDE).
- **Multiplexers:** `tmux` and `screen` allow persistent, detachable, splittable sessions.
- **Standard size:** **80 × 24** characters is the historical default; modern emulators support any geometry.
- **Colour depths:** 8, 256, and 24-bit (true colour ≈ 16.7 million).
- **`terminfo`** is the database that lets applications adapt to the terminal type declared in `$TERM`.
- A **physical terminal** uses `/dev/ttyS*`; a **virtual console** uses `/dev/tty*`; a **graphical terminal** uses `/dev/pts/*`.
- Always specify **master vs slave** when answering PTY questions — the kernel and the *emulator vs shell* roles hinge on this distinction.
- For KTU practicals, be ready to: open `gnome-terminal`/`konsole`, change `$TERM`, set `LANG`, install `tmux`, and use `tput`/`stty` in a shell script.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accessing CLI via a Linux Console Terminal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Accessing CLI via a Linux Console Terminal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Linux Console Terminal** is a text-based input/output interface that provides direct, low-level access to the Linux kernel's command-line interpreter (shell) without requiring a graphical desktop environment. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Linux console subsystem is mediated through three primary abstractions:

- **TTY (Teletypewriter)** — The historical hardware terminal abstraction, now emulated in software.
- **Virtual Console (VC)** — A kernel-provided full-screen text terminal accessible via keyboard shortcuts (e.g., `Ctrl + Alt + F1` through `F6`).
- **Pseudo-Terminal (PTY)** — A software-emulated terminal pair (master/slave) used by GUI terminal emulators (e.g., GNOME Terminal, Konsole, xterm).

The Linux kernel exposes these terminals as character device files under the `/dev/` directory hierarchy, with a default allocation of **6 virtual consoles** plus one graphical console.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The console terminal is the *primary* access channel to the CLI when the X Window System (graphical display) is unavailable — a critical recovery and administration mode in production Linux systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Walkie-Talkie to the Kernel"

Imagine the Linux kernel is a giant control room deep inside a secure building. The **console terminal** is your **walkie-talkie** that lets you speak directly to the operator in that room.

- The **physical terminal** of the 1970s was like a real, heavy radio — one per operator.
- The **virtual console** is like having **six walkie-talkies on the same desk**, switchable with a button (`Alt + F1…F6`).
- The **pseudo-terminal (PTY)** is like a **smartphone walkie-talkie app** — it pretends to be a hardware device, but it is entirely software.

When you "press the button" (press `Enter`), your keystrokes travel through this terminal abstraction, reach the **shell** (the command-line interpreter), and the shell relays your request to the kernel — which is the true "control room operator."

## 1.3 Glossary of Key Terms

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Terminology for KTU Board Exams:**

| Term | Standard Definition (KTU Style) |
|---|---|
| **Console** | The primary physical or virtual screen/keyboard directly connected to the system. |
| **Terminal** | A device or software that sends keystrokes to and receives output from a host. |
| **TTY** | Short for *Teletype*; the Unix device abstraction for a text terminal. |
| **VC** | Virtual Console; a kernel-managed, full-screen text terminal. |
| **PTY** | Pseudo-Terminal; a software pair (master + slave) emulating a TTY. |
| **Shell** | The command-line interpreter (e.g., Bash, Zsh) that processes user commands. |
| **Login Prompt** | The `login:` / `password:` interface presented by `getty`. |

## 1.4 Default System Metrics (Bolded Standards)

- **Default number of Virtual Consoles in Linux:** **6** (tty1 through tty6).
- **Graphical Console assignment (when X is running):** usually **tty7** (historically) or `tty1` on systemd-based distros.
- **Keyboard shortcut to switch to ttyN:** `Ctrl + Alt + FN` (where N = 1 to 6).
- **Keyboard shortcut to return to graphical session:** `Ctrl + Alt + F7` (or `F1` in systemd default).
- **Standard TTY device file range:** `/dev/tty1` to `/dev/tty63`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of Linux Console Access

The Linux CLI access model is built in **four conceptual layers**, each with a distinct role:

### Layer 1 — The Hardware (Physical Layer)
- Historically: RS-232 serial terminals, DEC VT100, IBM 3270.
- Modern: The laptop's built-in keyboard + monitor, attached via USB/HDMI/PS-2 to the system bus.
- The kernel's **keyboard controller driver** and **VGA/DRM framebuffer driver** service this layer.

### Layer 2 — The Virtual Console (Kernel Subsystem)
- Implemented in the kernel source as the **VT (Virtual Terminal) subsystem** (file: `drivers/tty/vt/`).
- Allocates **6 default VCs** (configurable via kernel parameter `nr_consoles=`).
- Each VC has its own independent screen buffer, font, and input device.
- Switching between VCs is handled by the **KBD driver** listening for `Alt + FN` chording.

### Layer 3 — The Line Discipline (Software Layer)
- The **line discipline** (historically `n_tty`) sits between the TTY driver and the application.
- Responsibilities:
  - **Canonical (cooked) mode:** line buffering, editing (`Backspace`, `Ctrl+U`).
  - **Non-canonical (raw) mode:** used by editors like `vim` and `nano`.
  - Signal generation (`Ctrl+C` → `SIGINT`, `Ctrl+Z` → `SIGTSTP`).
- Configurable via the `stty` command and the `termios` data structure.

### Layer 4 — The Shell (User-Space Layer)
- The shell (`/bin/bash`, `/bin/zsh`, `/bin/sh`) reads characters from the TTY, parses them, and executes them.
- The shell prompt (e.g., `student@ktu:~$`) is the user's primary feedback signal.

## 2.2 The `getty` Process — The Login Gatekeeper

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concept for KTU:** Every console login is mediated by the `getty` (or its modern replacement `agetty`) process.

The sequence of events when a virtual console is activated:

1. **Kernel boot** spawns the first process — `init` (PID 1) — which is now usually `systemd`.
2. `systemd` starts **`getty@.service`** units (one per VC).
3. `getty` opens the TTY device (`/dev/tty1` … `/dev/tty6`).
4. `getty` displays the banner: `KTU Linux 2024 hostname tty1` followed by the `login:` prompt.
5. The user types a username → `getty` invokes the **`login`** program.
6. `login` prompts for password → calls **`PAM`** (Pluggable Authentication Modules) for verification.
7. On success, `login` sets environment variables (`HOME`, `SHELL`, `USER`, `PATH`) and **`exec`s the user's shell**.
8. The shell takes over the TTY and displays the prompt.

## 2.3 Comparison Matrix: Console vs Terminal Emulator

| Feature | Virtual Console (`ttyN`) | Terminal Emulator (GUI) |
|---|---|---|
| **Access method** | `Ctrl + Alt + FN` | Click application icon |
| **Requires X Server?** | **No** | Yes |
| **Device file** | `/dev/ttyN` | `/dev/pts/N` |
| **Type** | Real TTY | Pseudo-Terminal (PTY) |
| **Number available** | **6** (default) | Limited by PTY allocation (default 4096) |
| **Used in server administration** | **Primary** (no GUI) | Requires X forwarding |
| **Recovery from X crash** | **Yes** — always available | No |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Commands & Shortcuts for Board Exam:**

| Command / Shortcut | Purpose | Example / Note |
|---|---|---|
| `tty` | Prints the file name of the terminal connected to standard input. | Output: `/dev/pts/0` or `/dev/tty1` |
| `who` | Lists currently logged-in users and their TTYs. | `root  tty1  2024-12-01 09:00` |
| `who am i` | Shows current session's login info. | |
| `w` | Shows who is logged in and what they are doing. | |
| `Ctrl + Alt + F1…F6` | Switch to virtual console N. | |
| `Alt + F7` (or F1) | Return to graphical session. | |
| `chvt N` | Switch VC from command line (root). | `sudo chvt 3` |
| `openvt -s command` | Run a command in a new VC. | |
| `deallocvt N` | Free a virtual console. | |
| `stty -a` | Display all terminal line settings. | Exam-favorite for termios |
| `echo $TERM` | Show the terminal type variable. | Output: `xterm-256color` |

## 2.5 The `/dev` Directory — Console Device Mapping

Linux exposes every terminal as a **character device file** under `/dev`:

- `/dev/console` — The system console (kernel messages, emergency output).
- `/dev/tty` — The controlling terminal of the current process (a synonym for the calling process's TTY).
- `/dev/tty1` … `/dev/tty6` — The six default **Virtual Consoles**.
- `/dev/ttyS0`, `/dev/ttyS1` — **Serial ports** (UART-based physical terminals).
- `/dev/pts/0`, `/dev/pts/1` — **Pseudo-Terminal Slaves** used by GUI emulators.
- `/dev/ptmx` — The **Pseudo-Terminal Master Multiplexer** (open to obtain a new PTY pair).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Server Administration:** Datacenter servers rarely have a graphical display; admins access them via **serial console** or **SSH** (which itself uses a PTY internally).
- **Embedded Systems:** IoT devices and routers use **serial TTYs** (e.g., `/dev/ttyS0` at **115200 baud**) as their primary CLI access.
- **System Recovery:** When the X Server crashes or a graphics driver fails, the **virtual consoles are the lifeline** to drop into single-user mode and fix the system.
- **DevOps / CI-CD Pipelines:** Build agents, Docker containers, and cloud VMs boot into CLI by default and expose access via PTY-based tools (e.g., `docker exec -it`).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Process Flow

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: From System Boot to CLI Access

Below is the **full, step-by-step trace** of what happens when a Linux system is powered on and the user accesses the CLI via a virtual console. This is the kind of analytical reasoning the KTU board expects in 14-mark questions.

> **Step 1 — Power-On and BIOS/UEFI Hand-off**
> The motherboard firmware performs POST (Power-On Self-Test), then locates the boot device and loads the **GRUB** bootloader (or systemd-boot on newer systems). GRUB reads its configuration from `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` and presents the kernel selection menu.

> **Step 2 — Kernel Initialization**
> The Linux kernel decompresses, initializes CPU subsystems, memory management, and mounts the **initramfs** (initial RAM filesystem). The kernel command line (passed by GRUB) is parsed; if the parameter `nomodeset` or `text` is present, X is not started later.

> **Step 3 — Init Process Spawning**
> The kernel executes `/sbin/init` (usually a symbolic link to `/lib/systemd/systemd`). This is **PID 1** — the ancestor of all user-space processes. systemd reads its unit files and begins parallel service activation.

> **Step 4 — systemd-logind Activation**
> systemd activates `systemd-logind.service`, which manages user sessions, seats, and VTs. It listens on **D-Bus** for seat attachment events.

> **Step 5 — getty Service Activation**
> systemd starts six instances of **`getty@.service`** (templated unit), one per virtual console:
> - `getty@tty1.service` → opens `/dev/tty1`
> - `getty@tty2.service` → opens `/dev/tty2`
> - … up to `getty@tty6.service`

> **Step 6 — `agetty` Opens the TTY and Waits**
> Each `agetty` process:
> 1. Configures the line discipline via `tcsetattr()` (sets canonical mode, echo).
> 2. Clears the screen and prints the issue file (`/etc/issue`).
> 3. Prints the **`login:`** prompt.
> 4. Blocks on `read()` syscall, waiting for keyboard input.

> **Step 7 — User Enters Credentials**
> The user types `student` and presses `Enter`. The line discipline assembles a complete line (in canonical mode), and `agetty` receives it. The user is then prompted for the password.

> **Step 8 — `login` Program Invocation**
> `agetty` `exec()`s `/bin/login` (or `util-linux`'s login), passing the username. The `login` program calls **`PAM`** (`pam_unix.so`) to verify the password against `/etc/shadow`.

> **Step 9 — Environment Setup**
> On successful authentication, `login` reads:
> - `/etc/passwd` → sets `USER`, `UID`, `GID`, `HOME`, `SHELL`.
> - `/etc/profile` and `~/.bash_profile` → sources shell startup files.
> - Sets the controlling TTY via `ioctl(TIOCSCTTY)`.

> **Step 10 — Shell Execution**
> `login` `exec()`s the user's login shell (e.g., `/bin/bash`). The shell prints the **MOTD** (Message of the Day from `/etc/motd`) and the prompt:
> ```
> student@ktu-lab:~$ 
> ```
> The user is now **at the CLI**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Identifying Your Current TTY

A classic KTU 14-mark sub-question asks: *"How do you identify the terminal you are currently using? Explain with commands and output."*

| Command | Output (Example) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| `tty` | `/dev/pts/0` | We are in a **pseudo-terminal** (GUI emulator) |
| `tty` | `/dev/tty1` | We are on **Virtual Console 1** |
| `tty` | `/dev/ttyS0` | We are on a **serial console** |
| `who` | `student tty1  2024-12-01 09:00 (:0)` | Logged into VC1 from display :0 |
| `who am i` | `student pts/0  2024-12-01 09:15` | Current session only |
| `echo $TERM` | `xterm-256color` | Terminal type capability string |
| `stty -a` | (long output of termios flags) | All line discipline settings |
| `logname` | `student` | The login name of the user |
| `users` | `student root` | All logged-in users |
| `w` | Table with USER, TTY, FROM, LOGIN@, IDLE, JCPU, PCPU, WHAT | Active sessions and processes |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Switching Between Consoles and Emulators

> **Scenario:** You are in the graphical desktop (Wayland/X11) and need to drop to a raw console to run `fsck`.

- **Step 1:** Press `Ctrl + Alt + F3`. The keyboard driver raises a VC-switch event.
- **Step 2:** The VT subsystem saves the current VC's framebuffer and restores VC3's framebuffer.
- **Step 3:** The GPU re-paints the screen with VC3's contents (or the saved text-mode buffer).
- **Step 4:** You see a `login:` prompt for tty3.
- **Step 5:** Log in as `root`, run `umount /home && fsck /dev/sda2`.
- **Step 6:** To return to the GUI, press `Ctrl + Alt + F1` (or `F2`, depending on distro).
- **Step 7:** The X Server (or Wayland compositor) is notified of the VT switch by `systemd-logind` and re-attaches.

## 3.4 Worked Example: TTY-Related `stty` Settings

Question: *"What is the effect of the command `stty intr ^C`?"*

> **Analytical Solution:**
>
> - The `stty` command modifies the **line discipline** settings stored in the kernel's `termios` structure for the current TTY.
> - The `intr` (interrupt) character is the key sequence that generates a `SIGINT` signal to the foreground process group, typically terminating the running process.
> - Default value: `^C` (ASCII 0x03, ETX).
> - The command `stty intr ^C` **re-affirms** that `Ctrl+C` will generate `SIGINT` (it is a no-op if already set).
> - To **change** it, one could write `stty intr ^G` (now `Ctrl+G` sends `SIGINT`).

Other high-yield `stty` settings:

- `stty -echo` — disable character echo (useful in scripts).
- `stty raw` — switch to non-canonical, no-echo mode (used by `vim`).
- `stty rows 50 cols 132` — manually set terminal size.
- `stty speed 115200` — set the baud rate for serial TTYs.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Linux Console Access Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Keyboard Input] --> B[Kernel Keyboard Driver]
    B --> C[Line Discipline n_tty]
    C --> D[TTY Driver VT Subsystem]
    D --> E1[Virtual Console tty1]
    D --> E2[Virtual Console tty2]
    D --> E3[Virtual Console tty3]
    D --> E4[Virtual Console tty4]
    D --> E5[Virtual Console tty5]
    D --> E6[Virtual Console tty6]
    D --> F1[Serial Console ttyS0]
    D --> G1[Pseudo Terminal PTY pts 0]
    D --> G2[Pseudo Terminal PTY pts 1]
    E1 --> H1[agetty login Prompt]
    E2 --> H1
    E3 --> H1
    E4 --> H1
    E5 --> H1
    E6 --> H1
    F1 --> H1
    H1 --> I[PAM Authentication]
    I --> J[Shell Bash Zsh]
    J --> K[Command Execution]
    K --> L[Kernel System Call]
    L --> M[Hardware Response]
    M --> N[Output to TTY]
    N --> D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Login Process Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[System Boot] --> B[Kernel Loads]
    B --> C[systemd PID 1 Starts]
    C --> D[getty Service Activated]
    D --> E[agetty Opens dev ttyN]
    E --> F[Display login Prompt]
    F --> G{User Enters Username}
    G --> H[Prompt for Password]
    H --> I{PAM Verify}
    I --> J[Authentication Failed]
    J --> F
    I --> K[Set Environment Variables]
    K --> L[exec User Shell]
    L --> M[Shell Prompt Displayed]
    M --> N[User at CLI]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — VC vs PTY Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need CLI Access] --> B{X Server Running?}
    B -- No --> C[Use Virtual Console tty1 to tty6]
    B -- Yes --> D{Need Multiple Sessions?}
    D -- No --> E[Switch to VC Ctrl Alt FN]
    D -- Yes --> F[Open Terminal Emulator]
    F --> G[Each Tab gets a PTY]
    C --> H[agetty login]
    E --> H
    G --> I[bash spawned on pts N]
    H --> J[Shell Prompt]
    I --> J
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Terminal Subsystem Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[User Space] --> A1[Shell bash zsh]
    A1 --> A2[Application vi nano top]
    A0 --> B0[Kernel Space]
    B0 --> B1[Line Discipline n_tty]
    B1 --> B2[TTY Driver VT and Serial]
    B2 --> B3[Character Device dev ttyN]
    B0 --> C0[Hardware Layer]
    C0 --> C1[Keyboard Controller]
    C0 --> C2[VGA Framebuffer GPU]
    C0 --> C3[Serial UART 16550]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A, Q1a]**

**Q1. Define the following terms with respect to Linux console access:**
**(a) TTY (b) Virtual Console (c) Pseudo-Terminal**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **(a) TTY (Teletypewriter):** The Unix/Linux device abstraction representing a text-based terminal. The name originates from the electromechanical teletypewriters used as early computer terminals. In Linux, every TTY is exposed as a character device file under `/dev/` (e.g., `/dev/tty1`, `/dev/pts/0`). TTYs handle both input (keyboard) and output (text display) for command-line interaction. **(1 mark)**

- **(b) Virtual Console (VC):** A full-screen, kernel-managed text terminal that allows multiple independent login sessions on a single physical display. Linux provides **6** default VCs (`tty1` to `tty6`), switchable via `Ctrl + Alt + F1…F6`. VCs do not require a graphical display server and are the primary access method in headless server environments. **(1 mark)**

- **(c) Pseudo-Terminal (PTY):** A software-emulated terminal pair consisting of a **master** side (e.g., `/dev/ptmx`) and a **slave** side (e.g., `/dev/pts/0`). PTYs are used by GUI terminal emulators and by network services like SSH to provide TTY-like behavior without real hardware. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B, Q1b]**

**Q2. List the keyboard shortcuts used to access virtual consoles in Linux. How many default virtual consoles does Linux provide, and what command displays the file name of the current terminal?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Keyboard shortcuts:** `Ctrl + Alt + F1` through `Ctrl + Alt + F6` to access virtual consoles 1 to 6 respectively; `Ctrl + Alt + F7` (or `F1` on systemd-based distros) to return to the graphical session. **(1 mark)**
- **Default number of virtual consoles:** **6** (tty1 to tty6). **(1 mark)**
- **Command to display the current terminal:** `tty` — outputs the device file path of the terminal connected to standard input, e.g., `/dev/tty1` or `/dev/pts/0`. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A, Q7]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the architecture of the Linux console subsystem. Describe the roles of the keyboard driver, line discipline, TTY driver, and shell with a neat diagram.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) With suitable examples, explain the difference between a Virtual Console and a Pseudo-Terminal. List the device files associated with each.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q7(a)

**Architecture of the Linux Console Subsystem (7 marks):**

The Linux console subsystem is a layered architecture spanning hardware, kernel, and user space:

1. **Keyboard Driver (Kernel Layer):** Located in `drivers/input/keyboard/`. Captures raw scancodes from the keyboard hardware (PS/2 or USB), translates them into keycodes, and pushes events to the input subsystem. Handles modifier keys (`Ctrl`, `Alt`, `Shift`) and the special chording for VC switching (`Alt + FN`). **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Line Discipline (`n_tty`):** Sits above the TTY driver. In **canonical mode**, it buffers input until `Enter` is pressed, supports line editing (`Backspace`, `Ctrl+U` to kill line, `Ctrl+W` to delete word), and generates signals on special characters (`Ctrl+C` → `SIGINT`, `Ctrl+Z` → `SIGTSTP`, `Ctrl+\` → `SIGQUIT`). In **raw mode**, characters are passed immediately to the reading process — used by `vim`, `less`, etc. Configurable via `stty` and the `termios` API. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **TTY Driver (VT Subsystem):** Implemented in `drivers/tty/vt/`. Manages the **6 virtual consoles**, each with its own screen buffer, cursor position, and font. Provides the `ioctl` interface (`VT_OPENQRY`, `KDSETMODE`) and the chvt mechanism. The display side writes to the **framebuffer** (text mode uses VGA memory directly; graphics mode uses DRM/KMS). **(2 marks)**

4. **Shell (User Space):** The shell (`/bin/bash`) is an ordinary user-space process. It opens the TTY as its standard input/output/error, reads command lines, parses them, performs globbing and variable expansion, and forks child processes for execution. The shell's prompt (e.g., `student@ktu:~$`) is the visual feedback confirming CLI readiness. **(1.5 marks)**

5. **Diagram (Block representation):**
```
+-------------------+      +-------------------+      +-------------------+
|   Keyboard HW     | ---> | Keyboard Driver   | ---> |  Line Discipline  |
+-------------------+      +-------------------+      +-------------------+
                                                                |
                                                                v
                      +-------------------+      +-------------------+
                      |  User Process     | <--- |    Shell (bash)   |
                      +-------------------+      +-------------------+
                                                                ^
                                                                |
                      +-------------------+      +-------------------+
                      |  Framebuffer /    | <--- |   TTY / VT       |
                      |  DRM/KMS Display  |      |   Driver         |
                      +-------------------+      +-------------------+
```
**(0.5 mark for diagram)**

#### Model Solution for Q7(b)

**Virtual Console vs Pseudo-Terminal (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Virtual Console (VC) | Pseudo-Terminal (PTY) |
|---|---|---|
| **Device file** | `/dev/tty1` … `/dev/tty6` | `/dev/pts/0`, `/dev/pts/1`, … (slave) + `/dev/ptmx` (master) |
| **Nature** | Real TTY — kernel allocates a dedicated screen buffer and input device per VC. | Software-emulated — a master/slave pair of character devices. |
| **Access method** | `Ctrl + Alt + FN` keyboard shortcut. | Opened by a GUI terminal emulator (GNOME Terminal, xterm) or by SSH/script. |
| **Requires X?** | **No** — works in pure text mode. | Yes (for GUI emulators); PTYs are used internally by SSH too. |
| **Number** | **6** (kernel default, configurable). | Up to **4096** by default (`kernel.pty.max`). |
| **Use case** | Headless servers, system recovery, kernel messages. | GUI terminal windows, remote shells, automation scripts. |

**Examples:**
- A system administrator fixing a broken graphics driver drops to **`/dev/tty3`** via `Ctrl+Alt+F3` and logs in. This is a **VC**.
- A developer opens **GNOME Terminal** on the desktop; the new tab is backed by **`/dev/pts/0`**. This is a **PTY**.
- An SSH connection from a remote client allocates a PTY pair on the server: the SSH daemon holds the master (`/dev/ptmx`), and the user's shell runs on the slave (`/dev/pts/N`). **(1 mark for examples, 6 marks for table/comparison)**

---

### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B, Q8]**

**(a) Describe the step-by-step login process from kernel boot to the appearance of the shell prompt. Name the key processes involved (`systemd`, `agetty`, `login`, `PAM`, `bash`) and their functions.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the following commands with examples: `tty`, `who`, `w`, `stty -a`, `chvt`. How does the `stty` command modify the line discipline?** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q8(a)

**Login Process from Boot to Shell (7 marks):**

1. **Kernel Boot (1 mark):** The kernel initializes hardware, mounts the root filesystem, and executes `/sbin/init` — usually `systemd` (PID 1). The kernel command line is parsed; parameters like `text` or `nomodeset` suppress X.

2. **systemd Activation (1 mark):** systemd reads unit files from `/etc/systemd/system/` and `/lib/systemd/system/`. It starts the **`getty@.service`** templated unit six times, one for each VC.

3. **agetty Execution (1 mark):** Each `agetty` instance:
   - Opens the TTY device (`/dev/ttyN`) with `O_RDWR | O_NOCTTY`.
   - Configures the line discipline (sets `ICANON`, `ECHO`, `ISIG`).
   - Prints the contents of `/etc/issue` (the pre-login banner).
   - Prints `login:` and blocks on `read()`.

4. **User Input & login Invocation (1 mark):** The user types a username; `agetty` calls `execvp("/bin/login", ...)`, passing the username as an argument.

5. **PAM Authentication (1 mark):** `login` calls **PAM** (`pam_start` → `pam_authenticate` → `pam_acct_mgmt` → `pam_setcred`). The `pam_unix.so` module checks `/etc/shadow`. On success, `pam_open_session` is called.

6. **Environment Setup (1 mark):** `login` reads `/etc/passwd` to set `HOME`, `SHELL`, `UID`, `GID`, and `PATH`. It also sources `/etc/profile` and the user's `~/.bash_profile`.

7. **Shell Spawn (1 mark):** `login` calls `setuid()` and `chdir($HOME)`, then `exec()`s the user's login shell (e.g., `/bin/bash`). The shell prints the prompt and the user is at the CLI.

#### Model Solution for Q8(b)

**Terminal Commands (7 marks):**

1. **`tty`** — Prints the device file name of the terminal connected to stdin.
   ```
   $ tty
   /dev/pts/0
   ```
   Indicates a pseudo-terminal (GUI emulator). If on a VC, it would print `/dev/tty1`. **(1 mark)**

2. **`who`** — Lists all currently logged-in users.
   ```
   $ who
   root     tty1         2024-12-01 09:00
   student  pts/0        2024-12-01 09:15 (:0)
   ```
   Format: `username  tty  login-time  (remote-host)`. **(1 mark)**

3. **`w`** — Combines `who` with current process activity.
   ```
   $ w
    09:30:00 up  2:15,  2 users,  load average: 0.05, 0.10, 0.08
   USER     TTY      FROM             LOGIN@   IDLE   JCPU   PCPU WHAT
   root     tty1                      09:00    1:30   0.05s  0.01s bash
   student  pts/0    :0               09:15    0.00s  0.10s  0.05s w
   ```
   **(1 mark)**

4. **`stty -a`** — Displays all current line discipline and terminal settings.
   ```
   $ stty -a
   speed 38400 baud; rows 50; columns 132; line = 0;
   intr = ^C; quit = ^\; erase = ^?; kill = ^U; eof = ^D; eol = <undef>;
   -icanon -echo ... 
   ```
   Shows baud rate, rows/cols, and all special characters. **(1.5 marks)**

5. **`chvt N`** — Switches to virtual console N (requires root).
   ```
   $ sudo chvt 3
   ```
   Equivalent to pressing `Ctrl + Alt + F3`. The kernel VT subsystem performs the switch. **(1 mark)**

6. **How `stty` Modifies the Line Discipline (1.5 marks):**
   The `stty` command issues `ioctl(fd, TCSETS, &termios)` system calls on the current TTY's file descriptor, modifying the kernel-resident `termios` structure. The `termios` struct contains flags like `c_iflag` (input modes), `c_oflag` (output modes), `c_cflag` (control modes), `c_lflag` (local modes — includes `ICANON`, `ECHO`, `ISIG`), and `c_cc[]` (control character array — `VINTR`, `VQUIT`, `VERASE`, etc.). Each invocation of `stty` reads the current struct, modifies the requested fields, and writes it back, causing immediate behavioral change for all processes reading from that TTY.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing TTY and VC:** Many students write *"TTY is a virtual console."* This is **wrong**. TTY is the *general abstraction*; VC is a *specific kind* of TTY. A PTY is also a TTY, but not a VC.
> 2. **Forgetting `Ctrl+Alt`:** A common mistake is stating the shortcut as just `Alt+F1`. The correct shortcut is **`Ctrl + Alt + F1`**. This costs 1 full mark.
> 3. **Not mentioning `agetty`:** Examiners specifically look for `getty`/`agetty` in the login process. Writing only "login program displays prompt" without naming the process loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring PAM:** Authentication is **not** done directly by `login`; it is delegated to **PAM**. Omitting this loses at least 1 mark in a 14-mark question.
> 5. **Mixing up device files:** Students often write `/dev/pts` for VCs or `/dev/tty1` for PTYs. Memorize the mapping.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Accessing CLI via Linux Console Terminal**

- **Three terminal types to remember:** TTY (general), VC (kernel-allocated, 6 default), PTY (software-emulated, used by GUI/SSH).
- **Default VCs:** **6** — `tty1` to `tty6`. Switch with **`Ctrl + Alt + F1…F6`**. Return to GUI with **`Ctrl + Alt + F7`** (or F1 on systemd distros).
- **The `/dev` device file map:**
  - `/dev/console` — system console
  - `/dev/ttyN` — virtual console N
  - `/dev/ttyS0` — serial port
  - `/dev/pts/N` — pseudo-terminal slave
  - `/dev/ptmx` — pseudo-terminal master multiplexer
- **The login process chain:** `kernel → systemd (PID 1) → agetty → login → PAM → exec shell`. Know each step and which file/device is involved.
- **The line discipline (`n_tty`)** is the software layer that handles `Ctrl+C` (SIGINT), canonical mode line buffering, and `stty` configuration.
- **Essential commands:** `tty`, `who`, `w`, `stty -a`, `chvt N`, `deallocvt N`, `openvt -s cmd`, `logname`, `users`.
- **`stty`** modifies the kernel's `termios` structure via `ioctl(TCSETS)`. Affects the **current TTY only**, not all terminals.
- **VC vs PTY:** VCs do **not** require X Server; PTYs (in GUI form) **do**. Use VCs for server recovery; use PTYs for desktop work.
- **Real-world application:** Datacenter server management, embedded Linux debugging, system recovery from graphical failures, and SSH/PTY-based remote access.
- **The shell is a user-space process** — it is not part of the TTY subsystem itself; it merely uses the TTY as its standard I/O channel.
- **KTU's favorite sub-questions:** "Differentiate VC and PTY", "Explain the login process", "Explain the role of `agetty` and `PAM`", "List console device files in `/dev`."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accessing CLI via Graphical Terminal Emulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Accessing CLI via Graphical Terminal Emulation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Terminal Emulator (KTU Syllabus Definition):** A *terminal emulator* is a software application that replicates the function of a classical text-based computer terminal within a graphical user interface (GUI) environment. It provides a windowed Command Line Interface (CLI) session by emulating hardware terminals such as the **DEC VT100**, **VT220**, or **ANSI-compliant** devices, and translates user keystrokes into system-level input for the underlying Linux shell.
