```mermaid
    Q1 --> FILES1[Reads etc profile and dot bash profile]
    Q2 --> FILES2[Reads only dot bashrc]
    Q3 --> FILES3[Reads BASH ENV only]
    Q4 --> FILES4[Reads etc profile and dot bash profile]
```

---

### 4.4 Startup File Search Precedence — Linear Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Bash wants user login file] --> B{dot bash_profile?}
    B -->|Found| C[Use this]
    B -->|Not found| D{dot bash_login?}
    D -->|Found| E[Use this]
    D -->|Not found| F{dot profile?}
    F -->|Found| G[Use this]
    F -->|Not found| H[No file executed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading hint:** This **left-to-right waterfall** is the most-commonly-tested visual in KTU paper 1 (Module 3). Memorise the order: **`.bash_profile` → `.bash_login` → `.profile`**.

---

### 4.5 Diagnostic Command Flow — Identifying Your Current Shell State

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Run diagnostic commands] --> B[echo zero dollar sign]
    A --> C[echo dash dash dollar sign]
    A --> D[shopt login_shell]
    B --> B1[Output dash bash equals login shell]
    B --> B2[Output bash equals non login shell]
    C --> C1[Contains i equals interactive]
    C --> C2[No i equals non interactive]
    D --> D1[on equals login]
    D --> D2[off equals non login]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Differentiate between `~/.bash_profile` and `~/.bashrc`. When is each sourced?**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `~/.bash_profile` | `~/.bashrc` |
|---|---|---|
| **Read by** | Login shells only | Interactive non-login shells |
| **Sourced by** | `/etc/profile` chain | Direct execution on new terminal |
| **Contains** | `$PATH`, env vars, one-time login setup | Aliases, prompt (`$PS1`), shell functions |
| **Trigger** | `ssh`, console login, `su -` | Opening terminal emulator, `bash` |

The key distinction is that `~/.bash_profile` is executed **once per session**, while `~/.bashrc` is executed **once per terminal window**. A standard convention is to source `.bashrc` from within `.bash_profile` so login shells inherit non-login customisations.

*(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark trigger, 1 mark comparison.)*

---

**Q2. What is the role of the `/etc/profile` file? How does it differ from `~/.bashrc`?**
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Module 3]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`/etc/profile` is a **system-wide initialisation script** located in the root filesystem (`/etc`). It is executed by **login shells of all users** and is responsible for:
1. Setting the **global `$PATH`** and other environment variables (`LANG`, `umask`).
2. Sourcing additional scripts from `/etc/profile.d/*.sh` (modular, package-specific config).
3. Applying **system-wide defaults** (motd, ulimits, locale).

**Difference from `~/.bashrc`:**

| Feature | `/etc/profile` | `~/.bashrc` |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | All users | Single user |
| Owner | root | The user |
| Edit permission | Requires `sudo` | User-writable |
| Run when | Login shells | Interactive non-login |
| Purpose | System policies, env vars | Personal aliases, prompt |

*(Award: 1 mark role, 1 mark features, 1 mark comparison.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both alternatives must be from the same module. Below, we present the module-level internal choice.

---

## ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

### (a) Explain the different types of shells available in Linux with their key features. Compare Bash with the Bourne Shell. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A **shell** in Linux is a command-line interpreter that bridges the user and the kernel. The major types are:

1. **Bourne Shell (`sh`)** — Created by Stephen Bourne (1977) at Bell Labs. It is the **POSIX standard** and the ancestor of all modern shells. It supports variables, control flow (`if`, `for`, `while`), and I/O redirection, but **lacks** command-line editing, history, and aliases.

2. **Bourne Again SHell (`bash`)** — Created by Brian Fox for the GNU Project (1989). It is a **superset of `sh`**, adding:
   - Command history (`~/.bash_history`).
   - Tab completion.
   - Aliases and shell functions.
   - Arrays, `$(( ))` arithmetic.
   - `[[ ]]` extended test command.
   - Process substitution `<( )` and `>( )`.

3. **C Shell (`csh` / `tcsh`)** — Bill Joy's C-syntax shell, popular in academia. `tcsh` adds command-line editing and history. **Weak for scripting** due to quirks.

4. **Korn Shell (`ksh`)** — David Korn (1983). Merges `sh` and `csh` features; superior arithmetic and associative arrays. Default on AIX.

5. **Z Shell (`zsh`)** — Highly customisable; default on macOS Catalina+. Powers the `oh-my-zsh` framework with plugins and themes.

6. **Fish** — User-friendly, syntax highlighting, autosuggestions out of the box.

**Comparison: Bash vs Bourne Shell (`sh`)**

| Feature | `sh` (Bourne) | `bash` |
|---|---|---|
| Command history | ❌ | ✅ (`history`, `!n`) |
| Tab completion | ❌ | ✅ |
| Aliases | ❌ | ✅ |
| Arrays | ❌ | ✅ |
| `[[ ]]` test | ❌ | ✅ |
| Arithmetic `$(( ))` | Limited | Full |
| POSIX compliance | ✅ | ✅ (superset) |
| Default on Linux | `/bin/sh` (often symlink) | `/bin/bash` |

**Conclusion:** Bash is the **de facto standard** for Linux, retaining full POSIX compatibility while adding interactive productivity features.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for listing types, 2 marks for key features, 2 marks for comparison table, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

### (b) Describe the complete startup process of Bash for a login shell. List all the files sourced in the correct order and explain the purpose of each. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

When a user logs in via **console**, **`ssh`**, or **`su -`**, the system invokes Bash with `argv[0]` beginning with a hyphen (`-bash`). This triggers the **login shell bootstrap sequence**:

**Step 1 — `/etc/profile`**
A system-wide initialisation file owned by `root`. It:
- Sets the global `$PATH`.
- Configures `umask`, `ulimit`, locale (`LANG`).
- Sources all scripts in `/etc/profile.d/*.sh` via a `for` loop.

**Step 2 — `/etc/profile.d/*.sh`**
Modular scripts contributed by installed packages. Example: `jdk.sh` sets `$JAVA_HOME`, `bash_completion.sh` enables tab-completion.

**Step 3 — User login file (first match wins)**

Bash searches the user's `$HOME` in this strict order:
$$\tilde{/}.bash\_profile \;\to\; \tilde{/}.bash\_login \;\to\; \tilde{/}.profile$$

- `~/.bash_profile` — **Bash-specific** login file. Most Linux distros place user customisations here.
- `~/.bash_login` — Fallback if `.bash_profile` is missing.
- `~/.profile` — POSIX-sh-compatible fallback (read by `sh` too).

The **first file found is executed**; the others are ignored.

**Step 4 — Explicit sourcing of `~/.bashrc`**
The canonical `~/.bash_profile` ends with:
```bash
if [ -f ~/.bashrc ]; then
    . ~/.bashrc
fi
```
This ensures interactive customisations (aliases, prompt) are inherited.

**Step 5 — `~/.bashrc`**
The user's interactive shell configuration. Contains aliases (`alias ll='ls -la'`), prompt definitions (`PS1`), and shell functions.

**Step 6 — `/etc/bashrc`** (sourced by `.bashrc`)
System-wide defaults for interactive shells.

**Step 7 — Prompt display**
Bash evaluates `$PS1` and renders the first prompt, e.g., `student@ktu-lab:~$`.

**Step 8 — `~/.bash_logout` on exit**
When the user types `exit` or `logout`, Bash sources this file. Common uses: clear screen, kill background jobs, log session time.

**Summary Order:**
```
/etc/profile → /etc/profile.d/*.sh → ~/.bash_profile → ~/.bashrc → /etc/bashrc → [prompt] → ~/.bash_logout
```

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for invocation detection, 2 marks for file order, 2 marks for purpose, 1 mark for logout.)*

---

## ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

### (a) Differentiate between a Login shell and a Non-Login shell. How can you identify the current shell type? Provide examples of commands that trigger each. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A **Login shell** is the first shell invoked when a user **authenticates** with the system. The system passes a leading hyphen in `argv[0]` (e.g., `-bash`) as a flag. A **Non-Login shell** is any subsequent shell started *after* the user is already logged in.

**Identification Commands:**

1. `echo "$0"` → returns `-bash` for login, `bash` for non-login.
2. `shopt login_shell` → returns `on` (login) or `off` (non-login).
3. `echo "$-"` → contains `i` if interactive.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Login Shell | Non-Login Shell |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | `ssh`, console login, `su -` | New terminal, typing `bash` |
| **Reads** | `/etc/profile`, `~/.bash_profile` | Only `~/.bashrc` |
| **`argv[0]`** | Starts with `-` | Does not start with `-` |
| **Frequency** | Once per session | Once per terminal window |
| **Use case** | Setting persistent env vars | Setting aliases, prompt tweaks |

**Commands that trigger each:**

| Command | Login? | Interactive? |
|---|---|---|
| `ssh user@host` | ✅ | ✅ |
| `su - user` | ✅ | ✅ |
| `su user` | ❌ | ✅ |
| `bash --login` | ✅ | ✅ |
| `bash` (at prompt) | ❌ | ✅ |
| Open GNOME Terminal | ❌ | ✅ |
| `bash -c "ls"` | ❌ | ❌ |

**Practical Significance:** Login shells are the right place to set `$PATH` and `$JAVA_HOME` (one-time per session). Non-login shells are the right place for aliases that you want in every new window.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks definition, 2 marks identification, 2 marks comparison, 1 mark examples.)*

---

### (b) Explain the purpose and typical contents of the following startup files: `/etc/profile`, `~/.bash_profile`, `~/.bashrc`, and `~/.bash_logout`. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. `/etc/profile`**
- **Owner:** `root` (system-wide).
- **Read by:** Login shells of all users.
- **Purpose:** Establishes system-wide environment defaults.
- **Typical contents:**
```bash
# System-wide environment
PATH="/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin"
export PATH

# Load profiles from /etc/profile.d
if [ -d /etc/profile.d ]; then
    for f in /etc/profile.d/*.sh; do
        [ -r "$f" ] && . "$f"
    done
fi

# Default umask
umask 022
```

**2. `~/.bash_profile`**
- **Owner:** The user.
- **Read by:** Login shells (Bash-specific).
- **Purpose:** User's personal login-time environment setup.
- **Typical contents:**
```bash
# Personal PATH additions
export PATH="$HOME/bin:$PATH"

# Source bashrc for interactive customisations
if [ -f ~/.bashrc ]; then
    . ~/.bashrc
fi

# Java setup
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-17
```

**3. `~/.bashrc`**
- **Owner:** The user.
- **Read by:** Interactive non-login shells.
- **Purpose:** Aliases, prompt, functions for daily use.
- **Typical contents:**
```bash
# Source global definitions
[ -f /etc/bashrc ] && . /etc/bashrc

# Aliases
alias ll='ls -la'
alias gs='git status'
alias c='clear'

# Coloured prompt
export PS1='\[\033[01;32m\]\u@\h\[\033[00m\]:\[\033[01;34m\]\w\[\033[00m\]\$ '

# History settings
export HISTSIZE=1000
export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
```

**4. `~/.bash_logout`**
- **Owner:** The user.
- **Read by:** Login shells, **only at exit**.
- **Purpose:** Cleanup operations when the session ends.
- **Typical contents:**
```bash
# Clear terminal
clear

# Log session end
echo "Session ended at $(date)"

# Kill any leftover background jobs
[ -n "$(jobs -p)" ] && kill $(jobs -p)
```

**Key Insight:** Among these four files, only `/etc/profile` and `~/.bash_profile` are read by **login shells**. `~/.bashrc` is the **workhorse** for interactive use, and `~/.bash_logout` is the **post-execution** hook.

*(Mark distribution: 1.75 marks per file for purpose + contents.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `.bashrc` and `.bash_profile`:** Many students write that `.bashrc` is read by login shells. **It is not.** Login shells read `.bash_profile`; only interactive *non-login* shells read `.bashrc`. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. **Wrong file precedence:** The order is `.bash_profile` → `.bash_login` → `.profile`. Reversing this order costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting `/etc/profile.d/*.sh`:** Students often list only `/etc/profile` and miss the modular scripts sourced by it.
> 4. **Confusing `source` with `bash`:** `source file` runs in the **current** shell; `bash file` spawns a child. This is a viva favourite.
> 5. **Missing the `argv[0]` hyphen rule:** The leading `-` in `-bash` is what flags a login shell — not the existence of a TTY.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Exam**

- [ ] A **shell** is a command-line interpreter between user and kernel; **Bash** is the Linux default.
- [ ] Bash has **four states**: Login/Non-Login × Interactive/Non-Interactive.
- [ ] A **login shell** is flagged by a leading hyphen in `argv[0]` (i.e., `argv[0] = "-bash"`).
- [ ] **Login shell sources** in order: `/etc/profile` → `/etc/profile.d/*` → `~/.bash_profile` (or `~/.bash_login` or `~/.profile` — first found) → `~/.bashrc` (via explicit `. ~/.bashrc`) → `/etc/bashrc`.
- [ ] **Non-login interactive shell sources** in order: `~/.bashrc` → `/etc/bashrc`.
- [ ] **Non-interactive shell** sources only `$BASH_ENV` (if set); does **not** read `.bashrc` or `.profile`.
- [ ] **`.bash_profile` is read ONCE per session**; `.bashrc` is read **once per terminal window**.
- [ ] The canonical idiom in `.bash_profile`: `[ -f ~/.bashrc ] && . ~/.bashrc`.
- [ ] `source file` (or `. file`) executes in the **current** shell; `bash file` spawns a **child**.
- [ ] `echo "$0"` returns `-bash` for login, `bash` for non-login.
- [ ] `echo "$-"` contains `i` if interactive.
- [ ] `shopt login_shell` returns `on`/`off`.
- [ ] `$PATH` is searched **left-to-right, colon-delimited**; the current directory is **not** searched by default (Unix security feature).
- [ ] `~/.bash_logout` runs **only at exit of a login shell**.
- [ ] **Aliases are NOT expanded in non-interactive scripts** — use functions or scripts instead.
- [ ] Shells in order of age: `sh` (1977) → `csh` (1978) → `ksh` (1983) → `bash` (1989) → `zsh` (1990) → `fish` (2005).
- [ ] `sh` is the **POSIX standard**; Bash is a **POSIX superset**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** When asked to list startup files, always mention the **invocation trigger first**, then the **file list in order**, then the **purpose of each**. This three-part structure is what earns full marks in KTU 7-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using the Shell Prompt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Using the Shell Prompt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shell Prompt (Definition as per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **shell prompt** is the textual interface indicator displayed by the command-line interpreter (shell) in a UNIX/Linux operating system, signalling that the system is ready to accept user input. It is the interactive gateway through which users invoke system utilities, execute programs, and manipulate the operating system environment.

In strict KTU terminology, the prompt is the *output string* printed by the shell to **standard output (stdout)** when it transitions into the **REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop)** state. The technical components that constitute a standard Bash prompt include:

- **Username** — the Linux account currently logged in.
- **Hostname** — the network name of the machine.
- **Current Working Directory (PWD)** — the present path location.
- **Privileged/Non-Privileged Indicator** — `$` (regular user) or `#` (root/superuser).
- **Special Characters** — like `~` (tilde, representing the home directory).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Restaurant Waiter"**
> Imagine you walk into a restaurant. Before you can order, the waiter (the **shell**) approaches your table and says, *"Good evening! May I take your order?"* — that verbal cue is the **prompt**. It tells you the system is **listening, attentive, and ready** to receive your command. If you say, *"I'd like pasta,"* the waiter (shell) **evaluates** your order, **executes** it in the kitchen (kernel), and returns with the result (output) before asking again.

| Shell Element | Real-Life Waiter Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| `$` or `#` symbol | Waiter's name tag (Junior vs. Manager) |
| `user@host` | "Who is asking" (customer identity) |
| `~` or path | "Where the customer is seated" |
| Command typed | The actual order |
| Pressing `<Enter>` | Handing over the order slip |

### 1.3 Standard Prompt Symbols in Linux

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key High-Yield Symbols for KTU Board Exam:**
> - **`$`** → Default prompt for an **ordinary, non-privileged user** (UID ≥ 1000).
> - **`#`** → Prompt for the **root superuser** (UID = 0). Indicates highest privilege.
> - **`%`** → Default prompt in **C-Shell (csh)** and **TC-Shell (tcsh)**.
> - **`>`** → PS2 continuation prompt (appears when a command spans multiple lines).
> - **`~`** → Tilde, an abbreviation for the current user's **home directory** (e.g., `/home/student`).

### 1.4 The Anatomy of a Linux Prompt

A typical Ubuntu/Debian Bash prompt by default appears as:

```
student@ktu-pc:~/Documents$
```

Decoded structurally:

| Segment | Meaning | Example Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `student` | Current username | `student` |
| `@` | The "at" separator (literal) | `@` |
| `ktu-pc` | System hostname | `ktu-pc` |
| `:` | Separator | `:` |
| `~/Documents` | Present working directory | `/home/student/Documents` |
| `$` | Non-root user indicator | `$` |

The equivalent **root** prompt would replace `$` with `#` and the username with `root`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The REPL Lifecycle of the Shell

The shell operates in a continuous, four-phase cycle known as the **REPL loop**. Every command you type goes through these stages:

> [!NOTE]
> **REPL = Read → Evaluate → Print → Loop**

1. **Read** — The shell reads the input string from the keyboard buffer.
2. **Evaluate / Parse** — The shell breaks the line into **tokens** (command, options, arguments).
3. **Execute** — The shell searches for the command in its hash table or `PATH` and forks a child process.
4. **Print** — Any output from the executed program is sent to stdout/stderr.
5. **Loop** — Control returns to step 1; a fresh prompt is displayed.

### 2.2 Generic Command Structure (KTU Favourite)

Every command typed at the prompt follows a strict syntactic pattern:

```
command [options] [arguments]
```

| Component | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Command** | The actual program/utility to invoke | `ls` |
| **Options** | Flags that modify behaviour (start with `-` or `--`) | `-l`, `--all` |
| **Arguments** | Targets the command operates on (files, dirs) | `/home/student` |

**Worked Examples:**

```bash
ls -la /home/student/Documents
```

Here, `ls` is the command, `-la` are two combined options (`-l` long-format and `-a` all files including hidden), and `/home/student/Documents` is the argument.

```bash
cp -v notes.txt backup.txt
```

`cp` is the command, `-v` is the verbose option, and `notes.txt`, `backup.txt` are arguments (source and destination).

### 2.3 Prompt Customization — The `PS1` Environment Variable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The shell prompt is fully **customizable** through special environment variables. The primary one is `PS1` (Prompt String 1). Other secondary prompts include `PS2` (continuation), `PS3` (`select` loop), and `PS4` (debug trace).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield reference** for KTU board questions on the shell prompt. Memorize the escape sequences — they are asked almost every semester.

| Symbol / Sequence | Meaning | Output Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `\u` | Current username | `student` |
| `\h` | Hostname (short, up to first `.`) | `ktu-pc` |
| `\H` | Full hostname (FQDN) | `ktu-pc.cs.ktu.ac.in` |
| `\w` | Current working directory (full path) | `~/Documents` |
| `\W` | Basename of PWD | `Documents` |
| `\d` | Date (Weekday Month Date) | `Mon Jan 15` |
| `\t` | Time (24-hour HH:MM:SS) | `14:32:08` |
| `\T` | Time (12-hour HH:MM:SS) | `02:32:08` |
| `\@` | Time (12-hour AM/PM) | `02:32 PM` |
| `\n` | Newline | (line break) |
| `\$` | UID-based prompt symbol | `$` or `#` |
| `\s` | Name of the shell | `bash` |
| `\v` | Bash version | `5.1` |
| `\j` | Number of background jobs | `0` |
| `\#` | Command number in history | `42` |
| `\!` | History number of this command | `87` |

### 2.5 Common Shell Prompt Configuration Commands

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These commands are **executable in real-time** and frequently appear in KTU lab viva and theory exams.

| Command | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `echo $PS1` | Display the current PS1 string |
| `PS1="> "` | Temporarily set a minimal prompt to `> ` |
| `export PS1="\u@\h:\w\$ "` | Permanently export prompt in current session |
| `source ~/.bashrc` | Reload the bash configuration file |
| `echo $0` | Display the name of the current shell |
| `whoami` | Display the current effective username |
| `hostname` | Display the system hostname |
| `pwd` | Print the current working directory |
| `tty` | Display terminal device name |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Customizing the shell prompt is not just aesthetic — it serves **practical engineering purposes**:

- **DevOps Engineers** embed git branch info (`$(__git_ps1)`) into PS1 to track the current code branch.
- **System Administrators** include the time `\t` to audit when each command was executed during incident response.
- **Multi-server Cluster Admins** colour-code prompts (red for root, green for user) to prevent accidental privilege escalation.
- **Embedded Linux Developers** on resource-constrained boards (Raspberry Pi) use a minimal PS1 to reduce CPU overhead on TTY refresh.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a CS/Operating-Systems topic, the "derivations" below are **prompt construction exercises** — building the prompt character-by-character and verifying its output. Every step is exhaustively written out, exactly as the KTU board examiner expects.

### 3.1 Exercise 1 — Build a Time-Stamped Prompt

**Objective:** Construct a prompt that displays the username, full hostname, current directory, and 24-hour time, ending with the `$` symbol.

**Step 1: Identify the required escape sequences**

From the formula sheet:

- Username → `\u`
- Full hostname → `\H`
- Current directory → `\w`
- Time (24h) → `\t`
- Prompt symbol → `\$`

**Step 2: Compose the PS1 string**

We concatenate the sequences using literal characters (spaces, `:`, `<`, `>`, `[`, `]`) for visual clarity.

$$\text{PS1} = "[\u@\H:\w] \t\$ "$$

**Step 3: Apply the prompt at the shell**

```bash
student@ktu:~$ export PS1="[\u@\H:\w] \t\$ "
```

**Step 4: Observe the rendered output**

After pressing `<Enter>`, the prompt transforms to:

```
[student@ktu-pc.cs.ktu.ac.in:~/Documents] 14:32:08$
```

**Step 5: Verify**

```bash
[student@ktu-pc.cs.ktu.ac.in:~/Documents] 14:32:08$ echo $PS1
[\u@\H:\w] \t$
```

The literal escape sequences are preserved in the variable, but Bash **interprets** them every time the prompt is displayed — a critical board concept.

### 3.2 Exercise 2 — Permanent Modification via `.bashrc`

**Objective:** Make a custom prompt survive across reboots and new terminal sessions.

**Step 1: Locate the user-level Bash configuration file**

The configuration file read by every new interactive non-login shell is:

$$\text{File Path} = \texttt{\textasciitilde/.bashrc} \;\; \equiv \;\; \texttt{/home/username/.bashrc}$$

**Step 2: Append the PS1 assignment using a text editor or `echo`**

```bash
echo 'export PS1="\u@\h [\t] \w\$ "' >> ~/.bashrc
```

**Step 3: Reload the configuration**

```bash
source ~/.bashrc
```

**Step 4: Validate persistence**

Open a fresh terminal. The prompt should now consistently display:

```
student@ktu-pc [14:32:08] ~/Documents$
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The difference between `~/.bashrc` (interactive non-login shells) and `~/.bash_profile` / `~/.profile` (login shells) is a frequently-tested concept. Bash reads `~/.bash_profile` first for **login shells**, and falls back to `~/.bashrc` for **interactive non-login shells**. Most desktop terminals open non-login shells, so `.bashrc` is the standard target.

### 3.3 Exercise 3 — Adding Colour to the Prompt (ANSI Escape Codes)

**Objective:** Apply red colour to the username and green colour to the directory, with default white for everything else.

**Step 1: Recall ANSI colour escape code format**

The general structure of an ANSI sequence is:

$$\text{ANSI Code} = \texttt{\textbackslash 033[} \; \text{style}\texttt{;}\text{fg}\texttt{;}\text{bg} \; \texttt{m}$$

For our needs:

- **Red foreground** → `\e[31m`
- **Green foreground** → `\e[32m`
- **Reset to default** → `\e[0m`

**Step 2: Compose the colour-aware PS1 string**

```bash
export PS1="\e[31m\u\e[0m@\e[32m\w\e[0m\$ "
```

**Step 3: Interpret the structure**

| Sequence | Effect |
| :--- | :--- |
| `\e[31m` | Switch foreground to red |
| `\u` | Print username (in red) |
| `\e[0m` | Reset attributes |
| `@` | Literal `@` symbol |
| `\e[32m` | Switch foreground to green |
| `\w` | Print working directory (in green) |
| `\e[0m` | Reset attributes |
| `\$` | Print `$` or `#` |
| ` ` | Trailing space for readability |

**Step 4: Resulting on-screen prompt**

The username appears **red**, the directory appears **green**, and the prompt symbol `$` reverts to the terminal's default colour.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Prompt Comparison Across Shells

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-frequency KTU question type**: "Compare the default prompts of Bash, C-Shell, and Korn Shell."

| Feature | **Bash (sh/bash)** | **C-Shell (csh)** | **Korn Shell (ksh)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Default user prompt | `$` | `%` | `$` |
| Default root prompt | `#` | `#` | `#` |
| Configuration variable | `PS1` | `prompt` | `PS1` |
| Syntax to change | `PS1="..."` | `set prompt = "..."` | `PS1="..."` |
| Escape sequences | Bash-specific (`\u`, `\h`, etc.) | `%n`, `%m`, `%~` (csh-specific) | ksh-specific |
| Tab completion | Yes (default) | Yes (programmable) | Yes |
| Command history | `history` builtin | `history` builtin | `history` builtin |
| Sample default prompt | `student@ktu:~$` | `%` | `$` |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Login vs Non-Login Shells

| Property | **Login Shell** | **Non-Login (Interactive) Shell** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Triggered by | `login`, `su -l`, SSH session | Opening a new terminal window |
| Reads file | `/etc/profile`, `~/.bash_profile`, `~/.bash_login`, `~/.profile` | `/etc/bash.bashrc`, `~/.bashrc` |
| Typical use | System-wide environment initialization | User-specific aliases, prompt, functions |
| For PS1 editing | Place in `~/.bash_profile` | Place in `~/.bashrc` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — REPL Loop Flowchart of the Shell

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **Read-Eval-Print-Loop** that the shell performs every time a user presses `<Enter>`.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Shell Started - Bash REPL Begins"] --> B["READ - Wait for user input at prompt"]
    B --> C{"User pressed ENTER?"}
    C -- "No" --> B
    C -- "Yes" --> D["EVALUATE - Tokenize command and options"]
    D --> E["Search PATH for command binary"]
    E --> F{"Command Found?"}
    F -- "No" --> G["PRINT Error - command not found"]
    G --> Z["Display new PS1 prompt"]
    F -- "Yes" --> H["EXECUTE - Fork child process"]
    H --> I["Run the program with options and arguments"]
    I --> J["PRINT - Output to stdout or stderr"]
    J --> K["Wait for child to finish or background"]
    K --> L["Return exit status to parent shell"]
    L --> Z
    Z --> B
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Anatomy of a Typical Bash Prompt

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["username: student"] --> B["SEPARATOR: @"]
    B --> C["hostname: ktu-pc"]
    C --> D["SEPARATOR: :"]
    D --> E["directory: ~/Documents"]
    E --> F["PRIVILEGE: $ or #"]
    F --> G["TRAILING SPACE"]
    G --> H["CURSOR BLINKS HERE - Awaiting typed command"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of PS1 Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["User stores: export PS1 equals backslash u at backslash h colon backslash w dollar"] --> P2["Bash stores literal string in PS1 environment variable"]
    P2 --> P3["Bash reads PS1 each time it needs to redraw the prompt"]
    P3 --> P4["Bash parser scans string for backslash escape sequences"]
    P4 --> P5["Substitution: backslash u becomes current username string"]
    P4 --> P6["Substitution: backslash h becomes short hostname string"]
    P4 --> P7["Substitution: backslash w becomes full path or tilde"]
    P4 --> P8["Substitution: backslash dollar becomes dollar or hash symbol"]
    P5 --> P9["Render final ANSI escape decoded text to terminal stdout"]
    P6 --> P9
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10["Terminal displays colored or plain prompt and waits for input"]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Shell Startup File Loading Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Linux Kernel boots"] --> S1["Init process PID 1 starts"]
    S1 --> S2{"User opened login or non-login shell?"}
    S2 -- "Login shell - SSH or console" --> S3["Read /etc/profile first"]
    S3 --> S4["Then read ~/.bash_profile or ~/.bash_login or ~/.profile in that order"]
    S4 --> S5["PS1 and PATH environment variables initialized"]
    S2 -- "Non-login interactive shell - new terminal" --> S6["Read /etc/bash.bashrc if exists"]
    S6 --> S7["Then read ~/.bashrc"]
    S7 --> S5
    S5 --> S8["Shell prompt displayed and REPL begins"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define the term "shell prompt" in Linux. List any four commonly used escape sequences used to customize the PS1 variable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **shell prompt** is a textual string displayed by the command-line interpreter (shell) to indicate that it is ready to accept and execute a user command. It typically includes the username, hostname, current working directory, and a privilege indicator (`$` for user, `#` for root).

Four commonly used PS1 escape sequences:

1. **`\u`** — Current username
2. **`\h`** — Short hostname
3. **`\w`** — Current working directory (full path)
4. **`\t`** — Current time in 24-hour `HH:MM:SS` format

*(Full marks awarded for correct definition + 4 valid escape sequences with their meanings.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Differentiate between `$` and `#` symbols at the Linux shell prompt. What do they signify?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| `$` | Default prompt for a **regular, non-privileged user** (UID ≥ 1000). Indicates limited system access. |
| `#` | Prompt for the **root superuser** (UID = 0). Grants unrestricted administrative control over the system. |

The `$` and `#` are themselves produced by the escape sequence `\$` inside PS1, which Bash **conditionally renders** based on the effective UID of the current user.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop) cycle of a Linux shell with a neat diagram. Mention the role of the `PATH` environment variable in command execution. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With suitable examples, explain at least eight commonly used escape sequences for customizing the Bash `PS1` variable. How would you make the customization **permanent** for a user? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The **REPL cycle** is the fundamental execution model of every interactive shell. It continuously performs the following four phases:

**1. Read [1 mark]**
The shell prints the prompt and waits for the user to type characters into the input buffer. The input is captured only when the user presses the `<Enter>` key.

**2. Evaluate / Parse [2 marks]**
The shell breaks the raw input string into **tokens**:
- The first token is identified as the **command** (e.g., `ls`).
- Tokens beginning with `-` or `--` are parsed as **options/flags**.
- Remaining tokens are treated as **arguments** (targets such as file names or paths).

**3. Execute [2 marks]**
The shell consults the `PATH` environment variable to locate the command's executable binary. The `PATH` is a colon-separated list of directories, for example:

```bash
/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
```

The shell sequentially searches each directory in `PATH` until it finds an executable file matching the command name. Once found, it `fork()`s a child process and `exec()`s the binary. If no match is found in any `PATH` directory, the shell prints `command not found` and returns a non-zero exit status (typically `127`).

**4. Print + Loop [2 marks]**
The executed program's output is sent to **stdout** (file descriptor `1`), and any errors to **stderr** (file descriptor `2`). After the child process terminates, the parent shell reads the **exit status** stored in `$?`, then loops back to display a fresh prompt and wait for the next command.

**Role of PATH [included above]:** PATH eliminates the need to type absolute paths like `/bin/ls` — typing just `ls` suffices because the shell automatically searches `/bin/`.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Eight common PS1 escape sequences [4 marks — 0.5 each]:**

| Sequence | Meaning | Example Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `\u` | Username | `student` |
| `\h` | Short hostname | `ktu-pc` |
| `\H` | Full hostname | `ktu-pc.cs.ktu.ac.in` |
| `\w` | Full current working path | `~/Documents/Project` |
| `\W` | Basename of PWD | `Project` |
| `\d` | Date | `Mon Jan 15` |
| `\t` | 24-hour time | `14:32:08` |
| `\$` | UID-based privilege symbol | `$` or `#` |

**Worked Example [2 marks]:**

```bash
export PS1="[\u@\H \t] \w\$ "
```

Output:

```
[student@ktu-pc.cs.ktu.ac.in 14:32:08] ~/Documents/Project$
```

**Making it permanent [1 mark]:**

Edit the user's `~/.bashrc` file (for interactive non-login shells) or `~/.bash_profile` (for login shells) and append the `export PS1=...` line. Then run `source ~/.bashrc` to apply it without logging out.

```bash
echo 'export PS1="[\u@\H \t] \w\$ "' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

---

> **Q3(B). [Alternative Choice, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** Compare the default shell prompts of **Bash**, **C-Shell (csh)**, and **Korn Shell (ksh)**. Explain how the prompt is configured differently in each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **login shell vs non-login shell** distinction. What configuration files are read in each, and where should PS1 modifications be placed? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table [4 marks]:**

| Feature | Bash | C-Shell (csh) | Korn Shell (ksh) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Default user prompt | `$` | `%` | `$` |
| Default root prompt | `#` | `#` | `#` |
| Config variable | `PS1` | `prompt` | `PS1` |
| Username escape | `\u` | `%n` | `\u` (ksh93+) or logname |
| Hostname escape | `\h` | `%m` | Hostname via `$(hostname)` |
| Path escape | `\w` | `%~` (auto-tilde) | `$PWD` (manual) |
| Time escape | `\t` | `%T` or `%P` | `$(date +%T)` |

**Configuration differences [3 marks]:**

- **Bash:** Use `PS1="\u@\h:\w\$ "`; supports rich escape sequences natively.
- **C-Shell:** Use `set prompt = "%n@%m[%t]% "`; uses `%`-prefixed sequences and `set` command.
- **Korn Shell:** Use `PS1="${LOGNAME}@$(hostname):\$PWD \$ "`; often requires command substitution `$(...)` for dynamic values.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Login Shell [3.5 marks]:**
- Triggered when a user **logs in** to the system (via console, SSH, or `su -l username`).
- Reads configuration files in this order:
  1. `/etc/profile` (system-wide)
  2. `~/.bash_profile` (user-specific, first found)
  3. `~/.bash_login` (if `~/.bash_profile` is absent)
  4. `~/.profile` (if neither of the above exist)
- Typically used to set global environment variables, `PATH`, and `umask`.

**Non-Login Interactive Shell [3.5 marks]:**
- Triggered when a **new terminal window** is opened inside an already-logged-in GUI session.
- Reads configuration files in this order:
  1. `/etc/bash.bashrc` (system-wide, on Debian/Ubuntu)
  2. `~/.bashrc` (user-specific)
- Typically used to set **aliases, functions, and the shell prompt (PS1)**.

**Where to place PS1 [included in marks]:**
- For prompt changes to appear in **every new terminal window**, edit `~/.bashrc`.
- For prompt changes to apply at **login time** (SSH/console), edit `~/.bash_profile` and source `.bashrc` from within it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_FOOTER_START -->
> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing `$PS1` (literal name) with `$` (prompt):** Many students write `export $=...` which is a syntax error. Always use `export PS1=...`.
> 2. **Forgetting to `source ~/.bashrc`:** Edits to `.bashrc` do NOT take effect in the current session unless sourced.
> 3. **Using double quotes around `export PS1=...` while placing `$` inside:** Single quotes are safer if you want to defer evaluation; double quotes cause `\$` to be evaluated by the current shell instead of Bash at prompt-display time.
> 4. **Confusing `\w` and `\W`:** `\w` shows the **full path**; `\W` shows only the **last directory** (basename).
> 5. **Writing `\#` instead of `\#` for command number:** `\#` expands to the command number of the current command (a literal `#` in interactive prompts must be escaped as `\#`).
> 6. **Not distinguishing between `.bashrc` and `.bash_profile`:** This is a **2-mark killer question** in KTU boards — always state which file applies to which shell type.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Shell prompt** = the textual cue that the shell is ready for input.
- **Default symbols:** `$` for users, `#` for root, `%` for csh/tcsh.
- **PS1** is the primary environment variable controlling the main prompt; **PS2** is the continuation prompt (`>`).
- **Permanent customization** goes in `~/.bashrc` (or `~/.bash_profile` for login shells), followed by `source`.
- **Key escape sequences:** `\u` (user), `\h` (host), `\w` (full path), `\W` (basename), `\t` (24h time), `\d` (date), `\$` (privilege), `\n` (newline).
- **REPL** = Read → Eval → Print → Loop — the heartbeat of every interactive shell.
- **`PATH`** is searched left-to-right when locating command binaries.
- **ANSI colour codes** (`\e[31m`, `\e[32m`, `\e[0m`) can be embedded in PS1 for coloured prompts.
- **Bash** uses `PS1` with backslash escapes; **csh** uses `prompt` with `%` escapes; **ksh** uses `PS1` with command substitution.
- **Login shell** reads `/etc/profile` and `~/.bash_profile`; **non-login shell** reads `/etc/bash.bashrc` and `~/.bashrc`.
- For KTU practicals, always verify the prompt change with `echo $PS1` before and after the assignment.
- The tilde `~` is a shell shortcut for the current user's **home directory** and is used inside PS1 via `\w`.
<!-- SECTION_5_FOOTER_END -->

topics.title: Interacting with the bash Manual
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interacting with the bash Manual

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Bash Manual Interaction** refers to the systematic use of the built-in and external documentation facilities provided by the GNU Bash shell and the underlying Linux operating system to retrieve authoritative reference information about commands, builtins, system calls, configuration files, and library functions. The primary interface is the **`man`** (manual) utility, which is a paginated hypertext viewer that displays the system's *reference manuals* — commonly referred to as **"man pages"**.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme learning outcomes, interacting with the bash manual is treated as a foundational **Shell Self-Help Competency (CO1 / CO2 mapping)** — the ability of a developer to autonomously discover command syntax, options, and operational semantics without external network access.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEEVT412 - Module 3):**
> *"Interacting with the bash manual — using `man`, `info`, `help`, `whatis`, `apropos` for command and builtin discovery."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the bash manual as the **"operator's instruction booklet"** that ships with every Linux distribution — analogous to the *user manual* that comes with a new microwave oven.

- You don't need to memorize every button — you just look it up.
- Pages are organized into **chapters (sections)**, just like a medical textbook indexes diseases by body system.
- The system has a **searchable index (`apropos` / `man -k`)** that works like a library's card catalog.

**Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are a chef in a professional kitchen. You don't memorize every recipe — instead, you have a *master cookbook* (the `man` system) where you can flip to the exact page for "knife skills" or "sauce béchamel" by section.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The bash manual is a **local, offline, authoritative** documentation source. Unlike web searches, `man` pages describe the *exact* version of the tool installed on your system — which is critical for scripting reliability.

## 1.3 The Three Documentation Layers in Bash

| Layer | Tool | Scope | Audience |
|---|---|---|---|
| **External Commands** | `man <cmd>`, `info <cmd>` | Compiled binaries (e.g., `ls`, `grep`, `cp`) | System administrators, users |
| **Bash Builtins** | `help <builtin>`, `man bash` | Internal shell commands (e.g., `cd`, `echo`, `export`) | Shell scripters |
| **Self-Discovery** | `whatis`, `apropos`, `type`, `which` | Index/searching the manual database | All users |

> [!TIP]
> The standard man page database on a typical Linux system contains **~2,500 to 8,000+ pages**, indexed by a background database called the `man-db` (managed via `mandb`).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The `man` Command — The Primary Manual Viewer

The `man` command invokes the `less` (or legacy `more`) pager to display a formatted manual page.

### Basic Syntax

```bash
man [options] [section] command_name
```

### The 8 (or 9) Manual Sections — Critical Knowledge

The UNIX/Linux manual is divided into **numbered sections**. When the same name appears in multiple sections (e.g., `printf`), the section number is **mandatory** for disambiguation.

| Section | Content | Example Entry |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | User commands (executables) | `man 1 ls` |
| **2** | System calls (kernel) | `man 2 open` |
| **3** | Library functions (C library) | `man 3 printf` |
| **4** | Special files (devices) | `man 4 tty` |
| **5** | File formats and configuration | `man 5 passwd` |
| **6** | Games and screensavers | `man 6 fortune` |
| **7** | Miscellaneous (conventions, protocols) | `man 7 utf-8` |
| **8** | System administration commands (root) | `man 8 mount` |
| **9** *(optional)* | Kernel routines | `man 9 printk` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classic KTU Exam Example:** The command `printf` exists in **Section 1** (shell builtin) and **Section 3** (C library). Without specifying the section, `man` displays them in a predefined search order. To force the C version: `man 3 printf`.

## 2.2 Standard `man` Page Layout (Anatomy)

Every well-formed man page follows a consistent structural template:

```text
NAME            →  Command name and one-line description
SYNOPSIS        →  Usage syntax and option syntax
DESCRIPTION     →  Detailed explanation of behavior
OPTIONS         →  Alphabetical list of command-line flags
EXAMPLES        →  Practical usage scenarios
FILES           →  Related configuration files
SEE ALSO        →  Cross-references to related commands
AUTHOR / BUGS  →  Maintainer info and known issues
```

## 2.3 Navigation Keys Inside `man` (using `less` pager)

| Key | Action | Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|
| `Space` / `f` | Forward one screen | **f**orward |
| `b` | Backward one screen | **b**ackward |
| `Enter` / `↓` | Forward one line | down |
| `↑` / `k` | Backward one line | **k**ursor up (vi-style) |
| `g` | Go to first line | **g**o to top |
| `G` | Go to last line | shift-**g** = bottom |
| `/pattern` | Search forward for pattern | vi search forward |
| `?pattern` | Search backward for pattern | vi search backward |
| `n` | Repeat last search (same direction) | **n**ext match |
| `N` | Repeat last search (opposite direction) | shift-**n** |
| `h` | Help screen | **h**elp |
| `q` | Quit the pager | **q**uit |

> [!NOTE]
> These navigation keys are inherited from the **`less`** pager. If `man` is configured to use `more` (older systems), many of these shortcuts will not work.

## 2.4 Self-Help / Discovery Commands — The KTU Cheat Sheet

| Command | Purpose | Example | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `whatis <cmd>` | One-line description from NAME field | `whatis grep` | Backed by `whatis` database |
| `apropos <keyword>` | Search NAME + DESCRIPTION for keyword | `apropos "copy file"` | Equivalent to `man -k` |
| `man -k <regex>` | Same as `apropos` | `man -k "^mount$"` | Supports regex with `-r` |
| `man -f <cmd>` | Same as `whatis` | `man -f passwd` | Direct equivalent |
| `type <name>` | Identifies if name is alias/keyword/function/builtin/file | `type cd` | Bash builtin — `help type` |
| `which <cmd>` | Locates executable path | `which python3` | Searches `$PATH` only |
| `command -v <cmd>` | Portable version of `which` | `command -v gcc` | POSIX-compliant |
| `help <builtin>` | Help for shell builtins | `help for` | Bash only |
| `<cmd> --help` | Quick usage from the program itself | `ls --help` | GNU long-option convention |
| `info <cmd>` | Detailed GNU hypertext manual | `info coreutils` | More verbose than `man` |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Command Sheet

| # | Invocation | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `man man` | The manual page about `man` itself (recursive help) |
| 2 | `man -a intro` | All intro pages from all sections (user-friendly overview) |
| 3 | `man -P cat ls` | Pipe `man` output to `cat` (print entire page) |
| 4 | `man -k .` | List **every** page in the manual database (huge output) |
| 5 | `MANSECT=1:8 man mount` | Restrict search to specific sections (env variable) |
| 6 | `man --pager='cat' bash` | Override pager globally for one invocation |
| 7 | `bash --version` | Shows bash version (matches against `man bash` edition) |
| 8 | `help -d cd` | Display short description for builtin `cd` |
| 9 | `help -m for` | Display help in `man`-like format |
| 10 | `mandb` | Rebuild the manual index database (run as root) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DevOps / SRE:** When responding to incidents on a server with no internet, `man` is the only authoritative reference for the *exact installed* binary version.
- **Scripting:** When writing POSIX-compliant scripts, consulting `man bash` section "INVOCATION" prevents relying on distro-specific behavior.
- **Security Auditing:** `man capabilities(7)` and `man pam.conf(5)` are the canonical references for privilege management.
- **Embedded Linux:** On minimal BusyBox-based systems, `man` is often stripped — knowing when to fall back to `--help` or `info` is critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Command Matrices

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Disambiguating `printf` (Classic KTU Problem)

**Problem:** Demonstrate how to view the **C library** version of `printf` and verify you have the correct page.

### Step-by-step execution

**Step 1: List all `printf` pages available**

```bash
$ whatis -r "^printf$"
```

This regex-restricted search returns the available sections:

```text
printf (1)           - format and print data
printf (3)           - formatted output conversion
```

> Logical inference: Two distinct pages exist — the bash builtin (Section 1) and the C library function (Section 3). Section numbers disambiguate them.

**Step 2: Open the C library version explicitly**

```bash
$ man 3 printf
```

The header bar will read:

```text
PRINTF(3)              Linux Programmer's Manual              PRINTF(3)
```

This is the authoritative source for the C prototype:

```text
int printf(const char *format, ...);
```

**Step 3: Open the shell builtin version**

```bash
$ man 1 printf
```

The header will read:

```text
PRINTF(1)                  User Commands                   PRINTF(1)
```

**Step 4: Cross-reference verification**

```bash
$ type -a printf
```

Output:

```text
printf is a shell builtin
printf is /usr/bin/printf
```

This confirms the *physical* existence of two distinct implementations on the filesystem.

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — Searching the Manual with `apropos` / `man -k`

**Problem:** Find all commands related to "archive" without knowing exact command names.

### Step-by-step execution

**Step 1: Broad keyword search**

```bash
$ apropos archive
```

Output (truncated):

```text
ar (1)               - create, modify, and extract from archives
cpio (1)             - copy files to and from archives
tar (1)              - an archiving utility
zip (1)              - package and compress (archive) files
```

**Step 2: Restrict to Section 1 only**

```bash
$ apropos -s 1 archive
```

This filters out library functions and device pages.

**Step 3: Regex search for a precise pattern**

```bash
$ man -k -r '^(cp|mv|ln)$'
```

Returns only pages whose command names are exactly `cp`, `mv`, or `ln` (no substring matches like `cpio`).

**Step 4: Build a custom quick-reference list**

```bash
$ man -k . | awk -F'(' '{print $1}' | sort > ~/cmd_index.txt
wc -l ~/cmd_index.txt
```

This dumps the full manual index into a text file — useful for offline searching via `grep` on machines without `mandb`.

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — Navigating Within a Long `man` Page

**Problem:** In `man bash`, locate every mention of the `IFS` variable.

### Step-by-step execution

**Step 1: Open the page**

```bash
$ man bash
```

**Step 2: Initiate a forward search**

Press the `/` key, then type the pattern:

```text
/IFS
```

The pager jumps to the first match and highlights it. The bottom of the screen shows:

```text
/IFS
```

**Step 3: Navigate through matches**

- Press `n` → next match forward.
- Press `N` → previous match (opposite direction).
- Press `q` → quit at any time.

**Step 4: Reverse search**

Press `?` to search backward:

```text
?PATH
```

**Step 5: Jump to specific structural anchor**

Many man pages have hyperlinks (in `man-db` ≥ 2.7) — press `Tab` to cycle through links, `Enter` to follow.

## 3.4 Walkthrough 4 — The Bash Builtin `help` Subsystem

**Problem:** Get help for the `[[ ]]` compound command without leaving the shell.

### Step-by-step execution

**Step 1: List all builtins with `help`**

```bash
$ help
```

Output is a numbered list of ~60 builtins.

**Step 2: Get specific help**

```bash
$ help test
```

Displays the full description of the `test` builtin with options, exit status, and examples.

**Step 3: `man`-like display mode**

```bash
$ help -m for
```

The `-m` flag formats output to mimic `man` (with NAME, SYNOPSIS, DESCRIPTION headers), which is the format most familiar to KTU students.

**Step 4: Short description only**

```bash
$ help -d while
```

Prints one line:

```text
while - Execute commands as long as a test succeeds.
```

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

```text
+--------------------------------+------------------------+--------------------+
| I need to know about...        | Best tool              | Why?               |
+--------------------------------+------------------------+--------------------+
| An external command (ls)       | man ls                 | Authoritative      |
| A shell builtin (cd)           | help cd                | man cd may mislead |
| A short one-liner              | whatis ls              | Fastest lookup     |
| A keyword in mind              | apropos keyword        | Index search       |
| A config file format           | man 5 fstab            | Section 5         |
| A system call                  | man 2 fork             | Section 2         |
| GNU-extended info              | info coreutils         | More examples      |
| Program's own quick help       | cmd --help             | Distro-independent |
+--------------------------------+------------------------+--------------------+
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Bash Manual Discovery Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User needs help with a command] --> B{Is it a shell builtin?}
    B -- Yes --> C[help builtin_name]
    B -- No --> D{Is it an external command?}
    D -- Yes --> E[man section_number command]
    D -- Unknown --> F[type -a command]
    F --> B
    C --> G{Need full reference?}
    G -- Yes --> H[man bash]
    G -- No --> I[Read short help]
    E --> J{Need to search?}
    J -- Yes --> K[apropos keyword or man -k]
    J -- No --> L[Navigate with /, n, N]
    H --> M[Exit q]
    I --> M
    L --> M
    K --> E
```

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram — The `man` Subsystem Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User types: man 3 printf] --> B[man binary]
    B --> C[Consults MANPATH env var]
    B --> D[Consults /etc/manpath.config]
    C --> E[Scans /usr/share/man/man3/]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Finds printf.3.gz]
    F --> G[Decompresses via gunzip]
    G --> H[Preprocesses with groff or mandoc]
    H --> I[Formatted troff output]
    I --> J[Pager: less or more]
    J --> K[User terminal display]
    B --> L[Updates /var/cache/man/ DB]
    L --> M[Indexed by whatis / apropos]
    M --> N[mandb command rebuilds DB]
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — Choosing the Right Help Command

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need information?] --> Q1{Know exact command name?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is it a builtin?}
    Q1 -- No --> S1[apropos keyword]
    S1 --> S2[Identify candidate commands]
    S2 --> Q1
    Q2 -- Yes --> S3[help name]
    Q2 -- No --> S4[man name]
    Q3{Need one-line summary?} -- Yes --> S5[whatis name]
    Q3 -- No --> S6[Read full man page]
    S5 --> End[Done]
    S6 --> End
    S3 --> End
    S4 --> Q3{Need one-line summary?}
```

## 4.4 Hierarchy — The 8 Manual Sections (Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Linux Manual Pages] --> S1[Section 1: User Commands]
    Root --> S2[Section 2: System Calls]
    Root --> S3[Section 3: C Library Functions]
    Root --> S4[Section 4: Devices and Special Files]
    Root --> S5[Section 5: File Formats and Configs]
    Root --> S6[Section 6: Games]
    Root --> S7[Section 7: Conventions and Misc]
    Root --> S8[Section 8: System Administration]
    S1 --> E1[ls, cp, mv, grep, bash]
    S2 --> E2[open, read, write, fork, execve]
    S3 --> E3[printf, malloc, strlen, fopen]
    S5 --> E4[passwd, fstab, sshd_config, crontab]
    S8 --> E5[mount, iptables, systemctl, fdisk]
```

## 4.5 Sequence Diagram — `man -k` Lookup Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Shell
    participant M as man binary
    participant DB as whatis Database
    participant FS as /usr/share/man
    participant P as Pager
    U->>M: man -k archive
    M->>DB: query index for keyword 'archive'
    DB-->>M: returns list of (name, section, desc) tuples
    M-->>U: prints formatted list to stdout
    U->>M: man 1 tar
    M->>FS: lookup /usr/share/man/man1/tar.1.gz
    FS-->>M: returns compressed source
    M->>M: decompress + format via groff
    M->>P: pipe to less
    P-->>U: interactive paginated display
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between `man`, `info`, and `help` commands in Linux. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `man` | `info` | `help` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Source** | System manual pages | GNU Texinfo manuals | Bash internal help |
| **Scope** | All commands, syscalls, configs | Mainly GNU utilities | Shell builtins only |
| **Format** | Paginated plain text | Hyperlinked nodes | Concise descriptions |
| **Navigation** | `less` shortcuts | `n`/`p`/`u`/`Tab` | Direct read |
| **Preferred when** | Need authoritative reference for any command | Need extended GNU examples | Quick lookup for `cd`, `echo`, `export`, etc. |

`help` is preferred for builtins because the external `man` page for a builtin (e.g., `man cd`) often shows the *external wrapper* rather than the shell's internal implementation. The `info` system provides richer documentation for GNU projects (e.g., `info coreutils`) but is rarely needed for system V utilities.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the significance of manual page sections. How would you view the `passwd` command's configuration file documentation?**

**Model Answer:**

The Linux manual is divided into **eight primary sections** to categorize different types of reference material — user commands (1), system calls (2), library functions (3), devices (4), file formats (5), games (6), conventions (7), and admin commands (8). This allows commands with identical names (e.g., `printf`, `passwd`) to coexist with disambiguated documentation.

The `passwd` utility exists in:
- **Section 1**: The `passwd` user command for changing passwords.
- **Section 5**: The `/etc/passwd` file format specification.

To view the **file format documentation**, the correct invocation is:

```bash
$ man 5 passwd
```

If we simply run `man passwd`, the system will display the first match in the configured section search order (typically Section 1), which would be the user command — not the configuration file format.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the different manual sections of Linux with suitable examples. Why is the section number sometimes mandatory when invoking `man`? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the navigation commands available within a `man` page when viewed through the `less` pager. How do you search for a specific keyword and navigate through all its occurrences? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Linux manual is divided into the following sections, each serving a specific documentation purpose:

| Section | Content | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Executable programs and shell commands | `man 1 ls` |
| 2 | System calls (kernel-provided functions) | `man 2 fork` |
| 3 | Library functions (e.g., glibc) | `man 3 strlen` |
| 4 | Devices and special files in `/dev` | `man 4 null` |
| 5 | File formats and configuration conventions | `man 5 fstab` |
| 6 | Games and screen savers | `man 6 chess` |
| 7 | Miscellaneous (macro packages, conventions) | `man 7 signal` |
| 8 | System administration commands (root) | `man 8 iptables` |

**Why section number is mandatory:**

When a name appears in multiple sections, `man` uses the **MANSECT** environment variable and `/etc/man_db.conf` directives to determine the default search order. For example, `printf` exists in Section 1 (shell builtin) and Section 3 (C library). Without specifying the section:

```bash
$ man printf
```

the system returns whichever page comes first in the configured order — this may not be the one the user intended. To get deterministic results, always specify:

```bash
$ man 3 printf   # C library version
$ man 1 printf   # Shell builtin version
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

The `man` command, by default, invokes the **`less`** pager to display manual pages interactively. The following navigation keys are available:

**Movement Commands:**

- `Space` or `f` — Scroll forward one full screen.
- `b` — Scroll backward one full screen.
- `Enter` / `↓` — Move forward by one line.
- `↑` / `k` — Move backward by one line.
- `g` — Jump to the first line of the page.
- `G` — Jump to the last line of the page.
- `PgUp` / `PgDn` — Standard terminal page navigation.

**Search Commands:**

- `/pattern` — Search **forward** for a regular expression pattern. After pressing `/`, type the keyword and press `Enter`. The pager highlights the first match and positions the cursor there.
- `?pattern` — Search **backward** for a pattern.
- `n` — Repeat the previous search in the **same** direction (next match).
- `N` — Repeat the previous search in the **opposite** direction.

**Example workflow for finding `IFS` in `man bash`:**

```bash
$ man bash
```

Then inside the pager:

```text
/IFS<Enter>    →  Jumps to first match
n              →  Jumps to second match
n              →  Third match
N              →  Back to second match
?PATH<Enter>   →  Searches backward for PATH
```

**Other useful keys:**

- `h` — Display help screen of `less` itself.
- `q` — Quit and return to the shell.
- `:n` and `:p` (in `less`) — Jump to next/previous file when multiple files are open.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Compare and contrast the commands `whatis`, `apropos`, `type`, and `which` in Linux. Give one example for each demonstrating its specific use case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the purpose of the `info` command? How does it differ from `man`? Demonstrate with an example how you would explore the GNU core utilities documentation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Command | Function | Database / Source | Example Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| `whatis` | One-line description from NAME field | `whatis` database (`/var/cache/man/`) | `ls (1) - list directory contents` |
| `apropos` | Searches NAME + DESCRIPTION for keyword | Same as above | Returns multiple matches |
| `type` | Identifies how a name would be interpreted by bash (alias, builtin, function, file) | Internal to bash | `cd is a shell builtin` |
| `which` | Locates the executable path on disk | Searches `$PATH` directories | `/bin/ls` |

**Detailed Examples:**

**1. `whatis` — Quick single-line lookup:**

```bash
$ whatis grep
grep (1) - file pattern searcher
```

This retrieves only the NAME field line from the man database — fastest possible lookup.

**2. `apropos` — Keyword search when name is unknown:**

```bash
$ apropos "list directory"
ls (1)               - list directory contents
dir (1)              - list directory contents
vdir (1)             - list directory contents
```

Here, the user did not know the exact command but searched by a descriptive phrase.

**3. `type` — Bash-internal command classification:**

```bash
$ type -a cd
cd is a shell builtin
$ type -a ls
ls is aliased to `ls --color=auto'
ls is /bin/ls
```

The `type` builtin reveals whether a name is a bash **keyword**, **builtin**, **alias**, **function**, or **external file** — information no other command provides.

**4. `which` — Path resolution:**

```bash
$ which python3
/usr/bin/python3
```

`which` only searches directories listed in the `$PATH` environment variable and returns the first executable match.

**Key distinction:** `type` is a bash builtin and understands shell-specific concepts like aliases; `which` is an external command and only sees the filesystem.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Purpose of `info`:**

The `info` command is a documentation reader for **GNU Texinfo** manuals — a hierarchical, hyperlinked documentation format used by the GNU Project for comprehensive manuals of utilities like `coreutils`, `gcc`, `bash`, `grub`, etc. It complements `man` by providing:

1. **More detailed explanations** with extensive examples.
2. **Hierarchical structure** — Nodes can contain sub-nodes (Next, Prev, Up).
3. **Cross-references and hyperlinks** that work like a web page.
4. **Topic-based navigation** rather than page-based.

**Differences from `man`:**

| Feature | `man` | `info` |
|---|---|---|
| Format | `troff` / `groff` | Texinfo |
| Navigation | Linear (less) | Hierarchical (nodes) |
| Coverage | Universal (all utilities) | Mainly GNU projects |
| Hyperlinks | Limited (recent `man-db` only) | Native and extensive |
| Length | Concise | Verbose, tutorial-style |

**Example — Exploring GNU coreutils:**

```bash
$ info coreutils
```

This opens the **coreutils manual index**, with nodes such as:

```text
* Coreutils: (coreutils).  Core GNU utilities.
* ls invocation: (coreutils)ls invocation.  List directory contents.
* cp invocation: (coreutils)cp invocation.  Copy files.
* dd invocation: (coreutils)dd invocation.  Convert and copy a file.
```

**Navigation keys in `info`:**

- `n` — Go to the **next** node.
- `p` — Go to the **previous** node.
- `u` — Go **up** to the parent node.
- `Tab` — Move to the next hyperlink within a node.
- `Enter` — Follow the highlighted link.
- `s <regex>` — **Search** through the entire manual.
- `q` — Quit.

**To view only the `ls` invocation specifically:**

```bash
$ info coreutils 'ls invocation'
```

This jumps directly to the detailed documentation of the `ls` command — usually 10–20× more detailed than the corresponding `man ls` page.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in `man` Page Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `man` and `info`:** Students often write "`info` is a graphical tool" — *it is not*; it is a terminal-based reader.
> 2. **Forgetting section numbers:** When a question asks for the `passwd` *file format* (Section 5) and the student writes `man passwd` (which defaults to Section 1), the examiner deducts 1–2 marks for ambiguity.
> 3. **Treating `help` as universal:** `help` works **only for bash builtins**, not for external commands. Writing `help ls` is a definite error.
> 4. **Mixing up `which` and `type`:** `which` is external and ignores aliases/builtins; `type` is a bash builtin and reveals them. Examiners expect this distinction.
> 5. **Writing `man -search`:** The correct flag is `man -k` (or the command `apropos`). `-search` does not exist.
> 6. **Forgetting to rebuild `mandb`:** On freshly installed systems, `apropos` returns "nothing appropriate" until `mandb` has been run as root. Mentioning this in long answers shows depth.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Interacting with the bash Manual"**

- ✅ `man <cmd>` is the **primary** documentation viewer; uses `less` by default.
- ✅ Manual pages are divided into **8 (sometimes 9) sections** — Section 1 = user commands, Section 5 = file formats, Section 8 = admin commands.
- ✅ The same name can exist in **multiple sections** (e.g., `passwd` in 1 and 5); use `man 5 passwd` for disambiguation.
- ✅ `man -k <keyword>` is **synonymous** with `apropos <keyword>`; both search the NAME + DESCRIPTION fields.
- ✅ `man -f <cmd>` is **synonymous** with `whatis <cmd>`; both show the one-line NAME field.
- ✅ `help <builtin>` is for **bash builtins only** (`cd`, `exit`, `export`, `read`, `declare`, `set`, `for`, `while`, `[[`, `((`, etc.).
- ✅ `type <name>` reveals if a name is a **keyword / alias / function / builtin / file** — it is the most authoritative bash-internal classifier.
- ✅ `which <name>` searches `$PATH` and returns the **first executable** found; it does **not** see aliases.
- ✅ Inside `less`, navigation uses `/` (search forward), `?` (search backward), `n` (next match), `N` (previous match), `g`/`G` (top/bottom), `q` (quit).
- ✅ `info <topic>` reads **GNU Texinfo** manuals — richer and more example-heavy than `man` for GNU utilities like `coreutils`, `bash`, `gcc`.
- ✅ `mandb` (run as root) **rebuilds the manual index**; required after installing new packages or fresh OS installation.
- ✅ `--help` is a **GNU long-option convention** for inline quick help — works on most modern utilities.
- ✅ The environment variable `MANPATH` controls where `man` searches; `MANSECT` overrides the section search order.
- ✅ On a system with **no internet**, `man` is the only way to discover command syntax, options, and exit codes — making this skill critical for real-world engineering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Looking at the Linux File System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Looking at the Linux File System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Linux File System** is a hierarchical, inverted-tree structured method of organizing, storing, retrieving, and managing data on secondary storage (disks, SSDs, partitions) under a UNIX-like operating system. It conforms to the **Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS)** maintained by the Linux Foundation, which prescribes a uniform directory layout so that user programs and system utilities can predict the location of installed files and configuration data.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for *PEEVT412 – Linux and Scripting*, "Looking at the Linux File System" encompasses:
- The logical organization of directories starting from the **root directory** (`/`)
- The classification of **seven (7) standard Unix file types**
- The use of navigation, inspection, and metadata commands (`pwd`, `ls`, `cd`, `stat`, `file`, `df`, `du`, `tree`)
- The distinction between **absolute paths** and **relative paths**
- The concept of **inodes**, **mount points**, and **virtual file systems** (e.g., `/proc`, `/sys`)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Office Building**
> Think of your Linux machine as a giant **upside-down office building**.
> - The **roof (root)** is `/` — the single doorway that connects to every floor.
> - Each **floor** is a directory (e.g., `/home`, `/etc`, `/var`).
> - Each **room** on a floor is a sub-directory or a file.
> - **Lifts (mounts)** connect external storages (USB drives, extra disks) as new "floors" attached to the building.
> - A **room number (path)** tells you exactly where to find a file. An *absolute room number* starts from the roof (`/home/student/report.txt`); a *relative room number* is given from where you currently stand (`./report.txt`).

This single mental model — *everything is a file, arranged in one tree starting at `/`* — is the key insight that makes Linux different from Windows' multi-root drive-letter scheme.

## 1.3 High-Yield Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board-Exam Definitions (Memorize Verbatim)**
> 1. **File System** – A logical method of organizing and storing data so it can be located, read, and written by the OS.
> 2. **Root Directory (`/`)** – The single top-level directory from which the entire hierarchy descends. It is *not* the same as the `/root` home directory of the superuser.
> 3. **Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS)** – A Linux Foundation specification that defines the directory structure and contents of a Linux distribution.
> 4. **Mount Point** – A directory at which an additional file system is attached to the currently accessible file system tree.
> 5. **Everything is a File** – A core Unix philosophy: hardware devices, processes, sockets, and ordinary data are all represented as file-like objects.

**Standard Metrics (Bold for emphasis):**
- Default block size on most modern Linux ext4 systems: **4096 bytes (4 KiB)**
- Maximum path length in Linux: **4096 characters** (`PATH_MAX`)
- Standard file-system partition types examined: **ext2, ext3, ext4, xfs, btrfs, vfat, ntfs**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS) — Top-Level Directories

The following table is **high-yield for KTU exams**. Examiners routinely ask "Explain the purpose of `/var`" or "Differentiate between `/bin` and `/sbin`".

| Directory | Full Form | Purpose | Static/Dynamic | Sharable? |
|-----------|-----------|---------|----------------|-----------|
| `/` | Root | The single ancestor of the entire tree | — | No |
| `/bin` | Binaries | Essential user commands (`ls`, `cp`, `mv`, `cat`) — required for boot | Static | Yes |
| `/sbin` | System Binaries | Essential admin/boot commands (`fsck`, `reboot`, `ifconfig`) | Static | Yes |
| `/etc` | Etcetera | Host-specific **configuration files** (no binaries) | Static | No |
| `/home` | Home | User home directories (`/home/student`) | Dynamic | Yes |
| `/root` | Root Home | Home directory of the superuser (`root`) | Dynamic | No |
| `/var` | Variable | Variable data: logs (`/var/log`), spool, caches, databases | Dynamic | Yes |
| `/tmp` | Temporary | World-writable temporary files; cleared on reboot | Dynamic | Yes |
| `/usr` | Unix System Resources | Secondary hierarchy for user utilities (`/usr/bin`, `/usr/lib`, `/usr/share`) | Static | Yes |
| `/opt` | Optional | Add-on third-party software (e.g., Google Chrome, MATLAB) | Static | No |
| `/boot` | Boot | Boot loader files (kernel `vmlinuz`, `initrd.img`, GRUB) | Static | No |
| `/lib` | Libraries | Shared libraries and kernel modules for `/bin` and `/sbin` | Static | Yes |
| `/dev` | Devices | Device files (terminals, disks) — `sda`, `tty`, `null`, `zero` | Dynamic | No |
| `/proc` | Process | **Virtual** FS exposing process & kernel info as files | Dynamic | No |
| `/sys` | System | **Virtual** FS exposing kernel objects, devices, buses | Dynamic | No |
| `/mnt` | Mount | Temporary mount point for filesystems | Dynamic | No |
| `/media` | Media | Auto-mount point for removable media (CD, USB) | Dynamic | No |
| `/srv` | Service | Data served by system (HTTP, FTP) | Dynamic | No |
| `/run` | Run | Runtime variable data (PIDs, sockets) since boot | Dynamic | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for Exams**: `/usr` was historically "user" but now means **Unix System Resources**. Mounting `/usr` as **read-only** after boot is a modern security hardening practice (used in `systemd` and CoreOS).

## 2.2 The Seven (7) Standard Unix File Types

When you run `ls -l`, the **first character** of the permission string identifies the file type. KTU questions often ask: *"List the file types in Linux and identify the symbol shown by `ls -l`."*

| Symbol | Type | Description | Example |
|:------:|------|-------------|---------|
| `-` | Regular file | Text, binary, image, executable | `report.txt` |
| `d` | Directory | A folder containing other files | `Documents` |
| `l` | Symbolic link | A pointer/shortcut to another file | `link → file.txt` |
| `c` | Character device | Stream-oriented device (terminal, keyboard) | `/dev/tty` |
| `b` | Block device | Block-oriented device (disk, SSD) | `/dev/sda1` |
| `p` | Named pipe (FIFO) | Inter-process communication channel | `/tmp/myfifo` |
| `s` | Unix domain socket | Local network socket for IPC | `/var/run/docker.sock` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Gate-Level Favourite Question**: *"What does the file type `c` represent? How is it different from `b`?"*
> → `c` (character) handles data byte-by-byte with no buffering (mouse, terminal). `b` (block) handles data in fixed-size blocks and supports random access (disks, RAM disks).

## 2.3 Absolute Path vs. Relative Path

| Feature | Absolute Path | Relative Path |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Starts with | Always begins with `/` (root) | Does **not** start with `/` |
| Reference point | The root directory `/` | The **current working directory** (PWD) |
| Uniqueness | Always unique system-wide | Depends on where you are |
| Example | `/home/student/Documents/report.pdf` | `Documents/report.pdf` (from `/home/student`) |
| Notation | Uses leading `/` | Uses `./` (current) or `../` (parent) |

**Special Directory Shorthands:**
- `.` → the current directory
- `..` → the parent directory
- `~` → current user's home directory
- `-` → previous working directory (used with `cd -`)

## 2.4 The Inode (Index Node) Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inode** — A data structure on disk that stores all metadata about a file *except* its name and actual contents. Every file has exactly one inode.

**What an inode contains (10 mandatory fields + extended attributes):**
1. File type
2. Permissions (rwx) and ownership (UID/GID)
3. File size (bytes)
4. Timestamps: `atime` (access), `mtime` (modification), `ctime` (change)
5. Link count (hard-link counter)
6. Pointers to data blocks (12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect)
7. Inode number (unique per filesystem)

**Key insight**: The *filename* is stored in the **directory entry**, not the inode. The directory is a mapping of `filename → inode_number`. This is why Linux allows multiple filenames (hard links) to point to the same inode.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Engineering & System Metrics)

| Metric / Formula | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|------------------|------------|--------------|
| Total disk usage of a directory | `du -sh /path` | Sum of sizes of all files; default block = **1024 bytes** |
| Free space on filesystem | `df -h /mount` | Reports in human-readable units (K, M, G) |
| Effective used blocks | `Used = Total - Free` | — |
| Hard-link constraint | All hard links must lie on **one** filesystem | Cross-filesystem ⇒ must use **symbolic link** |
| Inode usage warning | `df -i` | Filesystem may run out of inodes even with free space |
| Path-length limit | `PATH_MAX = 4096` | Including the null terminator |
| Filename max length | `NAME_MAX = 255` | Bytes (UTF-8) on ext4 |
| Mounted device count | `mount \| wc -l` or `cat /proc/mounts` | — |
| Block size | `stat -f /dev/sda1` | Default **4096 bytes** |
| File type identification | `file <name>` | Magic-byte + extension analysis |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam trick question**: *"A filesystem reports `df -h` shows 50% free, yet `touch newfile` returns 'No space left on device'. What happened?"*
> → The filesystem exhausted its **inode table**, not its data blocks. Use `df -i` to confirm.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DevOps / Cloud Engineering**: Docker layers, Kubernetes volumes, and AWS EBS mounts all rely on FHS conventions (`/var/lib/docker`, `/mnt/data`).
- **Embedded Systems**: Buildroot, Yocto, and OpenWrt generate *minimal* FHS trees for IoT devices.
- **Cybersecurity Forensics**: Investigators mount suspect disks at `/mnt/evidence` and use the FHS to locate logs (`/var/log/auth.log`), shell histories, and SUID binaries.
- **System Administration**: Knowing `/etc` allows rapid config edits; knowing `/proc` allows live kernel tuning (`/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward`).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — How `ls -l` Decodes a File Listing

A complete `ls -l` output line has 10 fields. KTU frequently asks you to interpret a sample line. Below is the exact decoding procedure.

**Generic line format:**

$$
