\text{type} \;\;\underbrace{rwx}_{\text{owner}}\;\;\underbrace{rwx}_{\text{group}}\;\;\underbrace{rwx}_{\text{others}} \;\; \text{links}\;\; \text{owner}\;\; \text{group}\;\; \text{size}\;\; \text{mtime}\;\; \text{name}
$$

### Worked Example

Sample output of `ls -l /etc/passwd`:

```
-rw-r--r--  1 root  root  2841  Aug 12 09:14  /etc/passwd
```

**Step 1 — Identify the file type (column 1):**

$$
\text{First character} = `-`
$$

Since the character is `-`, the entry is a **regular file**.

**Step 2 — Decode permission triplets (columns 2-10):**

The remaining 9 characters split into three triplets:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Owner (user)}  &: \texttt{rw-} \;\;\Rightarrow\; \text{read} + \text{write},\; \text{no execute} \\
\text{Group}         &: \texttt{r--} \;\;\Rightarrow\; \text{read only} \\
\text{Others}        &: \texttt{r--} \;\;\Rightarrow\; \text{read only}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to octal (numeric) form (optional but examiner-favourite):**

Using the canonical Linux binary weights `r=4, w=2, x=1`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Owner} &= 4 + 2 + 0 = 6 \\
\text{Group} &= 4 + 0 + 0 = 4 \\
\text{Others} &= 4 + 0 + 0 = 4 \\
\therefore \text{Octal mode} &= 644
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Link count (column 2):**

$$
\text{links} = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\; \text{no hard links; this is the only filename}
$$

**Step 5 — Ownership (columns 3-4):**

$$
\text{Owner} = \texttt{root},\qquad \text{Group} = \texttt{root}
$$

**Step 6 — Size in bytes (column 5):**

$$
\text{size} = 2841 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 7 — Modification time (column 6-8):**

$$
\text{mtime} = \text{Aug 12, 09:14}
$$

**Step 8 — Full path (column 9):**

$$
\text{path} = \texttt{/etc/passwd}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Validation rule**: Permissions `r--r--r--` (octal 444) is the *minimum readable* mode. `/etc/passwd` is a classic example because historically all users needed to read it for UID lookups (now replaced by `/etc/nsswitch.conf` + `systemd-userdb`).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — File-Type Diagnostic Flowchart Logic

A KTU-typical 7-mark question: *"Given the output of `ls -l`, how do you identify the type of each entry?"*

**Step 1 — Extract the first character** of the permission string.

**Step 2 — Apply the decision table:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } c_1 = `d` &\Rightarrow \textbf{Directory} \\
\text{If } c_1 = `-` &\Rightarrow \textbf{Regular file} \\
\text{If } c_1 = `l` &\Rightarrow \textbf{Symbolic link (use `ls -l` to see target)} \\
\text{If } c_1 = `c` &\Rightarrow \textbf{Character device} \\
\text{If } c_1 = `b` &\Rightarrow \textbf{Block device} \\
\text{If } c_1 = `p` &\Rightarrow \textbf{Named pipe (FIFO)} \\
\text{If } c_1 = `s` &\Rightarrow \textbf{Unix domain socket}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — For ambiguous or special cases**, escalate to the `file` or `stat` command:

```bash
$ file /dev/sda1
/dev/sda1: block special (8/1)

$ stat /etc/passwd
  File: /etc/passwd
  Size: 2841       	Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file
Device: 803h/2051d	Inode: 131235      Links: 1
Access: (0644/-rw-r--r--)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (    0/    root)
```

**Step 4 — Resolve the "what is the target of this link?" case:**

```bash
$ ls -l /usr/bin/python3
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 16  May 10 14:00 /usr/bin/python3 -> python3.11

$ readlink -f /usr/bin/python3
/usr/bin/python3.11
```

The arrow `->` after the name reveals the target. Use `readlink -f` to canonicalize (resolve the entire chain).

## 3.3 Derivation — Mount-Point Size Calculation

A common KTU problem: *"You have a 100 GB disk with 60 GB used. How many free inodes remain if the inode ratio is one inode per 16 KB?"*

**Step 1 — Convert total disk to KB:**

$$
\text{Total}_{KB} = 100 \text{ GB} \times 1024 \times 1024 = 104{,}857{,}600 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total inodes provisioned at format time:**

$$
\text{Inodes}_{total} = \frac{\text{Total}_{KB}}{\text{Bytes per inode}} = \frac{104{,}857{,}600}{16} = 6{,}553{,}600
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate inodes used (proportional to data):**

$$
\text{Inodes}_{used} = \text{Inodes}_{total} \times \frac{60 \text{ GB}}{100 \text{ GB}} = 6{,}553{,}600 \times 0.6 = 3{,}932{,}160
$$

**Step 4 — Inodes free:**

$$
\text{Inodes}_{free} = 6{,}553{,}600 - 3{,}932{,}160 = 2{,}621{,}440
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This estimate is illustrative. The actual `df -i` output is the only authoritative source. Real systems that store millions of tiny files (e.g., mail spoolers) must use filesystems like **ReiserFS** or **XFS** with custom inode ratios at format time: `mkfs.ext4 -i 4096 /dev/sda1`.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Path-Resolution Algorithm

Linux's `namei(1)` command visualizes each lookup. Algorithmically, kernel function `link_path_walk()` proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Identify the starting point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If path starts with `/`} &\Rightarrow \text{start} = \text{root inode} \\
\text{If path starts with `~`} &\Rightarrow \text{expand to } \texttt{\$HOME} \\
\text{If path starts with `./` or `../`} &\Rightarrow \text{start} = \text{CWD inode} \\
\text{Otherwise} &\Rightarrow \text{start} = \text{CWD inode (relative path)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Walk component-by-component:**

For each `/`-separated token, call `lookup()` on the parent inode's directory entries (using a hashed lookup for performance).

**Step 3 — Handle `.` and `..` shortcuts:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{./}  &\Rightarrow \text{skip — stay in current} \\
\texttt{../} &\Rightarrow \text{decrement reference to parent inode} \\
\texttt{../..} &\Rightarrow \text{decrement twice, etc.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Check permissions at every step:**

The kernel must have `execute (x)` permission on **every** directory along the path, not just the final one. *This is a frequent KTU viva question.*

**Step 5 — Return final inode or `ENOENT` (error 2).**

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Hard Link vs. Symbolic Link

| Property | Hard Link | Symbolic (Soft) Link |
|----------|-----------|----------------------|
| Same inode? | **Yes** | No (creates a new inode) |
| Cross-filesystem allowed? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Continues if original is deleted? | Yes (data preserved until last link removed) | **No** (becomes a *dangling link*) |
| Creates with | `ln source link` | `ln -s source link` |
| Permissions | Mirror the target | `lrwxrwxrwx` (always 777 nominally) |
| Directory hard-link? | Forbidden (since Linux 2.6, to prevent cycles) | **Allowed** |
| Exam tip | Use `ls -li` to match inode numbers | Use `ls -l` and look for `l` + arrow |

**Proof they share the same inode:**

```bash
$ echo "hello" > original.txt
$ ln original.txt hardlink.txt
$ ls -li original.txt hardlink.txt
131235 -rw-r--r-- 2 student student 6 Aug 12 09:14 original.txt
131235 -rw-r--r-- 2 student student 6 Aug 12 09:14 hardlink.txt
```

The identical inode number `131235` and link count `2` confirm the link.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linux File-System Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode["/ Root Directory"]
    rootNode --> binNode["/bin Essential User Binaries"]
    rootNode --> sbinNode["/sbin System Binaries"]
    rootNode --> etcNode["/etc Configuration Files"]
    rootNode --> homeNode["/home User Home Directories"]
    rootNode --> rootUserNode["/root Superuser Home"]
    rootNode --> varNode["/var Variable Data"]
    rootNode --> tmpNode["/tmp Temporary Files"]
    rootNode --> usrNode["/usr Unix System Resources"]
    rootNode --> optNode["/opt Optional Third Party Apps"]
    rootNode --> bootNode["/boot Bootloader and Kernel"]
    rootNode --> libNode["/lib Shared Libraries"]
    rootNode --> devNode["/dev Device Files"]
    rootNode --> procNode["/proc Virtual Process FS"]
    rootNode --> sysNode["/sys Virtual Kernel FS"]
    rootNode --> mntNode["/mnt Manual Mount Point"]
    rootNode --> mediaNode["/media Removable Media"]
    rootNode --> srvNode["/srv Service Data"]
    rootNode --> runNode["/tmp Runtime Data"]
    
    homeNode --> userA["student"]
    homeNode --> userB["faculty"]
    
    usrNode --> usrBin["/usr/bin User Commands"]
    usrNode --> usrLib["/usr/lib Libraries"]
    usrNode --> usrShare["/usr/share Architecture Independent Data"]
    usrNode --> usrLocal["/usr/local Local Software"]
    
    varNode --> varLog["/var/log System Logs"]
    varNode --> varCache["/var/cache Application Cache"]
    varNode --> varLib["/var/lib State Information"]
    
    procNode --> proc1["/proc/cpuinfo"]
    procNode --> proc2["/proc/meminfo"]
    procNode --> proc3["/proc/1/status"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Path Resolution Engine

```mermaid
graph LR
    userInput["User Input Path String"] --> parser["Path Parser"]
    parser --> absCheck{"Starts with / ?"}
    absCheck -- Yes --> rootAnchor["Anchor at Root Inode 2"]
    absCheck -- No --> cwdAnchor["Anchor at CWD Inode"]
    rootAnchor --> walker
    cwdAnchor --> walker["Component-by-Component Walker"]
    walker --> permCheck{"Execute Permission on Each Dir ?"}
    permCheck -- No --> errEnoent["Return ENOENT or EACCES"]
    permCheck -- Yes --> lookup["Hash Table Lookup in dir inode"]
    lookup --> symRes{"Symbolic Link ?"}
    symRes -- Yes --> follow["Follow Target Recursively"]
    symRes -- No --> nextComp["Next Path Component"]
    follow --> nextComp
    nextComp --> moreComp{"More Components ?"}
    moreComp -- Yes --> permCheck
    moreComp -- No --> finalInode["Return Final Inode"]
    finalInode --> result["VFS File Object to User"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Inode ↔ Data Blocks Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    inodeBox["Inode Number 131235"]
    inodeBox --> meta["Metadata: Type Perm Owner Size Timestamps"]
    inodeBox --> d0["Direct Pointer 0"]
    inodeBox --> d1["Direct Pointer 1"]
    inodeBox --> d2["Direct Pointer 2"]
    inodeBox --> d3["Direct Pointer 3"]
    inodeBox --> d4["Direct Pointer 4"]
    inodeBox --> d5["Direct Pointer 5"]
    inodeNode --> d6["Direct Pointer 6"]
    inodeBox --> d7["Direct Pointer 7"]
    inodeBox --> d8["Direct Pointer 8"]
    inodeBox --> d9["Direct Pointer 9"]
    inodeBox --> d10["Direct Pointer 10"]
    inodeBox --> d11["Direct Pointer 11"]
    inodeBox --> indir["Single Indirect Pointer"]
    inodeBox --> dindir["Double Indirect Pointer"]
    inodeBox --> tindir["Triple Indirect Pointer"]
    
    d0 --> blkA["Data Block A"]
    d1 --> blkB["Data Block B"]
    indir --> indirBlk["Indirect Block with 1024 Pointers"]
    indirBlk --> blkC["Data Block C"]
    indirBlk --> blkD["Data Block D"]
    dindir --> dindirBlk["Double Indirect Block"]
    dindirBlk --> indirBlk2["Indirect Block 2"]
    indirBlk2 --> blkE["Data Block E"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The classical ext4 inode supports files up to **2 TiB** with the 48-bit i_blocks_lo/i_blocks_hi counters, and 4 KiB block size: $12 + 1024 + 1024^2 + 1024^3$ blocks.

## 4.4 Functional Flow — `ls -l` Interpretation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    cmdLS["Command: ls -l /etc"] --> readDir["sys_getdents64 read directory entries"]
    readDir --> inodeQ["For each entry fetch inode via sys_stat64"]
    inodeQ --> fmt["Format permission string"]
    fmt --> typeMap["Map first char to file type"]
    typeMap --> permMap["Map rwx triplets to octal 0 to 7"]
    permMap --> render["Render to terminal with column alignment"]
    render --> out["Output: drwxr-xr-x 5 root root 4096 Aug 12 /etc"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between absolute and relative paths in Linux. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Absolute Path** — A path that begins at the root directory `/` and uniquely locates a file regardless of the current working directory.
  - *Example*: `/home/student/Documents/result.pdf`
- **Relative Path** — A path resolved from the current working directory; it does *not* start with `/`.
  - *Example*: `Documents/result.pdf` (when CWD is `/home/student`)
- **Key Distinction**: Absolute paths are system-wide unique; relative paths are valid only with respect to the current directory.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any **six** file types supported by the Linux file system along with the symbol used by `ls -l`.

**Model Answer (3 marks — list any 6):**

| # | File Type | `ls -l` Symbol |
|---|-----------|:---:|
| 1 | Regular file | `-` |
| 2 | Directory | `d` |
| 3 | Symbolic link | `l` |
| 4 | Character device | `c` |
| 5 | Block device | `b` |
| 6 | Named pipe (FIFO) | `p` |
| 7 | Unix domain socket | `s` |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not** confuse `/` (root directory) with `/root` (superuser's home). Examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** every time this slips.
> - **Do not** confuse "static" with "read-only" in FHS. `/var` is dynamic and read-write, but `/usr` is static and may be mounted read-only.
> - When asked to list file types, examiners want the **symbol** (`d`, `l`, `c`, `b`, `p`, `s`) plus a one-line description. A bare list of names scores partial credit only.
> - Permission-string questions: **always show the octal conversion** (`rwx = 7`, `rw- = 6`, `r-x = 5`, `r-- = 4`, `-wx = 3`, `-w- = 2`, `--x = 1`, `--- = 0`).
> - For hard links, you must explicitly state that the inode number is **identical**, not just the file size.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS). With a neat diagram, describe the purpose of any **eight** top-level directories under `/`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on: (i) Inode, (ii) Mount Point, (iii) Virtual File System (`/proc` and `/sys`). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS)** is a specification maintained by the Linux Foundation that prescribes a uniform directory structure for Linux distributions. It defines the **content** and **purpose** of every top-level directory so that software can be installed predictably.

**Purpose of eight top-level directories (5 marks — ½ mark each + ½ for diagram):**

1. **`/bin`** — Essential user command binaries (`ls`, `cp`, `mv`) needed in single-user mode.
2. **`/sbin`** — Essential system-administration binaries (`fsck`, `iptables`, `reboot`).
3. **`/etc`** — Host-specific configuration files; no binaries belong here.
4. **`/home`** — User home directories; one sub-directory per user.
5. **`/var`** — Variable data such as log files (`/var/log`), mail spools, and databases.
6. **`/tmp`** — World-writable scratch space; files are typically purged at reboot.
7. **`/usr`** — Secondary hierarchy for read-only user utilities; contains `/usr/bin`, `/usr/lib`, `/usr/share`.
8. **`/dev`** — Device files representing hardware (`/dev/sda` for the first disk, `/dev/null` as a data sink).

**Diagram** — A simplified tree of `/` with the eight branches above. (See Section 4.1 for a complete reference diagram.)

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**(i) Inode (3 marks):**
An **inode** (index node) is a data structure on disk that stores all metadata about a file — its type, permissions, ownership, size, timestamps, link count, and pointers to data blocks — *except* its name. Each file has exactly one inode, identified by an integer inode number unique within a filesystem. Directories map filenames to inode numbers; this indirection is what allows hard links and separate metadata.

**(ii) Mount Point (2 marks):**
A **mount point** is a directory in the currently accessible file-system tree at which an additional filesystem is attached. For example, `mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/data` makes the contents of `/dev/sdb1` accessible under `/mnt/data`. The command `mount` (no arguments) lists all current mounts; `/etc/fstab` declares persistent ones.

**(iii) Virtual File Systems `/proc` and `/sys` (2 marks):**
`/proc` and `/sys` are **virtual (pseudo) file systems** generated in memory by the kernel. `/proc` exposes per-process information (`/proc/<PID>/status`, `/proc/cpuinfo`, `/proc/meminfo`) and is the canonical way to interact with running processes. `/sys` (sysfs) exposes kernel objects, devices, and buses for userspace tools like `udev` and `lsof`. They consume no disk space and disappear on shutdown.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the different **file types** in Linux. With an example output of `ls -l`, show how to identify each one. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the difference between a **hard link** and a **symbolic link**? Demonstrate with Linux commands how you would create each and prove that they behave differently. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Linux supports **seven** file types. The first character of the permission string in `ls -l` identifies the type.

**Description with sample output (6 marks — ~1 mark per type):**

```bash
$ ls -l /dev/null /dev/sda1 /etc /etc/passwd /usr/bin/python3
crw-rw-rw- 1 root root  1,   3  Aug 12 09:14 /dev/null
brw-rw---- 1 root disk  8,   1  Aug 12 09:14 /dev/sda1
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root  4096  Aug 12 09:14 /etc
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  2841  Aug 12 09:14 /etc/passwd
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root    16  Aug 12 09:14 /usr/bin/python3 -> python3.11
```

| File | First char | Type | Reason |
|------|:---:|------|--------|
| `/dev/null` | `c` | **Character device** | Stream-oriented, byte-by-byte I/O. |
| `/dev/sda1` | `b` | **Block device** | Block-oriented (disk partition). |
| `/etc` | `d` | **Directory** | Contains other files. |
| `/etc/passwd` | `-` | **Regular file** | Plain text data. |
| `/usr/bin/python3` | `l` | **Symbolic link** | The `->` arrow shows it points to `python3.11`. |

(Additional types `p` named-pipe and `s` socket can be created with `mkfifo` and `nc -l` for demonstration.)

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Hard link** — An additional directory entry that points to the **same inode** (and therefore the same data) as the original. Both filenames are equal citizens; deleting one does not affect the other until the link count drops to zero.
- **Symbolic (soft) link** — A small file of type `l` whose contents are the pathname of the target. It is a *pointer by name*, not by inode.

**Demonstration (5 marks):**

```bash
$ echo "Original" > orig.txt
$ ln orig.txt hard.txt          # Create hard link
$ ln -s orig.txt soft.txt       # Create symbolic link

$ ls -li orig.txt hard.txt soft.txt
131235 -rw-r--r-- 2 student student 9  Aug 12 09:14  orig.txt
131235 -rw-r--r-- 2 student student 9  Aug 12 09:14  hard.txt
131236 lrwxrwxrwx 1 student student 8  Aug 12 09:14  soft.txt -> orig.txt
```

**Proof of behaviour (1 mark each for the three points):**

1. **Same inode**: `orig.txt` and `hard.txt` share inode `131235`; `soft.txt` has a different inode `131236`.
2. **Hard link survives deletion of original**:
   ```bash
   $ rm orig.txt
   $ cat hard.txt          # Outputs: Original
   $ cat soft.txt          # Outputs: cat: soft.txt: No such file or directory
   ```
3. **Symbolic link can cross filesystems**:
   ```bash
   $ ln -s /mnt/usb/file.txt ~/myfile   # Allowed across mount points
   $ ln /mnt/usb/file.txt ~/myfile      # NOT allowed: Invalid cross-device link
   ```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Looking at the Linux File System**

- ☐ Linux is **single-rooted**; everything branches from `/`. There are no drive letters.
- ☐ The **FHS** defines directory contents — memorize `/bin`, `/sbin`, `/etc`, `/home`, `/var`, `/tmp`, `/usr`, `/opt`, `/dev`, `/proc`, `/sys`, `/boot`, `/lib`, `/mnt`, `/media`, `/srv`, `/run`.
- ☐ Seven file types: **`- d l c b p s`** — know the symbol **and** a one-line definition.
- ☐ `pwd` shows current directory; `cd` changes it; `ls -l` shows details.
- ☐ **Absolute path** starts with `/`; **relative path** does not.
- ☐ Special tokens: `.` (current), `..` (parent), `~` (home), `-` (previous dir via `cd -`).
- ☐ An **inode** is a file's metadata. The filename is *not* in the inode; the directory maps name → inode.
- ☐ **Hard links** share the inode; **symbolic links** are separate inodes pointing by *name*.
- ☐ To create a hard link: `ln source link`. For a symbolic link: `ln -s source link`.
- ☐ `df -h` shows disk space; `df -i` shows inode usage. A full inode table causes *"No space left on device"* errors even with free space.
- ☐ `du -sh <path>` measures directory size. `stat <file>` shows full metadata.
- ☐ Execute (`x`) permission is required on **every** directory in a path — not just the final one.
- ☐ `/proc` and `/sys` are **virtual** filesystems that exist only in RAM; they expose process and kernel data.
- ☐ Device files live in `/dev`; block devices (`b`) for disks, character devices (`c`) for terminals and mice.
- ☐ Mounting attaches an extra filesystem to the tree at a chosen mount point (e.g., `/mnt`).
- ☐ `PATH_MAX = 4096` characters; `NAME_MAX = 255` bytes on ext4.
- ☐ The classic 10-field format of `ls -l`: **type, owner-rwx, group-rwx, other-rwx, links, owner, group, size, mtime, name**.
- ☐ Octal permission weights: `r=4, w=2, x=1`. Common modes: `644` (files), `755` (executables), `600` (private), `700` (private directory).
- ☐ "Everything is a file" is the unifying Unix philosophy — processes, sockets, pipes, and devices all expose a file-like interface.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traversing Directories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Traversing Directories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition
**Traversing Directories** in Linux/Unix refers to the process of moving the shell's **current working directory (CWD)** pointer from one location in the hierarchical filesystem tree to another, enabling the user (or a script) to access, list, and manipulate files located in different paths of the **single-rooted inverted tree filesystem** structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of *PEEVT412 – Linux and Scripting* emphasizes directory traversal as a foundation for shell navigation. The official syllabus prescribes the commands `cd`, `pwd`, `pushd`, `popd`, `dirs`, and the use of `~`, `..`, `.` along with path resolution logic.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a massive **multi-storey library building** with one main entrance (the **root `/`** directory). Every floor is a directory, every room is a subdirectory, and every book is a file. **Traversing directories** is exactly like walking around the building — you have a *current location* (your *current working directory*), and you can move **up** a floor (`cd ..`), **down** into a specific room (`cd /home/student/projects`), or **jump** to a previously visited room (`pushd` / `popd`).

The shell keeps a small **"memory book"** (the *directory stack*) where it jots down rooms you were just in, so you can bounce back to them quickly — like leaving a trail of bread crumbs.

## Key Vocabulary Box
> [!NOTE]
> - **Root Directory (`/`)**: The single top-most directory in the Linux Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS). **There is exactly one root.**
> - **Current Working Directory (CWD)**: The directory in which the shell is currently "standing". Accessed via `pwd`.
> - **Home Directory (`~`)**: A per-user convenience shortcut. For user `alice`, `~` expands to `/home/alice`.
> - **Parent Directory (`..`)**: One level *above* the CWD in the tree.
> - **Current Directory (`.`)**: A self-reference to the CWD itself.
> - **Directory Stack**: A **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** memory structure maintained by the shell to remember recent directories.

## Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024
- It is a **direct syllabus topic** under Module 3 of PEEVT412.
- It forms the **prerequisite** for all file operations (`ls`, `cp`, `mv`, `rm`).
- Frequently asked as a **2-mark definition** or **7-mark command-based** question in University ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Inverted-Tree Model of Linux Filesystem
Linux organizes all storage under a **single inverted tree** whose root is `/`. To move through this tree, the shell maintains two implicit pieces of state:

1. **Current Working Directory (CWD)** — a path string.
2. **Directory Stack** — a LIFO list of recently visited directory path strings.

The traversal workflow is governed by these *pure-string transformations*. There is no GUI, no "drive letters" — only paths.

## 2.2 Two Kinds of Paths
| Path Type | Begins With | Resolution Rule | Example |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|---------|
| **Absolute Path** | `/` | Read from root; ignore CWD. | `/var/log/syslog` |
| **Relative Path** | Letter, `~`, `.`, `..` | Read from CWD. | `../project/main.c` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Favorite):** "If the path begins with `/`, it is absolute; otherwise, it is relative."

## 2.3 Core Traversal Commands — Cheat Sheet
| Command | Full Form | Purpose | Affects CWD? | Affects Stack? |
|---------|-----------|---------|--------------|----------------|
| `pwd` | Print Working Directory | Displays absolute CWD | No | No |
| `cd <dir>` | Change Directory | Sets CWD to `<dir>` | **Yes** | No |
| `cd` (no arg) | Change to Home | Sets CWD to `$HOME` | **Yes** | No |
| `cd -` | Change to Previous | Toggles between two most recent CWDs | **Yes** | No |
| `pushd <dir>` | Push Directory | Changes to `<dir>` and pushes it onto stack | **Yes** | **Yes (push)** |
| `popd` | Pop Directory | Removes top of stack and cd's to it | **Yes** | **Yes (pop)** |
| `dirs` | List Stack | Prints current directory stack | No | No |
| `dirs -c` | Clear Stack | Empties the directory stack | No | **Yes (clear)** |

## 2.4 Special Path Tokens
| Token | Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|-------|---------|---------------------|
| `~` | User's home directory | Scripts that run for *any* logged-in user |
| `~user` | Another user's home | Admin scripts |
| `-` (in `cd -`) | **OLDPWD** — previous CWD | Quick toggle between two dirs |
| `.` | Current directory | Running executables: `./a.out` |
| `..` | Parent directory | Moving up one level |
| `../..` | Grandparent | Escaping deep project trees |
| `cd -` | Swap with previous CWD | Compare two directories quickly |

## 2.5 The Directory Stack — Conceptual Model
The shell's directory stack behaves like a **stack data structure**:
- **`pushd dir`** → *push* `dir` onto the stack AND change CWD to `dir`.
- **`popd`** → *pop* the top entry, remove it from the stack, AND change CWD to the new top.
- **`dirs -v`** → numbered list of stack contents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `pushd` and `popd` are **bash builtins**, not external programs. They are part of the *shell* and reside in RAM. They are **not persistent** across login sessions unless saved explicitly in `~/.bashrc`.

## 2.6 Logical Resolution Algorithm (How `cd` Decides)
When you type `cd X`, the shell follows this exact decision order:

1. **If `X` is empty** → `cd` goes to `$HOME`.
2. **If `X` is `-`** → CWD becomes `$OLDPWD`, OLDPWD becomes old CWD (swap).
3. **If `X` starts with `/`** → It is an *absolute path*. Set CWD to that exact path.
4. **If `X` starts with `~`** → Expand the tilde to a home directory, then proceed.
5. **Otherwise** → Treat as *relative path*, resolve against current CWD.
6. **If `CDPATH` is set** → Search each directory in `CDPATH` for `X` (like `$PATH` for `cd`).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **DevOps Engineers** use `pushd`/`popd` to switch rapidly between source, build, and test directories.
- **Embedded Linux developers** frequently `cd /sys/class/gpio` and back, needing fast toggling.
- **System Administrators** use directory traversal in shell scripts to perform bulk operations on user homes, logs, and config files.
- **Cybersecurity Analysts** traverse `/proc`, `/etc`, and `/var/log` to investigate system state.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Command Behavior Traces
The following tables show **deterministic, traceable execution** of traversal commands. Each row represents one shell event.

### 3.1.1 Initial State Assumption
$$
\text{Stack} = [\,], \quad \text{CWD} = \text{/home/alice}, \quad \text{OLDPWD} = \text{undefined}
$$

### 3.1.2 Trace of `cd` Operations
| Step | Command | New CWD | New OLDPWD | Stack After |
|:----:|---------|---------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | `cd /var/log` | `/var/log` | `/home/alice` | `[ ]` |
| 2 | `cd -` | `/home/alice` | `/var/log` | `[ ]` |
| 3 | `cd -` | `/var/log` | `/home/alice` | `[ ]` |
| 4 | `cd` (no arg) | `/home/alice` | `/var/log` | `[ ]` |
| 5 | `cd ..` | `/home` | `/home/alice` | `[ ]` |
| 6 | `cd ../../etc` | `/etc` | `/home` | `[ ]` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The `cd` family **never modifies** the directory stack. It only juggles `CWD` and `OLDPWD`.

### 3.1.3 Trace of `pushd` / `popd` Operations
| Step | Command | New CWD | Stack After (bottom → top) |
|:----:|---------|---------|----------------------------|
| 1 | `pushd /etc` | `/etc` | `[/etc]` |
| 2 | `pushd /var` | `/var` | `[/etc, /var]` |
| 3 | `pushd /tmp` | `/tmp` | `[/etc, /var, /tmp]` |
| 4 | `pushd` (no arg) | `/var` | `[/etc, /tmp, /var]` |
| 5 | `popd` | `/tmp` | `[/etc, /tmp]` |
| 6 | `popd` | `/etc` | `[/etc]` |
| 7 | `popd` | `/etc` (stack empty) | `[]` |

## 3.2 Derivation of the `pushd` (no-argument) Behavior
A common KTU question is: *"What does `pushd` with no argument do?"*

**Logical derivation:**

1. Let the current directory stack be $S = [d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_{n-1}, d_n]$, where $d_n$ is the **top**.
2. Let the current CWD be $C = d_n$ (the top is always the CWD by convention).
3. Calling `pushd` with no argument performs a **stack rotation**:

$$
S_{\text{new}} = [d_2, d_3, \ldots, d_n, d_1]
$$

4. The new CWD is the new top of stack, which is $d_1$.
5. Therefore, `pushd` without arguments **swaps the bottom two elements** of the stack and cd's to the former bottom.

**Verification with our trace (Step 4 above):**
- Stack before: `[/etc, /var, /tmp]`, CWD = `/tmp` (top).
- Stack after: `[/etc, /tmp, /var]`, CWD = `/var` (top).
- The element `/var` was previously at index 1, and it is now the top. ✔

## 3.3 Derivation of `cd -` Behavior
The shell maintains an internal variable `OLDPWD` representing the **previous CWD**.

$$
\text{CWD}_{\text{new}} = \text{OLDPWD}_{\text{old}}
$$

$$
\text{OLDPWD}_{\text{new}} = \text{CWD}_{\text{old}}
$$

This is a textbook **swap operation** between the two scalar variables. There is **no** directory-stack involvement.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Resolving `cd ../../projects/shell`
Assume initial CWD = `/home/alice/notes/os/unit3`.

**Step 1 — Start from CWD:**
$$
\text{CWD}_0 = \text{/home/alice/notes/os/unit3}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply `..` (first):**
$$
\text{CWD}_1 = \text{/home/alice/notes/os}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply `..` (second):**
$$
\text{CWD}_2 = \text{/home/alice/notes}
$$

**Step 4 — Append `projects`:**
$$
\text{CWD}_3 = \text{/home/alice/notes/projects}
$$

**Step 5 — Append `shell`:**
$$
\text{CWD}_4 = \text{/home/alice/notes/projects/shell}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{Final CWD} = \text{/home/alice/notes/projects/shell}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** Students often process path components *left-to-right*. The correct model is to **go up for `..` first**, then descend. Always evaluate each `..` against the *current* path, not the original CWD.

## 3.5 Scripting Implication — Why `cd` in Scripts Fails
A subtle KTU-favorite question: *"Why does `cd /tmp` inside a script not change my interactive shell's CWD?"*

**Derivation:**
- A shell script runs inside a **child subshell** (unless sourced with `.`).
- The `cd` builtin modifies the CWD variable of the **child process only**.
- The parent interactive shell's CWD variable remains unchanged.
- Therefore, on script exit, the CWD change is **discarded**.

**Fix — source the script:**
$$
\text{`. ./mysetup.sh`} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \text{execute in current shell, persist CWD change.}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Traversal Engine Architecture
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Input: cd X] --> B{X is Empty?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Set CWD to HOME]
    B -- No --> D{X is "-"?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Swap CWD and OLDPWD]
    D -- No --> F{X starts with /?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Set CWD to absolute path X]
    F -- No --> H{X starts with ~?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Expand tilde, then resolve]
    H -- No --> J{X contains .. or . ?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Resolve relative to CWD]
    J -- No --> L[CDPATH Lookup]
    L -- Found --> M[Set CWD to found path]
    L -- Not Found --> N[Error: No such file or directory]
    C --> O[Update OLDPWD]
    E --> O
    G --> O
    I --> O
    K --> O
    M --> O
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Directory Stack as a LIFO Structure
```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Stack[Directory Stack - bottom to top]
        D1[/home/alice]
        D2[/var/log]
        D3[/etc/network]
        D4[/usr/local/bin]
    end
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4
    D4 -.->|CWD pointer| CWD((Current Working Directory))
    OP1[pushd /opt] -. push .-> D4
    D4 -. pop .-> OP2[popd]
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — `pushd` / `popd` Lifecycle
```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EmptyStack
    EmptyStack --> Pushed1: pushd /etc
    Pushed1 --> Pushed2: pushd /var
    Pushed2 --> Pushed3: pushd /tmp
    Pushed3 --> Rotated: pushd (no arg)
    Rotated --> Pushed2: popd
    Pushed2 --> Pushed1: popd
    Pushed1 --> EmptyStack: popd
    EmptyStack --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Absolute vs. Relative Path Resolution
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Path String] --> B{Begins with /?}
    B -- Yes --> ABS[Absolute Resolver: Start at root /]
    B -- No --> C{Begins with ~?}
    C -- Yes --> TILDE[Tilde Resolver: Expand to /home/user]
    C -- No --> REL[Relative Resolver: Start at CWD]
    ABS --> OUT[Final Absolute Path]
    TILDE --> OUT
    REL --> OUT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between absolute and relative paths in Linux with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **absolute path** begins with the root directory `/` and locates a file or directory *uniquely* from the root, regardless of the current working directory. Example: `/home/alice/projects/main.c`. A **relative path** is specified *with respect to the current working directory* (CWD) and does not start with `/`. Example: `../projects/main.c` (1.5 marks for definition, 1 mark for examples, 0.5 marks for distinction).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the purpose of the `pushd` and `popd` commands?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> `pushd` (push directory) changes the current working directory to the specified one **and pushes** it onto the **directory stack**, a LIFO memory structure (1.5 marks). `popd` removes the **top entry** from the directory stack and changes the CWD to the new top of the stack, allowing the user to quickly return to a previously visited directory (1.5 marks).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** confuse `cd` (no stack effect) with `pushd` (stack effect). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> - **Do not** say `pushd` "saves" directories permanently — the stack is in-memory and lost on logout.
> - **Do not** forget to mention `dirs -v` when listing the stack — a fully-marked answer shows the *command* used to *verify* the state.
> - Always state the **type of path** (absolute/relative) when explaining a `cd` example.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the concept of the **current working directory (CWD)** in Linux. Describe the commands `pwd`, `cd`, and `cd -` with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **directory stack** concept in detail. Explain `pushd`, `popd`, and `dirs` commands with examples illustrating at least 4 stack operations. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the difference between **absolute and relative paths**. Demonstrate, with a step-by-step trace, how the path `../../projects/shell` is resolved when CWD is `/home/alice/notes/os/unit3`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a shell script that accepts a directory path as an argument, navigates to it, and prints the contents of the current directory along with the absolute path. Also explain why `cd` inside a script does not affect the parent shell. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions — Question A

### A(a) Model Solution

**Current Working Directory (CWD):**
The CWD is the directory in which the shell is currently operating. It is a single path string maintained internally by the shell. Every relative path is resolved against the CWD. The CWD is initialized to the user's home directory (`$HOME`) at login. **(2 marks)**

**`pwd` (Print Working Directory):**
The `pwd` builtin displays the **absolute** path of the CWD on the standard output.
```
$ pwd
/home/alice/projects
```
It has two common options: `-L` (logical, follows symlinks) and `-P` (physical, resolves all symlinks). **(2 marks)**

**`cd <dir>` (Change Directory):**
The `cd` builtin changes the CWD to `<dir>`. The `<dir>` may be an absolute path (e.g., `cd /etc`) or a relative path (e.g., `cd ../notes`). If `<dir>` is omitted, CWD becomes `$HOME`. **(2 marks)**

**`cd -` (Toggle to Previous CWD):**
The shell stores the previous CWD in the variable `OLDPWD`. `cd -` swaps CWD and OLDPWD, allowing quick toggling.
```
$ pwd             →  /home/alice
$ cd /var/log
$ pwd             →  /var/log
$ cd -
$ pwd             →  /home/alice
```
**(1 mark)**

### A(b) Model Solution

**Directory Stack:**
The directory stack is an **in-memory LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** data structure maintained by the shell. It records directories recently visited via `pushd`. The **top of the stack is always the CWD**. Built-in commands: `pushd`, `popd`, `dirs`. **(2 marks)**

**Command `pushd <dir>`:**
Changes CWD to `<dir>` and pushes `<dir>` onto the top of the stack.
```
$ pushd /etc            →  /etc /etc
$ pushd /var            →  /var /etc /var
```
**(1.5 marks)**

**Command `pushd` (no argument):**
Rotates the stack so that the second-from-top becomes the new top, and cd's into it.
```
$ dirs
/etc /var /tmp
$ pushd
/tmp /etc /var        ← CWD is now /var
```
**(1.5 marks)**

**Command `popd`:**
Removes the top entry from the stack and cd's to the new top.
```
$ dirs
/etc /var /tmp
$ popd
/etc /var              ← CWD is now /var (was second)
```
**(1 mark)**

**Command `dirs`:**
Lists the current contents of the directory stack. The `dirs -v` variant shows a **numbered list** for easy reference. **(1 mark)**

## 5.4 Model Solutions — Question B

### B(a) Model Solution

**Absolute vs Relative Paths:** **(2 marks)**
- *Absolute path:* Begins with `/`. Identifies a file uniquely. Independent of CWD.
- *Relative path:* Does not begin with `/`. Resolved against the CWD.

**Step-by-Step Resolution of `../../projects/shell` from CWD `/home/alice/notes/os/unit3`:** **(5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial CWD: } & \text{/home/alice/notes/os/unit3} \\
\text{Apply first `..`: } & \text{/home/alice/notes/os} \\
\text{Apply second `..`: } & \text{/home/alice/notes} \\
\text{Descend into `projects`: } & \text{/home/alice/notes/projects} \\
\text{Descend into `shell`: } & \text{/home/alice/notes/projects/shell} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{Final CWD} = \text{/home/alice/notes/projects/shell}
$$

### B(b) Model Solution

**Shell Script:** **(4 marks)**

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Script: navlist.sh
# Accepts a directory path, cd's to it, lists contents and prints absolute path.

if [ $# -ne 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <directory_path>"
    exit 1
fi

TARGET="$1"

# Change to the target directory
cd "$TARGET" || { echo "Error: Cannot cd to $TARGET"; exit 1; }

# Print absolute path and contents
echo "Absolute Path: $(pwd)"
echo "Contents:"
ls -la
```

**Explanation of why `cd` inside a script does not affect the parent shell:** **(3 marks)**

When a shell script is executed (e.g., `bash navlist.sh` or `./navlist.sh`), the kernel spawns a **new child process** (a subshell). This subshell has its own copy of the CWD variable. The `cd` builtin modifies the **child's CWD** only. When the script exits, the child process terminates, and its CWD change is **discarded**. The parent interactive shell's CWD remains untouched. To make a script's `cd` persist, the script must be **sourced** using `. ./navlist.sh` or `source navlist.sh`, which executes commands in the **current shell process** without forking a child.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
- **CWD** = the directory the shell is "in" right now. Verified with `pwd`.
- **Absolute paths** start with `/`; **relative paths** do not. KTU questions always test this distinction.
- `cd` changes CWD but **does not** modify the directory stack.
- `cd -` swaps CWD with `OLDPWD` (no stack involvement).
- `cd` (no argument) takes you to `$HOME`.
- `pushd dir` = cd to `dir` **and** push it onto the stack.
- `pushd` (no argument) = **rotate** the stack (swap bottom two elements).
- `popd` = remove top of stack and cd to new top.
- `dirs` lists the stack; `dirs -v` shows a numbered list; `dirs -c` clears it.
- `~` = home directory; `~user` = that user's home; `.` = current; `..` = parent.
- Directory stack is **RAM-only** and **lost** when the shell session ends.
- `cd` inside a script does not affect the parent shell — **source** the script to persist changes.
- `CDPATH` is a colon-separated list of *search roots* for relative `cd` lookups (similar to `$PATH` for executables).
- **Common 14-mark question pattern:** "Explain directory traversal commands with examples and a working shell script."
- **Memorize the trace** — KTU examiners love asking you to manually walk through a `pushd`/`popd` sequence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Listing Files and Directories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Listing Files and Directories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Listing Files and Directories** refers to the systematic enumeration, visualization, and metadata extraction of files and directories (folders) within a Linux/Unix file system hierarchy. In the **POSIX (Portable Operating System Interface) compliant** GNU coreutils environment, this operation is primarily executed through the **`ls`** (list) command, which is part of the **GNU coreutils package** and reads directory entries from the underlying filesystem using system calls such as `opendir(3)`, `readdir(3)`, and `closedir(3)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic forms a foundational pillar of Module 3 (File and Directory Operations). Mastery of `ls` options, wildcards, and output interpretation is a **mandatory pre-requisite** for advanced shell scripting modules and lab examinations under the **PEEVT412** course.

From a formal operating systems perspective, when a user invokes the `ls` command without arguments, the **shell (typically `bash`, `zsh`, or `sh`)** interprets it via the **PATH** environment variable, executes the binary located at `/bin/ls` or `/usr/bin/ls`, which then queries the **inode table** of the current working directory (`$PWD`) and formats the retrieved metadata for terminal display.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just walked into a **vast library** with millions of books stored in countless shelves. Before you can pick a book, you need to know:

- **Which shelves (directories) exist?**
- **What books (files) are on each shelf?**
- **What are the sizes, types, and ownerships of these books?**

The `ls` command acts like a **digital librarian's catalog system** — it gives you an instant inventory of any section of your file system. Just as a librarian can sort the catalog **alphabetically, by date added, or by size**, the `ls` command provides flags (options) to sort and display the directory contents in different ways.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Explanation:** Typing `ls` in a Linux terminal is equivalent to asking your computer: *"Hey, show me everything inside this folder!"* The folder is called a **directory** in Linux terminology. The contents shown may include files (documents, images, scripts) and sub-folders (other directories nested inside).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Block Size:** Default **1024 bytes** (1 KiB) in traditional Unix, **4096 bytes** commonly on modern Linux ext4/xfs filesystems.
- **Standard Output Stream (`stdout`):** File descriptor **1** — where `ls` writes its results.
- **Standard Error Stream (`stderr`):** File descriptor **2** — where `ls` writes error messages.
- **Inode:** A **data structure** storing information about a file except its name and actual data.
- **POSIX:** **Portable Operating System Interface** — the IEEE standard that defines API for Unix-like systems.

> [!TIP]
> Think of an **inode** as the "**ID card**" of a file — it holds the metadata (permissions, owner, size, timestamps) but the filename itself is just a "**name tag**" attached to this card by the directory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The `ls` Command — Operational Breakdown

The `ls` utility, when executed, performs the following **internal operational steps**:

1. **Argument Parsing:** The shell parses the command line, identifies options (flags starting with `-`) and operands (file/directory names).
2. **Directory Resolution:** For each operand, the system resolves the path. If no operand is specified, it defaults to the current directory represented by the dot operator `.`.
3. **System Call Invocation:** The `ls` binary invokes `opendir()` to obtain a directory stream handle.
4. **Directory Traversal:** It calls `readdir()` iteratively to retrieve each `dirent` structure containing `d_ino` (inode number) and `d_name` (entry name).
5. **Metadata Retrieval (for `-l`):** The `lstat()` system call fetches detailed inode metadata.
6. **Sorting:** Entries are sorted alphabetically by default (using `strcmp()`), unless `-t`, `-S`, `-v`, or other sort options are specified.
7. **Output Formatting:** The output is formatted as a multi-column (default) or long-listing (`-l`) format and flushed to `stdout`.

## 2.2 The `ls` Command — Most Important Options

Below is a comprehensive list of the **most frequently tested `ls` options** in KTU examinations:

### 2.2.1 Display Modification Options

| Option | Long Form | Description |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| `-l` | `--format=long` | Long listing format with permissions, owner, size, date. |
| `-1` | `--format=single-column` | One entry per line. |
| `-x` | `--format=across` | Sort entries by rows (columns first). |
| `-C` | `--format=vertical` | Default multi-column sort by columns. |

### 2.2.2 What to Show Options

| Option | Long Form | Description |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| `-a` | `--all` | Show **all** entries including hidden (those starting with `.`). |
| `-A` | `--almost-all` | Show all entries except `.` and `..`. |
| `-d` | `--directory` | List directory entries themselves, not their contents. |
| `-R` | `--recursive` | Recursively list subdirectories encountered. |
| `-h` | `--human-readable` | Print sizes in human-readable units (K, M, G). |
| `-i` | `--inode` | Print the inode number of each file. |
| `-s` | `--size` | Print the allocated size of each file in blocks. |
| `-F` | `--classify` | Append indicator ( `/`, `*`, `@`, `=`, `\|` ) to entries. |

### 2.2.3 Sorting and Filtering Options

| Option | Long Form | Description |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| `-t` | `--sort=time` | Sort by modification time, newest first. |
| `-S` | `--sort=size` | Sort by file size, largest first. |
| `-r` | `--reverse` | Reverse the order of the sort. |
| `-X` | `--sort=extension` | Sort alphabetically by file extension. |
| `-v` | `--sort=version` | Natural sort of version numbers. |

### 2.2.4 Color and Visual Options

| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--color=auto` | Colorize output based on file type (commonly used in `.bashrc`). |
| `-G` | Enable colorized output in BSD-style `ls` (GNU alternative: `--color`). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The `-l`, `-a`, `-h`, `-R`, `-t`, `-S`, `-r`, `-d`, and `-F` options appear in **over 85%** of KTU Linux module exam questions. Memorize these.

## 2.3 Wildcard Patterns (Globbing)

The Linux shell supports **filename expansion** (globbing) using wildcard metacharacters defined in `fnmatch(3)`. These are processed by the shell **before** `ls` is invoked.

| Wildcard | Matches | Example Pattern | Matches |
|----------|---------|-----------------|---------|
| `*` | Zero or more characters | `*.txt` | `file.txt`, `notes.txt`, `a.txt` |
| `?` | Exactly one character | `file?.sh` | `file1.sh`, `fileA.sh` |
| `[...]` | Any one character in the set | `[abc]*.log` | `apple.log`, `banana.log` |
| `[!...]` or `[^...]` | Any one character NOT in the set | `[!a]*` | Anything not starting with `a` |
| `{...}` | Brace expansion (comma-separated) | `{a,b,c}.sh` | `a.sh`, `b.sh`, `c.sh` |
| `~` | Home directory of current user | `~` | Expands to `/home/user` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction:** `*` and `?` are expanded by the **shell**, NOT by `ls`. If no files match the pattern, the shell passes the literal pattern to `ls` (unless `nullglob` option is set in bash).

## 2.4 Decoding the `ls -l` Output

A typical `ls -l` output line has **7 distinct fields**:

$$ \text{Output Line} = \text{Type} + \text{Perms} + \text{Links} + \text{Owner} + \text{Group} + \text{Size} + \text{Date} + \text{Name} $$

**Example output:**

```
-rw-r--r-- 1 ktuuser ktugroup 2048 Jan 15 10:30 report.txt
drwxr-xr-x 2 ktuuser ktugroup 4096 Jan 14 09:15 Documents
```

**Field-by-field interpretation:**

1. **Type** (1 character): `-` (regular file), `d` (directory), `l` (symlink), `b` (block device), `c` (character device), `p` (named pipe), `s` (socket).
2. **Permissions** (9 characters in 3 triplets): Owner, Group, Others — each triplet is `r` (read, value 4), `w` (write, value 2), `x` (execute, value 1).
3. **Link Count** (numeric): Number of hard links to the file.
4. **Owner** (string): Username of the file's owner.
5. **Group** (string): Group ownership.
6. **Size** (numeric, in bytes by default): Use `-h` for human-readable.
7. **Modification Timestamp** (string): Last modification date/time.
8. **Filename** (string): The actual file or directory name.

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

Listing files and directories is not just an academic exercise. In **real-world engineering and DevOps workflows**, it is foundational to:

- **Build System Audits:** Verifying artifact presence after compilation (`ls target/*.jar`).
- **Backup Verification:** Confirming files were copied (`ls -lR /backup/ | wc -l`).
- **Log Rotation Scripts:** Identifying old log files for archival.
- **Disk Space Analysis:** Finding large files (`ls -lSh /var/log/ | head`).
- **CI/CD Pipelines:** Checking for specific files before deployment.
- **Cybersecurity Forensics:** Detecting suspicious hidden files (`ls -la /tmp`).
- **Embedded Systems:** Listing firmware components and binaries on resource-constrained devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Skill Outcome:** After studying this module, students are expected to demonstrate **CO3 (Apply shell commands for file operations)** with Bloom's Taxonomy level **L3 (Apply)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Permission Octal Computation — Exhaustive Derivation

The **`ls -l`** output shows symbolic permissions (e.g., `rwxr-xr--`). These can be converted to a **3-digit octal number** using the standard POSIX formula. Let us derive this step-by-step.

### 3.1.1 Base Value Assignment

Each permission bit is assigned a power of 2:

$$
r = 2^2 = 4, \quad w = 2^1 = 2, \quad x = 2^0 = 1
$$

### 3.1.2 Per-Triplet Sum

For any triplet (e.g., owner, group, or others), the octal value is:

$$
V_{\text{triplet}} = (r \times 4) + (w \times 2) + (x \times 1)
$$

where each of $r, w, x$ is either **1** (granted) or **0** (denied).

### 3.1.3 Worked Example

**Given permission string:** `-rwxr-xr--`

**Step 1:** Split into 3 triplets: `rwx` `r-x` `r--`

**Step 2:** Compute Owner triplet (`rwx`):

$$
V_{\text{owner}} = (1 \times 4) + (1 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Group triplet (`r-x`):

$$
V_{\text{group}} = (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Others triplet (`r--`):

$$
V_{\text{others}} = (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (0 \times 1) = 4 + 0 + 0 = 4
$$

**Step 5:** Concatenate to form the octal:

$$
\text{Octal Permission} = V_{\text{owner}} V_{\text{group}} V_{\text{others}} = 754
$$

**Step 6:** Verification with `stat` command (conceptual):

$$
\text{Expected} = \texttt{chmod 754 file.sh}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Read permission bits **right-to-left** as 4-2-1. So `r-x` = $4+0+1 = 5$, and `r--` = $4+0+0 = 4$.

## 3.2 Human-Readable Size Conversion

The `-h` flag triggers the `human_readable()` function (from coreutils). The formula used internally is:

$$
\text{Display Unit} = \begin{cases} \text{K} & \text{if } 1024 \le \text{size} < 1024^2 \\ \text{M} & \text{if } 1024^2 \le \text{size} < 1024^3 \\ \text{G} & \text{if } 1024^3 \le \text{size} < 1024^4 \\ \text{T} & \text{if } 1024^4 \le \text{size} < 1024^5 \end{cases}
$$

### 3.2.1 Worked Example

**Given:** A file's actual size is **536,870,912 bytes** (exactly 0.5 GiB).

**Step 1:** Check the value against thresholds:

$$
536{,}870{,}912 \ge 1024^3 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \quad \text{?}
$$

**Result:** **False** (536M < 1G). Check next lower unit.

$$
536{,}870{,}912 \ge 1024^2 = 1{,}048{,}576 \quad \text{?}
$$

**Result:** **True**. The display unit is **M** (megabytes).

**Step 2:** Compute the displayed value:

$$
V_{\text{display}} = \frac{536{,}870{,}912}{1{,}048{,}576} = 512.0
$$

**Step 3:** Format the output:

$$
\text{Output} = \texttt{512M}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — `ls` Output Scenarios

The following matrix maps **user intent → recommended `ls` invocation → expected output format**.

| User Intent | Command | Output Description |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|
| Quick directory listing | `ls` | Multi-column, hidden files excluded |
| List with hidden files | `ls -a` | Includes `.bashrc`, `.profile`, etc. |
| Detailed permissions view | `ls -l` | Long format with metadata |
| Human-readable sizes | `ls -lh` | Long format with K, M, G units |
| Sort by largest files first | `ls -lSh` | Long format, size-sorted, human-readable |
| List only directories | `ls -d */` | Shows only subdirectories |
| Recursive tree view | `ls -R` | Lists all subdirectories' contents |
| Inode number for hard links | `ls -i` | Prepends inode number |
| Newest files first | `ls -lt` | Sorted by modification time |
| All files including hidden, detailed | `ls -la` | Combines `-l` and `-a` |
| Mark executable files | `ls -F` | Appends `*` to executables |
| Show directory itself, not contents | `ls -ld /home` | Long format for `/home` only |

## 3.4 Recursive Listing — Pseudocode Logic

While KTU examinations expect **shell commands** (not code), understanding the recursion logic helps. The conceptual flow of `ls -R` is:

$$
\text{ls\_recursive}(\text{path}) = \begin{cases} \text{list entries in } \text{path} \\ \text{for each entry } e \text{ in } \text{path:} \\ \quad \text{if } e \text{ is a directory:} \\ \quad\quad \text{print } e \\ \quad\quad \text{ls\_recursive}(e) \end{cases}
$$

**Boundary condition:** A directory is *not* descended into if it is a **symbolic link** to avoid infinite loops, or if the user lacks **execute (search) permission** on it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the `ls` Command

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **internal data flow** of the `ls` command when a user executes it in a Linux terminal:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User types ls -lh in Terminal Shell] --> B[Shell parses command line]
    B --> C{Valid command and options?}
    C -- No --> D[Display error to stderr]
    C -- Yes --> E[Locate ls binary via PATH variable]
    E --> F[Execute /bin/ls binary]
    F --> G[opendir system call on target path]
    G --> H{Directory readable?}
    H -- No --> I[Permission denied error]
    H -- Yes --> J[readdir loop to fetch entries]
    J --> K[For each entry call lstat for metadata]
    K --> L{ls -l flag set?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Format long listing with perms and size]
    L -- No --> N[Format default multi-column output]
    M --> O{ls -h flag set?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Convert size to human readable units]
    O -- No --> Q[Keep size in raw bytes]
    P --> R[Apply sort order from -t -S or -X]
    Q --> R
    N --> R
    R --> S[Write final output to stdout]
    S --> T[Terminal displays result]
```

## 4.2 Permission Bit Decomposition Diagram

The following diagram shows how the **9-character permission string** decomposes into the three-class, three-permission matrix:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Permission String rwxr-xr--] --> B[Position 1-3: Owner Class]
    A --> C[Position 4-6: Group Class]
    A --> D[Position 7-9: Others Class]
    B --> B1[r = read = 4]
    B --> B2[w = write = 2]
    B --> B3[x = execute = 1]
    C --> C1[r = read = 4]
    C --> C2[hyphen = no write = 0]
    C --> C3[x = execute = 1]
    D --> D1[r = read = 4]
    D --> D2[hyphen = no write = 0]
    D --> D3[hyphen = no execute = 0]
    B1 --> E[Owner Octal = 7]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    C1 --> F[Group Octal = 5]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    D1 --> G[Others Octal = 4]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    E --> H[Final Octal Permission = 754]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

## 4.3 File Type Indicator Reference

The first character of the `ls -l` output is a **file type indicator**. The following Mermaid graph maps the character to its semantic meaning:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[First Character of ls -l Output] --> B{Dash or Letter?}
    B --> C[Dash: Regular File]
    B --> D[Letter: Special Type]
    D --> E[d: Directory]
    D --> F[l: Symbolic Link]
    D --> G[b: Block Device]
    D --> H[c: Character Device]
    D --> I[p: Named Pipe FIFO]
    D --> J[s: Unix Domain Socket]
```

## 4.4 Wildcard Pattern Matching Tree

A conceptual tree showing how the shell's **globbing** mechanism matches filenames against the pattern `file*.?`:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Pattern file star dot question] --> B[Match literal file prefix]
    B --> C[Match any characters via asterisk]
    C --> D[Match literal dot character]
    D --> E[Match exactly one character via question mark]
    E --> F1[file1.txt]
    E --> F2[fileA.py]
    E --> F3[fileZ.sh]
    E --> F4[file_final.c]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The pattern `file*.?` would match `file1.txt`, `fileA.py`, and `fileZ.sh` but **NOT** `file.txt` (no single character after dot) or `file10.java` (no single character after dot).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the `ls` command and explain the difference between `ls -a` and `ls -A`. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The `ls` (list) command in Linux is a **POSIX-compliant utility** used to display the contents of a directory. It reads directory entries from the filesystem and prints them to standard output.

**Difference between `ls -a` and `ls -A`:**

| Aspect | `ls -a` | `ls -A` |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Meaning | Lists all entries | Lists almost all entries |
| Includes `.` (current dir) | **Yes** | No |
| Includes `..` (parent dir) | **Yes** | No |
| Includes hidden files | **Yes** | **Yes** |

**Example:**

```bash
$ ls -a
.  ..  .bashrc  report.txt
$ ls -A
.bashrc  report.txt
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write that `ls -A` "shows hidden files" but forget to explicitly mention the **exclusion of `.` and `..`**. This loses 1 mark.

---

### Question 2: What is a wildcard in Linux? Explain the difference between `*` and `?` wildcards. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **wildcard** (also called a **glob pattern**) is a special character used in shell commands to match filenames based on patterns. Wildcards are expanded by the **shell** before the command is executed.

**Difference between `*` and `?`:**

| Wildcard | Matches | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `*` | Zero or more characters | `*.c` matches `a.c`, `main.c`, `.c` |
| `?` | Exactly one character | `?.c` matches `a.c`, `b.c` (not `ab.c`) |

**Demonstration:**

```bash
$ touch file1 file22 file333 report.txt
$ ls file*        # Matches all files starting with "file"
file1  file22  file333
$ ls file?        # Matches files with exactly one char after "file"
file1
```

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Long Listing Format and Permission Analysis

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the `ls -l` command in detail. Describe each field of its output with a suitable example. How can you display hidden files in long format?

**(b)** [7 Marks] The output of `ls -l script.sh` is given below. Decode every field and compute the octal permission value.

```
-rwxr-x--- 1 ktuadmin ktugroup 8192 Feb 20 14:35 script.sh
```

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The `ls -l` command invokes the **long listing format** in Linux. It provides detailed metadata about files and directories including permissions, ownership, size, and timestamps.

**Field-by-field breakdown** (using `ls -l report.txt`):

```
-rw-r--r-- 1 ktuuser ktugroup 2048 Jan 15 10:30 report.txt
```

| # | Field | Value in Example | Meaning |
|---|-------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | File Type | `-` | Regular file |
| 2 | Permissions | `rw-r--r--` | Owner: rw-, Group: r--, Others: r-- |
| 3 | Hard Links | `1` | One hard link exists |
| 4 | Owner | `ktuuser` | Owner's username |
| 5 | Group | `ktugroup` | Owner's group |
| 6 | Size | `2048` | 2048 bytes |
| 7 | Modified Date | `Jan 15 10:30` | Last modified |
| 8 | Filename | `report.txt` | Name of the entry |

**To display hidden files in long format**, combine `-l` and `-a`:

```bash
ls -la
```

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

Given output: `-rwxr-x--- 1 ktuadmin ktugroup 8192 Feb 20 14:35 script.sh`

**Step 1: Identify File Type**

The first character is `-` → This is a **regular file**.

**Step 2: Decode Permissions**

Split `rwxr-x---` into three triplets:

- **Owner** (`rwx`): read, write, execute — all granted
- **Group** (`r-x`): read and execute granted, write denied
- **Others** (`---`): nothing granted

**Step 3: Compute Octal Values**

For Owner: $r=1, w=1, x=1$

$$
V_{\text{owner}} = (1 \times 4) + (1 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7
$$

For Group: $r=1, w=0, x=1$

$$
V_{\text{group}} = (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5
$$

For Others: $r=0, w=0, x=0$

$$
V_{\text{others}} = (0 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (0 \times 1) = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0
$$

**Step 4: Final Octal Permission**

$$
\text{Octal} = 750
$$

**Step 5: Interpret Other Fields**

| Field | Value | Interpretation |
|-------|-------|----------------|
| Hard Links | 1 | No additional links |
| Owner | ktuadmin | Owned by user "ktuadmin" |
| Group | ktugroup | Belongs to group "ktugroup" |
| Size | 8192 bytes | Exactly 8 KiB |
| Modified | Feb 20 14:35 | Last modified on Feb 20 |

---

### Question B: Wildcard Patterns and Recursive Listing

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain wildcard patterns in Linux with examples. Write shell commands to: (i) List all files with `.txt` extension, (ii) List files starting with "data" followed by a single character, (iii) List all `.c` or `.cpp` files in current directory.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the `ls -R` and `ls -d` commands with examples. How is the `ls -lSh` command different from `ls -lh`? Demonstrate with a practical scenario.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Wildcard patterns** (or **glob patterns**) are special characters used in the Linux shell to match filenames. They are processed by the shell **before** the command is executed. The most common wildcards are:

| Wildcard | Name | Function |
|----------|------|----------|
| `*` | Asterisk | Matches zero or more characters |
| `?` | Question Mark | Matches exactly one character |
| `[ ]` | Square Brackets | Matches any one character in the set |
| `{ }` | Braces | Generates multiple strings (brace expansion) |
| `~` | Tilde | Represents home directory |

**Shell Commands:**

**(i) List all files with `.txt` extension:**

```bash
ls *.txt
```

The `*` matches any prefix (including empty), and `.txt` is matched literally.

**(ii) List files starting with "data" followed by a single character:**

```bash
ls data?
```

The `?` matches **exactly one** character, so this matches `data1`, `dataA`, `data_`, but not `data12` or `data`.

**(iii) List all `.c` or `.cpp` files:**

```bash
ls *.c *.cpp
```

This uses **two glob patterns** — the shell expands both and passes the resulting list to `ls`.

Alternatively, using **brace expansion** (bash-specific):

```bash
ls *.{c,cpp}
```

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**`ls -R` — Recursive Listing:**

The `-R` (recursive) flag makes `ls` traverse subdirectories. For each directory encountered, `ls` prints the directory name and lists its contents.

**Example:**

```bash
$ ls -R project
project:
src  docs  README.md

project/src:
main.c  utils.c

project/docs:
manual.txt  notes.txt
```

**`ls -d` — Directory Itself:**

The `-d` flag lists directory entries as files, **without descending into them**.

**Example:**

```bash
$ ls -l /home/user
total 12
drwxr-xr-x 2 user user 4096 Jan 15 10:30 Documents
drwxr-xr-x 3 user user 4096 Jan 15 10:30 Downloads
$ ls -ld /home/user
drwxr-xr-x 25 user user 4096 Jan 15 10:30 /home/user
```

In the first case, `ls` listed the **contents** of `/home/user`. In the second case, it listed `/home/user` **as a single entry**.

**Difference between `ls -lh` and `ls -lSh`:**

| Aspect | `ls -lh` | `ls -lSh` |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| Display | Long format, human-readable | Long format, human-readable, sorted by size |
| Sort order | Alphabetical (default) | Largest files first |
| Practical use | General inspection | Disk space auditing |

**Practical Scenario:**

```bash
$ ls -lh
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 2.5K Jan 15 report.txt
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 15M  Jan 14 video.mp4
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 200M Jan 13 backup.tar.gz
$ ls -lSh
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 200M Jan 13 backup.tar.gz
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 15M  Jan 14 video.mp4
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 2.5K Jan 15 report.txt
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse `ls -R` (recursive) with `ls -r` (reverse sort). Memorize: **R** = **R**ecurse, **r** = **r**everse. Also, omitting `-h` while showing sizes in lab exams shows a lack of professionalism and may lose partial marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PEEVT412 Module 3**

- **`ls` = list directory contents** (the most fundamental Linux command).
- **Default behavior:** Multi-column, alphabetical, excludes hidden files.
- **`-l`** = Long format (permissions, owner, size, date, name).
- **`-a`** = All files including hidden (those starting with `.`).
- **`-A`** = All files except `.` and `..`.
- **`-h`** = Human-readable sizes (K, M, G, T).
- **`-R`** = Recursive (descend into subdirectories).
- **`-d`** = Directory itself, not its contents.
- **`-t`** = Sort by modification time (newest first).
- **`-S`** = Sort by size (largest first).
- **`-r`** = **Reverse** the sort order.
- **`-i`** = Show inode number.
- **`-F`** = Classify entries with `/`, `*`, `@`, `=`, `|` indicators.
- **File type indicators:** `-` (file), `d` (dir), `l` (symlink), `b/c` (devices), `p` (FIFO), `s` (socket).
- **Permission octal formula:** $V = 4r + 2w + 1x$ for each class.
- **Wildcards:** `*` (zero or more), `?` (exactly one), `[abc]` (set), `[!a]` (negation), `{a,b}` (brace expansion).
- **Globbing is done by the shell, NOT by `ls`.**
- **Combining options:** `ls -lAhR` works just like `ls -l -a -h -R`.
- **Real-world use:** Backup verification, disk audits, build system checks, forensic analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Handling Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEEVT412 – Linux and Scripting**, *Handling Files* refers to the systematic set of operations and mechanisms used by the Linux operating system and shell interpreters (Bash, Sh) to **create, read, write, append, modify, redirect, and manage persistent data stored in secondary storage devices**. A *file* in Linux is a named collection of information (data) recorded on secondary storage, accessed through the **Virtual File System (VFS)** layer, and represented to user processes via non-negative integers known as **file descriptors**.

The Linux kernel treats *everything* as a file — ordinary files, directories, sockets, pipes, block devices, and character devices — all unified under a single I/O paradigm, a philosophy inherited from the **Unix philosophy**: *"Write programs that do one thing well, and work together."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Office Filing Cabinet**
>
> Imagine your college office as the **Linux system**:
> - The **filing cabinet** = your hard disk (persistent storage).
> - Each **drawer/folder** = a directory.
> - Each **document** = a file.
> - The **stamp the clerk puts on a document** when you request it = the **file descriptor** (a numeric token used to refer to the file).
> - The **clerk's desk** = the **shell**, which acts as your intermediary.
> - **Three default trays** on the clerk's desk (Inbox, Outbox, Error tray) = **stdin (0), stdout (1), stderr (2)**.
>
> When you say *"read this file and send it to the printer"*, the clerk (kernel) takes the file from the cabinet, shows it to you on his desk (process), and routes the output to the printer (redirection). This is *file handling* in Linux.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Constants

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **File Descriptor (fd)** | A non-negative integer (0–1023+ for user) identifying an open file |
| **Standard Input (stdin, fd 0)** | Default data stream fed **into** a process |
| **Standard Output (stdout, fd 1)** | Default data stream emitted **by** a process (normal output) |
| **Standard Error (stderr, fd 2)** | Default data stream for error/diagnostic messages |
| **VFS** | Virtual File System — kernel abstraction layer over all filesystem types |
| **Inode** | Data structure storing file metadata (size, permissions, timestamps, block pointers) |
| **MAX_FD (default)** | **1024** soft limit per process (configurable via `ulimit -n`) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** File handling covers the basic file commands, redirection, pipes, file tests in shell scripts, and read/write operations using shell built-ins such as `read`, `echo`, `cat`, `tee`, and the file-test operators `[ -f ]`, `[ -r ]`, `[ -w ]`, `[ -x ]`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Linux File Model — A Layered View

The Linux file system architecture consists of four logical layers:

1. **Application Layer (User Space)** — Shell commands, scripts, programs issue `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `close()` system calls (wrapped by glibc).
2. **System Call Interface** — The boundary to the kernel; e.g., `open(2)`, `read(2)`, `write(2)`, `lseek(2)`, `close(2)`.
3. **Virtual File System (VFS) Switch** — A kernel abstraction that maps operations to the correct underlying file system driver.
4. **Concrete File System Drivers** — `ext4`, `XFS`, `Btrfs`, `NTFS-3G`, `tmpfs`, `procfs`, `sysfs`, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why':** VFS allows a single shell command (e.g., `cat /proc/cpuinfo`) to work uniformly across many file system types without modification. This is the cornerstone of Unix's *"everything is a file"* doctrine.

## 2.2 File Descriptors — The Core Abstraction

When a process opens a file, the kernel returns a **file descriptor** — a small non-negative integer that indexes the process's **file descriptor table**. Every Linux process starts life with three pre-opened descriptors:

| fd | Symbolic Name | Default Destination | Redirection Operator |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | `stdin` | Keyboard | `<` |
| 1 | `stdout` | Terminal | `>` / `>>` |
| 2 | `stderr` | Terminal | `2>` / `2>>` |

The C runtime typically allows descriptors `0`–`1023` (configurable; `ulimit -n` controls the soft limit, the kernel hard limit being **1048576** on modern kernels).

## 2.3 File Operations — Bulleted Logic Breakdown

### A. Opening a File (conceptual flow)

1. Process calls `open("data.txt", O_RDONLY)`.
2. Kernel resolves the **pathname** by walking the dentry tree from root `/`.
3. Kernel locates the **inode** for `data.txt` via the parent directory's data blocks.
4. Kernel allocates a free slot in the process's **fd table** (lowest unused integer).
5. Kernel creates an **open file description** (file offset, status flags, access mode).
6. Kernel returns the **fd integer** to the process.

### B. Reading/Writing

- **read(fd, buf, n)** — copies *up to* `n` bytes from the file's current offset into `buf`; advances the offset.
- **write(fd, buf, n)** — copies *up to* `n` bytes from `buf` to the file at the current offset.
- Both operations are **buffered** in user space (glibc `FILE*` streams) and **page-cached** in kernel space.

### C. Closing

- **close(fd)** — releases the fd table entry, decrements the inode's reference count, and flushes pending writes to the page cache (which is later flushed to disk by `pdflush`/`writeback` threads).

## 2.4 Shell I/O Redirection — How It Works

When you type `ls > out.txt`, the shell:

1. **Forks** the child process.
2. In the child, **calls `dup2(1, new_fd)`** to make the new file's fd point to fd 1 (stdout).
3. **Closes** the original stdout (or doesn't, depending on shell).
4. **Executes** `ls`, whose output now flows to `out.txt`.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the high-yield reference table for KTU 2024 Module 3 questions. Note the use of `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` character to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| Operator | Syntax | Meaning | fd Affected |
|---|---|---|---|
| `<` | `cmd < file` | Redirect stdin from file | 0 |
| `>` | `cmd > file` | Redirect stdout (truncate) to file | 1 |
| `>>` | `cmd >> file` | Redirect stdout (append) to file | 1 |
| `2>` | `cmd 2> file` | Redirect stderr (truncate) to file | 2 |
| `2>&1` | `cmd > out 2>&1` | Send stderr to wherever stdout goes | 2 → 1 |
| `&>` | `cmd &> file` | Redirect both stdout and stderr (Bash) | 1, 2 |
| `\|` (pipe) | `cmd1 \vert cmd2` | stdout of cmd1 → stdin of cmd2 | 1 → 0 |
| `tee` | `cmd \vert tee file` | Split stdout: terminal + file | — |
| `<<` (heredoc) | `cmd << EOF ... EOF` | Provide multi-line stdin inline | 0 |
| `<<<` (herestring) | `cmd <<< "text"` | Provide single-string stdin inline | 0 |

### File Test Operators in Shell Scripts

| Test | True Condition |
|---|---|
| `[ -e file ]` | File **exists** |
| `[ -f file ]` | Is a **regular file** |
| `[ -d dir  ]` | Is a **directory** |
| `[ -r file ]` | Is **readable** by current user |
| `[ -w file ]` | Is **writable** by current user |
| `[ -x file ]` | Is **executable** by current user |
| `[ -s file ]` | Exists and has **size > 0** |
| `[ -L link ]` | Is a **symbolic link** |
| `[ f1 -nt f2 ]` | f1 is **newer than** f2 (modification time) |
| `[ f1 -ot f2 ]` | f1 is **older than** f2 |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** Embedded Linux boards (Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone, ESP32-hosted Yocto systems) use shell scripts to log sensor data to files, redirect boot-time diagnostics to a serial console (`/dev/ttyS0`), and parse configuration files (`/etc/network/interfaces`). Industrial PLCs running Linux RTOS rely on FIFO/named-pipe redirections for inter-process telemetry. Mastering file handling is therefore a **prerequisite** for automation, DevOps, and edge-AI deployment — exactly the skill profile expected from KTU 2024 graduates under NEP 2020's outcome-based pedagogy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> In scripting, "derivations" are the *trace-by-trace execution paths* the kernel and shell take to honour a file operation. Below we exhaustively derive the **mechanics** of the most frequently examined KTU 2024 patterns. Every shell command is shown with its **logic**, **process-tree evolution**, and **fd state transitions**.

## 3.1 Worked Example A — Tracing `cat < input.txt > output.txt 2> error.log`

### Step 1: Shell parses the command

The shell tokenises the line and recognises three redirections attached to `cat`:
- `< input.txt` → assign fd 0 from `input.txt`
- `> output.txt` → assign fd 1 from `output.txt` (truncate)
- `2> error.log` → assign fd 2 from `error.log` (truncate)

### Step 2: Shell forks a child

The child inherits the parent's fd table (initially `{0: keyboard, 1: terminal, 2: terminal}`).

### Step 3: Shell applies redirections in the child (BEFORE `execve`)

The shell opens the three files using `open(2)`, getting fresh descriptors (say **3, 4, 5**).

$$
\text{open}(\text{"input.txt"}, O\_RDONLY) = 3
$$

$$
\text{open}(\text{"output.txt"}, O\_WRONLY \mid O\_CREAT \mid O\_TRUNC, 0644) = 4
$$

$$
\text{open}(\text{"error.log"}, O\_WRONLY \mid O\_CREAT \mid O\_TRUNC, 0644) = 5
$$

Then the shell calls `dup2(3, 0)`, `dup2(4, 1)`, `dup2(5, 2)`, closing **3, 4, 5** afterwards. The child's fd table now becomes:

$$
\text{fd} = \begin{cases} 0 \to \text{input.txt} \\ 1 \to \text{output.txt} \\ 2 \to \text{error.log} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 4: Shell calls `execve("/bin/cat", …)`

The `cat` binary replaces the child process image but **inherits the fd table** (this is the crux of Unix redirection). When `cat` reads from fd 0 and writes to fd 1, it unknowingly speaks to the files.

### Step 5: Kernel serves I/O
