## 3.3 Example 3 — Creating Nested Directories with `-p`

**Scenario:** You want to create a full path: `project/src/utils` in one command, but `project` and `project/src` do not exist yet.

**Command:**

```bash
mkdir -p project/src/utils
```

**Explanation of the `-p` flag:**

- `-p` stands for **parents** (or **path**).
- It tells `mkdir`: *"If the parent directories in the path do not exist, create them too. If the final directory already exists, do not raise an error."*
- Without `-p`, this command would **fail** because `project/src` does not yet exist.

**Step-by-step internal execution:**

1. Check if `project/src/utils` exists → No.
2. Check if `project/src` exists → No. → Create it.
3. Check if `project` exists → No. → Create it.
4. Create `project/src/utils`.

**Verification:**

```bash
ls -R project
```

**Expected Output:**

```
project:
src

project/src:
utils
```

## 3.4 Example 4 — Setting Permissions During Creation with `-m`

**Scenario:** You want to create a `private` directory that only the owner can access (mode `700`).

**Command:**

```bash
mkdir -m 700 private
```

**Explanation:**

- `-m 700` sets the permission bits using **octal notation**.
- `7` (owner) = read (4) + write (2) + execute (1) = **rwx**.
- `0` (group) = no permissions = **---**.
- `0` (others) = no permissions = **---**.

**Verification:**

```bash
ls -ld private
```

**Expected Output:**

```
drwx------ 2 student student 4096 Jan 15 10:30 private
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use `-m` instead of `chmod`?**
> Using `-m` sets permissions at the moment of creation, avoiding a brief window where the directory exists with default (more permissive) permissions. This is a **security best practice** in production environments.

## 3.5 Example 5 — Combining `-p`, `-v`, and `-m`

**Scenario:** Create a full directory tree `app/logs/2024/jan` with permission `750` and verbose output.

**Command:**

```bash
mkdir -p -v -m 750 app/logs/2024/jan
```

**Expected Verbose Output:**

```
mkdir: created directory 'app'
mkdir: created directory 'app/logs'
mkdir: created directory 'app/logs/2024'
mkdir: created directory 'app/logs/2024/jan'
```

**Explanation:**

- `-p` → Creates parents as needed.
- `-v` → Prints a confirmation message for each directory made.
- `-m 750` → Sets mode `rwxr-x---` for every directory created.

**Note:** The `-m` mode applies to **each directory** in the chain, not just the final one.

## 3.6 Example 6 — Handling Errors (Directory Already Exists)

**Scenario:** You accidentally run `mkdir projects` when `projects` already exists.

**Command (without `-p`):**

```bash
mkdir projects
```

**Expected Output:**

```
mkdir: cannot create directory 'projects': File exists
```

**Exit Status:** `1` (non-zero, indicating failure).

**Command (with `-p`):**

```bash
mkdir -p projects
```

**Expected Output:** *(silent — no error)*

**Exit Status:** `0` (success — idempotent operation).

> [!TIP]
> **Idempotency in Shell Scripts:**
> The `-p` flag makes `mkdir` an **idempotent** command — running it multiple times produces the same result. This is critical when writing deployment scripts that may be re-executed safely.

## 3.7 Example 7 — Using Brace Expansion for Mass Directory Creation

**Scenario:** Create directories `day1` through `day7` for a week's logs.

**Command:**

```bash
mkdir day{1,2,3,4,5,6,7}
```

**Or more elegantly:**

```bash
mkdir day{1..7}
```

**Explanation:**

- Brace expansion `{1..7}` is a **bash shell feature** (not a `mkdir` feature).
- The shell expands it to: `day1 day2 day3 day4 day5 day6 day7`.
- `mkdir` then receives all seven arguments.

**Verification:**

```bash
ls
```

**Expected Output:**

```
day1  day2  day3  day4  day5  day6  day7
```

## 3.8 Analytical Summary Table

| Command | Creates | Verbose? | Custom Mode? | Idempotent? |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `mkdir dir1` | `dir1` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `mkdir dir1 dir2 dir3` | 3 siblings | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `mkdir -p a/b/c` | 3 levels | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| `mkdir -m 700 secret` | `secret` (700) | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `mkdir -pv -m 755 a/b/c` | 3 levels (755) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `mkdir day{1..7}` | 7 dirs (shell expansion) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Decision Logic of `mkdir` Execution

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates how the `mkdir` command processes its arguments and handles various edge cases during execution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User invokes mkdir with arguments"] --> B{"Are arguments valid?"}
    B -- "No" --> C["Print usage error and exit code 1"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D["Parse each directory name"]
    D --> E{"For each directory path:"}
    E --> F{"Does directory already exist?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G{"Is -p flag set?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Silent success, exit code 0"]
    G -- "No" --> I["Print error: File exists, exit code 1"]
    F -- "No" --> J{"Do all parent directories exist?"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Create the directory with default or -m permissions"]
    J -- "No" --> L{"Is -p flag set?"}
    L -- "Yes" --> M["Recursively create missing parent directories first"]
    L -- "No" --> N["Print error: No such file or directory, exit code 1"]
    M --> K
    K --> O{"Is -v flag set?"}
    O -- "Yes" --> P["Print confirmation message"]
    O -- "No" --> Q["Continue silently"]
    P --> R["Move to next directory argument"]
    Q --> R
    R --> E
    H --> S["All directories processed, exit code 0"]
    I --> S
    N --> S
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of `mkdir`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER["User Space"]
        U1["Shell Terminal"]
    end

    subgraph COREUTIL["GNU Coreutils Layer"]
        M1["mkdir Binary"]
        M2["Argument Parser"]
        M3["Permission Handler"]
        M4["Recursive Handler -p"]
        M5["Verbose Output -v"]
    end

    subgraph KERNEL["Linux Kernel Layer"]
        K1["VFS Virtual File System"]
        K2["Inode Allocator"]
        K3["Permission Checker"]
    end

    subgraph FS["Physical Filesystem Layer"]
        F1["ext4 / xfs / btrfs Driver"]
        F2["Disk Block Allocation"]
    end

    U1 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M2 --> M4
    M2 --> M5
    M3 --> K1
    M4 --> K1
    K1 --> K2
    K1 --> K3
    K2 --> F1
    K3 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
```

## 4.3 Tree Diagram — Conceptual View of `-p` Recursion

The diagram below shows how `mkdir -p` builds a directory tree step-by-step when none of the intermediate directories exist.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Requested path = app/src/utils"] --> S2["Step 2: Check app - not found, create it"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Check app/src - not found, create it"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Check app/src/utils - not found, create it"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Final tree structure created"]
    S5 --> N1["app"]
    N1 --> N2["src"]
    N2 --> N3["utils"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The `-p` flag is essentially a depth-first traversal of the requested path, creating each missing node from the topmost parent downward to the target leaf.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: What is the purpose of the `mkdir -p` command? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `mkdir -p` command is used to create a directory along with its **parent directories** if they do not already exist. The `-p` flag stands for "parents". It also **suppresses errors** if the target directory already exists, making the command **idempotent**.

**Example:**

```bash
mkdir -p project/src/utils
```

If neither `project`, `project/src`, nor `project/src/utils` exist, all three will be created in sequence. Without `-p`, the command would fail because `project/src` does not exist.

**Mark Distribution:**

- Definition of `-p` flag → **1 mark**
- Parent creation explanation → **1 mark**
- Example with explanation → **1 mark**

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: Differentiate between `mkdir dir1` and `mkdir -m 700 dir1`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `mkdir dir1` | `mkdir -m 700 dir1` |
|---|---|---|
| **Permission Mode** | Uses system `umask` (default 0775) | Explicitly sets mode to `700` (rwx------) |
| **Security** | Less secure; default permissions may allow group/others access | More secure; only owner has full access |
| **Use Case** | Normal, shared directories | Private/sensitive directories |

**Key Distinction:** The `-m` flag allows setting **explicit octal permissions** at the moment of creation, bypassing the umask, which is essential for security-sensitive directories.

**Mark Distribution:**

- Command 1 explanation → **1 mark**
- Command 2 explanation → **1 mark**
- Key distinction → **1 mark**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 3 questions typically appear as a single 14-mark question with internal choice. The question has two sub-parts: (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**.

---

### Question A — Choice 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`**

**Q (a): Explain the `mkdir` command in Linux with its syntax. List and explain any four commonly used options with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Definition & Syntax (2 marks):**

The `mkdir` (make directory) command is used to create new directories in the Linux filesystem. It is part of the GNU Coreutils package.

**Syntax:**

```bash
mkdir [OPTION]... DIRECTORY...
```

**2. Commonly Used Options (5 marks — 1.25 marks each):**

**Option 1: `-p` (parents)** — Creates parent directories as needed and suppresses errors if the directory already exists.

```bash
mkdir -p project/src/main
```

**Option 2: `-v` (verbose)** — Displays a message for each directory created.

```bash
mkdir -v test
# Output: mkdir: created directory 'test'
```

**Option 3: `-m` (mode)** — Sets the file permission bits at creation time using octal notation.

```bash
mkdir -m 750 shared
```

**Option 4: Multiple directories** — Creates several directories in a single command.

```bash
mkdir src bin docs
```

---

**Q (b): Write a shell script that creates a project directory tree `myapp/config/db`, `myapp/logs`, and `myapp/src/core` with permission `755`, and displays each directory created. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Shell Script:**

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Script to create a project directory tree
# Filename: create_project.sh

echo "Creating project directory tree..."

# Create the complete tree with -p, verbose output, and 755 permissions
mkdir -p -v -m 755 myapp/config/db
mkdir -p -v -m 755 myapp/logs
mkdir -p -v -m 755 myapp/src/core

echo "Project structure created successfully."

# Verification step
echo "Verifying structure:"
find myapp -type d -exec ls -ld {} \;
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**

- `#!/bin/bash` — Shebang line declaring the script interpreter (**0.5 marks**).
- `mkdir -p` — Creates parent directories as needed (**1 mark**).
- `-v` — Enables verbose mode to show each creation (**1 mark**).
- `-m 755` — Sets read-write-execute for owner, read-execute for group and others (**1 mark**).
- `find myapp -type d` — Lists all created directories for verification (**1.5 marks**).
- Proper script structure with comments (**1 mark**).
- Correct output shown when executed (**1 mark**).

**Expected Output:**

```
Creating project directory tree...
mkdir: created directory 'myapp'
mkdir: created directory 'myapp/config'
mkdir: created directory 'myapp/config/db'
mkdir: created directory 'myapp/logs'
mkdir: created directory 'myapp/src'
mkdir: created directory 'myapp/src/core'
Project structure created successfully.
```

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Supplementary]`**

**Q (a): Explain the concept of default permissions in Linux. How does the `umask` value affect directory creation? Show the calculation for a umask of `0027`. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Default Permission Concept (2 marks):**

In Linux, every new file or directory is created with a set of default permissions. For **directories**, the maximum base permission is `0777` (rwxrwxrwx). However, the actual permission granted to the user is computed by subtracting the **umask** value from this base.

**2. Umask Formula (2 marks):**

$$\text{Actual Permission} = \text{Base Permission} \ \text{AND} \ \text{NOT(umask)}$$

**3. Calculation for umask `0027` (3 marks):**

Given:
- Base permission for directories = `0777`
- Umask = `0027`

**Step 1:** Convert umask to binary: `0027` → `000 010 111`

**Step 2:** Apply NOT operation: `NOT(0027)` → `750` (in octal)

**Step 3:** Perform bitwise AND:

```
  0777  =  111 111 111
  0750  =  111 101 000
  -------------------
  AND   =  111 101 000  =  0750
```

**Result:** New directories will be created with permission `0750` (rwxr-x---).

**Interpretation:** Owner has full access, group has read+execute, others have no access.

---

**Q (b): Demonstrate with a single `mkdir` command how to create a complete nested structure for a Java project with directories `MyApp/src/com/example/model`, `MyApp/src/com/example/view`, `MyApp/src/com/example/controller`, `MyApp/lib`, and `MyApp/docs`. All directories should have permission `775` and verbose output enabled. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Command:**

```bash
mkdir -p -v -m 775 MyApp/src/com/example/{model,view,controller} MyApp/lib MyApp/docs
```

**Step-by-Step Breakdown:**

- `-p` → Creates all missing parent directories (`MyApp`, `MyApp/src`, `MyApp/src/com`, `MyApp/src/com/example`) automatically (**2 marks**).
- `-v` → Prints a confirmation message for each directory created (**1 mark**).
- `-m 775` → Sets permissions to `rwxrwxr-x` (owner + group: full, others: read+execute) (**2 marks**).
- `MyApp/src/com/example/{model,view,controller}` → Brace expansion creates three sibling directories under `example` (**1 mark**).
- `MyApp/lib` and `MyApp/docs` → Additional sibling directories created in the same command (**1 mark**).

**Expected Output:**

```
mkdir: created directory 'MyApp'
mkdir: created directory 'MyApp/src'
mkdir: created directory 'MyApp/src/com'
mkdir: created directory 'MyApp/src/com/example'
mkdir: created directory 'MyApp/src/com/example/model'
mkdir: created directory 'MyApp/src/com/example/view'
mkdir: created directory 'MyApp/src/com/example/controller'
mkdir: created directory 'MyApp/lib'
mkdir: created directory 'MyApp/docs'
```

**Verification Command:**

```bash
find MyApp -type d -exec ls -ld {} \;
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `-p` flag** when creating nested paths — results in `No such file or directory` error. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing `-m` with `-p`** — students write `mkdir -p 700 dir` thinking it sets permissions. Always remember: **-m = mode, -p = parents**.
> 3. **Incorrect umask calculation** — using subtraction instead of bitwise AND with NOT. The formula is `Base AND NOT(umask)`, not `Base - umask`.
> 4. **Not including shebang line** `#!/bin/bash` in shell script answers — **Loss: 0.5–1 mark.**
> 5. **Forgetting to verify** the created directories with `ls` or `find` in script-based questions.
> 6. **Using Python syntax in bash scripts** — KTU strictly evaluates Linux/bash answers using shell syntax, not programming language logic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`mkdir` = make directory** — the primary command for creating directories in Linux.
- **Syntax:** `mkdir [OPTION]... DIRECTORY...`
- **`-p` (parents):** Creates intermediate parent directories and is **idempotent** (no error if exists).
- **`-v` (verbose):** Prints a message for each directory created.
- **`-m MODE`:** Sets **octal permission bits** at creation time, overriding umask.
- **Multiple directories** can be created in one command by space-separating names.
- **Brace expansion** `{a,b,c}` and range expansion `{1..5}` are **bash shell features**, not `mkdir` features.
- **Default permission** for new directories = `0777 AND NOT(umask)`.
- **Newly created directories** automatically contain `.` (self) and `..` (parent) entries.
- **Exit status:** `0` on success, non-zero on failure — critical for shell script error handling.
- **Maximum path length** in Linux is typically **4096 bytes**; filename length is **255 bytes**.
- **`mkdir -p` is idempotent** — safe to run multiple times in deployment scripts.
- **Common KTU exam focus:** syntax, options (`-p`, `-m`, `-v`), umask calculation, and combined scripting scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deleting Directories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deleting Directories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
In the Linux operating system, **deleting a directory** refers to the act of removing a directory entry (inode reference) from the parent directory's file system hierarchy, thereby disassociating the data blocks of that directory from the active file system tree. Linux provides two primary command-line utilities for this operation:

- **`rmdir`** (Remove Directory) — The POSIX-standard utility restricted to removing **only empty directories**.
- **`rm`** (Remove) — The general-purpose file deletion utility that, when used with the recursive flag (`-r` or `-R`), is capable of deleting directories along with their entire contents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the difference between `rmdir` and `rm -r`, including failure conditions, exit statuses, and the implications of the `-f` (force) flag. Mastery of both commands is mandatory.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of your **home computer's folder structure** like a **physical filing cabinet**:
- An **empty drawer** is like an empty directory. You can pull it out and throw it away easily with a simple tug — that's `rmdir`.
- A **drawer full of files and sub-drawers** is like a non-empty directory. To throw it away, you must first empty the contents (or throw everything in the bin together) — that's `rm -r`.
- If the drawer is **locked, stubborn, or has "do not touch" labels**, you need a stronger command (`rm -rf`) that forces the action without asking for confirmation.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- The standard exit status code for a successful directory removal is **0**.
- The standard exit status code for a failed removal is **non-zero (typically 1 or 2)**.
- The recursive depth limit is bounded only by the file system's maximum path length, conventionally **4096 bytes (PATH_MAX)** on modern Linux ext4 file systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Inode:** An inode (index node) is a data structure on a Unix-like file system that stores information about a file or directory, such as its metadata and disk block locations. Deleting a directory merely unlinks its inode; data blocks are reclaimed only when no other hard links reference them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `rmdir` Command (Restricted to Empty Directories)

The `rmdir` utility is part of the **POSIX.1-2017** standard and is the safest method for directory removal because it is **inherently non-destructive** — it refuses to operate on a non-empty directory.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Parse Arguments:** The shell parses the command line and the kernel's `execve` system call loads the `rmdir` binary.
2. **Path Resolution:** For each supplied path, the kernel resolves the directory using the `stat()` system call to verify it is indeed a directory.
3. **Empty Check:** The directory is scanned using `opendir()` and `readdir()`. If any entry other than `.` (current) and `..` (parent) is found, the operation **fails immediately** with the error: `Directory not empty`.
4. **Unlink Operation:** If empty, the `unlink()` system call is invoked to remove the directory entry from its parent.
5. **Exit Status:** Returns **0** on success, **non-zero** on failure.

**Key Flags for `rmdir`:**

| Flag | Long Form | Purpose |
|------|-----------|---------|
| `-p` | `--parents` | Remove the directory and its empty parent directories recursively |
| `-v` | `--verbose` | Display a diagnostic message for every directory processed |
| `--ignore-fail-on-non-empty` | — | Suppress error when directory is non-empty |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `-p` flag is highly examinable. Example: `rmdir -p /a/b/c` will attempt to remove `c`, then `b`, then `a`, stopping at the first non-empty parent.

### 2.2 The `rm` Command (General Purpose)

The `rm` command is a more aggressive utility. When applied to directories without flags, modern Linux distributions often print a warning or refuse the operation. The recursive flag is **mandatory** for directory removal.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Argument Parsing:** Options like `-r`, `-f`, `-i`, `-v` are interpreted by GNU coreutils.
2. **Recursive Traversal:** With `-r` (or `-R`), the command performs a **depth-first post-order traversal** of the directory tree.
3. **Leaf-First Deletion:** Files and empty subdirectories are deleted first (post-order), then their parents.
4. **Confirmation Prompts:** Without `-f`, the user is prompted for confirmation on write-protected files.
5. **System Calls Used:** `opendir()`, `readdir()`, `unlink()`, `rmdir()`, `stat()`.

**Key Flags for `rm`:**

| Flag | Long Form | Purpose |
|------|-----------|---------|
| `-r` / `-R` | `--recursive` | Recursively remove directories and their contents |
| `-f` | `--force` | Suppress prompts; ignore non-existent files; never prompt |
| `-i` | `--interactive` | Prompt before every removal |
| `-I` | `--interactive=once` | Prompt once before removing more than 3 files or recursively |
| `-v` | `--verbose` | Explain what is being done |
| `-d` | `--dir` | Remove empty directories (equivalent to `rmdir`) |
| `--no-preserve-root` | — | Allow recursive removal of `/` (DANGEROUS) |

### 2.3 The `rm -rf` Combination (Nuclear Option)

The combination `rm -rf` is the most powerful and **most dangerous** directory deletion command in Linux. It performs:
- **Recursive** descent through the entire directory tree.
- **Force** suppression of all safety prompts.

> [!WARNING]
> `rm -rf /` will attempt to delete the entire root file system. Always double-check the path, especially when using shell variables or wildcards. KTU examinations often test awareness of this footgun.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Command | Scope | Empty Only? | Recursive? | Interactive? | Exit 0 on Success? |
|---------|-------|-------------|------------|--------------|--------------------|
| `rmdir <dir>` | Directory | **Yes** | No (use `-p`) | No | Yes |
| `rmdir -p <path>` | Path | Yes (each level) | Yes (upward) | No | Yes |
| `rm -d <dir>` | Directory | Yes | No | Optional `-i` | Yes |
| `rm -r <dir>` | Directory + contents | No | **Yes** | Optional `-i` | Yes |
| `rm -rf <dir>` | Directory + contents | No | **Yes** | **No (forced)** | Yes |
| `rm -ri <dir>` | Directory + contents | No | Yes | **Yes (prompts)** | Yes |
| `rm -rvf <dir>` | Directory + contents | No | Yes | No | Yes (verbose) |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **DevOps & CI/CD Pipelines:** Build artifacts and temporary caches (`rm -rf node_modules`, `rm -rf ~/.cache`) are routinely purged.
- **Embedded Linux Systems:** Storage-constrained devices must aggressively delete log directories (`/var/log/old`) using scheduled `rm -rf` jobs.
- **Data Science Workstations:** Dataset staging directories are cleaned with `rm -rf` between experiments to reclaim disk space.
- **System Administration:** Cleaning `/tmp` directories on multi-user servers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: `rmdir` vs `rm -r` Decision Flow

The following logical derivation shows how the Linux shell processes a deletion request:

**Step 1 — Command Invocation**

The user types a command in the bash shell. The shell's tokenizer splits the input into tokens by whitespace.

$$
\text{Input: } \texttt{rmdir\ \ myFolder} \longrightarrow \text{Tokens: } [\texttt{rmdir}, \texttt{myFolder}]
$$

**Step 2 — System Call Resolution**

The shell invokes the `execve("rmdir", argv, envp)` system call, which loads the binary from `/usr/bin/rmdir` into memory.

**Step 3 — Directory Status Check**

The `rmdir` binary uses the `stat()` system call to retrieve the inode metadata:

$$
\texttt{struct\ stat\ st} = \texttt{stat(\textquotedbl myFolder\textquotedbl)}
$$

The `S_ISDIR(st.st_mode)` macro is evaluated. If `false`, the program prints `Not a directory` and exits with status **1**.

**Step 4 — Emptiness Verification**

`opendir("myFolder")` returns a directory stream handle. A `while(readdir(dp) != NULL)` loop counts entries, skipping `.` and `..`:

$$
\text{Count} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \text{entry}_i \neq \text{`.`} \right) \land \left( \text{entry}_i \neq \text{`..`} \right)
$$

If Count $\geq 1$, the operation aborts with `Directory not empty` and exits with status **1**.

**Step 5 — Unlink Operation**

If Count $= 0$, the system call `unlinkat(AT_FDCWD, "myFolder", AT_REMOVEDIR)` is executed, which removes the directory entry from the parent and decrements the link count. The kernel reclaims the inode and its data blocks.

**Step 6 — Exit**

The program returns **0** to the parent shell process.

### 3.2 Comparative Execution Matrix

| Condition | `rmdir myFolder` | `rm -r myFolder` | `rm -rf myFolder` |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Folder is empty | ✅ Succeeds | ✅ Succeeds | ✅ Succeeds |
| Folder contains files | ❌ Fails (exit 1) | ✅ Succeeds | ✅ Succeeds |
| Folder contains subdirs | ❌ Fails (exit 1) | ✅ Succeeds (recurses) | ✅ Succeeds (recurses) |
| Folder is write-protected | ❌ Fails (exit 1) | ⚠️ Prompts user | ✅ Succeeds silently |
| Path does not exist | ❌ Fails (exit 1) | ⚠️ Prompts user | ❌ Silently ignores (no error) |
| User lacks write permission on parent | ❌ Fails (exit 1) | ❌ Fails (exit 1) | ❌ Fails (exit 1) |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Multi-Level Deletion

Consider a directory tree:

$$
\texttt{/project/} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \texttt{src/} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \texttt{main.c} \\ \texttt{utils.c} \end{cases} \\ \texttt{build/} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \texttt{app.o} \\ \texttt{app} \end{cases} \end{cases}
$$

**Execution Trace for `rm -rf /project`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} & \quad \text{Open } \texttt{/project} \\
\text{Step 2:} & \quad \text{Recurse into } \texttt{src} \\
\text{Step 3:} & \quad \text{Delete } \texttt{src/main.c} \\
\text{Step 4:} & \quad \text{Delete } \texttt{src/utils.c} \\
\text{Step 5:} & \quad \text{Rmdir } \texttt{src} \\
\text{Step 6:} & \quad \text{Recurse into } \texttt{build} \\
\text{Step 7:} & \quad \text{Delete } \texttt{build/app.o} \\
\text{Step 8:} & \quad \text{Delete } \texttt{build/app} \\
\text{Step 9:} & \quad \text{Rmdir } \texttt{build} \\
\text{Step 10:} & \quad \text{Rmdir } \texttt{/project}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **post-order traversal** logic: children are removed before parents. This is the only correct order because Linux cannot remove a non-empty directory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Directory Deletion Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[User issues deletion command] --> parseA{Command parsed}
    parseA -->|rmdir| checkEmptyA{Directory empty?}
    parseA -->|rm -r| recurseA[Begin recursive traversal]
    parseA -->|rm -rf| recurseA

    checkEmptyA -->|Yes| unlinkA[Invoke unlinkat AT_REMOVEDIR]
    checkEmptyA -->|No| failA[Error: Directory not empty]
    unlinkA --> doneA[Exit status 0]

    recurseA --> openDirA[Opendir target]
    openDirA --> readEntryA{Read next entry}
    readEntryA -->|File| deleteFileA[Unlink file]
    readEntryA -->|Subdir| recurseA
    readEntryA -->|Null end| rmdirA[Rmdir current dir]
    deleteFileA --> readEntryA
    rmdirA --> doneA

    failA --> exitFailA[Exit status 1]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: System Call Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[User Shell] --> bashA[bash execve]
    bashA --> rmdirBinA[rmdir binary]
    bashA --> rmBinA[rm binary with -r flag]

    rmdirBinA --> statCallA[stat system call]
    rmdirBinA --> opendirCallA[opendir system call]
    rmdirBinA --> unlinkatCallA[unlinkat AT_REMOVEDIR]

    rmBinA --> opendirCallB[opendir system call]
    rmBinA --> readdirCallB[readdir system call]
    rmBinA --> unlinkCallB[unlink system call]
    rmBinA --> rmdirCallB[rmdir system call]

    statCallA --> kernelA[Linux Kernel VFS Layer]
    opendirCallA --> kernelA
    unlinkatCallA --> kernelA
    opendirCallB --> kernelA
    readdirCallB --> kernelA
    unlinkCallB --> kernelA
    rmdirCallB --> kernelA

    kernelA --> diskA[Disk inode and data blocks]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: rmdir vs rm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SafetyTier[Safety Tier - rmdir]
        s1A[Input validation] --> s2A[Stat check]
        s2A --> s3A{Empty directory?}
        s3A -->|Yes| s4A[Unlink]
        s3A -->|No| s5A[Refuse and exit 1]
    end

    subgraph PowerTier[Power Tier - rm -r]
        p1A[Input validation] --> p2A[Recursive descent]
        p2A --> p3A[Depth-first post-order]
        p3A --> p4A[Unlink files and rmdir subdirs]
        p4A --> p5A[Finally rmdir top-level]
    end

    subgraph ForceTier[Force Tier - rm -rf]
        f1A[Input validation] --> f2A[Recursive descent]
        f2A --> f3A[Suppress all prompts]
        f3A --> f4A[Ignore missing files]
        f4A --> f5A[Silent destructive purge]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
*What is the difference between `rmdir` and `rm -r` commands in Linux? When would you prefer one over the other?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- `rmdir` removes only **empty directories** and refuses to delete a directory that contains any files or subdirectories. It is the safer option (1 mark).
- `rm -r` (recursive remove) deletes a directory **along with all its contents**, including files and nested subdirectories, traversing the tree in post-order (1 mark).
- `rmdir` is preferred in shell scripts and automation pipelines where accidental data loss must be avoided; `rm -r` is preferred when full cleanup of a project tree is intended (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Explain the purpose of the `-p` flag in the `rmdir` command with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The `-p` (or `--parents`) flag in `rmdir` removes the specified directory **and then attempts to remove its parent directories recursively**, working upward through the path (1 mark).
- Each parent is removed only if it becomes empty after the previous deletion (1 mark).
- **Example:** `rmdir -p /home/student/lab/test` will first remove `test`, then attempt to remove `lab` (if empty), then `student` (if empty), and finally `home` (if empty). If any intermediate directory is non-empty, the command stops at that point (1 mark).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `rmdir` Deep Dive

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the operational steps executed by the `rmdir` command when removing an empty directory. Discuss the system calls invoked and the exit status conventions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `rmdir` command follows a strict sequence of operations to ensure safe directory removal:

1. **Argument Parsing (1 mark):** The shell invokes the `rmdir` binary via `execve()`. The command line options such as `-p` and `-v` are parsed using GNU `getopt_long()`.

2. **Path Validation (1 mark):** For each supplied path, the `lstat()` or `stat()` system call is invoked to verify that:
   - The path exists.
   - The path refers to a directory (checked via the `S_ISDIR()` macro on the `st_mode` field).
   - The user has write and execute permissions on the **parent** directory (required to remove an entry).

3. **Emptiness Check (2 marks):** The `opendir()` system call opens the target directory. A loop using `readdir()` enumerates all entries. The pseudo-entries `.` and `..` are skipped. If **any** other entry is found, the command prints `rmdir: failed to remove 'dir': Directory not empty` to `stderr` and exits with status **1**.

4. **Unlink Operation (2 marks):** If the directory is empty, the system call `unlinkat(AT_FDCWD, path, AT_REMOVEDIR)` is invoked. This atomically removes the directory entry from its parent and deallocates the inode. The kernel decrements the link count; when it reaches zero, the data blocks are freed for reuse.

5. **Recursive Parent Removal (1 mark):** If the `-p` flag is set, the algorithm repeats upward: each parent directory is subjected to the same stat-and-empty-check sequence until a non-empty parent is encountered or the path is exhausted.

**Exit Status Convention:** A return value of **0** indicates success; **1** indicates a minor failure (e.g., directory non-empty); **2** indicates a serious error (e.g., invalid path).

---

**(b)** Write a shell script that uses `rmdir -p` to remove a deeply nested project directory `/tmp/work/lab1/taskA` only if every level is empty. The script should print a success or failure message based on the exit status. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Script: clean_project.sh
# Purpose: Safely remove nested empty directory tree using rmdir -p

TARGET_DIR="/tmp/work/lab1/taskA"

# Check if the target exists
if [ ! -d "$TARGET_DIR" ]; then
    echo "Error: Target directory $TARGET_DIR does not exist."
    exit 1
fi

# Attempt recursive empty-directory removal
rmdir -p -v "$TARGET_DIR"

# Capture the exit status of rmdir
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "SUCCESS: Directory tree $TARGET_DIR removed successfully."
    exit 0
else
    echo "FAILURE: Could not remove $TARGET_DIR. It may not be empty."
    exit 1
fi
```

**Explanation of Key Lines (Mark Allocation):**

- **Shebang and Comments (1 mark):** `#!/bin/bash` declares the interpreter; comments document purpose.
- **Existence Check (1 mark):** The `[ ! -d "$TARGET_DIR" ]` test guards against running on a missing path.
- **rmdir Invocation (2 marks):** `rmdir -p -v "$TARGET_DIR"` uses double quotes to handle spaces; `-p` for parent recursion; `-v` for verbose feedback.
- **Exit Status Handling (2 marks):** `$?` captures the previous command's exit code; the `if` branch differentiates success (0) from failure (non-zero).
- **User Feedback (1 mark):** Distinct success and failure messages aid the operator.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `rm -r` and `rm -rf` Operations

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast `rm -r`, `rm -rf`, and `rm -ri` with appropriate examples. What are the safety implications of each? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | `rm -r` | `rm -rf` | `rm -ri` |
|--------|---------|----------|----------|
| Recursive deletion | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Prompts user | Yes (for write-protected files only) | **No prompts at all** | **Prompts before every single removal** |
| Ignores missing files | No (errors out) | **Yes** | No |
| Use case | Interactive cleanup with minimal friction | **Scripts, automation, CI/CD** | Cautious manual deletion |
| Safety | Moderate (low risk) | **Very low safety margin** | **Highest safety** |

**Examples (3 marks):**

- `rm -r old_project` — Recursively deletes `old_project` and prompts only for write-protected files.
- `rm -rf /var/cache/apt/archives/*` — Silently purges the apt cache directory; commonly used in shell scripts.
- `rm -ri Documents/` — Walks through `Documents/` and asks `remove file 'report.pdf'?` before each deletion, ideal when the user is unsure.

**Safety Implications (2 marks):**
- `rm -rf` is the most dangerous: a single typo (`rm -rf /` or `rm -rf ~`) can wipe the entire system or the user's home directory.
- `rm -ri` provides the highest safety through per-file confirmation.
- `rm -r` strikes a balance but can still destroy large amounts of data if the path is wrong.

---

**(b)** A junior administrator accidentally typed `rm -rf /*` in the root directory. Explain step-by-step what happens at the system level, why this command is partially protected against on modern Linux, and what recovery options exist. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Shell Expansion (1 mark):** The shell expands the glob `/*` to a list of all top-level entries (`/bin`, `/etc`, `/usr`, `/var`, `/lib`, ...). The `rm` command receives this expanded list as arguments.

2. **Recursive Deletion Begins (1 mark):** `rm` enters each top-level directory and begins a **post-order depth-first traversal**, deleting files and subdirectories recursively.

3. **Mount Point Protection (2 marks):** Modern Linux distributions include a kernel-level safeguard in `coreutils`. The `rm` binary checks if the target is the root directory `/`; if `--preserve-root` is the default (it is on virtually all modern distros), the command prints:
   ```
   rm: it is dangerous to operate recursively on '/'
   rm: use --no-preserve-root to override this failsafe
   ```
   and aborts. However, `rm -rf /*` expands to `rm -rf /bin /etc /usr ...` — each path is **not** literally `/`, so the safeguard does **not** trigger.

4. **System Destabilization (1 mark):** As critical binaries (`/bin/ls`, `/bin/bash`, `/usr/lib/...`) are deleted, the system rapidly becomes unusable. Running processes continue from memory, but new commands cannot be invoked.

5. **Recovery Options (2 marks):**
   - **Do NOT reboot.** Restarting will cause kernel panic since `/sbin/init` is gone.
   - Use a **live USB/CD** to boot into a rescue environment.
   - **Mount** the affected file system and copy data to an external drive.
   - Reinstall the OS if backups are unavailable; selectively restore `/etc` configuration from backups.
   - **Lesson:** Always use `rm -ri` for unfamiliar paths, and never run `rm -rf` as root without `--preserve-root` verification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to mention exit status codes (0 vs 1 vs 2)** when describing `rmdir` — this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> - Confusing the **post-order traversal order** with pre-order is a common error; remember children are deleted **before** parents.
> - Writing `rm -rf /` (without the glob) in an answer script is technically safer, but examiners expect awareness of the `/*` wildcard expansion trick.
> - Forgetting to **quote variables** (e.g., `rm -rf $DIR` instead of `rm -rf "$DIR"`) in scripts costs the safety mark for handling spaces in pathnames.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`rmdir` is for empty directories only; it is the safest deletion tool.** ✅
- **`rm -r` recursively deletes a directory and all its contents using post-order traversal.** ✅
- **`rm -rf` is the most dangerous — it suppresses all prompts and ignores missing files.** ⚠️
- **The `-p` flag of `rmdir` removes empty parents upward through the path.** ✅
- **Exit status 0 means success; non-zero (1 or 2) indicates failure.** ✅
- **The `/*` wildcard bypasses the `--preserve-root` safeguard in `rm -rf`.** ⚠️
- **System calls involved: `stat()`, `opendir()`, `readdir()`, `unlink()`, `unlinkat(AT_REMOVEDIR)`.** ✅
- **Always quote variables in shell scripts: `rm -rf "$DIR"` not `rm -rf $DIR`.** ✅
- **Use `rm -ri` for cautious interactive deletion in unfamiliar directories.** ✅
- **Never run `rm -rf` as root without absolute certainty about the path.** ⚠️

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linux Security: Removing a User
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linux Security: Removing a User

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Linux system administration and security management, **removing a user** refers to the administrative operation of permanently deleting a user account and its associated system resources from the host. This is primarily executed using the `userdel` (user delete) command, which manipulates the system authentication databases — namely `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, `/etc/group`, and `/etc/gshadow` — to revoke the user's identity, authentication credentials, group memberships, and optionally, the user's home directory and mail spool.

From a **security perspective**, user removal is a critical identity lifecycle operation that must be performed when an employee leaves an organization, when a temporary account has expired, or when a compromised account must be eradicated. Improper user removal is a leading cause of **security vulnerabilities**, including *orphaned files*, *lingering privileges*, and *undocumented access pathways* known to attackers as *ghost accounts*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** This topic falls under the **Linux Security** submodule. The expected cognitive level is **Apply / Analyze** — students must be able to identify the correct command, its options, the affected files, and the security implications of each option.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large apartment complex** (your Linux server) where every resident (user) has:
- A **name tag** at the front desk (`/etc/passwd`)
- A **key to their apartment** (`/etc/shadow` — the encrypted password)
- A **mailbox** in the lobby (mail spool at `/var/mail/username` or `/var/spool/mail/username`)
- A **personal apartment** with belongings (home directory at `/home/username`)

When you "remove a user," it is like **evicting a resident**. A careless eviction might:
- Take away the name tag and key (basic `userdel`) — but leave the apartment door wide open, with all the belongings still inside, and a mailbox still receiving mail.
- A proper eviction with `userdel -r` will: take away the name tag, retrieve the key, **AND bulldoze the apartment, destroy the mailbox, and ensure no mail can ever reach them again**.

The **security lesson**: any belongings left behind become *orphaned files* with no owner — they may contain sensitive data, scripts, or backdoors that survive the user's deletion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Deleting a user is *not* a single-step action. It is a multi-file system transaction that touches at least 4 system databases, and its security correctness depends on whether you choose to remove the home directory and mail spool.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Standard Value / Path |
|---|---|
| Primary command | `userdel` |
| Privilege required | **root** (UID 0) |
| Primary config affected | `/etc/passwd` |
| Password DB affected | `/etc/shadow` |
| Group DB affected | `/etc/group` |
| Default home base | `/home/` |
| Default mail spool base | `/var/spool/mail/` |
| Return code on success | **0** |
| Return code on failure | non-zero (typically **1** or **6**) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Security Pitfall:** Running `userdel` without `root` privileges will fail silently in scripts. Always verify the effective UID using `id -u` before scripted user removal operations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `userdel` Command — Operational Breakdown

The `userdel` command has the following canonical syntax:

```
userdel [options] LOGIN
```

Where `LOGIN` is the username of the account to be removed.

#### Available Options (High-Yield for KTU)

| Option | Long Form | Function | Security Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| `-r` | `--remove` | Removes the home directory, mail spool, and all contents recursively. | **Strongly recommended** for full account removal. Without it, files persist with old UID assigned to no one. |
| `-f` | `--force` | Forces removal even if the user is currently **logged in** or has **running processes**. | **Dangerous in production.** May leave zombie processes holding files open. |
| `-Z` | `--selinux-user` | Removes any SELinux user mapping for the user. | Required on RHEL/CentOS systems using SELinux. |
| `-h` | `--help` | Displays help. | None. |
| `-R` | `--root CHROOT_DIR` | Applies changes in the chroot directory. | Used in recovery/sandboxed environments. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default Behavior:** By default, `userdel` removes the user from `/etc/passwd` and `/etc/shadow` and from supplementary groups in `/etc/group`. It does **NOT** remove the home directory unless `-r` is supplied. This is the single most common source of exam questions in KTU.

### 2.2 Files Modified by `userdel`

The operation is a **multi-file transactional update**. A successful `userdel` modifies:

1. **`/etc/passwd`** — The user's entry line is removed.
2. **`/etc/shadow`** — The user's encrypted password line is removed.
3. **`/etc/group`** — The username is removed from any supplementary group lines (e.g., `sudo`, `developers`).
4. **`/etc/gshadow`** — Group administrator and member references are updated.
5. **`/etc/subgid`** and **`/etc/subuid`** — Removed on modern distributions supporting user namespaces.
6. **Mail spool** (`/var/spool/mail/USERNAME` or `/var/mail/USERNAME`) — Only with `-r`.
7. **Home directory** (`/home/USERNAME`) — Only with `-r`.
8. **Crontabs** under `/var/spool/cron/crontabs/` — Only with `-r`.
9. **Print jobs** under `/var/spool/cups/` — Only with `-r`.
10. **At jobs** under `/var/spool/at/` — Only with `-r`.

### 2.3 Security Concerns and Orphaned Files

When `userdel` is executed **without** `-r`:
- The home directory `/home/olduser` still exists on disk.
- All files inside retain their original UID (e.g., UID 1005).
- After the user is removed, UID 1005 becomes available to a *new* user.
- If a new user is created and inherits UID 1005, they **automatically own** all of olduser's files — including `.bash_history`, SSH keys, scripts, and any sensitive data.

> [!WARNING]
> **This is the #1 security misconfiguration in user account lifecycle management.** Always use `userdel -r` in production systems, OR follow up with a manual `find / -uid <OLD_UID> -delete` audit.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield "Formula Sheet" — Decision Matrix

Since Linux user removal has no mathematical formulas, here is the **decision-matrix** that KTU expects students to internalize:

| Scenario | Recommended Command | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Standard employee offboarding | `sudo userdel -r username` | Full cleanup; no leftover data. |
| User has running processes | `sudo pkill -u username` then `sudo userdel -r username` | Cleanly terminates processes first. |
| User is currently logged in (cannot kill) | `sudo userdel -f -r username` (with caution) | Forces removal despite active session. |
| Account exists but user never logged in | `sudo userdel username` | No home directory to remove. |
| SELinux-enabled system (RHEL) | `sudo userdel -r -Z username` | Also clears SELinux mapping. |
| Bulk removal from a list | `for u in $(cat users.txt); do sudo userdel -r $u; done` | Scripted batch operation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In **DevOps and Cloud Administration**, user removal is automated through **Ansible**, **Puppet**, or **Terraform** modules that wrap `userdel` with idempotency checks. In **Incident Response**, the `userdel -f` option is sometimes used to instantly revoke access for a suspected compromised account.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **complete, auditable, step-by-step process** of securely removing a user from a Linux system, exactly as a KTU examiner would expect a student to articulate in a 14-mark question.

### 3.1 Pre-Removal Verification (Mandatory Security Step)

Before issuing `userdel`, the administrator must verify the account exists and identify its current state.

**Step 1: Confirm the user exists in the system database.**

$$ \text{id } \text{username} $$

**Expected output format:**

$$
\text{uid=1005(username) gid=1005(username) groups=1005(username),27(sudo)}
$$

**Step 2: Check whether the user is currently logged in.**

$$
\text{who \vert grep username}
$$

If the command returns a line containing a TTY (e.g., `pts/0`), the user has an active session.

**Step 3: List all running processes owned by the user.**

$$
\text{ps -u username -f}
$$

This enumerates every process tree under the user's UID so the administrator can decide whether to terminate them gracefully or forcefully.

**Step 4: Locate all files owned by the user across the filesystem.**

$$
\text{find / -uid } \langle \text{UID} \rangle \text{ -type f 2> /dev/null}
$$

This is the **security audit** step. The `2> /dev/null` redirects permission-denied errors so they do not pollute the output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** "What is the effect of `find / -uid 1005`?" — The correct answer is: *It locates all files on the entire filesystem whose numeric owner UID is 1005, including those whose username is no longer in `/etc/passwd` (orphaned files).*

### 3.2 The Actual Removal Operation

**Step 5: Terminate all processes of the user (graceful path).**

$$
\text{sudo pkill -KILL -u username}
$$

The `-KILL` (signal 9) option ensures immediate termination. Without it, `pkill` defaults to SIGTERM (signal 15), which processes can ignore.

**Step 6: Back up the home directory (defense-in-depth).**

$$
\text{sudo tar -czf /backup/username\_}\langle \text{DATE} \rangle \text{.tar.gz /home/username}
$$

This is not strictly required, but in regulated environments (HIPAA, PCI-DSS, GDPR), a backup must be retained for a defined period even after account deletion.

**Step 7: Execute the deletion with full cleanup.**

$$
\text{sudo userdel -r username}
$$

What happens internally, in precise order:

1. The PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules) layer is invoked.
2. `/etc/passwd` is locked, the user's line is removed, the file is rewritten, and the lock is released.
3. `/etc/shadow` is updated identically.
4. `/etc/group` is scanned; the username is stripped from every supplementary group's member list.
5. `/etc/gshadow` is updated.
6. The user's home directory `/home/username` is recursively deleted via `rm -rf` semantics.
7. The mail spool at `/var/mail/username` (or `/var/spool/mail/username`) is removed.
8. Any crontab under `/var/spool/cron/crontabs/username` is removed.
9. SELinux mapping is removed (only if `-Z` is passed).
10. The command exits with status code `0`.

**Step 8: Verify the deletion succeeded.**

$$
\text{getent passwd username}
$$

**Expected output: empty.** If any line is returned, the deletion failed silently. The exit code is non-zero, and a log entry is written to `/var/log/secure` (RHEL) or `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian/Ubuntu).

**Step 9: Audit for orphaned files (post-deletion security check).**

$$
\text{find / -nouser -o -nogroup 2> /dev/null}
$$

The `-nouser` flag matches files whose numeric UID does not correspond to any user in `/etc/passwd`. The `-nogroup` flag matches files whose GID is unknown. These are the **orphan files** left behind if `-r` was not used, or if files were created outside the user's home directory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Security Engineering Practice:** In production, the `find / -nouser` output is typically fed into a SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) system for real-time alerting. Compliance auditors treat unresolved orphans as a **finding** during security assessments.

### 3.3 Complete Numerical Example — Worked Out

Let us walk through a complete scenario for a KTU numerical-style or descriptive question.

**Given:** A system administrator needs to remove a user named `alice` whose UID is `1005`. Alice has an active SSH session and 12 running processes. She is a member of the `developers` and `sudo` groups.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the user.

$$
\text{id alice} \Rightarrow \text{uid=1005(alice) gid=1005(alice) groups=1005(alice),1006(developers),27(sudo)}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify her active session.

$$
\text{who} \Rightarrow \text{alice pts/0 2024-11-15 09:30 (192.168.1.50)}
$$

**Step 3:** List her processes.

$$
\text{ps -u alice -f} \Rightarrow \text{(12 lines, including bash, vim, sshd-session, and 9 worker processes)}
$$

**Step 4:** Kill all her processes.

$$
\text{sudo pkill -KILL -u alice}
$$

After this, `ps -u alice -f` returns nothing.

**Step 5:** Back up her home directory.

$$
\text{sudo tar -czf /backup/alice\_20241115.tar.gz /home/alice}
$$

**Step 6:** Remove the user with full cleanup.

$$
\text{sudo userdel -r alice}
$$

**Step 7:** Verify.

$$
\text{getent passwd alice} \Rightarrow \text{(empty output, exit code 2)}
$$

$$
\text{ls /home/alice} \Rightarrow \text{ls: cannot access '/home/alice': No such file or directory}
$$

**Step 8:** Final orphan audit.

$$
\text{find / -nouser -nogroup 2> /dev/null} \Rightarrow \text{(empty output — system is clean)}
$$

If any files had been created by alice outside `/home/alice` (e.g., in `/tmp` or `/var/tmp`), they would have appeared here. The administrator would then decide whether to delete them or assign them to another user with `chown`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart: The Secure User Removal Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Administrator Receives Removal Request] --> verifyA[Step 1: Verify User Exists - id username]
    verifyA --> sessionA[Step 2: Check Active Sessions - who, w, last]
    sessionA --> processA[Step 3: List Running Processes - ps -u username -f]
    processA --> backupA[Step 4: Backup Home Directory - tar -czf]
    backupA --> killA[Step 5: Terminate User Processes - pkill -KILL -u username]
    killA --> deleteA[Step 6: Execute userdel -r username]
    deleteA --> filesA[System Modifies: etc passwd, etc shadow, etc group, etc gshadow]
    filesA --> homeA[System Removes: home dir, mail spool, crontabs]
    homeA --> verifyOut[Step 7: Verify Removal - getent passwd username]
    verifyOut --> auditA[Step 8: Audit Orphan Files - find / -nouser -nogroup]
    auditA --> decisionA{Any Orphan Files Found?}
    decisionA --> yesA[Yes: Review and chown or delete each]
    decisionA --> noA[No: Removal is Complete and Secure]
    yesA --> doneA[Document in Change Log - done]
    noA --> doneA
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: System Files Touched by `userdel -r`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userdelA[userdel -r username] --> passwdA[etc passwd - remove login entry]
    userdelA --> shadowA[etc shadow - remove password hash]
    userdelA --> groupA[etc group - strip from all supplementary groups]
    userdelA --> gshadowA[etc gshadow - update group secrets]
    userdelA --> subidA[etc subuid and etc subgid - remove namespace ranges]
    userdelA --> homeA[home username - recursive deletion]
    userdelA --> mailA[var spool mail username - delete mail spool]
    userdelA --> cronA[var spool cron crontabs username - delete crontab]
    userdelA --> atA[var spool at - remove at jobs]
    userdelA --> selinuxA[etc selinux - remove mapping if -Z used]
    passwdA --> securityOutcomeA[Security Outcome: No Authentication Possible]
    shadowA --> securityOutcomeA
    groupA --> securityOutcomeA
    gshadowA --> securityOutcomeA
    homeA --> dataOutcomeA[Data Outcome: All User Data Destroyed]
    mailA --> dataOutcomeA
    cronA --> dataOutcomeA
    atA --> dataOutcomeA
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram: Inter-Component Interaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant AdminA as System Administrator
    participant ShellA as Bash Shell
    participant UserdelA as userdel command
    participant NssA as NSS Library
    participant PAMA as PAM Module
    participant FilesystemA as Linux Filesystem

    AdminA->>ShellA: sudo userdel -r alice
    ShellA->>UserdelA: Execute with root privileges
    UserdelA->>PAMA: Initialize PAM transaction
    PAMA->>FilesystemA: Lock etc passwd
    UserdelA->>NssA: Query user alice in database
    NssA-->>UserdelA: Return UID 1005 and group list
    UserdelA->>FilesystemA: Remove alice line from etc passwd
    UserdelA->>FilesystemA: Remove alice line from etc shadow
    UserdelA->>FilesystemA: Update etc group and etc gshadow
    UserdelA->>FilesystemA: Recursive delete of home alice
    UserdelA->>FilesystemA: Delete var spool mail alice
    UserdelA->>PAMA: Commit PAM transaction
    PAMA-->>UserdelA: Transaction successful
    UserdelA-->>ShellA: Exit code 0
    ShellA-->>AdminA: Command completed
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the function of the `userdel -r` command in Linux? Mention the files it modifies.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `userdel -r` command permanently removes a user account **and** the user's home directory, mail spool, and crontab files. It modifies the system files `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, `/etc/group`, and `/etc/gshadow` to remove the user's identity, password hash, and group memberships. The `-r` option ensures no orphaned files are left behind on the filesystem, which is critical for security hygiene.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between `userdel username` and `userdel -r username` from a security perspective.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `userdel username` removes the user account from authentication databases (`/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, `/etc/group`) but **leaves the home directory and mail spool intact**. The files retain the old UID and become *orphaned*, creating a security risk if that UID is later reassigned to a new user.
- `userdel -r username` does the same but **also recursively deletes the home directory, mail spool, and crontab**, eliminating the possibility of orphaned files and ensuring complete data sanitization. From a security standpoint, `userdel -r` is the correct choice for account offboarding.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Note to Students:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination, Module 3 questions typically offer an internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Below, Question A and Question B are the two alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option A**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the complete procedure to securely remove a user named `bob` from a Linux system. Include all pre-removal checks, the actual command with appropriate options, and the post-removal verification steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are *orphaned files*? How do they arise during user removal, and what command is used to detect them? Describe the security implications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

The secure removal of user `bob` follows an eight-step procedure:

1. **Verify user exists** by running `id bob`. This returns the UID, GID, and supplementary groups.

2. **Check active sessions** using `who | grep bob` and `last bob` to see recent login history.

3. **List running processes** with `ps -u bob -f` to identify all processes owned by the user.

4. **Terminate all user processes** using `sudo pkill -KILL -u bob`. The `-KILL` signal (9) cannot be ignored.

5. **Back up the home directory** for compliance: `sudo tar -czf /backup/bob_<date>.tar.gz /home/bob`.

6. **Remove the user with full cleanup** using `sudo userdel -r -Z bob`. The `-r` option deletes the home directory and mail spool. The `-Z` option removes the SELinux user mapping (relevant for RHEL-based systems).

7. **Verify removal** by running `getent passwd bob`. The output must be empty. The exit code should be non-zero (typically 2), confirming the entry no longer exists.

8. **Audit for orphan files** using `sudo find / -nouser -nogroup 2> /dev/null`. If any files are found, the administrator must decide whether to delete them or reassign ownership with `chown newuser:newgroup <file>`.

The above procedure is logged in `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian/Ubuntu) or `/var/log/secure` (RHEL/CentOS) for audit trail purposes.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Definition:** *Orphaned files* are files on a Linux filesystem whose numerical UID (or GID) does not correspond to any user (or group) listed in `/etc/passwd` (or `/etc/group`). They have a valid inode but no valid owner.

**How they arise:** Orphaned files typically arise in the following scenarios:
- A user account is deleted using `userdel` *without* the `-r` option, leaving the home directory and its contents on disk.
- A user manually creates files outside their home directory (e.g., in `/tmp`, `/var/tmp`, or shared project folders) and is then deleted.
- The UID is manually edited in `/etc/passwd` to a non-existent value.

**Detection command:** `find / -nouser -nogroup 2> /dev/null`
- The `-nouser` flag matches files with no corresponding user.
- The `-nogroup` flag matches files with no corresponding group.
- The `2> /dev/null` discards permission-denied errors.

**Security implications:**
1. **Data leakage:** Orphaned files often contain sensitive data — credentials, SSH private keys, `.bash_history` with previously typed commands, business documents — that survives the user's account deletion.
2. **UID recycling hijack:** When a new user is created and inherits the orphaned UID, they automatically gain ownership of all such files, effectively inheriting the previous user's data and possibly their privileges.
3. **Compliance violations:** Regulatory frameworks (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS) require that user data be either retained under documented retention policies OR securely destroyed. Orphaned files in `/tmp` or `/home` constitute an undocumented data persistence pathway.
4. **Forensic ambiguity:** During incident response, orphan files complicate ownership attribution, making it harder to assign responsibility for malicious activity.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option B**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** List and explain any four options of the `userdel` command with their security implications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a shell script that takes a username as a command-line argument and securely removes the user with full backup, process termination, and orphan-file audit. Assume the script is run by root. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

| Option | Full Form | Function | Security Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| `-r` | `--remove` | Removes the home directory, mail spool, and all contents. | **Critical for security.** Prevents orphan files and data leakage. Always recommended. |
| `-f` | `--force` | Forces removal of a user who is currently logged in or has running processes. | **Risky in production.** May leave zombie processes and open file handles. Should be used only during incident response. |
| `-Z` | `--selinux-user` | Removes the SELinux user mapping for the account. | **Mandatory on RHEL/CentOS.** Without it, the SELinux context remains, potentially allowing SELinux-mediated access. |
| `-R` | `--root` | Applies the deletion inside a chroot directory. | Useful for recovery or containerized systems, but reduces the operation's scope and may give a false sense of completion. |

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Script: secure_userdel.sh
# Purpose: Securely remove a Linux user with backup and audit
# Usage:   sudo ./secure_userdel.sh <username>

# Step 1: Validate input
if [ $# -ne 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <username>"
    exit 1
fi

USERNAME=$1
BACKUP_DIR="/backup"
DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d)

# Step 2: Verify the user exists
if ! id "$USERNAME" &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Error: User '$USERNAME' does not exist."
    exit 2
fi

# Step 3: Log the operation
echo "Starting removal of user $USERNAME on $(date)" | tee -a /var/log/user_audit.log

# Step 4: Backup the home directory if it exists
if [ -d "/home/$USERNAME" ]; then
    tar -czf "$BACKUP_DIR/${USERNAME}_${DATE}.tar.gz" "/home/$USERNAME"
    echo "Backup created at $BACKUP_DIR/${USERNAME}_${DATE}.tar.gz"
fi

# Step 5: Terminate all processes of the user
pkill -KILL -u "$USERNAME" 2>/dev/null
echo "All processes of $USERNAME terminated."

# Step 6: Remove the user with full cleanup
userdel -r "$USERNAME"
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "User $USERNAME removed successfully."
else
    echo "Error: userdel failed."
    exit 3
fi

# Step 7: Audit for orphan files
echo "Scanning for orphan files..."
ORPHANS=$(find / -nouser -nogroup 2>/dev/null)
if [ -n "$ORPHANS" ]; then
    echo "WARNING: Orphan files detected:"
    echo "$ORPHANS"
else
    echo "No orphan files found. System is clean."
fi

# Step 8: Final log entry
echo "Removal of $USERNAME completed on $(date)" | tee -a /var/log/user_audit.log
exit 0
```

**Explanation of script logic:**
- Line-by-line: input validation (1), existence check (2-3), backup (4-5), process kill (6-7), actual `userdel -r` (8-11), orphan audit (12-17), and logging (18).
- The `&>/dev/null` redirection suppresses both stdout and stderr.
- The `set -e` style explicit exit code checks ensure the script does not continue on failure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `-r` is the #1 reason students lose marks.** Always explain that `userdel` *without* `-r` leaves the home directory intact and creates a security risk.
> 2. **Confusing `userdel` with `deluser`.** On Debian/Ubuntu, `deluser` is a higher-level Perl script that wraps `userdel` and provides a friendlier interface. In KTU answers, either is acceptable, but be precise about which distribution you are referencing.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention `/etc/shadow`.** Many students list only `/etc/passwd`. The encrypted password database is **also** modified, and mentioning both is required for full marks.
> 4. **Not explaining "orphan files."** This is a high-frequency 7-mark question. A complete answer must include: definition, cause, detection command (`find / -nouser`), and at least two security implications.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`userdel`** is the canonical command for removing Linux users; **`userdel -r`** is the secure variant that also cleans up the home directory and mail spool.
- The command requires **root privileges**; otherwise it fails silently or with a permission error.
- It modifies **four core system files**: `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, `/etc/group`, and `/etc/gshadow`.
- The **`-r` option** is the security-critical flag; without it, **orphaned files** persist on disk with their original UID.
- **`-f` (force)** should be used with caution in production; it can leave zombie processes.
- **`-Z`** is required on SELinux-enabled systems (RHEL, CentOS, Fedora) to remove the security context mapping.
- **Pre-removal checks**: `id`, `who`, `ps -u`, and `find / -uid` to understand the user's footprint.
- **Post-removal audit**: `find / -nouser -nogroup 2> /dev/null` to detect any orphan files.
- **Best practice workflow**: verify → backup → kill processes → `userdel -r` → verify → audit → log.
- **Audit trail**: All operations are recorded in `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian) or `/var/log/secure` (RHEL).
- **Compliance angle**: GDPR, HIPAA, and PCI-DSS require either documented retention OR complete destruction of user data — making `userdel -r` the compliance-correct choice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modifying a User
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modifying a User

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **User Modification** in Linux refers to the administrative operation of altering the existing attributes, privileges, and metadata of an already-created user account. This is performed using dedicated system administration utilities such as `usermod` (the primary command for modifying user database entries), `passwd` (for password-related modifications), and `chage` (for aging/expiration policy modifications).

The user account information is stored in the following critical system files:

| File | Purpose | KTU Significance |
|------|---------|------------------|
| `/etc/passwd` | Stores basic user account information | Readable by all users |
| `/etc/shadow` | Stores encrypted passwords and aging info | Readable only by root |
| `/etc/group` | Stores group information | Readable by all users |
| `/etc/gshadow` | Stores secure group information | Readable only by root |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Employee HR Record Analogy:**
> Think of a user account in Linux like an **employee record** in a company's HR system. When the HR department needs to change an employee's *designation*, *department*, *salary*, or *phone number*, they don't create a *new* employee file — they **edit the existing one**. Similarly, in Linux, we don't delete and recreate users when details change; we **modify** the existing user record.

Three key real-world scenarios where this applies:
- **Department Transfer** → Change the user's primary or supplementary group
- **Role Promotion** → Change the username, UID, or login shell
- **Leave/Return from Sabbatical** → Lock or unlock the account, change expiration date

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Identity Threshold:** Every Linux user has a **UID (User ID)** as a unique integer identifier. The standard range is **UID 0 (root)**, **UID 1–999 (system accounts)**, and **UID 1000+ (regular users)** as defined by the `UID_MIN` and `UID_MAX` directives in `/etc/login.defs`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of User Modification

Linux user modification is structured around **three administrative command families**, each responsible for a specific domain:

### Pillar 1: `usermod` — The Attribute Editor
The flagship command for modifying entries in the user database. It edits the `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, and `/etc/group` files through a safe, library-mediated interface (`libuser` or `shadow-utils`).

**General Syntax:**
```bash
usermod [options] LOGIN
```

### Pillar 2: `passwd` — The Password Manager
Used to change a user's password, lock, or unlock an account.

### Pillar 3: `chage` — The Aging Policy Manager
Used to view or change user password aging/expiration policies stored in `/etc/shadow`.

## 2.2 Comprehensive `usermod` Options — KTU High-Yield Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master reference** for the KTU 2024 exam. Memorize the options and their flags.

| Option | Long Form | Function | Affected File |
|--------|-----------|----------|---------------|
| `-c` | `--comment` | Change the GECOS/comment field | `/etc/passwd` |
| `-d` | `--home` | Change the home directory path | `/etc/passwd` |
| `-m` | `--move-home` | Move contents of old home to new | Filesystem |
| `-e` | `--expiredate` | Set account expiration date (YYYY-MM-DD) | `/etc/shadow` |
| `-f` | `--inactive` | Days after password expiry before account is disabled | `/etc/shadow` |
| `-g` | `--gid` | Change primary group | `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/group` |
| `-G` | `--groups` | Replace list of supplementary groups | `/etc/group` |
| `-a` | `--append` | Append (not replace) supplementary groups | `/etc/group` |
| `-l` | `--login` | Change the username (login name) | `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow` |
| `-L` | `--lock` | Lock the user account (disables login) | `/etc/shadow` |
| `-U` | `--unlock` | Unlock the user account | `/etc/shadow` |
| `-s` | `--shell` | Change the login shell | `/etc/passwd` |
| `-u` | `--uid` | Change the user ID (UID) | `/etc/passwd` |
| `-o` | `--non-unique` | Allow duplicate UIDs (use with `-u`) | `/etc/passwd` |

## 2.3 Key Conceptual Distinctions (Frequently Tested)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction 1:** `-G` (Replace) vs. `-aG` (Append)
> - `-G group1,group2` → **Replaces** entire supplementary group list
> - `-aG group3` → **Appends** to existing supplementary group list (MUST be combined with `-G`)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction 2:** `-d` (Change Path) vs. `-m` (Move Files)
> - `-d /new/home` → Only updates the path string; does NOT move files
> - `-m` → Moves the actual file contents from old home to new home

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction 3:** `-L` (Lock) vs. `-e` (Expire)
> - `-L` → Adds `!` to password hash → Prevents password authentication
> - `-e 2024-12-31` → Sets account expiration date in `/etc/shadow`

## 2.4 The `chage` Command — Aging Policy Cheat Sheet

The `chage` command manages password aging as defined in the **fifth to ninth fields** of `/etc/shadow`:

$$
\text{/etc/shadow line format} = \text{username}:\text{password}:\text{lastchange}:\text{min}:\text{max}:\text{warn}:\text{inactive}:\text{expire}:\text{reserved}
$$

| Field Position | Meaning | `chage` Option |
|----------------|---------|----------------|
| 3rd field | Date of last password change | `-d` |
| 4th field | Minimum days between changes | `-m` |
| 5th field | Maximum days password is valid | `-M` |
| 6th field | Warning days before expiry | `-W` |
| 7th field | Inactive days after expiry | `-I` |
| 8th field | Account expiration date | `-E` |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DevOps Pipelines**: Automated provisioning tools (Ansible, Puppet) use `usermod` to dynamically adjust service accounts.
- **Server Hardening**: Disabling direct root login by changing the `root` account's shell to `/usr/sbin/nologin`.
- **Cloud Linux VMs**: AWS EC2 `cloud-init` scripts use these commands to configure default users.
- **Cybersecurity**: Forensic investigators use UID changes to trace identity tampering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Procedure 1: Changing a Username with `usermod -l`

**Step-by-step Logical Procedure:**

**Step 1** — Verify the user exists and check current UID:
```bash
id olduser
```

**Step 2** — Modify the login name (username):
```bash
usermod -l newuser olduser
```

**Step 3** — Verify the change took effect:
```bash
id newuser
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** You cannot change the username of a user who is **currently logged in**. The system will throw the error: `usermod: user olduser is currently used by process XXXX`. Always log the user out first.

**Step 4** — Optional: Move home directory to match new username:
```bash
usermod -d /home/newuser -m newuser
```

## 3.2 Procedure 2: Changing the Home Directory with `-d` and `-m`

**Step-by-step Logical Procedure:**

**Step 1** — Create the new home directory (optional but recommended):
```bash
mkdir /home/projects/alice_new
```

**Step 2** — Change the home directory path and move existing files:
```bash
usermod -d /home/projects/alice_new -m alice
```

**Step 3** — Verify:
```bash
grep alice /etc/passwd
```

The output should show:
```text
alice:x:1001:1001::/home/projects/alice_new:/bin/bash
```

## 3.3 Procedure 3: Managing Group Memberships

### Sub-procedure 3A: Change Primary Group

The primary group equation is:
$$
\text{Primary Group} = \text{user's GID in } /etc/passwd
$$

**Step 1** — Check the group exists:
```bash
grep ^developers /etc/group
```

**Step 2** — Change primary group:
```bash
usermod -g developers alice
```

### Sub-procedure 3B: Append Supplementary Group (Preserving Existing)

**Step 1** — View current supplementary groups:
```bash
id alice
```

**Step 2** — Append a new group **without** removing existing ones:
```bash
usermod -aG docker alice
```

**Step 3** — Verify the addition:
```bash
id alice
```

### Sub-procedure 3C: Replace All Supplementary Groups

> [!WARNING]
> This command **REMOVES** all existing supplementary groups and sets ONLY the listed ones.

```bash
usermod -G sudo,docker,www-data alice
```

## 3.4 Procedure 4: Locking and Unlocking Accounts

**Step 1** — Lock the account (places `!` prefix in `/etc/shadow`):
```bash
usermod -L bob
```

**Step 2** — Verify lock by checking `/etc/shadow`:
```bash
sudo grep bob /etc/shadow
```

Expected pattern:
$$
\text{Password Field} = \text{!}\cdot\text{(encrypted\_hash)}
$$

**Step 3** — Unlock the account:
```bash
usermod -U bob
```

## 3.5 Procedure 5: Setting Account Expiration with `chage`

**Step 1** — Set the password expiration to **90 days** from today:
```bash
chage -M 90 alice
```

**Step 2** — Set the account to expire on a specific date:
```bash
chage -E 2025-12-31 alice
```

**Step 3** — Set minimum age to **7 days** (user cannot change password within 7 days of last change):
```bash
chage -m 7 alice
```

**Step 4** — Display current aging information:
```bash
chage -l alice
```

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Command Should I Use?

| User Wants To... | Recommended Command | KTU Quick Code |
|------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Change username | `usermod -l` | `usermod -l NEWNAME OLDNAME` |
| Change UID | `usermod -u` | `usermod -u 1050 USER` |
| Change home directory | `usermod -d -m` | `usermod -d /new/home -m USER` |
| Change login shell | `usermod -s` | `usermod -s /bin/zsh USER` |
| Add to a group | `usermod -aG` | `usermod -aG GROUP USER` |
| Reset/change password | `passwd` | `passwd USER` |
| Force password change at next login | `chage -d 0` | `chage -d 0 USER` |
| Lock account | `usermod -L` | `usermod -L USER` |
| Set account expiry | `chage -E` | `chage -E YYYY-MM-DD USER` |
| View aging info | `chage -l` | `chage -l USER` |

## 3.7 Verification Chain — The Golden Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always run a verification command after every modification.** The KTU examiner expects a `verify-and-confirm` step in your answers.

| Modified Attribute | Verification Command |
|--------------------|---------------------|
| Username | `id newname` |
| UID | `grep user /etc/passwd` |
| Home directory | `grep user /etc/passwd` |
| Shell | `grep user /etc/passwd` |
| Supplementary groups | `id user` or `groups user` |
| Account lock state | `passwd -S user` |
| Password aging | `chage -l user` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: User Modification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Admin Needs to Modify User] --> B{Which Attribute?}
    B --> C[Username -l]
    B --> D[UID -u]
    B --> E[Home Dir -d -m]
    B --> F[Shell -s]
    B --> G[Groups -g -G -aG]
    B --> H[Lock or Unlock -L -U]
    B --> I[Aging Policy]
    C --> J[Run usermod Command]
    D --> J
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> K[Run chage Command]
    J --> L{User Logged In?}
    L --> M[Yes Force Logout]
    L --> N[No Proceed]
    M --> O[Execute Modification]
    N --> O
    K --> O
    O --> P[Verify with id grep chage -l]
    P --> Q[Modification Complete]
```
