### Step 3: Compute the resulting permission

| Class | Before | Operation | After | Binary | Octal |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| User | rw- (6) | +x | rwx | 111 | **7** |
| Group | r-- (4) | -r | --- | 000 | **0** |
| Others | r-- (4) | +w | rw- | 110 | **6** |

### Step 4: Final verification

```bash
$ chmod u+x,g-r,o+w script.sh
$ ls -l script.sh
-rwx---rw- 1 user user 0 Jun 15 10:30 script.sh
```

The final octal mode is **706**.

## 3.4 The `umask` Computation — Full Derivation

**Problem:** Given `umask = 027`, calculate the default permissions of newly created files and directories.

### Step 1: Recall the kernel defaults

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Default file mode} &= 666_8 = 110\,110\,110_2 \\
\text{Default dir mode} &= 777_8 = 111\,111\,111_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the umask subtraction (bitwise AND with complement)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{umask} &= 027_8 = 000\,010\,111_2 \\
\sim \text{umask} &= 111\,101\,000_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute new file mode

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{File mode} &= 666 \;\&\; \sim 027 \\
&= 110\,110\,110 \;\&\; 111\,101\,000 \\
&= 110\,100\,000 = 640_8
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute new directory mode

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dir mode} &= 777 \;\&\; \sim 027 \\
&= 111\,111\,111 \;\&\; 111\,101\,000 \\
&= 111\,101\,000 = 750_8
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Interpret the result

- New files → `rw-r-----` (owner: read+write; group: read only; others: none)
- New directories → `rwxr-x---` (owner: full; group: read+execute; others: none)

This is a **secure default**, suitable for sensitive multi-user environments.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Permission Bit-Position Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Inode Mode Field 16-bit"] --> B["Bits 0-2: Others"]
    A --> C["Bits 3-5: Group"]
    A --> D["Bits 6-8: User"]
    A --> E["Bits 9-11: Special Bits"]
    A --> F["Bits 12-15: File Type"]

    B --> B1["x bit 0 value 1"]
    B --> B2["w bit 1 value 2"]
    B --> B3["r bit 2 value 4"]

    C --> C1["x bit 3 value 1"]
    C --> C2["w bit 4 value 2"]
    C --> C3["r bit 5 value 4"]

    D --> D1["x bit 6 value 1"]
    D --> D2["w bit 7 value 2"]
    D --> D3["r bit 8 value 4"]

    E --> E1["sticky bit 9 value 1000"]
    E --> E2["setgid bit 10 value 2000"]
    E --> E3["setuid bit 11 value 4000"]

    F --> F1["regular file dash"]
    F --> F2["directory d"]
    F --> F3["symlink l"]
    F --> F4["device block c"]
```

## 4.2 chmod Command Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User runs chmod command"] --> B{"Numeric or Symbolic?"}

    B -- Numeric --> C["Parse 3-digit octal"]
    C --> C1["Digit 1 to User"]
    C --> C2["Digit 2 to Group"]
    C --> C3["Digit 3 to Others"]
    C1 --> D["Update inode mode field"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D

    B -- Symbolic --> E["Parse who op perm"]
    E --> E1["who: u g o a"]
    E --> E2["op: plus minus equals"]
    E --> E3["perm: r w x X s t"]
    E1 --> F["Modify specific bits"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> D

    D --> G["Return success or EPERM"]
```

## 4.3 The Three-Class Permission Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FileSystem["Linux File: data.txt"]
        direction TB
        USER["USER Owner bit pattern 111 octal 7"]
        GROUP["GROUP Members bit pattern 101 octal 5"]
        OTHERS["OTHERS World bit pattern 100 octal 4"]
    end

    USER --> UA1["Read view contents"]
    USER --> UA2["Write modify contents"]
    USER --> UA3["Execute run as program"]

    GROUP --> GA1["Read only allowed"]
    GROUP --> GA2["Write DENIED"]
    GROUP --> GA3["Execute allowed for scripts"]

    OTHERS --> OA1["Read only allowed"]
    OTHERS --> OA2["Write DENIED"]
    OTHERS --> OA3["Execute DENIED"]
```

## 4.4 umask Default-Mask Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Kernel Default 666 files 777 dirs"] --> B["Apply umask subtraction"]
    C["umask value e.g. 022 or 027"] --> B
    B --> D["Resulting Mode"]
    D --> E1["File permission"]
    D --> E2["Directory permission"]
    D --> E3["Special bits preserved"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Explain the difference between symbolic and absolute (octal) modes of the `chmod` command with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

The `chmod` command supports two notations to modify file permissions:

1. **Symbolic Mode** uses a human-readable triplet `[who][operator][permission]` like `chmod u+x file.sh`. It is *relative* — it modifies the existing permissions without affecting other bits. Supported operators are `+` (add), `-` (remove), and `=` (assign exactly).

2. **Absolute (Octal) Mode** uses a 3- or 4-digit octal number like `chmod 755 file.sh`. It is *absolute* — it overwrites the existing permission with the exact new value. Each digit represents a class (user, group, others) and is the sum of 4 (read), 2 (write), and 1 (execute).

Symbolic mode is preferred for incremental changes; octal mode is preferred for scripts where the final state must be deterministic.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**What is the significance of the `setuid` bit? Mention one practical example.**

**Model Answer (8 lines):**

The **setuid** (Set User ID) bit is a special permission (octal value **4000**) applied to executable files. When a user runs a setuid program, the process executes with the **file owner's privileges**, not the invoker's. This is essential for system utilities that ordinary users must invoke but that require root access to function.

**Practical example:** `/usr/bin/passwd` has the setuid bit set so that any user can change their own password — the program temporarily runs as root to write the updated credential hash to `/etc/shadow`.

Other examples: `ping` (needs raw socket access), `mount`, `sudo`.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has **internal choice**. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in the exam. Each contains sub-parts (a) and (b), worth 7 marks each.

---

### ❓ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A file `project.txt` has the permission string `-rw-r-----` (owner: read+write, group: read, others: none). Perform the following operations **step-by-step** using the `chmod` command and show the resulting octal mode and `ls -l` output after each step:
1. Add execute permission for the owner.
2. Add write permission to the group.
3. Revoke all permissions from others (already 0, but show symbolically).
4. Apply the setgid bit to the file.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Initial state:** `-rw-r-----` → octal **640**

```bash
$ chmod u+x project.txt        # Operation 1
$ ls -l project.txt
-rwxr----- 1 user grp 0 Jun 15 11:00 project.txt
# New mode: 740
```

After step 1: user now has `rwx` (7), group is `r--` (4), others `---` (0) → **740**.

```bash
$ chmod g+w project.txt        # Operation 2
$ ls -l project.txt
-rwxrw---- 1 user grp 0 Jun 15 11:00 project.txt
# New mode: 760
```

After step 2: user `rwx` (7), group `rw-` (6), others `---` (0) → **760**.

```bash
$ chmod o= project.txt         # Operation 3 (already 0)
$ ls -l project.txt
-rwxrw---- 1 user grp 0 Jun 15 11:00 project.txt
# Mode unchanged: 760
```

After step 3: mode remains **760** (others already had no permission).

```bash
$ chmod g+s project.txt        # Operation 4
$ ls -l project.txt
-rwxrwS--- 1 user grp 0 Jun 15 11:00 project.txt
# New mode: 2760
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the capital **S** instead of lowercase `s` — this happens when the setgid bit is set but the group does **not** have execute permission. Lowercase `s` would appear if execute were also granted to the group.

**Final octal mode: 2760** (4-digit because of the setgid bit **2000**).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the `umask` command in detail. Given `umask = 027`, calculate and justify the default permissions for newly created files and directories. How would you set the `umask` permanently for a user?

**Model Solution:**

The `umask` (user file-creation mask) is a shell builtin and system call that defines the **default permission bits to be removed** from newly created files and directories. The kernel subtracts the umask from the base default modes:

- Base file mode: **666** (read+write for everyone; execute is intentionally excluded for security)
- Base directory mode: **777** (read+write+execute for everyone)

**Calculation for umask = 027:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New file mode} &= 666 - 027 = 639 \quad \text{(but using bitwise: } 666 \,\&\, \sim 027 = 640\text{)} \\
\text{New dir mode} &= 777 - 027 = 750 \quad \text{(bitwise: } 777 \,\&\, \sim 027 = 750\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using bitwise AND with complement:**

- `027` = `000 010 111`
- `~027` = `111 101 000`
- `666` (`110 110 110`) AND `~027` (`111 101 000`) = `110 100 000` = **640**
- `777` (`111 111 111`) AND `~027` (`111 101 000`) = `111 101 000` = **750**

**Interpretation:**
- New files: `rw-r-----` — secure, no world-readable
- New directories: `rwxr-x---` — group can browse but not modify

**Setting umask permanently:**
- Temporarily (current session): `umask 027`
- Permanently for a user: add `umask 027` to `~/.bashrc` or `~/.profile`
- System-wide default: edit `/etc/profile` or `/etc/login.defs`

---

### ❓ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat examples, explain the `chown` and `chgrp` commands. How do they differ from `chmod`? List any two situations where changing ownership is necessary in real-world system administration.

**Model Solution:**

**`chown` (Change Owner):** Modifies the user ownership of a file. Syntax: `chown [options] newowner[:newgroup] file`.

```bash
chown alice report.txt           # Change owner to 'alice'
chown alice:developers app.py   # Change owner to 'alice' and group to 'developers'
chown -R www-data:www-data /var/www/html  # Recursive change for a web directory
```

**`chgrp` (Change Group):** Modifies only the group ownership.

```bash
chgrp developers project/        # Change group of directory
chgrp -R team data/              # Recursive group change
```

**Difference from chmod:**

| Aspect | `chmod` | `chown` / `chgrp` |
|---|---|---|
| **What it changes** | Permission *bits* (rwx) | Ownership *metadata* (UID/GID) |
| **Who can run** | Owner of file or root | Almost always requires root |
| **Effect on access** | Changes what is *allowed* | Changes *who* is considered the owner/group |

**Two real-world situations requiring ownership change:**
1. **Web server deployment:** When uploading files via FTP as user `alice` but the Apache/Nginx web server runs as `www-data`, the files must be `chown`ed to `www-data` so the server can read them.
2. **User account migration / handoff:** When a developer leaves a project, the files are `chown`ed to the new owner, transferring the administrative control without copying data.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the sticky bit with an example. How is it represented in symbolic and octal modes? What happens if a regular user tries to delete a file in a sticky-bit directory owned by another user?

**Model Solution:**

The **sticky bit** (octal value **1000**) is a special directory permission that restricts file deletion. When set on a directory, **only the file's owner, the directory's owner, or root** can delete or rename files within it — even if other users have write permission to the directory.

**Symbolic notation:** `chmod +t /shared` (adds sticky bit; appears as `t` in the `ls -l` output for *others*).

**Octal notation:** `chmod 1755 /shared` (the leading **1** indicates the sticky bit).

**Classic example: `/tmp`**

```bash
$ ls -ld /tmp
drwxrwxrwt 10 root root 4096 Jun 15 12:00 /tmp
#               ^
#               t = sticky bit
```

The `/tmp` directory has permissions `rwxrwxrwt` — everyone (user, group, others) can read, write, and execute (browse). But because of the **sticky bit**, user `bob` cannot delete a file created by user `alice` inside `/tmp`, even though `bob` has write access to the directory.

**What happens if Bob tries:**
```bash
$ rm /tmp/alice_secret.txt
rm: cannot remove '/tmp/alice_secret.txt': Operation not permitted
```

**Use cases:** Shared upload directories, public bulletin boards, `/var/tmp`, project collaboration folders.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sticky bit vs. setuid confusion:** Do not mix up the three special bits. Sticky bit is for *directories* (restricted deletion); setuid/setgid are for *executables* (privilege elevation). Examiners **will** deduct marks for confusing them.
> 2. **Octal subtraction vs. bitwise AND:** When calculating umask effects, use **bitwise AND with the complement** (e.g., `666 & ~027`), NOT plain subtraction. They differ when the umask has bits set that are zero in the default (e.g., execute bit on files).
> 3. **Capital vs. lowercase `s`/`t`:** A capital letter (e.g., `S`, `T`) means the special bit is set BUT the underlying execute bit is missing. Examiners check this nuance.
> 4. **Forgetting `-R` for directories:** When applying `chmod` or `chown` to a directory tree, the `-R` (recursive) flag is mandatory. Marks are lost if a student writes `chmod 755 /project` expecting it to apply to subdirectories.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Every Linux file/directory has a **9-bit permission mask** split into 3 classes × 3 modes.
- ✅ `r = 4`, `w = 2`, `x = 1`. The octal digit is the **sum** of the granted bits.
- ✅ `chmod` modifies **permission bits**; `chown` modifies **ownership**.
- ✅ Symbolic mode is **relative** (`+`, `-`, `=`); octal mode is **absolute** (overwrites).
- ✅ Special bits: **setuid (4000)**, **setgid (2000)**, **sticky (1000)**.
- ✅ The `umask` is **subtracted** (bitwise) from kernel defaults (666 for files, 777 for dirs).
- ✅ Only **root** can change file ownership arbitrarily; owners can change group to a group they belong to.
- ✅ Capital letters (`S`, `T`) in `ls -l` indicate special bits set *without* the corresponding execute bit.
- ✅ Always use `-R` for recursive operations on directory trees.
- ✅ The `ls -l` 10-character string: **1 file type + 3 class triplets**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 questions on this topic frequently combine a **command demonstration** (3-4 marks) with a **conceptual explanation** of *why* the permission matters. Memorize the octal table by heart and practice converting between symbolic and numeric forms in both directions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Changing Ownership
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Changing Ownership ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the Linux/Unix file system architecture, every file and directory is associated with exactly **three ownership identifiers**: a **User Owner (UID)**, a **Group Owner (GID)**, and a set of **Permission Bits** (read, write, execute). **Changing Ownership** is the administrative operation that modifies the **User Owner** and/or the **Group Owner** of a file or directory without altering the permission bits themselves. The two principal system utilities used for this purpose are: - **`chown` (Change Owner):** Modifies the user owner and optionally the group owner. - **`chgrp` (Change Group):** Modifies only the group owner. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ownership modification is distinct from permission modification. The `chmod` command changes **what** can be done with the file (rwx), whereas `chown`/`chgrp` change **who** owns or controls the file. Both are critical components of the DAC (Discretionary Access Control) model in Linux. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The House Deed:** > Imagine a house with a registered deed. The **owner** of the house is the person whose name appears on the deed (this is the **User Owner** in Linux). The **society/association** managing that house is the **Group Owner**. The **locks and keys** on the doors represent the **permission bits**. > > - **Changing Ownership (`chown`):** Transferring the deed to a new person. Now the new owner has full control. > - **Changing Group (`chgrp`):** Moving the house from one society to another. The owner stays the same, but the managing body changes. > - **Changing Permissions (`chmod`):** Replacing the locks on the doors. The owner and society remain, but who can enter changes. > > Just as in real life only a government registrar (or the owner themselves with proper documents) can change a deed, in Linux only the **root superuser** (or the file owner in certain cases) can change ownership. ### 1.3 Core Ownership Triad in Linux Every file in Linux carries the following triplet in its **inode metadata**: | Identifier | Abbreviation | Storage | Description | |---|---|---|---| | User ID | UID | 32-bit integer | Numeric ID of the user owner | | Group ID | GID | 32-bit integer | Numeric ID of the group owner | | Permission Mode | Mode Bits | 12-bit flags | rwx for user, group, others + special bits | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction:** A user may **own** a file without having any permissions on it, and conversely, a user may have permissions without owning the file. Ownership and permissions are **orthogonal concepts**. ### 1.4 Who Can Change Ownership? This is one of the most heavily tested concepts in KTU examinations: | Actor | Can Change User Owner? | Can Change Group Owner? | |---|---|---| | **Root (Superuser)** | ✅ Yes (to anyone) | ✅ Yes (to any group) | | **File Owner (non-root)** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (only to a group they belong to) | | **Any other user** | ❌ No | ❌ No | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often wrongly state that "the file owner can change the ownership to any other user." This is **incorrect**. The `chown` command for changing the *user* owner is restricted to **root only**. Non-root users can only use `chgrp` (or `chown :groupname` syntax) to change the group to one of which they are already a member. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The `chown` Command — Formal Syntax The complete POSIX-standard syntax for the change owner utility is: ``` chown [OPTIONS] [OWNER][:[GROUP]] FILE... ``` Where each component is defined as: - **`[OWNER]`** — The new user owner. Can be specified as a username (e.g., `alice`) or a numeric UID (e.g., `1001`). - **`[GROUP]`** — The new group owner. **Preceded by a colon `:`**. Can be a group name (e.g., `developers`) or a numeric GID (e.g., `1002`). - **`FILE...`** — One or more target files or directories. - **`[OPTIONS]`** — Modifier flags controlling recursion, verbosity, and symbol resolution. ### 2.2 The `chgrp` Command — Formal Syntax ``` chgrp [OPTIONS] GROUP FILE... ``` This utility is functionally a **restricted subset** of `chown`, accepting only the group specifier. ### 2.3 Critical Syntax Variants of `chown` The colon-prefix logic in `chown` is the most frequently tested area in KTU exams: | Command | Effect on User | Effect on Group | Example | |---|---|---|---| | `chown alice file` | Change to `alice` | **Unchanged** | U:alice, G:original | | `chown alice: file` | Change to `alice` | Change to `alice`'s **login group** | U:alice, G:alice's group | | `chown :devs file` | **Unchanged** | Change to `devs` | U:original, G:devs | | `chown alice:devs file` | Change to `alice` | Change to `devs` | U:alice, G:devs | | `chown .devs file` | **Unchanged** | Change to `devs` (BSD-style) | U:original, G:devs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Pearl:** The **trailing colon** in `chown alice: file` is interpreted as "set the group to alice's primary login group." This is a subtle but frequently tested nuance. ### 2.4 Key Options for `chown` and `chgrp` | Option (Short) | Option (Long) | Function | |---|---|---| | `-R` | `--recursive` | Recursively change ownership of all files and subdirectories | | `-v` | `--verbose` | Output a diagnostic for every file processed | | `-c` | `--changes` | Output only when a change is actually made | | `-f` | `--silent` / `--quiet` | Suppress most error messages | | `-h` | `--no-dereference` | Affect symbolic links themselves, not their targets | | `--reference=RFILE` | — | Use RFILE's owner and group as the reference | | `--from=CURRENT_OWNER:CURRENT_GROUP` | — | Change only if the file currently matches the specified owner/group | ### 2.5 Symbolic Link Handling — A Critical Edge Case By default, when `chown` encounters a **symbolic link**, it **dereferences** the link and modifies the **target file**, not the link itself. To modify the link itself, the `-h` flag must be used. ``` # Default behavior: changes the target of the link chown alice symlink_to_file # To change the link itself (requires root) chown -h alice symlink_to_file ``` ### 2.6 Verification of Ownership The `ls -l` command displays ownership in its **third and fourth columns**: ``` -rw-r--r-- 1 alice developers 4096 Jan 15 10:30 report.txt ^^^^ ^^^^^^^^^^^ UID GID ``` The `stat` command provides comprehensive metadata: ``` stat report.txt ``` ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula / Rule Sheet | Rule # | Statement | Constraint | |---|---|---| | **R1** | Only **root** can change the user owner of a file. | EUID must be 0 | | **R2** | The file owner can change the group to any group they are a member of. | Owner must be in target group | | **R3** | `chown user:group` is equivalent to setting UID then GID atomically. | One system call pair | | **R4** | `chown :group` only changes GID, UID is preserved. | Colon prefix is mandatory | | **R5** | `-R` (recursive) descends into directories but follows symlinks by default. | Use `-h` to prevent dereference | | **R6** | Numeric IDs and symbolic names are interchangeable in `chown`. | Resolution via `/etc/passwd` and `/etc/group` | | **R7** | Ownership change does **not** alter mode bits. | `chmod` is separate | ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility Ownership management is foundational in: - **Web Server Administration:** Ensuring `nginx`/`apache` processes own their document roots. - **Container Orchestration:** Maintaining correct UID/GID mappings between host and containers (Docker, Kubernetes). - **Multi-tenant Systems:** Isolating user workspaces in shared compute clusters. - **Security Hardening:** Removing ownership of sensitive files from application service accounts to enforce least-privilege. - **Backup & Recovery:** Preserving original ownership during `tar` extraction with `--same-owner`. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Ownership Resolution Algorithm When the Linux kernel processes a `chown` system call, it executes the following decision tree: ``` Step 1: Verify the calling process has CAP_CHOWN capability (i.e., effective UID = 0). IF NOT root THEN IF new_user_owner ≠ current_user_owner THEN RETURN EPERM (Operation not permitted) END IF END IF Step 2: Resolve symbolic names to numeric IDs. user_id = lookup_in_/etc/passwd(OWNER) // If OWNER is numeric, use as-is group_id = lookup_in_/etc/group(GROUP) // If GROUP is numeric, use as-is Step 3: If OWNER is empty AND colon is present (chown :group), THEN user_id = inode->i_uid (preserve current UID) Step 4: If GROUP is empty AND colon is present (chown user:), THEN group_id = lookup_primary_group(user_id) Step 5: Apply POSIX check for non-root: IF calling_euid ≠ 0 AND new_group_id ≠ current_group_id THEN IF user_id ∉ groups_of(calling_user) THEN RETURN EPERM END IF END IF Step 6: Update the inode metadata. inode->i_uid = user_id inode->i_gid = group_id inode->i_ctime = current_time() // Change time is updated Step 7: Invalidate kernel page cache entries for the inode. RETURN SUCCESS ``` ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Examples #### Example 1: Basic User Ownership Change **Problem:** Change the owner of `project.txt` to user `bob`. **Solution Walkthrough:** ``` $ ls -l project.txt -rw-r--r-- 1 alice developers 2048 Jan 10 09:00 project.txt ``` ``` $ sudo chown bob project.txt ``` **Explanation:** - `sudo` invokes root privileges (required since `alice` ≠ `bob`). - `chown bob` specifies the new user owner as `bob`. - The group `developers` remains untouched. - The mode bits `-rw-r--r--` remain untouched. **Verification:** ``` $ ls -l project.txt -rw-r--r-- 1 bob developers 2048 Jan 10 09:00 project.txt ``` Notice that only the **third column** (UID) changed; the **fourth column** (GID) is preserved. #### Example 2: Simultaneous User and Group Change **Problem:** Change the owner to `bob` and group to `admin`. **Solution Walkthrough:** ``` $ sudo chown bob:admin project.txt ``` **Explanation:** - The colon `:` acts as a delimiter between user and group specifiers. - `bob` is the new user owner. - `admin` is the new group owner. - Both UID and GID are modified in a single atomic operation. **Verification:** ``` $ ls -l project.txt -rw-r--r-- 1 bob admin 2048 Jan 10 09:00 project.txt ``` #### Example 3: Group-Only Change Using `chown` **Problem:** Change only the group of `project.txt` to `research`. **Solution Walkthrough:** ``` $ sudo chown :research project.txt ``` **Explanation:** - The leading colon with no user specifier signals "preserve current user owner." - Only the GID is updated. - The UID `bob` is preserved. **Verification:** ``` $ ls -l project.txt -rw-r--r-- 1 bob research 2048 Jan 10 09:00 project.txt ``` #### Example 4: Recursive Ownership Change on a Directory Tree **Problem:** Recursively change the owner of `/var/www/html` and all its contents to `www-data`. **Solution Walkthrough:** ``` $ sudo chown -R www-data:www-data /var/www/html ``` **Explanation:** - The `-R` flag causes the command to descend into all subdirectories. - The user and group are both set to `www-data`. - All files and directories under `/var/www/html` are affected. - The `-v` flag can be added for verbose output to monitor progress: ``` $ sudo chown -Rv www-data:www-data /var/www/html changed ownership of '/var/www/html/index.html' from root:root to www-data:www-data changed ownership of '/var/www/html/css/style.css' from root:root to www-data:www-data ... ``` #### Example 5: Numeric ID Specification **Problem:** Set the owner of `data.bin` to UID 1001 and GID 1002. **Solution Walkthrough:** ``` $ sudo chown 1001:1002 data.bin ``` **Explanation:** - Numeric IDs are resolved directly without consulting `/etc/passwd` or `/etc/group`. - This is useful in scripts where the username may not be consistent across systems. - Useful for matching ownership in containerized environments. #### Example 6: Reference-Based Change **Problem:** Make `fileB` have the same ownership as `fileA`. **Solution Walkthrough:** ``` $ ls -l fileA fileB -rw-r--r-- 1 alice devs 100 Jan 10 09:00 fileA -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 200 Jan 10 09:00 fileB $ sudo chown --reference=fileA fileB $ ls -l fileA fileB -rw-r--r-- 1 alice devs 100 Jan 10 09:00 fileA -rw-r--r-- 1 alice devs 200 Jan 10 09:00 fileB ``` **Explanation:** - The `--reference=RFILE` option copies both UID and GID from the reference file. - This is highly efficient in deployment scripts. #### Example 7: Changing Group to a User's Login Group **Problem:** Change only the group of `notes.md` to the login group of user `charlie`. **Solution Walkthrough:** ``` $ sudo chown :charlie notes.md # This changes to charlie's PRIMARY group # OR equivalently $ sudo chown charlie: notes.md ``` **Explanation:** - The trailing colon with no group name means "set the group to the specified user's primary login group." - This is resolved by looking up the user in `/etc/passwd` and retrieving the GID from the 4th field. ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Command | Scenario | Recommended Command | Reason | |---|---|---| | Change only user owner | `chown user file` | Most explicit; group preserved | | Change only group owner | `chown :group file` | More portable than `chgrp` | | Change both atomically | `chown user:group file` | Single command, fewer race conditions | | Change recursively | `chown -R user:group dir/` | Handles entire directory tree | | Use numeric IDs in scripts | `chown 1001:1002 file` | Avoids name resolution failures | | Match another file's ownership | `chown --reference=R file` | Cleanest for mirroring | | Change group of file you own | `chgrp group file` | No root needed if you are the file owner | | Affect symlink itself, not target | `chown -h user symlink` | Prevents dereference | ### 3.4 Error Analysis and Diagnostic Matrix | Error Message | Cause | Solution | |---|---|---| | `chown: changing ownership of 'X': Operation not permitted` | Non-root user attempting UID change | Use `sudo` | | `chown: invalid user: 'X'` | Username does not exist in `/etc/passwd` | Verify with `id X` | | `chown: invalid group: 'Y'` | Group name does not exist in `/etc/group` | Verify with `getent group Y` | | `chown: cannot dereference 'X': No such file or directory` | Broken symbolic link | Use `-h` or fix the link | | `chown: cannot read directory 'X': Permission denied` | Insufficient traverse permission on parent | Adjust parent directory permissions | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Ownership Resolution Flow — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[chown Command Invoked] --> B{Is Effective
UID 0 root?} B -- Yes --> C[Resolve Symbolic
Names to IDs] B -- No --> D{Changing
User Owner?} D -- Yes --> E[ERROR: EPERM
Operation Not Permitted] D -- No --> F{Is new GID in
caller's groups?} F -- Yes --> C F -- No --> G[ERROR: EPERM
Group Change Denied] C --> H{Is Target a
Symbolic Link?} H -- Yes --> I{-h flag
specified?} H -- No --> J[Proceed to
Inode Update] I -- Yes --> J I -- No --> K[Dereference Link
to Target] K --> J J --> L[Update i_uid
and i_gid in Inode] L --> M[Update ctime
Change Timestamp] M --> N[Invalidate Page
Cache Entries] N --> O[Return SUCCESS] ``` ### 4.2 Permission vs. Ownership — Conceptual Layer Model ```mermaid graph TB subgraph L1[Layer 1: Identity Layer] UID[User Owner UID] GID[Group Owner GID] end subgraph L2[Layer 2: Permission Layer] UPERM[User Permissions rwx] GPERM[Group Permissions rwx] OPERM[Other Permissions rwx] end subgraph L3[Layer 3: Special Bits Layer] SUID[Setuid Bit] SGID[Setgid Bit] STICKY[Sticky Bit] end subgraph L4[Layer 4: File Operations] READ[Read Content] WRITE[Modify Content] EXEC[Execute File] end UID --> UPERM GID --> GPERM UPERM --> READ UPERM --> WRITE UPERM --> EXEC GPERM --> READ GPERM --> WRITE GPERM --> EXEC OPERM --> READ OPERM --> WRITE OPERM --> EXEC SUID --> EXEC SGID --> EXEC STICKY --> WRITE ``` ### 4.3 Authority Hierarchy for `chown` Operations ```mermaid graph TD ROOT[Root Superuser
UID 0] --> ROOTA[Can change UID to ANY user] ROOT --> ROOTB[Can change GID to ANY group] ROOT --> ROOTC[Can use all flags freely] OWNER[File Owner
Non-root] --> OWNERA[Cannot change UID] OWNER --> OWNERB[Can change GID to groups
they are a member of] OTHERS[Other Users] --> OTHERSA[No ownership change rights] ``` ### 4.4 `chown` Syntax Decomposition Map ```mermaid graph LR A[chown] --> B[OPTIONS] A --> C[OWNER] A --> D[COLON] A --> E[GROUP] A --> F[FILE TARGETS] B --> B1[-R Recursive] B --> B2[-v Verbose] B --> B3[-h No Dereference] B --> B4[-c Changes Only] C --> C1[Username] C --> C2[UID Numeric] D --> D1[colon : delimiter] D --> D2[dot . BSD style] E --> E1[Groupname] E --> E2[GID Numeric] E --> E3[Empty sets to login group] F --> F1[Single file] F --> F2[Multiple files] F --> F3[Directory tree] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the difference between the `chown` and `chgrp` commands in Linux? Who is permitted to execute each of them?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `chown` command is used to change the **user owner** of a file or directory, and optionally the group owner, in a single operation. Its syntax is `chown [owner][:group] file(s)`. In contrast, the `chgrp` command is used to change **only the group owner** of a file or directory, with the syntax `chgrp group file(s)`. Regarding permissions: - **`chown`** for changing the **user owner** can be executed **only by the root superuser** (UID 0). Ordinary users cannot transfer ownership of their files to another user. - **`chgrp`** can be executed by **root** (to any group) or by the **file owner** (but only to a group that the file owner is a member of). - Non-owners who are not root cannot execute either command for changing ownership to a different user or unrelated group. *(Award: 1 mark for difference between commands, 1 mark for chown privilege, 1 mark for chgrp privilege)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Explain the significance of the colon `:` in the `chown` command with suitable examples.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The colon `:` in the `chown` command serves as a **delimiter** between the user owner and group owner specifications. Its placement carries specific semantic meaning: 1. **`chown alice file`** — No colon present. Changes only the user owner to `alice`; the group is preserved. 2. **`chown :devs file`** — Colon with no preceding user. Preserves the current user owner and changes the group to `devs`. Functionally equivalent to `chgrp devs file`. 3. **`chown alice:devs file`** — Colon separates both values. Changes user to `alice` and group to `devs` simultaneously. 4. **`chown alice: file`** — Trailing colon with no group specified. Changes the user to `alice` and the group to `alice`'s **primary login group** as defined in `/etc/passwd`. The colon is therefore the **critical syntactic element** that determines which combination of UID and GID changes is applied. *(Award: 1 mark for delimiter role, 1 mark for two examples, 1 mark for the trailing colon login-group nuance)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **a)** Explain the concept of file ownership in Linux. Discuss the rules governing who can change the user owner and group owner of a file. **(7 marks)** **b)** Describe the `chown` command in detail, including its syntax, common options, and the behavior with symbolic links. Provide at least four distinct examples demonstrating different use cases. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — File Ownership Concepts and Authorization Rules (7 marks):** In the Linux file system, every file and directory is associated with a specific **User Owner (UID)** and a **Group Owner (GID)**, both stored in the file's **inode** structure. This ownership triad is the foundation of the Linux **Discretionary Access Control (DAC)** model. **The Ownership Triplet:** - **User Owner (UID):** A 32-bit integer identifying a single user account. - **Group Owner (GID):** A 32-bit integer identifying a user group. - **Permission Mode:** 12 bits defining read, write, execute access for user, group, and others. **Authorization Rules for Ownership Changes:** | Rule | Applicable Actor | Permitted Action | |---|---|---| | **Rule 1** | Root (UID 0) | Can change UID to **any** user and GID to **any** group | | **Rule 2** | File Owner (non-root) | Can change GID to any group of which they are a **member** | | **Rule 3** | File Owner (non-root) | **Cannot** change UID to another user | | **Rule 4** | Any other user | Cannot change UID or GID under any circumstance | The kernel enforces these rules through the **CAP_CHOWN** capability check in the `chown()` system call. If the effective UID is non-zero and the target UID differs from the current UID, the system call returns `-EPERM`. The rationale is security: preventing users from "gifting" files to other users to evade disk quotas, hide malicious content, or create shared write-access files that escape their own accountability. *(Marks distribution: 2 marks ownership concept, 3 marks rules table, 2 marks rationale and system call reference)* --- **Part (b) — Detailed `chown` Command Analysis (7 marks):** **Formal Syntax:** ``` chown [OPTION]... [OWNER][:[GROUP]] FILE... ``` **Detailed Options:** | Option | Function | |---|---| | `-R` / `--recursive` | Recursively changes ownership of all files and subdirectories | | `-v` / `--verbose` | Prints a diagnostic for every file processed | | `-c` / `--changes` | Prints output only when an actual change occurs | | `-f` / `--silent` | Suppresses most error messages | | `-h` / `--no-dereference` | Affects symbolic links themselves, not their targets | | `--reference=RFILE` | Copies ownership from a reference file | | `--from=CURRENT` | Changes ownership only if the current owner matches | **Symbolic Link Behavior:** By default, `chown` **dereferences** symbolic links and changes the ownership of the **target file**, not the link itself. This is because the link is merely a pointer, and the actual file metadata resides with the target. To modify the link's own metadata (e.g., to prevent it from being followed), the `-h` flag must be used. This is a critical distinction in scripts that handle large symbolic-link-heavy directory trees. **Four Distinct Examples:** 1. **Change only the user owner:** ``` $ sudo chown bob report.txt ``` UID changes to `bob`; GID remains unchanged. 2. **Change only the group owner:** ``` $ sudo chown :developers report.txt ``` GID changes to `developers`; UID remains unchanged. 3. **Change both user and group recursively:** ``` $ sudo chown -R alice:admin /home/project ``` All files and directories under `/home/project` are updated atomically. 4. **Reference-based ownership copying:** ``` $ sudo chown --reference=template.conf newconfig.conf ``` `newconfig.conf` inherits the UID and GID of `template.conf` in a single operation. 5. **Numeric ID specification (for portability in scripts):** ``` $ sudo chown 1001:1002 data.bin ``` Avoids name-resolution failures across different systems with different user databases. *(Marks distribution: 2 marks syntax and options, 2 marks symbolic link behavior, 3 marks examples with explanations)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023] **a)** Compare and contrast the `chown` and `chgrp` commands. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other? Provide at least three use-case scenarios. **(7 marks)** **b)** Explain the `-R`, `-v`, `-h`, and `--reference` options of the `chown` command with practical examples. How does the command interact with hard links and symbolic links? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — `chown` vs `chgrp` Comparative Analysis (7 marks):** **Structural Comparison:** | Aspect | `chown` | `chgrp` | |---|---|---| | **Primary Function** | Change user owner and optionally group | Change only the group owner | | **Syntax** | `chown [user][:group] file` | `chgrp [group] file` | | **Who Can Run (UID change)** | Root only | N/A — does not change UID | | **Who Can Run (GID change)** | Root (any group) or file owner (member groups) | Root (any group) or file owner (member groups) | | **Atomic UID+GID Change** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (only GID) | | **Portability** | Universal across all Unix-like systems | Standardized by POSIX | **Use-Case Scenarios:** 1. **Web Server Deployment:** When deploying a website to `/var/www/html`, the system administrator needs to set ownership to the `www-data` user and group simultaneously. `chown -R www-data:www-data /var/www/html` accomplishes both changes in one command. Using `chgrp` would require a separate `chown` step for the user. 2. **Project Handoff in Collaborative Development:** When developer Alice transfers project files to developer Bob, she (or root) must use `chown bob file` because the user owner must change. `chgrp` is insufficient since it only handles groups. 3. **Group Reorganization Without Owner Change:** When reorganizing teams, the project manager may want to move all files from the `old_team` group to the `new_team` group while keeping individual file owners intact. Here, `chgrp new_team file` is cleaner and more semantically clear than `chown :new_team file`. 4. **Script Portability:** In shell scripts intended to run on diverse systems where the target username may vary but group names are standardized, `chgrp shared_group file` is preferred because group policies tend to be more stable across organizations. *(Marks distribution: 2 marks comparison table, 3 marks use-case scenarios, 2 marks preference reasoning)* --- **Part (b) — Option Deep Dive and Link Behavior (7 marks):** **Option `-R` (Recursive):** The `-R` flag causes `chown` to descend into directories and change ownership of all contained files, subdirectories, and their contents. Without `-R`, only the top-level directory's ownership changes, while the files within retain their original ownership. ``` $ sudo chown -R alice:research /data/experiment ``` **Option `-v` (Verbose):** The `-v` flag prints a line of output for every file processed, regardless of whether its ownership actually changed. This is invaluable for auditing and debugging. ``` $ sudo chown -Rv alice /data changed ownership of '/data/file1.txt' from root:root to alice:root changed ownership of '/data/file2.txt' from root:root to alice:root ownership of '/data/empty_dir' retained as root:root ``` **Option `-h` (No Dereference):** By default, when `chown` encounters a symbolic link, it modifies the **target file's** ownership, not the link itself. The `-h` flag reverses this behavior, modifying the link itself. This is particularly relevant when symbolic links point to files in restricted directories where dereferencing would fail. ``` $ sudo chown -h alice symlink_to_secret # Changes the link, not the target ``` **Option `--reference=RFILE`:** This option instructs `chown` to copy the ownership (both UID and GID) from a reference file `RFILE` and apply it to the target. It eliminates the need to know the exact UID/GID values. ``` $ sudo chown --reference=/etc/passwd /tmp/newfile ``` **Interaction with Hard Links:** A **hard link** is an alternative directory entry pointing to the **same inode** as the original file. Since both the original and the hard link share the **same inode**, any `chown` operation affects both names simultaneously — they will always display the same ownership because ownership is an **inode property**, not a directory-entry property. **Interaction with Symbolic Links:** A **symbolic (soft) link** is a special file containing a path string. It has its **own inode** with its own ownership metadata. However, by default, `chown` follows the link to its target. The `-h` flag must be used to change the symbolic link's own ownership. | Link Type | Default `chown` Behavior | Effect with `-h` | |---|---|---| | **Hard Link** | Modifies the shared inode (both names reflect change) | N/A — `-h` has no effect on hard links since they are not dereferenceable | | **Symbolic Link** | Dereferences and modifies the target | Modifies the symbolic link's own inode | *(Marks distribution: 2 marks options explanation with examples, 2 marks hard link behavior, 2 marks symbolic link behavior, 1 mark comparison table)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — `chown` Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing `chown` permission rules:** Many students incorrectly claim that "the file owner can change the user owner." This is **wrong** — only **root** can change the user owner. File owners can only change the **group** to one they belong to. This single error costs **2-3 marks** in long-answer questions. > > 2. **Forgetting the colon syntax:** Writing `chown alice devs file` (with a space) instead of `chown alice:devs file` (with a colon) causes the shell to interpret `devs` as a second filename, resulting in an error. Examiners look for the **colon syntax explicitly**. > > 3. **Ignoring symbolic link behavior:** Students often write `chown alice symlink` and expect only the link to be modified. In reality, the **target** is modified. The `-h` flag is the key term examiners search for. > > 4. **Conflating `chown` with `chmod`:** `chown` changes **ownership** (UID/GID), while `chmod` changes **permissions** (rwx). Mixing them up is a critical conceptual error. > > 5. **Not mentioning root/sudo:** Any `chown` example that changes UID must include `sudo` or state that it is executed as root. Omitting this loses 1 mark. > > 6. **Forgetting `-R` for directories:** When asked to change ownership of a "directory and all its contents," students often forget `-R` and change only the top-level directory. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Changing Ownership:** > > ✅ `chown` = Change Owner (user and/or group); `chgrp` = Change Group only. > > ✅ Only **root** can change the **user owner** of a file. > > ✅ The **file owner** (non-root) can change the **group** to a group they are members of. > > ✅ Syntax variants: `chown user file`, `chown :group file`, `chown user:group file`, `chown user: file` (login group). > > ✅ The **colon `:`** is the critical delimiter; a space instead of a colon causes syntax errors. > > ✅ `-R` = recursive (entire directory tree); `-v` = verbose; `-c` = changes only; `-h` = no-dereference (affect symlink itself). > > ✅ `--reference=RFILE` copies ownership from a reference file. > > ✅ By default, `chown` **dereferences symbolic links** and modifies the target, not the link. > > ✅ **Hard links** share an inode, so `chown` affects all hard link names simultaneously. > > ✅ Ownership and permissions are **independent**: `chown` does not change mode bits; `chmod` does not change UID/GID. > > ✅ Verify ownership with `ls -l` (columns 3 and 4) or `stat` (comprehensive metadata). > > ✅ Numeric UIDs/GIDs are interchangeable with symbolic names and are preferred in **portable scripts**. > > ✅ The kernel capability **CAP_CHOWN** is the internal authorization mechanism for UID/GID changes. > > ✅ Non-root users attempting UID change receive `EPERM` (Operation not permitted) error. > > ✅ The `chown` command updates the inode's **change time (`ctime`)** upon successful modification. --- **End of Module 3 — Changing Ownership Notes** *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Pattern*

topics.title: Sharing Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sharing Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**File Sharing in Linux** refers to the mechanism by which multiple users, processes, or networked systems are granted controlled access to files and directories. It is governed by a multi-layered permission model combining **traditional UNIX permissions**, **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**, **special permission bits (SUID, SGID, Sticky Bit)**, and **network file sharing protocols** such as **NFS (Network File System)**, **Samba (SMB/CIFS)**, **SFTP**, **SCP**, and **rsync**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, file sharing encompasses three primary domains:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEVT412 - Module 3):**
> File sharing in Linux is a **multi-tiered access control mechanism** that determines *who* (identity: user/group/others) can do *what* (read/write/execute) on a given file, and *how* that file is propagated across *local* (same host) and *remote* (networked) boundaries.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Filing Cabinet" Model

Imagine your Linux filesystem as a **large office building with thousands of locked filing cabinets** (directories and files). Every cabinet has:

- A **label on the front** (filename)
- A **nameplate on the door** (owner — usually the user who created it)
- A **department sticker** (group ownership)
- A **lock with three keys**: 🔑 **Read** (open and look), ✏️ **Write** (modify contents), ▶️ **Execute** (run as a program)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** When you `chmod 755 file.txt`, you are saying: *"The owner has all 3 keys (rwx = 7), the department members can only look (r-x = 5), and the public can also only look (r-x = 5)."* Sharing files is essentially **distributing these keys to the right people and the right departments**, and sometimes **copying the whole cabinet to another building** (network sharing).

## 1.3 The Three Layers of File Sharing

| Layer | Purpose | Example Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer 1 — Local Permissions** | Control access among users on the same host | `chmod`, `chown`, `chgrp`, `umask` |
| **Layer 2 — Advanced ACLs & Special Bits** | Granular, per-user, per-group rules | `setfacl`, `getfacl`, SUID/SGID, Sticky Bit |
| **Layer 3 — Network Sharing** | Propagate files across machines | `scp`, `rsync`, `sftp`, `nfs`, `samba` |

## 1.4 The Identity Triad: `user`, `group`, `others`

Linux identifies every file operation through three principals:

- **`u` (user / owner)** — The single user account that owns the file. Only the owner (or `root`) can change permissions.
- **`g` (group)** — A named set of users who share a common access role (e.g., `developers`, `finance`).
- **`o` (others / world)** — All other users on the system who are neither the owner nor in the owning group.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test the precise *precedence order* of permission evaluation: **owner → group → others**. If the requesting user IS the owner, ONLY the user bits are checked — the group and other bits are completely IGNORED, even if they would have granted more access.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classic UNIX Permission Triplet

Every file in Linux carries a **10-character mode string**, decomposable as:

$$M = [t_1]\,[u_3]\,[g_3]\,[o_3]$$

where:
- $t_1$ is the **file type indicator** (`-` regular file, `d` directory, `l` symlink, `c` character device, `b` block device, `p` named pipe, `s` socket)
- $u_3, g_3, o_3$ are **3-bit triplets** for user, group, and others respectively
- Each bit represents **Read (r=4)**, **Write (w=2)**, **Execute (x=1)**

### 2.1.1 The Symbolic-to-Octal Mapping

| Symbolic | Octal Value | Meaning for Files | Meaning for Directories |
|:---:|:---:|---|---|
| `r` | **4** | Read contents (`cat`, `less`) | List entries (`ls`) |
| `w` | **2** | Modify contents (`echo >`, `vi`) | Create/delete/rename entries |
| `x` | **1** | Execute as program (`./script.sh`) | Traverse (`cd` into it) |
| `-` | **0** | No permission | No access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why directories need `x` to be useful:** A directory with `r--` lets you *list* filenames but not access their inode metadata, sizes, or contents. A directory with `--x` lets you *traverse* (pass through) it by name even though you cannot list it. This is a classic KTU trick question.

## 2.2 The `chmod` Command — Two Notation Systems

### 2.2.1 Symbolic Notation
`chmod [who][operator][permission] file`

- **Who:** `u` (user), `g` (group), `o` (others), `a` (all)
- **Operator:** `+` (add), `-` (remove), `=` (set exactly)
- **Permission:** `r`, `w`, `x`, `X` (execute only if dir or already executable)

### 2.2.2 Octal (Numeric) Notation
The octal digit for each class is computed as:

$$P_{class} = 4 \cdot r + 2 \cdot w + 1 \cdot x$$

where each variable is a **binary indicator** (1 = granted, 0 = denied).

## 2.3 Special Permission Bits — The "Beyond 7" Bits

| Bit | Octal | Symbol on User/Group/Other | Purpose |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| **Set User ID (SUID)** | **4000** | user position becomes `s` | Process runs with file **owner's** UID |
| **Set Group ID (SGID)** | **2000** | group position becomes `s` | Process runs with file **group's** GID; new files in dir inherit group |
| **Sticky Bit** | **1000** | other position becomes `t` | Only owner (or `root`) can delete/rename files in dir (e.g., `/tmp`) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When SUID is set but the user execute bit is removed, the symbol becomes a capital `S` (uppercase), meaning the SUID is set but functionally useless. Same for SGID → `S` and Sticky Bit → `T` (uppercase T means bit is set but no execute).

## 2.4 Access Control Lists (ACLs) — Beyond UNIX Triplets

ACLs extend the **3-class model** to **per-user** and **per-group** granularity. Standard ACL entries:

- **`u:username:perms`** — Additional user
- **`g:groupname:perms`** — Additional group
- **`m::perms`** — The **effective rights mask** (intersection limit)
- **`o::perms`** — Others (must match the standard `o` bits if ACL is present)

Tools: `getfacl <file>` (read), `setfacl -m u:alice:rw file` (modify), `setfacl -x u:alice file` (remove), `setfacl -b file` (clear all), `setfacl -d -m u:alice:rwx dir/` (default ACL for inheritance).

## 2.5 `umask` — Default Permission Filter

When a file is created, the kernel applies:

$$P_{final} = P_{base} \ \text{AND NOT} \ \text{umask}$$

- **Files** base = `666` (no execute by default)
- **Directories** base = `777` (full access by default)

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Octal digit for one class | $P = 4r + 2w + 1x$ | $r,w,x \in \{0,1\}$ |
| 2 | Three-digit `chmod` | $\text{chmod } P_u P_g P_o$ | Each $P \in [0,7]$ |
| 3 | Four-digit `chmod` | $4000 \cdot s + 2000 \cdot s_g + 1000 \cdot t + P_u P_g P_o$ | Leading digit combines special bits |
| 4 | Default file perms | $666 \ \text{AND NOT umask}$ | Example: umask `022` → files get `644` |
| 5 | Default dir perms | $777 \ \text{AND NOT umask}$ | Example: umask `022` → dirs get `755` |
| 6 | ACL mask interaction | Effective perms = entry perms AND mask | Mask is the upper bound |
| 7 | Precedence rule | owner match → use `u` bits only | Group bits ignored for owners |
| 8 | Sticky dir deletion | `drwxrwxrwt` on `/tmp` | Only owner/`root` may delete |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DevOps teams** use SGID on shared project directories so that all new files automatically belong to the team's group.
- **Web servers** (Apache, Nginx) use SUID on helper binaries like `ping` or `mount` to allow non-root users controlled root operations.
- **Multi-tenant systems** use ACLs to grant a specific contractor access to exactly one directory without creating a new group.
- **Cloud infrastructure** uses `rsync` over SSH for fast, incremental backups and deployments across geographically distributed servers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting Symbolic `rwx` to Octal

**Problem:** Convert the symbolic string `rwxr-x---` into its octal `chmod` equivalent.

**Step 1 — Decompose the 9 permission characters into three triplets:**

$$\underbrace{r\,w\,x}_{user} \quad \underbrace{r{-}x}_{group} \quad \underbrace{---}_{others}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula $P = 4r + 2w + 1x$ to each class:**

For the **user** class:
$$P_u = 4(1) + 2(1) + 1(1) = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7$$

For the **group** class:
$$P_g = 4(1) + 2(0) + 1(1) = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5$$

For the **others** class:
$$P_o = 4(0) + 2(0) + 1(0) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Concatenate the three octal digits:**

$$\text{chmod value} = 750$$

**Step 4 — Verification using binary representation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
7_{10} &= 111_2 = \text{rwx} \\
5_{10} &= 101_2 = \text{r-x} \\
0_{10} &= 000_2 = \text{---}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\therefore \texttt{chmod 750 file.txt} \equiv \texttt{chmod u=rwx,g=rx,o= file.txt}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Computing Final Permissions with `umask`

**Problem:** A user has `umask = 027`. What permissions will a newly created **file** and **directory** receive?

**Step 1 — Write umask in binary for clarity:**

$$\text{umask} = 027 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 2 & 7 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 000 & 010 & 111 \end{pmatrix}_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply to file base permissions (666):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base (file)} &= 110\,110\,110_2 = 666_8 \\
\text{NOT umask}  &= 111\,101\,000_2 \\
\text{Final}      &= 110\,101\,000_2 = 111\,101\,000_2 \ \text{AND}\ 110\,110\,110_2 \\
                  &= 110\,100\,000_2 = 640_8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to directory base permissions (777):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base (dir)} &= 111\,111\,111_2 = 777_8 \\
\text{Final}      &= 111\,111\,111 \ \text{AND}\ 111\,101\,000 = 111\,101\,000_2 = 750_8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

> [!NOTE]
> With `umask 027`: new files → **`-rw-r-----` (640)**, new directories → **`drwxr-x---` (750)**. This is the standard umask for a privacy-conscious user account.

## 3.3 Derivation: Four-Digit `chmod` with Special Bits

**Problem:** Set the SGID bit on a directory, with permissions `rwxr-s---` for owner and group, and no access for others. Express as a `chmod` numeric value.

**Step 1 — Identify special bit:** SGID = **2000**

**Step 2 — Compute user octal:**

$$P_u = 4(1) + 2(1) + 1(1) = 7$$

**Step 3 — Compute group octal (note the `s` replaces `x` but counts as execute for arithmetic):**

$$P_g = 4(1) + 2(0) + 1(1) = 5$$

**Step 4 — Compute others octal:**

$$P_o = 0$$

**Step 5 — Construct the four-digit command:**

$$\texttt{chmod 2750 shared\_dir/}$$

**Verification with `ls -ld`:**

```
drwxr-s---  2 root developers 4096 Jun 10 10:30 shared_dir/
```

The `s` in the group execute position confirms SGID is active — any file created inside `shared_dir/` will automatically inherit the `developers` group ownership.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right File-Sharing Tool

| Scenario | Best Tool | Command Sketch | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quick one-off copy to remote host | `scp` | `scp file.txt user@host:/path/` | Simple, secure (SSH-based) |
| Recursive directory sync with delta | `rsync` | `rsync -avz src/ user@host:/dst/` | Only transfers changes; resumable |
| Edit files on remote server interactively | `sftp` | `sftp user@host` | FTP-like interface over SSH |
| Mount remote filesystem transparently | `NFS` | `mount -t nfs server:/share /mnt` | Appears as local disk; Unix-native |
| Share with Windows machines | `Samba` | `smbclient //server/share` | Implements SMB/CIFS protocol |
| Anonymous public downloads | `vsftpd` / `proftpd` | `ftp ftp.server.com` | Plain FTP (insecure — use only for public data) |

## 3.5 Derivation: ACL Mask Truncation

**Problem:** A file has ACL entries `user:alice:rw-` and `user:bob:rwx`, with mask `r--`. What are Alice's and Bob's **effective** permissions?

**Rule:** Effective permission = entry permission $\text{AND}$ mask

**Step 1 — Alice:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Entry} &= 110_2 = \text{rw-} \\
\text{Mask}  &= 100_2 = \text{r--} \\
\text{AND}   &= 100_2 = \text{r--}
\end{aligned}
$$

Alice's effective permission is **`r--`** (read only).

**Step 2 — Bob:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Entry} &= 111_2 = \text{rwx} \\
\text{Mask}  &= 100_2 = \text{r--} \\
\text{AND}   &= 100_2 = \text{r--}
\end{aligned}
$$

Bob's effective permission is also **`r--`**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Students write the *named* permissions (`rw-`, `rwx`) as the effective permissions. The mask **always** intersects and may downgrade. Always re-compute the effective rights from the mask.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: The UNIX File Permission Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Linux File Permission Model"]:::root --> B["File Type Bit t1"]:::lvl1
    A --> C["Owner u: 3 bits rwx"]:::lvl1
    A --> D["Group g: 3 bits rwx"]:::lvl1
    A --> E["Others o: 3 bits rwx"]:::lvl1

    C --> C1["Read r = 4"]:::leaf
    C --> C2["Write w = 2"]:::leaf
    C --> C3["Execute x = 1"]:::leaf

    D --> D1["Read r = 4"]:::leaf
    D --> D2["Write w = 2"]:::leaf
    D --> D3["Execute x = 1"]:::leaf

    E --> E1["Read r = 4"]:::leaf
    E --> E2["Write w = 2"]:::leaf
    E --> E3["Execute x = 1"]:::leaf

    A --> F["Special Bits Layer"]:::lvl1
    F --> F1["SUID = 4000"]:::leaf
    F --> F2["SGID = 2000"]:::leaf
    F --> F3["Sticky = 1000"]:::leaf

    A --> G["ACL Extension Layer"]:::lvl1
    G --> G1["Named User u:alice"]:::leaf
    G --> G2["Named Group g:devs"]:::leaf
    G --> G3["Effective Mask m"]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lvl1 fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef leaf fill:#dbeafe,color:#0f172a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Diagram: Permission Evaluation Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["User requests access to file"]:::start --> P2{"Is user the file owner?"}:::decide
    P2 -- "Yes" --> P3["Apply USER bits u"]:::apply
    P2 -- "No" --> P4{"Is user in file's group?"}:::decide
    P4 -- "Yes" --> P5["Apply GROUP bits g"]:::apply
    P4 -- "No" --> P6["Apply OTHERS bits o"]:::apply
    P3 --> P7["Access Granted or Denied"]:::endnode
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7

    P1 --> ACL1{"ACL present?"}:::decide
    ACL1 -- "Yes" --> ACL2["Check named user entries first"]:::apply
    ACL2 --> ACL3["Intersect with mask m"]:::apply
    ACL3 --> P7
    ACL1 -- "No" --> P2

    classDef start fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decide fill:#facc15,color:#0f172a,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef apply fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef endnode fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Multi-Tier File-Sharing Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Tier 1: Local Permissions
chmod / chown / chgrp / umask"]:::t1
    L2["Tier 2: Advanced Controls
setfacl / getfacl / SUID / SGID / Sticky"]:::t2
    L3["Tier 3: Secure Transfer
scp / sftp / rsync over SSH"]:::t3
    L4["Tier 4: Network File Systems
NFS / Samba SMB / FTP"]:::t4
    L5["Tier 5: Application Layer
WebDAV / Cloud Sync APIs"]:::t5

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5

    classDef t1 fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef t2 fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef t3 fill:#7dd3fc,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef t4 fill:#38bdf8,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef t5 fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Diagram: `umask` Filter Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Base Permissions
Files 666 / Dirs 777"]:::inbox
    B["User umask
e.g. 022"]:::mask
    C["Final Permissions
via AND NOT"]:::outbox

    A --> C
    B --> C

    C --> D["Files: 644 rw-r--r--"]:::out
    C --> E["Dirs: 755 rwxr-xr-x"]:::out

    classDef inbox fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#451a03
    classDef mask fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#450a0a
    classDef outbox fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#052e16
    classDef out fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#052e16
```

flowchart TD
    S1["Source Host
/src/data/"]:::src
    S2["rsync algorithm
compares checksums"]:::logic
    S3["Detects delta
only changed blocks"]:::logic
    S4["Compresses with -z
encrypts via SSH"]:::logic
    S5["Transmits delta
over network"]:::logic
    S6["Destination Host
/backup/data/"]:::dst
    S7["Reconstructs full file
from base + delta"]:::logic

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7
