<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the three special permission bits in Linux. Write the symbolic and octal representation of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The three special permission bits in Linux extend the basic rwx model:

1. **SUID (Set User ID)** — Octal value **4000**; symbolically shown as `s` in the user-execute position. When set on an executable file, the process runs with the **file owner's** UID, not the invoking user's.

2. **SGID (Set Group ID)** — Octal value **2000**; symbolically shown as `s` in the group-execute position. On a directory, it forces all newly created files to inherit the directory's group ownership, enabling collaborative team folders.

3. **Sticky Bit** — Octal value **1000**; symbolically shown as `t` in the other-execute position. When set on a directory (e.g., `/tmp`), only the file owner, directory owner, or `root` can delete or rename files within — even if others have write permission.

A combined four-digit `chmod` such as `chmod 4755` sets SUID + standard `755`.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between `scp`, `sftp`, and `rsync`. State one typical use case for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Tool | Protocol | Key Feature | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `scp` | SSH | Simple one-shot secure copy; non-resumable | Quickly pushing a single config file to a remote server |
| `sftp` | SSH | Interactive FTP-like session over SSH; supports resume and directory listing | Editing/managing remote files interactively during deployment |
| `rsync` | SSH (or native) | Delta-transfer algorithm; only sends changed blocks; supports resume, compression, dry-run | Nightly incremental backups of large directory trees |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the UNIX file permission model in detail, including the three identity classes, the three permission types, the symbolic and octal representations, and the file-type indicator. Illustrate with one example of `ls -l` output.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A project team has 5 developers. The team lead wants to create a shared directory `/home/team/projects/` where:

  1. The directory owner (`lead`) has full access (`rwx`).
  2. All team members (in group `devs`) can read, write, and traverse the directory.
  3. Any new file/folder created inside automatically belongs to the `devs` group.
  4. Other users should not be able to enter the directory at all.
  5. Even team members must NOT be able to delete each other's files.

Write the complete sequence of Linux commands to achieve this. Explain the role of each special bit used.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

##### Part (a) — UNIX File Permission Model

The UNIX file permission model is a **10-character mode string** displayed by `ls -l`, structured as:

$$M = [\text{type}]\,[\text{user}_{rwx}]\,[\text{group}_{rwx}]\,[\text{other}_{rwx}]$$

**File Type Indicator (1 char):**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `-` | Regular file |
| `d` | Directory |
| `l` | Symbolic link |
| `c` / `b` | Character / Block device |
| `p` | Named pipe (FIFO) |
| `s` | Unix domain socket |

**Three Identity Classes (3 chars each):**

- **`u` (user)** — the single owning user account
- **`g` (group)** — the owning group
- **`o` (others / world)** — everyone else on the system

**Three Permission Bits per Class:**

- **`r` (read, octal 4)** — open and view contents
- **`w` (write, octal 2)** — modify contents
- **`x` (execute, octal 1)** — execute as a program (or `cd` into, for directories)

**Octal Conversion Rule:**

$$P_{class} = 4 \cdot r + 2 \cdot w + 1 \cdot x$$

**Worked Example:**

```
-rwxr-x--x  1 alice devs  2048 Jun 10 10:00 script.sh
```

Decomposition:

- `-` → regular file
- `rwx` (7) → owner `alice`: read, write, execute
- `r-x` (5) → group `devs`: read and execute
- `--x` (1) → others: execute only

Octal equivalent: **`751`**, applied via `chmod 751 script.sh`.

**Precedence Rule:** The kernel checks the **owner's bits first**. If the requesting user IS the owner, ONLY the user bits apply — the group and other bits are ignored, even if they would have granted more access.

##### Part (b) — Shared Project Directory Commands

**Step 1 — Create the directory (as `root` or `lead`):**

```bash
sudo mkdir -p /home/team/projects
```

**Step 2 — Set ownership: owner = `lead`, group = `devs`:**

```bash
sudo chown lead:devs /home/team/projects
```

**Step 3 — Apply SGID + standard permissions in a single command:**

```bash
sudo chmod 2770 /home/team/projects
```

Here:
- Leading `2` activates **SGID** → new files inherit the `devs` group automatically.
- `7` (user `lead`) = `rwx` → full access.
- `7` (group `devs`) = `rwx` → read, write, traverse.
- `0` (others) = `---` → no access, cannot even `cd` into it.

**Step 4 — Add the Sticky Bit to protect inter-user files:**

```bash
sudo chmod +t /home/team/projects
```

This adds the `1000` octal, making the final mode `3770` — shown as `drwxrws--T` in `ls -ld`. Even though team members have `rwx`, the sticky bit ensures **a developer cannot delete or rename another developer's files**, only their own.

**Step 5 — Add all developers to the `devs` group:**

```bash
sudo usermod -aG devs dev1
sudo usermod -aG devs dev2
sudo usermod -aG devs dev3
sudo usermod -aG devs dev4
sudo usermod -aG devs dev5
```

**Step 6 — Verify with:**

```bash
ls -ld /home/team/projects
```

Expected output:

```
drwxrws--T  2 lead devs 4096 Jun 10 10:30 /home/team/projects
```

**Role of each special bit:**

- **SGID (`2`)**: Forces group inheritance — any new file becomes `group=devs` regardless of who creates it, so all team members can read/write each other's files.
- **Sticky Bit (`+t` / `1000`)**: Prevents accidental deletion of colleagues' files, even with full group write access. Essential for collaborative directories.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are Access Control Lists (ACLs) in Linux? Explain `getfacl` and `setfacl` commands with at least three distinct examples covering named users, named groups, and default ACLs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare NFS and Samba as Linux file-sharing protocols. Write the configuration steps to share a directory `/srv/shared` via NFS to a client `192.168.1.50`, and explain the role of `/etc/exports`.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

##### Part (a) — Access Control Lists (ACLs)

**Definition:** ACLs (Access Control Lists) extend the standard UNIX `user/group/others` model to allow **per-user** and **per-group** permission entries on a single file or directory. They are implemented in Linux via the `acl` package and the `setfacl` / `getfacl` commands.

**Why ACLs?** Standard UNIX permissions force you to choose *one* group per file. If `alice`, `bob`, and `carol` each need different access — and they are not in the same group — plain `chmod` is insufficient. ACLs solve this.

**Tool 1: `getfacl`** — Reads the ACL of a file.

```bash
getfacl /home/lead/report.txt
```

Sample output:

```
# file: report.txt
# owner: lead
# group: devs
user::rw-
user:alice:rw-
group::r--
group:auditors:r--
mask::rw-
other::---
```

**Tool 2: `setfacl`** — Modifies the ACL.

**Example 1 — Grant named user `alice` read+write access:**

```bash
setfacl -m u:alice:rw /home/lead/report.txt
```

The `-m` flag means "modify". The syntax is `type:name:permissions`.

**Example 2 — Grant named group `auditors` read-only access:**

```bash
setfacl -m g:auditors:r /home/lead/report.txt
```

**Example 3 — Set a default ACL on a directory so all new files inherit permissions:**

```bash
setfacl -d -m u:alice:rw /home/lead/projects/
setfacl -d -m g:devs:rwx /home/lead/projects/
```

The `-d` flag makes the ACL a **default ACL** — it propagates to any new file or subdirectory created within `projects/`.

**Example 4 — Remove a specific entry:**

```bash
setfacl -x u:alice /home/lead/report.txt
```

**Example 5 — Clear all ACLs and revert to standard UNIX permissions:**

```bash
setfacl -b /home/lead/report.txt
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The ACL Mask Rule:** When an ACL contains named users or named groups, the kernel computes a `mask` field. **The effective permission of any named entry is `entry_perms AND mask_perms`.** Always re-check the mask when permissions seem to fail.

##### Part (b) — NFS vs Samba, and NFS Configuration

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | NFS (Network File System) | Samba (SMB/CIFS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Native platform** | UNIX / Linux | Windows (also supports Linux) |
| **Protocol** | Sun RPC / NFSv3, NFSv4 | Microsoft's SMB/CIFS |
| **Authentication** | UNIX UIDs/GIDs; Kerberos in v4 | Username/password; integrates with Active Directory |
| **Performance** | High on Linux-Linux | Good across mixed OS environments |
| **Use case** | Linux server → Linux clients | Sharing files with Windows desktops |
| **Configuration file** | `/etc/exports` (server), `/etc/fstab` (client) | `/etc/samba/smb.conf` |
| **Default port** | 2049 (TCP) | 445 (TCP) |

**Step-by-Step NFS Configuration to share `/srv/shared` with client `192.168.1.50`:**

**Step 1 — Install the NFS server package:**

```bash
sudo apt install nfs-kernel-server
```

**Step 2 — Create and populate the shared directory:**

```bash
sudo mkdir -p /srv/shared
sudo chown nobody:nogroup /srv/shared
sudo chmod 755 /srv/shared
echo "Welcome to the NFS share" | sudo tee /srv/shared/readme.txt
```

**Step 3 — Edit `/etc/exports` to declare the share:**

```bash
sudo nano /etc/exports
```

Append the following line:

```
/srv/shared  192.168.1.50(rw,sync,no_subtree_check)
```

**Explanation of options in `/etc/exports`:**

- `/srv/shared` — the directory to export
- `192.168.1.50` — the single permitted client (use `*` or a subnet `192.168.1.0/24` to widen)
- `rw` — read-write access (vs `ro` read-only)
- `sync` — replies only after changes are flushed to disk (safer)
- `no_subtree_check` — disables a slow security check; recommended for simple shares

**Step 4 — Apply the export and start the NFS service:**

```bash
sudo exportfs -a
sudo systemctl restart nfs-kernel-server
```

**Step 5 — On the client (`192.168.1.50`), mount the share:**

```bash
sudo mkdir -p /mnt/nfs_shared
sudo mount 192.168.1.10:/srv/shared /mnt/nfs_shared
```

**Step 6 — Verify the mount:**

```bash
mount | grep nfs
ls -l /mnt/nfs_shared
```

**Step 7 — Make the mount persistent across reboots (add to `/etc/fstab` on the client):**

```
192.168.1.10:/srv/shared  /mnt/nfs_shared  nfs  defaults,_netdev  0  0
```

The `_netdev` option tells the system to wait for the network to come up before attempting the mount.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Module 3 — "Sharing Files":**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the precedence rule** — stating that group permissions apply to the owner. The owner check is *exclusive*.
> 2. **Mixing SUID and SGID numeric positions** — SUID is the *user* bit (4000), SGID is the *group* bit (2000). Examiners deduct 1 mark for swapping them.
> 3. **Ignoring the umask direction** — confusing `P_final = P_base AND NOT umask` with `OR`. Always remember umask *removes* bits.
> 4. **Omitting ACL mask effects** — writing named-user permissions verbatim without intersecting with the mask.
> 5. **Confusing `scp` with `rcp`** — `scp` is SSH-based and secure; `rcp` is insecure Berkeley r-tools, deprecated. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 6. **Writing `/etc/exports` syntax without parentheses** — the client host and its option list must be in parentheses with no space, e.g., `client(rw,sync)`.
> 7. **Using plain FTP in security-sensitive contexts** — always justify SFTP/SSH-based alternatives.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Sharing Files" — KTU Module 3:**

- **Permission mode string** is 10 characters: 1 type + 3×3 permission bits.
- **Octal digit formula:** $P = 4r + 2w + 1x$. Use `|` in LaTeX as `\vert` inside tables to avoid breaking markdown.
- **Precedence:** owner match → user bits; otherwise group membership → group bits; else others.
- **SUID = 4000, SGID = 2000, Sticky = 1000.** Combine in 4-digit `chmod`.
- **SGID on a directory** = new files inherit the directory's group — the foundation of team collaboration.
- **Sticky bit on a directory** = users can only delete *their own* files — protects shared writable directories like `/tmp`.
- **Uppercase `S` or `T`** means the special bit is set but the underlying `x` is missing — the special bit is functionally dead.
- **`umask` formula:** `final = base AND NOT umask`. File base = 666, directory base = 777.
- **ACLs use `setfacl -m` to add, `-x` to remove, `-b` to clear, `-d` to make default.** Always check the `mask` field.
- **`scp`** = secure one-shot copy, **`sftp`** = interactive SSH file transfer, **`rsync`** = delta-sync for efficiency.
- **NFS** = UNIX-native file sharing, configured via `/etc/exports` on the server and mounted via `/etc/fstab` on the client.
- **Samba** = SMB/CIFS protocol for cross-platform (Linux ↔ Windows) file sharing, configured via `/etc/samba/smb.conf`.
- **Default ports to remember:** NFS → **2049/TCP**, Samba → **445/TCP**, SSH/SFTP/SCP → **22/TCP**, FTP → **21/TCP** (insecure).
- **Practical command trio:** `chown` (change owner), `chgrp` (change group), `chmod` (change mode). Remember the order: `chown user:group file`.
- **Group management:** `usermod -aG groupname username` adds a user to a *supplementary* group; the `-a` (append) is critical to avoid removing existing memberships.
- **Always test with `ls -l`** (files) and `ls -ld`** (directories) to visually confirm the final mode string.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to vim Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to vim Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vim** (an acronym for **Vi IMproved**) is a highly configurable, modal, screen-oriented text editor originally created by **Bram Moolenaar** in **1991** as an enhanced, free-software clone of the Unix `vi` editor developed by **Bill Joy** in **1976**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, vim is presented as a powerful command-line based editor that ships by default with nearly every major Linux distribution and is engineered to enable rapid, keyboard-driven text manipulation without ever requiring the user to leave the home row of the keyboard.

Formally, vim is classified as a **modal editor** — meaning that the meaning of every keystroke changes depending on which **mode** the editor is currently in. This is the single most important conceptual property of vim and the one that distinguishes it from non-modal editors such as `nano`, `gedit`, or `notepad`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, the term *"vim Features"* specifically refers to the **modal editing paradigm**, **command/normal mode navigation**, **visual selection**, **search and replace**, **multi-window/multi-buffer file handling**, **ex-mode commands**, and **user customization via `.vimrc`**. Students are expected to be able to launch vim, switch between modes, perform basic editing, save and quit, and explain the feature set.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart-Pen" Metaphor

Imagine a normal pen: every time you touch paper with it, it leaves ink. That is exactly how a non-modal editor behaves — every key you press *immediately* types a character. Now imagine a **smart-pen** that has three physical switches on its side:

- **Switch A — "Write"**: keys type letters into the document.
- **Switch B — "Direct"**: keys move a cursor around the page, select words, delete chunks, copy/paste.
- **Switch C — "Command"**: keys issue high-level instructions such as *"find the word `kernel` and replace it with `module`"*, *"split the page in two"*, *"save and close"*.

Vim is precisely this smart-pen. The "switches" are the **modes** of vim, and the most important one is the default state you enter the editor in: **Normal Mode** — where the keys do NOT type text, but instead drive a *language* of editing commands. Once this mental model clicks, vim stops feeling like an obstacle and starts feeling like a *second language* for editing text at the speed of thought.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** System administrators editing `/etc/fstab` or `/var/log/messages` over an SSH session with no GUI, kernel developers writing C patches, and DevOps engineers editing `Dockerfile`, `nginx.conf`, or YAML manifests in CI pipelines all depend on vim. On a remote server with no display, vim is often the *only* editor available, making it a non-negotiable skill in the KTU Linux curriculum.

### 1.3 Why vim is the *de facto* Linux Editor

The following properties justify vim's continued dominance in the Linux ecosystem and its inclusion in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **Ubiquity** — preinstalled on virtually every Linux/Unix system (and on macOS by default).
- **Lightweight** — runs identically on a 1990s terminal and on a modern TTY.
- **Composability** — every editing operation is a *verb + noun* (e.g., `d2w` = delete 2 words).
- **Extensibility** — supports scripting in vimscript and integration with languages like Python, Lua, Perl.
- **Reproducibility** — all editing actions can be expressed as a sequence of keystrokes that can be recorded, replayed, and shared.
- **Modal efficiency** — once internalized, the user can edit code at near-synaptic latency because the most-used keys (`h`, `j`, `k`, `l`, `w`, `b`, `i`, `a`, `d`, `y`, `p`, `:`) are all on the home row.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Modal Editing Paradigm — The Heart of vim

A *mode* in vim is a distinct **input interpretation context**. The same physical key, `d`, performs three entirely different actions depending on the active mode:

$$
\text{Key } d \;\longmapsto\;
\begin{cases}
\text{Insert "d" character into buffer} & \text{if mode = Insert} \\[4pt]
\text{Delete the character under the cursor (operator pending)} & \text{if mode = Normal} \\[4pt]
\text{Mark the "d" boundary of a visual selection} & \text{if mode = Visual}
\end{cases}
$$

Vim ships with **seven primary modes**, though students in the KTU Module 4 scope are only required to master the first four:

1. **Normal Mode (default)** — the *command* mode; used for navigation and operators.
2. **Insert Mode** — keys insert literal text; this is where "typing" happens.
3. **Visual Mode** — used to make selections by moving the cursor.
4. **Command-Line / Ex Mode** — invoked with `:` for file operations, search/replace, settings.
5. **Replace Mode** — invoked with `R`; overwrites existing characters.
6. **Virtual Replace Mode** — treats tab characters as virtual columns.
7. **Select Mode** — similar to Visual but behaves like a Windows-style selection.

### 2.2 Mode-Transition Logic

The transitions between the four core modes form a finite state machine. The KTU board expects students to draw or describe this FSM. The following table captures every transition trigger:

| Current Mode | Key / Trigger | Resulting Mode | Purpose of Transition |
|--------------|---------------|----------------|------------------------|
| Normal       | `i`           | Insert         | Begin typing before cursor |
| Normal       | `a`           | Insert         | Begin typing after cursor |
| Normal       | `o`           | Insert         | Open new line below and type |
| Normal       | `O`           | Insert         | Open new line above and type |
| Normal       | `v`           | Visual         | Begin character-wise selection |
| Normal       | `V`           | Visual-Line    | Begin line-wise selection |
| Normal       | `<C-v>`       | Visual-Block   | Begin column-wise block selection |
| Normal       | `:`           | Command-Line   | Issue an Ex command |
| Normal       | `R`           | Replace        | Begin overwriting text |
| Normal       | `r<char>`     | Normal         | Replace exactly one char, stay in Normal |
| Insert       | `<Esc>`       | Normal         | Return to command layer |
| Visual       | `:`           | Command-Line   | Apply Ex command to selection |
| Visual       | `d`, `y`, `c` | Normal        | Operate on the selection |
| Command-Line | `<Enter>`     | Normal         | Execute the command |
| Command-Line | `<Esc>`       | Normal         | Cancel the command |

> [!NOTE]
> `<C-v>` above means **Ctrl + v**. The angle-bracket notation is the standard vim-help way of writing control keys.

### 2.3 The Operator–Motion Grammar

In Normal Mode, the most powerful concept is that an **operator** (a verb) and a **motion** (a noun) combine. The general grammar is:

$$
\text{Editing Action} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{[count]}}_{\text{optional multiplier}} \;\cdot\; \underbrace{\text{operator}}_{\text{d, c, y, >, <, !, =, gq, gu, gU, g?}} \;\cdot\; \underbrace{\text{motion}}_{\text{h,j,k,l,w,b,e,W,B,E,0,\^,\$,gg,G,f,t,F,T,/}}
$$

This is the *single most exam-relevant concept* in Module 4. KTU repeatedly tests it.

**KTU Reference Sheet — Core Operators (Verbs)**

| Operator | Name          | Effect on Motion Target                    |
|----------|---------------|--------------------------------------------|
| `d`      | delete        | Cut to unnamed register                    |
| `c`      | change        | Delete then enter Insert mode              |
| `y`      | yank          | Copy to register (does not delete)         |
| `>`      | indent right  | Shift text right by one `shiftwidth`       |
| `<`      | indent left   | Shift text left by one `shiftwidth`        |
| `=`      | auto-indent   | Re-indent per `indentexpr`                 |
| `gu`     | lowercase     | Force to lower case                        |
| `gU`     | uppercase     | Force to upper case                        |
| `g~`     | toggle case   | Swap case of every letter                  |
| `!`     | filter        | Pipe motion through external command       |

**KTU Reference Sheet — Core Motions (Nouns)**

| Motion | Meaning                                    |
|--------|--------------------------------------------|
| `h`    | one character left                         |
| `j`    | one line down                              |
| `k`    | one line up                                |
| `l`    | one character right                        |
| `w`    | forward to start of next word              |
| `b`    | backward to start of current/prev word     |
| `e`    | forward to end of current/next word        |
| `0`    | start of current line (column 0)           |
| `^`    | first non-blank char of current line       |
| `$`    | end of current line                        |
| `gg`   | first line of file                         |
| `G`     | last line of file                          |
| `f<x>`  | forward to next char `x` on current line   |
| `t<x>`  | forward to *just before* char `x`          |
| `/pat`  | forward to next match of pattern           |
| `n`     | repeat last `/` or `?` motion              |

### 2.4 Composed Examples (Operator + Motion)

| Keystrokes | Decomposition                     | Net Effect                                    |
|------------|-----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| `dw`       | delete + word                     | Delete from cursor to start of next word      |
| `d2w`      | [count=2] + delete + word         | Delete two words                              |
| `y$`       | yank + to-end-of-line             | Copy from cursor to end of line               |
| `c3e`      | [count=3] + change + end-of-word  | Change three words, drop into Insert mode     |
| `>ip`      | indent + inner paragraph          | Indent the whole current paragraph            |
| `gUit`     | uppercase + inner tag             | Uppercase the text inside an HTML tag         |

> [!TIP]
> The phrase **"Operator + Motion"** is the *answer* to a huge number of KTU 14-mark questions. Memorize the table above verbatim and you will pick up easy marks.

### 2.5 Ex-Mode Command Catalogue (the `:` commands)

When you press `:` from Normal mode, vim drops to the bottom of the screen and waits for a command. These commands cover file I/O, search/replace, settings, and window control.

| Command                       | Purpose                                            |
|-------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------|
| `:w`                          | Save (write) the current buffer                    |
| `:q`                          | Quit (fails if unsaved changes)                    |
| `:wq`  or  `:x`               | Save and quit                                      |
| `:q!`                         | Quit, **discarding** all unsaved changes           |
| `:w newfile.txt`              | Save buffer to a new filename                      |
| `:e filename`                 | Edit a file (load it into current buffer)          |
| `:r filename`                 | Read file contents *into* the current buffer       |
| `:set nu` / `:set nonu`       | Toggle line numbers                                |
| `:set ic` / `:set noic`       | Toggle ignore-case in searches                     |
| `:%s/old/new/g`               | Substitute globally in the whole file              |
| `:%s/old/new/gc`              | Substitute globally with confirm prompt            |
| `:5,10s/old/new/g`            | Substitute only on lines 5 through 10              |
| `:sp filename`                | Horizontal split with another file                 |
| `:vsp filename`               | Vertical split with another file                   |
| `:!command`                   | Run a shell command without leaving vim            |
| `:help topic`                 | Open vim's built-in help (press `q` to close)     |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The substitution form `:%s/pattern/replacement/flags` is the most-tested Ex command. The four flags are:
> - `g` — replace **g**lobally on every line (not just the first match per line).
> - `c` — **c**onfirm every replacement interactively.
> - `i` — case-**i**nsensitive match.
> - `e` — suppress **e**rrors when the pattern is not found.
> Flags may be combined, e.g. `:s/foo/bar/gci`.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain                       | Why vim is used                                                          |
|------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| System Administration        | Editing `/etc/passwd`, `crontab -e`, sshd config on headless servers     |
| Software Development         | Writing C, Python, Go, Rust source code with syntax highlighting         |
| DevOps / SRE                 | Patching `Dockerfile`, `k8s` YAML, `Ansible` playbooks in pipelines      |
| Embedded / Kernel work       | Editing `Kconfig`, `Makefile`, device-tree `.dts` files                  |
| CTF / Forensics              | `xxd` integration, binary inspection, hex-edit mode                      |
| Academic writing             | Markdown editing with live preview plugins                               |
| Shell scripting              | The very subject of this KTU course (PEEVT412)                           |

## 3. Step-by-Step Workflows & Analytical Walkthroughs

This section walks through the *exact* sequence of keystrokes required to perform the canonical vim operations a KTU 2024 student is expected to demonstrate in a lab or theory exam. Every keypress is broken down to its logical step so the workflow can be reproduced on paper.

### 3.1 Workflow A — Launching vim, Writing Text, Saving, and Quitting

**Scenario:** A student must create a file called `hello.sh` containing the line `echo Hello KTU` and exit vim cleanly.

**Step 1 — Launch vim with a filename**

$$
\text{Terminal prompt: } \texttt{\$ vim hello.sh}
$$

- The shell forks `vim` and passes `hello.sh` as an argument.
- vim opens the file in a *buffer*. Since the file does not yet exist, the buffer is empty and is in **Normal Mode**.

**Step 2 — Confirm you are in Normal Mode**

If you have just entered vim, you are guaranteed to be in Normal Mode. If unsure, press the `<Esc>` key once or twice — pressing `<Esc>` in Normal mode is a no-op and is the canonical *"go home"* gesture.

**Step 3 — Enter Insert Mode**

Press `i`. The mode indicator at the bottom of the screen changes from `-- NORMAL --` to `-- INSERT --`. Now keystrokes are literal characters.

**Step 4 — Type the content**

Type the characters exactly as you would in any editor:

```
echo Hello KTU
```

**Step 5 — Return to Normal Mode**

Press `<Esc>`. The mode indicator flips back to `-- NORMAL --`. The cursor is positioned on the last character typed.

**Step 6 — Save and quit**

The full command is two characters: `:wq`.

| Key | Effect                                                          |
|-----|-----------------------------------------------------------------|
| `:` | Opens the command-line area at the bottom; enters Ex mode       |
| `w` | Issue the `write` Ex command — flushes the buffer to disk       |
| `q` | Issue the `quit` Ex command — closes the buffer and exits vim   |
| `<Enter>` | Executes the composed command `:wq`                       |

If, during typing, you realize you want to discard everything and quit, use `:q!` — the `!` forces the quit, bypassing the "unsaved changes" guard.

### 3.2 Workflow B — Navigating a File Using Motions

**Scenario:** A 50-line file `server.cfg` is open in vim. The cursor is on line 1, column 1. Move the cursor to line 30, then to the start of the next word.

| Step | Keystroke | Decomposition                                  | Resulting cursor position          |
|------|-----------|------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| 1    | `5j`      | [count=5] × motion `j`                         | Line 6, same column                |
| 2    | `24G`     | jump to line 24 (count on `G` = absolute line) | Line 24, column 1                  |
| 3    | `gg`      | go to first line                               | Line 1, column 1                   |
| 4    | `G`       | go to last line (no count)                     | Line 50, column 1                  |
| 5    | `30G`     | jump to line 30                                | Line 30, column 1                  |
| 6    | `w`       | forward to start of next word                  | Line 30, first char of next word   |
| 7    | `b`       | backward to start of current/prev word         | Line 30, start of the word before  |
| 8    | `$`       | to end of line                                 | Line 30, last non-blank char       |
| 9    | `^`       | to first non-blank char                        | Line 30, first non-blank char      |
| 10   | `H`       | cursor to **H**ighest line on screen           | Topmost visible line               |
| 11   | `L`       | cursor to **L**owest line on screen            | Bottom-most visible line           |
| 12   | `M`       | cursor to **M**iddle line on screen            | Middle visible line                |
| 13   | `<C-d>`   | **Ctrl-D**: half-page **d**own                 | 12 lines lower (or half-screen)    |
| 14   | `<C-u>`   | **Ctrl-U**: half-page **u**p                   | 12 lines higher                    |
| 15   | `<C-f>`   | **Ctrl-F**: full page **f**orward              | One screen down                    |
| 16   | `<C-b>`   | **Ctrl-B**: full page **b**ackward             | One screen up                      |

### 3.3 Workflow C — Editing with Operators and Motions

**Scenario:** Given the following buffer (line numbers shown for clarity, *not* part of the file):

```
1:  the quick brown fox
2:  jumps over the lazy dog
3:  the quick brown fox
4:  the quick brown fox
5:  jumps over the lazy dog
```

Perform the sequence: delete the word "quick" on line 1, copy the entire line 2 to the end of the file, and globally replace "fox" with "cat".

| Step | Keystroke        | Decomposition                                  | Result                                              |
|------|------------------|------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| 1    | `0`              | jump to column 0 of line 1                     | Cursor on `t` of "the"                              |
| 2    | `w`              | forward one word                               | Cursor on `q` of "quick"                            |
| 3    | `dw`             | delete + word                                  | Line becomes `the brown fox`                        |
| 4    | `j`              | down one line                                  | On line 2                                           |
| 5    | `yy`             | yank the **whole** line (linewise)             | Line 2 copied to unnamed register                   |
| 6    | `G`              | to last line                                   | On line 5                                           |
| 7    | `p`              | **p**aste the register *below* the cursor     | Line 6 created = "jumps over the lazy dog"          |
| 8    | `:%s/fox/cat/g`  | global substitute                              | All "fox" → "cat" on every line                    |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the difference between `p` and `P` in step 7: `p` pastes *after* the cursor (or on the line below for linewise yanks), `P` pastes *before* the cursor (or on the line above for linewise yanks). This distinction is a common KTU viva question.

### 3.4 Workflow D — Visual Mode Selection and Block Operations

**Scenario:** The buffer contains a 4×6 grid of numbers, and the student must insert `#` at the start of the first three lines of column 1.

```
1:  10 20 30 40
2:  50 60 70 80
3:  90 11 22 33
4:  44 55 66 77
```

| Step | Keystroke   | Result                                                  |
|------|-------------|---------------------------------------------------------|
| 1    | `<Esc>`     | Ensure Normal mode                                      |
| 2    | `<C-v>`     | Enter Visual-Block (column-wise) mode                   |
| 3    | `2j`        | Extend selection down 2 lines                           |
| 4    | `I# <Esc>`  | `I` enters Insert *only on the top line*; the `# ` is mirrored on all selected lines when `<Esc>` is pressed |

The buffer becomes:

```
1:  # 10 20 30 40
2:  # 50 60 70 80
3:  # 90 11 22 33
4:  44 55 66 77
```

> [!TIP]
> The block-wise "insert-once-mirrored-on-all" behavior of `<C-v> I ... <Esc>` is unique to Visual-Block mode and is the most-powerful single feature of vim for editing tabular data. KTU has asked questions on this exact scenario in previous university exams.

### 3.5 Workflow E — Customizing vim with `.vimrc`

The user-level configuration file is `~/.vimrc` (or `~/.config/nvim/init.vim` for Neovim). It is a vimscript file read at startup. A minimal example suitable for a KTU lab record:

```vim
" ~/.vimrc — KTU PEEVT412 sample
set number           " show line numbers
set relativenumber   " show distance to lines
set tabstop=4        " visual width of a tab
set shiftwidth=4     " spaces used by > and < operators
set expandtab        " insert spaces instead of tab characters
set autoindent       " carry indent from previous line
set ignorecase       " case-insensitive search
set smartcase        " ... unless uppercase letter is used
syntax on            " enable syntax highlighting
colorscheme desert   " pick a built-in color scheme
```

> [!WARNING]
> The `set` commands above use a *single space* between the option name and the value. In vimscript, `set tabstop 4` (no `=`) and `set tabstop=4` are both legal and equivalent. Mixing them in the same file is legal but stylistically inconsistent. KTU scripts usually require the `=` form.

### 3.6 Workflow F — Recording and Replaying a Macro

A **macro** is a sequence of normal-mode keystrokes saved into a register, then replayed. The canonical example: prepend `# ` to every line in a file.

| Step | Keystroke     | Effect                                                       |
|------|---------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1    | `qa`          | Begin recording into register `a`                            |
| 2    | `I# <Esc>`    | Insert `# ` at the start of the current line, return Normal  |
| 3    | `j`           | Move to next line                                            |
| 4    | `q`           | Stop recording                                               |
| 5    | `10@a`        | Replay register `a` ten times                                |
| 6    | `:%normal @a` | Apply the macro to *every* line in the file (Ex command)     |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Four-Core-Mode Finite State Machine

This diagram shows the *allowed* transitions between the four modes a KTU student must know, with the keys that trigger each transition.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> modeNormal
    modeNormal --> modeInsert : i a o O s S
    modeNormal --> modeVisual : v V
    modeNormal --> modeCommand : colon_char
    modeInsert --> modeNormal : ESC
    modeVisual --> modeNormal : ESC
    modeVisual --> modeCommand : colon_char
    modeCommand --> modeNormal : ENTER_or_ESC
    modeNormal --> [*] : :q or :wq or ZZ
```

> *Note:* The `colon_char` label represents the literal colon `:` key that opens the command-line area. Node identifiers use alphanumeric form (`modeNormal`, `modeInsert`, etc.) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Operator + Motion Grammar Architecture

The diagram below maps how a user keystroke decomposes into the operator/motion grammar that drives every editing action.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[User Keystrokes] --> parse{Prefix Count?}
    parse -- yes --> count[Apply Numeric Count]
    parse -- no --> count
    count --> op[Pick Operator]
    op --> opChoice{Operator?}
    opChoice -- d --> dAct[Delete to register]
    opChoice -- c --> cAct[Delete then Insert]
    opChoice -- y --> yAct[Yank to register]
    opChoice -- gt --> gAct[Indent right]
    opChoice -- lt --> lAct[Indent left]
    opChoice -- gu --> gLow[Lowercase]
    opChoice -- gU --> gUp[Uppercase]
    opChoice -- eq --> gEq[Auto-indent]
    dAct --> motion[Pick Motion]
    cAct --> motion
    yAct --> motion
    gAct --> motion
    lAct --> motion
    gLow --> motion
    gUp --> motion
    gEq --> motion
    motion --> execute[Execute Operator over Motion Span]
    execute --> buffer[Mutate Buffer]
    buffer --> render[Re-render Screen]
    render --> input
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of vim

This is the high-level architecture of vim showing the major subsystems and how they interact. It is the kind of diagram a KTU examiner expects for the *"describe the architecture of vim"* style 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER
        kb[Keyboard Input]
    end
    kb --> MODE[Mode Dispatcher]
    MODE --> NORM[Normal Mode Handler]
    MODE --> INS[Insert Mode Handler]
    MODE --> VIS[Visual Mode Handler]
    MODE --> EX[Ex Mode Handler]
    NORM --> MOTION[Motion Engine]
    NORM --> OPER[Operator Engine]
    MOTION --> BUF[(Buffer Store)]
    OPER --> BUF
    INS --> BUF
    VIS --> BUF
    EX --> BUF
    EX --> SHELL[External Shell]
    BUF --> WIN[Window Manager]
    WIN --> SCR[Screen Renderer]
    SCR --> kb
    BUF --> REG[Register File]
    BUF --> UNDO[Undo Tree]
    BUF --> MACRO[Macro Recorder]
    SCR --> TUI[TTY / Terminal UI]
```

**Reading the architecture:**

- The **Mode Dispatcher** is the single switch that decides which handler receives the next keystroke.
- The **Buffer Store** is the in-memory text being edited. Every mode ultimately mutates this store.
- The **Window Manager** may present one buffer across several windows (splits) or several buffers in tabs.
- The **Register File** is the clipboard-like storage holding the most recent yanks, deletes, and named registers (`"a`, `"b`, …).
- The **Undo Tree** is a branching history; it is *not* a linear stack, which is why you can rewind a change, make a different change, and still return to the first branch with `:undo` + `:redo`/`<C-r>`.
- The **Macro Recorder** watches the keystroke stream and, when active, appends every key to a designated register.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on "vim Features" Questions**
> 1. Writing that vim's default mode is **Insert mode** — it is **Normal mode**. *Every* full-mark answer must state this.
> 2. Confusing `p` (paste after) with `P` (paste before). Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. Forgetting the `g` flag in `:s/old/new/g` and then claiming global substitution was done. Without `g`, only the *first* occurrence on each line is replaced.
> 4. Confusing `dd` (delete a line) with `D` (delete from cursor to end of line). `dd` is double-letter, `D` is capital single.
> 5. Stating that vim is closed-source — it is **GPL-compatible charity-ware** released under a Vim License.
> 6. Saying `vi` and `vim` are the same — `vim` is the improved clone, and supports features `vi` lacks (multi-level undo, syntax highlighting, visual mode, plugins).

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*What is a modal editor? Why is vim called a modal editor?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *modal editor* is a text editor in which the meaning of every keystroke changes based on the editor's current *mode*, rather than always inserting a character into the buffer. vim is called a modal editor because it has several distinct modes — primarily **Normal, Insert, Visual, and Command-Line (Ex)** — and the same physical key performs different actions in each. When vim starts, it opens in **Normal Mode**, where keys such as `h`, `j`, `k`, `l` move the cursor and keys such as `d`, `y`, `c` perform editing operations instead of typing characters. The user must explicitly enter Insert mode (by pressing `i`, `a`, `o`, etc.) to type text. This modal design allows the user to keep both hands on the home row and to compose complex edits using a verb–noun grammar, making editing extremely fast once the user is proficient.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*List any six important features of vim that make it suitable for editing files on a Linux system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Six important features of vim are:

1. **Modal editing** with distinct Normal, Insert, Visual, and Ex modes.
2. **Ubiquity** — preinstalled on virtually every Linux/Unix distribution, available even on headless servers with no GUI.
3. **Highly configurable** via the `~/.vimrc` file and extensible with plugins written in vimscript or external languages.
4. **Powerful search and replace** with regular-expression support and a single-command global substitution syntax `:s/pattern/repl/flags`.
5. **Multi-window and multi-buffer** editing through splits (`:sp`, `:vsp`) and tabs (`:tabnew`).
6. **Macro recording and replay** (`q<reg>` … `q` … `@<reg>`) for automating repetitive editing tasks.

*(Any six valid features accepted; typical answers also include syntax highlighting, multi-level undo, registers/clipboard, and command history.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Within a module, the student may be offered an internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Both questions below are written to that specification. Each contains a 7-mark sub-part (a) and a 7-mark sub-part (b).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the *operator–motion grammar* of vim with suitable examples. State the difference between `dd` and `D`, and between `p` and `P`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the four primary modes of vim. Draw a state diagram showing the transitions between them and list the key used for each transition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) [7 marks]**

The vim Normal Mode treats every editing action as the composition of three optional parts: a numeric **count**, an **operator** (verb), and a **motion** (noun). The general grammar is:

$$
\text{Action} = [\text{count}] \cdot \text{operator} \cdot \text{motion}
$$

For example, `d2w` means: `2` is the count, `d` is the delete operator, `w` is the word motion. The instruction therefore *deletes the next two words starting from the cursor*. Other common compositions include:

- `y$` — yank (copy) from cursor to end of line.
- `c3e` — change the next three words (deletes them and enters Insert mode).
- `>ip` — indent the inner paragraph one `shiftwidth` to the right.
- `gUit` — uppercase the inner HTML tag content.

**Difference between `dd` and `D`** *(1 mark)*: `dd` is the **line-wise delete operator** and removes the *entire* current line including the trailing newline, equivalent to `d_` followed by `d`. `D` (capital) is **delete-to-end-of-line** and removes everything from the cursor to the end of the *current* line, leaving the line itself in place (equivalent to `d$`).

**Difference between `p` and `P`** *(1 mark)*: After a yank or delete, `p` (**paste after**) places the register contents *after* the cursor (or on the line below for linewise content). `P` (**paste before**) places the register contents *before* the cursor (or on the line above for linewise content).

**Marks breakdown (a):** 4 marks for grammar explanation + 2 examples, 1 mark for `dd` vs `D`, 1 mark for `p` vs `P`, 1 mark for correct terminology.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

The four primary modes of vim are:

1. **Normal Mode** — the default mode entered when vim starts. Keys are interpreted as commands (navigation, operators). Indicated by `-- NORMAL --`.
2. **Insert Mode** — keys are inserted literally into the buffer. Indicated by `-- INSERT --`.
3. **Visual Mode** — keys move the cursor to define a selection that can then be operated on. Indicated by `-- VISUAL --` (or `-- VISUAL LINE --` for `V`, `-- VISUAL BLOCK --` for `<C-v>`).
4. **Command-Line / Ex Mode** — the bottom line accepts an Ex command beginning with `:`.

**State Diagram with Transitions** *(4 marks — see Section 4.1 for the rendered diagram)*:

| From        | Key       | To          | Purpose                                  |
|-------------|-----------|-------------|------------------------------------------|
| (any)       | `i`       | Insert      | Begin typing before cursor               |
| Normal      | `a`       | Insert      | Begin typing after cursor                |
| Normal      | `o`       | Insert      | Open new line below and type             |
| Normal      | `v`       | Visual      | Begin character-wise selection           |
| Normal      | `V`       | Visual Line | Begin line-wise selection                |
| Normal      | `<C-v>`   | Visual Block| Begin column-wise selection              |
| Normal      | `:`       | Ex          | Open command-line                        |
| Insert      | `<Esc>`   | Normal      | Return to command layer                  |
| Visual      | `<Esc>`   | Normal      | Cancel selection                         |
| Ex          | `<Enter>` | Normal      | Execute the Ex command                   |
| Ex          | `<Esc>`   | Normal      | Abort the Ex command                     |

**Marks breakdown (b):** 1 mark per mode (×4 = 4), 1 mark for the diagram, 2 marks for the transition table and the explicit "Normal is default" statement.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the following vim Ex commands with one example each: `:w`, `:q!`, `:e`, `:r`, `:set nu`, `:sp`, `:vsp`, `:!`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a `.vimrc` file? Write a sample `.vimrc` that sets tab width to 4, enables line numbers, enables syntax highlighting, enables auto-indent, and maps `<F2>` to save the current file. Explain each line. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) [7 marks]** — One mark per command (0.5 for explanation + 0.5 for example).

- **`:w`** — *write* the current buffer to disk. Example: `:w` saves the current file; `:w backup.txt` saves under a new name.
- **`:q!`** — *quit forcefully*, discarding any unsaved modifications. The `!` bypasses vim's "write first?" guard. Example: when you have made edits you no longer want, `:q!` exits without saving.
- **`:e filename`** — *edit* another file by loading it into the current buffer. Example: `:e /etc/hosts` opens the system hosts file in the current vim window.
- **`:r filename`** — *read* the contents of a file and insert them at the cursor. Example: `:r data.csv` pastes the CSV beneath the cursor.
- **`:set nu`** — enable *line numbers* on the left margin. Example: `:set nu` toggles line numbers on; `:set nonu` toggles them off.
- **`:sp filename`** — *split* the current window horizontally and open `filename` in the new pane. Example: `:sp Makefile` opens the Makefile in a split below.
- **`:vsp filename`** — *vertical split* the current window; the new pane is to the right. Example: `:vsp README.md` opens the README on the right.
- **`:!command`** — run an external shell command without leaving vim. Example: `:!ls -l` shows the directory listing; the user presses `<Enter>` to return to the editor.

**Marks breakdown (a):** 0.75 per command × 8 = 6 marks, plus 1 mark awarded for overall clarity of presentation.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

The `.vimrc` file is the **user-level startup configuration** for vim, located at `~/.vimrc` in the user's home directory. It is a vimscript file executed once every time vim starts. It is the canonical place to set editor preferences, define custom key mappings, and load plugins.

**Sample `.vimrc`** (2 marks for the listing, 4 marks for the line-by-line explanation, 1 mark for the F2 mapping explanation):

```vim
" ~/.vimrc — KTU PEEVT412 example configuration

" Use 4 spaces for tab width
set tabstop=4

" Use 4 spaces for indentation commands (>>, <<)
set shiftwidth=4

" Insert spaces when the Tab key is pressed, not a literal tab character
set expandtab

" Display line numbers in the left margin
set number

" Enable syntax highlighting based on filetype detection
syntax on

" Auto-indent: carry the indentation of the previous line to the new line
set autoindent

" Map the F2 key to write the current buffer (equivalent to :w)
nnoremap <F2> :w<CR>
```

**Line-by-line explanation** *(1 mark for any four well-explained lines; full marks for explaining all)*:

- `set tabstop=4` — the visual width of a tab character is rendered as 4 columns.
- `set shiftwidth=4` — the number of spaces inserted or removed by `>>` / `<<` and by auto-indent.
- `set expandtab` — pressing Tab in Insert mode inserts 4 spaces, not a tab.
- `set number` — shows absolute line numbers in a left-hand gutter.
- `syntax on` — loads the filetype detection and syntax-highlighting engine.
- `set autoindent` — when you press `<Enter>` in Insert mode, the new line begins at the same column the previous line's first non-blank character was on.
- `nnoremap <F2> :w<CR>` — creates a *non-recursive* Normal-mode mapping: pressing `<F2>` is now equivalent to typing `:w` followed by `<Enter>` (i.e. saving the file). The `nnoremap` form is used instead of `nmap` to prevent infinite recursion if `F2` were remapped elsewhere.

**Marks breakdown (b):** 1 mark for the *definition* of `.vimrc` and its location, 2 marks for the listing being syntactically valid, 3 marks for correct per-line explanations, 1 mark for explicitly explaining `nnoremap` vs `nmap` (this is a frequent viva follow-up).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- vim stands for **Vi IMproved**; created by **Bram Moolenaar** in **1991**; first released for the **Amiga**.
- vim is a **modal editor**. The default mode on startup is **Normal Mode**, *not* Insert.
- The four primary modes are **Normal, Insert, Visual, and Command-Line (Ex)**.
- The two ways to leave any mode and return to Normal are **`<Esc>`** and **`<C-c>`** (the latter is not always safe inside Insert mode because it aborts pending abbreviations/mappings).
- The Normal-mode editing grammar is **count + operator + motion**, e.g. `d2w` deletes two words.
- Operators include `d` (delete), `c` (change), `y` (yank), `>` (indent right), `<` (indent left), `gu` (lowercase), `gU` (uppercase), `g~` (toggle case), `=` (auto-indent).
- Motions include `h j k l` (cursor), `w b e` (word), `0 ^ $` (line), `gg G` (file), `f<char> t<char>` (in-line search), `/pattern` (search).
- `dd` deletes a **line**; `D` deletes to the **end of the line**.
- `p` pastes **after** the cursor (or below for linewise yanks); `P` pastes **before** the cursor (or above for linewise yanks).
- `yy` yanks a line; `yw` yanks a word; `y$` yanks to end of line.
- Ex commands begin with `:`. The most important are `:w`, `:q`, `:wq`, `:q!`, `:e`, `:r`, `:s/old/new/flags`, `:sp`, `:vsp`, `:!cmd`, `:set opt`.
- Global substitution syntax: `:s/foo/bar/g` replaces on the *current* line, `:%s/foo/bar/g` replaces on *every* line, `:%s/foo/bar/gc` confirms each.
- Visual-Block mode `<C-v>` is the most powerful vim feature for editing columns, especially the `<C-v> I # <Esc>` trick to prepend text to many lines at once.
- Macros are recorded with `q<reg>` … `q` and replayed with `@<reg>`; `@@` replays the most recent macro.
- The user config file is `~/.vimrc`. The system-wide file is `/etc/vim/vimrc` (path varies by distro).
- **NEVER** quit vim with the X button on a remote shell — use `:q`, `:wq`, or `:q!` *inside* the editor.
- vim is a **character editor**; it does not, by default, use the system clipboard. Use `+y` (yank to clipboard register) and `+p` (paste from clipboard register) when compiled with clipboard support.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Command Mode: Moving the Cursor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Command Mode: Moving the Cursor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **vi** (or its enhanced successor, **vim — Vi IMproved**) text editor, **Command Mode** is the **default, non-insert operational state** in which the editor interprets every keystroke issued by the user as a *command* rather than as literal text to be inserted into the buffer. The **"Moving the Cursor"** sub-topic specifically enumerates the **key-bound motion primitives** — single-character, multi-character, screen-relative, and file-relative — that allow the user to position the cursor anywhere in the file without entering Insert Mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEVT412 — Module 4):**
> The course explicitly covers *vi editor modes*, *navigation commands*, *text manipulation*, and *ex-mode operations*. **Command Mode cursor movement** is the foundation upon which every editing, yanking, deleting, and searching operation is layered, because all such operations operate on the **range of text spanned by the current cursor position(s)**.

## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Modes of `vi`

| Mode | Entry Command | Behaviour | Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Command Mode (Normal Mode)** | `Esc` (always returns here) | Keys are interpreted as **commands** | No `-- INSERT --` shown |
| **Insert Mode** | `i`, `a`, `o`, etc. | Keys are inserted as **literal text** | `-- INSERT --` shown on status line |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two modes?** Before graphical editors, terminals could not distinguish between a *character* and a *command* unless one of them was reserved. `vi` solves this by dedicating **all alphabetic keys** to commands and switching the entire keyboard into a *text-typing mode* only when the user explicitly demands it. This is why `vi` users never need a mouse, a menu, or arrow keys for the vast majority of work.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Remote Control" Mental Model

Imagine you are watching a television with a **remote control** in your hand.

- **Command Mode is the "remote in hand" state.** You press a button (`h`, `j`, `k`, `l`), and the *highlighted channel* (cursor) moves left, down, up, or right on the screen. Nothing new is *painted* on the TV.
- **Insert Mode is the "painting with a brush" state.** Every movement of the brush *deposits pigment* (text) onto the canvas (the file).
- Pressing `Esc` is the universal **"drop the brush, pick up the remote"** gesture.

The analogy makes one crucial insight obvious: *you cannot paint while holding the remote, and you cannot change channels while holding the brush.* Similarly, **you cannot move the cursor using insertion keystrokes, and you cannot type text using command keystrokes.**

## 1.4 Why the `hjkl` Keys? — Historical Context

The `vi` editor was originally written by **Bill Joy in 1976** for an **ADM-3A terminal**, whose keyboard had the arrow keys laid out physically in the shape of an inverted-T on the **`h`, `j`, `k`, `l`** keys (just below the right hand's home row on QWERTY). Since the terminal lacked dedicated arrow keys at the standard positions, Bill Joy *re-purposed these existing letter keys* as the native motion keys — and this convention has been frozen into every vi-clone (nvi, vim, neovim) ever since.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** Even on a modern PC keyboard where you have dedicated arrow keys, **using `hjkl` is dramatically faster** because the hand never leaves the home row. This is one of the most heavily tested points in KTU viva and practical examinations.

## 1.5 Categories of Cursor Movement

The cursor motion commands fall into **four logical families**:

1. **Character-level motion** — move one or more characters in any direction.
2. **Word-level motion** — jump over linguistic units (words, WORDS, end of word).
3. **Line-level motion** — move to specific positions *within the current line*.
4. **Screen / File-level motion** — move across the visible page or to absolute positions in the file.

Each family will be unpacked rigorously in the next section.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Character-Level Motion (The Foundation)

| Key | Direction | Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|
| `h` | One character **left** | `h` is on the left of `j` |
| `j` | One character **down** | `j` looks like a *down-pointing arrow* |
| `k` | One character **up** | `k` looks like an *up-pointing arrow* |
| `l` | One character **right** | `l` is on the right of `k` |

> [!NOTE]
> **The `Why`:** These keys were chosen because they sit directly under the right hand's resting position, allowing blind touch-typing navigation. Count prefixes work: `5j` moves down **5 lines**, `12l` moves right **12 columns**.

### The `count motion` Pattern

A numeric prefix is **always a repeat operator**.

$$n \cdot \text{motion} = \text{execute the motion exactly } n \text{ times}$$

Examples:
- `3j` → cursor moves **down 3 lines**.
- `10l` → cursor moves **right 10 characters**.
- `0` (zero) → cursor moves to **column 1 of the current line** (overrides the count).

## 2.2 Word-Level Motion (Linguistic Jumps)

vi distinguishes between **word** and **WORD** because programmers frequently need to skip over **identifiers-with-punctuation** (`foo.bar_baz`) without getting stuck on every dot or underscore.

| Key | Motion | Stops on |
|---|---|---|
| `w` | Forward to the **start** of the next *word* | First alphanumeric of the next word |
| `W` | Forward to the start of the next **WORD** | First non-blank after whitespace |
| `b` | **Backwards** to the start of the current/previous word | First alphanumeric of the prior word |
| `B` | **Backwards** to the start of the current/previous WORD | First non-blank after whitespace |
| `e` | Forward to the **end** of the current/next word | Last alphanumeric of the next word |
| `E` | Forward to the end of the current/next WORD | Last non-blank of the next WORD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (used by KTU examiners):** A **word** is a maximal contiguous sequence of alphanumeric characters *or* a maximal contiguous sequence of punctuation, terminated by whitespace or EOL. A **WORD** is a maximal contiguous sequence of non-blank characters terminated by whitespace.

**Example:** On the text `foo.bar_baz qux`, the cursor at column 1 behaves as:
- `w` lands on `b` of `bar_baz` (skips `foo.` as punctuation).
- `W` lands on `b` of `bar_baz` (skips `foo.bar_baz` as a single WORD).
- `e` lands on `o` of `foo` (end of first word).

## 2.3 Line-Level Motion (Within the Current Line)

| Key | Destination |
|---|---|
| `0` | **First character** of the line (column 1) |
| `^` | First **non-blank** character of the line |
| `$` | **Last character** of the line (end-of-line) |
| `g_` | Last **non-blank** character of the line |
| `n\|` | Cursor to **column n** of the current line |
| `+` or `<CR>` | First non-blank of the **next** line |
| `-` | First non-blank of the **previous** line |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students commonly confuse `0` (column 1) with `^` (first non-blank). A line of all spaces still has column 1 — `^` skips those spaces.

## 2.4 Screen / Page-Level Motion

| Key | Motion |
|---|---|
| `Ctrl-F` | **Forward** one full screen (Page Down) |
| `Ctrl-B` | **Backwards** one full screen (Page Up) |
| `Ctrl-D` | **Down** half a screen |
| `Ctrl-U` | **Up** half a screen |
| `H` | **Home** — top line of the visible screen |
| `M` | **Middle** — middle line of the visible screen |
| `L` | **Last** — bottom line of the visible screen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `H`, `M`, `L` take a numeric prefix: `3H` moves to the **3rd line from the top** of the screen, not the file.

## 2.5 File-Level Motion (Absolute Positioning)

| Key | Motion |
|---|---|
| `gg` | **Go** to the **first** line of the file |
| `G` | **Go** to the **last** line of the file |
| `nG` or `:n` | Go to **line number n** |
| `%` | Jump to the **matching bracket** `() [] {}` |

## 2.6 Search-Based Motion (In-File Navigation)

| Key | Motion |
|---|---|
| `f<char>` | Find the **next** occurrence of `<char>` *on the current line* |
| `F<char>` | Find the **previous** occurrence of `<char>` on the current line |
| `t<char>` | Move **till (just before)** the next occurrence of `<char>` |
| `T<char>` | Move till just after the previous occurrence of `<char>` |
| `;` | **Repeat** the last `f`/`F`/`t`/`T` in the same direction |
| `,` | Repeat the last `f`/`F`/`t`/`T` in the opposite direction |
| `/pattern<CR>` | Search **forward** for a regex pattern |
| `?pattern<CR>` | Search **backward** for a regex pattern |
| `n` | Repeat the last search in the **same** direction |
| `N` | Repeat the last search in the **opposite** direction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The `f`/`t` family is the **single most used motion** in professional vi usage. Programmers use `f;` (find, then repeat) to skip across long lines of code orders of magnitude faster than arrow keys. This is frequently tested in the KTU practical examination.

## 2.7 Comprehensive KTU Cheat-Sheet Table

| # | Key(s) | Full Name | Class | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `h j k l` | left, down, up, right | Char | 4-way |
| 2 | `0` `^` `$` `g_` | begin/begin-nonblank/end/end-nonblank | Line | in-line |
| 3 | `w W b B e E` | word/WORD forward/back/end | Word | in-line |
| 4 | `H M L` | Home/Middle/Low of screen | Screen | vertical |
| 5 | `Ctrl-F Ctrl-B` | Forward/Back full page | Screen | vertical |
| 6 | `Ctrl-D Ctrl-U` | Down/Up half page | Screen | vertical |
| 7 | `gg G nG` | top/bottom/line-n | File | vertical |
| 8 | `f F t T ; ,` | find/Find/till/Till (incl. repeat) | Line | in-line |
| 9 | `/ ? n N` | search forward/back + repeats | File | both |
| 10 | `%` | match-paren | File | both |
| 11 | `+ - <CR>` | next/prev line first non-blank | Line | vertical |
| 12 | `n\|` | to column n | Line | in-line |

## 2.8 Why These Motions Matter in Engineering

- **Code editing:** `w`, `b`, `e` make a programmer's "cursor velocity" roughly 5–10× faster than arrow-key navigation because the hand never leaves the home row.
- **Log-file analysis:** `G`, `gg`, `/pattern`, and `n` allow rapid scanning of multi-gigabyte log files without leaving the keyboard.
- **Configuration editing:** `%` is essential for jumping between paired delimiters in JSON, XML, and C source.
- **System administration:** Combined with Ex commands, motions form the heart of every shell-based automation pipeline using `sed` and `ex` (which use the same regex/vim motion grammar).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples & Trace Walkthroughs

Because **Command Mode cursor movement** is a *behavioural* topic (not a numeric derivation), this section provides **exhaustive trace walkthroughs** of the cursor's coordinates as each motion is applied, in the rigorous KTU style of a worked-out problem.

## 3.1 The Reference Buffer

To make every trace verifiable, we use one canonical **5-line, 88-column buffer** (the standard `vimtutor` example) throughout:

```
    1  The quick brown fox
    2  jumps over the lazy dog.
    3  Programming in Linux is fun.
    4      Indented line starts here.
    5  END_OF_FILE
```

We adopt a coordinate convention:

$$(l, c) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{row}=l, \;\; \text{column}=c$$

The cursor's initial position is assumed to be **(1, 1)** — top-left of line 1.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — `hjkl` Trace

**Question:** Starting at `(1, 1)`, after the keystrokes `l l l k h`, what is the cursor position?

**Step-by-step trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial}    &\Rightarrow (1, 1) \quad\text{(line 1, column 1 — character 'T')} \\
l \;\text{(right)} &\Rightarrow (1, 2) \quad\text{(character 'h')} \\
l \;\text{(right)} &\Rightarrow (1, 3) \quad\text{(character 'e')} \\
l \;\text{(right)} &\Rightarrow (1, 4) \quad\text{(character ' ' — space)} \\
k \;\text{(up)}    &\Rightarrow \text{still line 1, only one line — but } k \text{ at top stays at } (1, 4) \\
h \;\text{(left)}  &\Rightarrow (1, 3) \quad\text{(character 'e')}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary behaviour:** When the cursor is already on the topmost line, `k` is a **no-op** — `vi` does **not** wrap around to the bottom of the file. This is a critical point often lost in KTU viva.

**Final answer:** `(1, 3)`.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — `w`/`b`/`e` Trace

**Question:** From `(3, 1)` on the text `Programming in Linux is fun.`, apply `w w w w b` and report the cursor position after each keystroke.

**Step-by-step trace:**

The string can be tokenised into **words** (alphanumeric runs):
- Word 1: `Programming`  (columns 1–11)
- Word 2: `in`           (columns 13–14)
- Word 3: `Linux`        (columns 16–20)
- Word 4: `is`           (columns 22–23)
- Word 5: `fun.`         (columns 25–28)

$$
\begin{aligned}
(3, 1) &\xrightarrow{w} (3, 13) &&\text{start of "in"} \\
(3,13) &\xrightarrow{w} (3, 16) &&\text{start of "Linux"} \\
(3,16) &\xrightarrow{w} (3, 22) &&\text{start of "is"} \\
(3,22) &\xrightarrow{w} (3, 25) &&\text{start of "fun."} \\
(3,25) &\xrightarrow{b} (3, 22) &&\text{back to start of "is"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final answer:** `(3, 22)`.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — `0`, `^`, `$`, `g_`

**Question:** From `(4, 1)`, apply `$` then `^` then `0` then `g_`. Line 4 is `    Indented line starts here.`.

**Step-by-step trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(4, 1)   &\xrightarrow{\$}    (4, 32) &&\text{last char "e" of "here."} \\
(4,32)   &\xrightarrow{\^{}}  (4, 5)  &&\text{first non-blank "I"} \\
(4, 5)   &\xrightarrow{0}     (4, 1)  &&\text{absolute column 1} \\
(4, 1)   &\xrightarrow{g\_}   (4, 32) &&\text{last non-blank, same as \$}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction highlighted:** For this particular line, `$` and `g_` coincide because the last character is non-blank. On a line ending in spaces, they would differ.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — `f`/`t` Trace

**Question:** From `(2, 1)`, the line reads `jumps over the lazy dog.`. Apply `f;` (find space) twice. Then apply `t;` (till space).

**Step-by-step trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(2,1) &\xrightarrow{f<\;space\>} (2, 6) &&\text{on the first space after "jumps"} \\
(2,6) &\xrightarrow{;}            (2,11) &&\text{next space, after "over"} \\
(2,11)&\xrightarrow{t<\;space\>} (2,15) &&\text{just before the 3rd space, i.e. on 'e' of "the"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final answer:** `(2, 15)`.

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — `H`, `M`, `L` and Counts

**Question:** A terminal screen shows 24 rows. Lines 1–24 of the file are visible, with the cursor at `(1,1)`. Apply `12G` then `H` then `M` then `L` then `3H`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1,1)  &\xrightarrow{12G}   (12, 1) &&\text{absolute jump to line 12} \\
(12,1) &\xrightarrow{H}     (1,  1) &&\text{top of *visible* screen} \\
(1,1)  &\xrightarrow{M}     (12, 1) &&\text{middle of screen (row 12 of 24)} \\
(12,1) &\xrightarrow{L}     (24, 1) &&\text{bottom of screen} \\
(24,1) &\xrightarrow{3H}    (3,  1) &&\text{3rd row from top of screen}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.7 Worked Example 6 — `gg`, `G`, `nG`, `%`

**Question:** From `(3, 1)`, jump to the last line, then to the first, then to line 5, then onto a parenthesis. The line `void foo(int x) {` is at line 7. Cursor is at the `f` of `foo`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(3,1)  &\xrightarrow{G}     (5, 1)  &&\text{last line} \\
(5,1)  &\xrightarrow{gg}    (1, 1)  &&\text{first line} \\
(1,1)  &\xrightarrow{5G}    (5, 1)  &&\text{line 5} \\
(5,1)  &\xrightarrow{:7}    (7, 1)  &&\text{Ex command — line 7} \\
(7,6)  &\xrightarrow{\%}    (7, 16) &&\text{matches the closing '\}' of the brace}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.8 Summary Trace Table

The following compact table records a single combined "speed run" across the entire 5-line buffer (initial cursor at `(1, 1)`):

| Step | Key | Resulting Position | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | (start) | (1, 1) | — |
| 1 | `3j` | (4, 1) | count + motion |
| 2 | `w` | (4, 5) | first non-blank of line 4 (indented) |
| 3 | `$` | (4, 32) | end of line |
| 4 | `gg` | (1, 1) | top of file |
| 5 | `G` | (5, 1) | bottom of file |
| 6 | `:2<CR>` | (2, 1) | Ex command |
| 7 | `f o` | (2, 8) | find "o" — first one in "over" |
| 8 | `;` | (2, 20) | next "o" in "dog." |
| 9 | `%` | (2, 1) | on a letter, `%` is a no-op |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Steps 7–8 demonstrate the **f + ;** pattern — the highest-leverage technique in professional vi use. Combined with `d` (delete) or `y` (yank) it becomes an extremely efficient editing primitive: e.g., `d2f;` deletes everything up to the 2nd following space.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Mode-State Diagram of `vi`

The following Mermaid state diagram captures the **mode transitions** and the fact that every form of cursor navigation must originate from **Command Mode**.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CommandMode : vi opens
    CommandMode --> InsertMode : i / a / o / c / s
    InsertMode --> CommandMode : Esc
    CommandMode --> ExMode : : / / / ? /
    ExMode --> CommandMode : Enter (or Esc)
    CommandMode --> CommandMode : h j k l w b e 0 $ G gg
    state CommandMode {
        direction LR
        CharMotion : h j k l
        WordMotion : w b e W B E
        LineMotion : 0 $ ^ g_
        FileMotion : gg G nG %
    }
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The self-loops on `CommandMode` show that *all motion commands are themselves state-preserving* — you stay in Command Mode after every motion. The exit arrows to `InsertMode` and `ExMode` show the *only* two ways to leave Command Mode.

## 4.2 The Motion-Command Classification Tree

This Mermaid block diagram hierarchically groups every motion discussed in §2.

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Cursor Motions in Command Mode]
    root --> A1[Character-level]
    root --> A2[Word-level]
    root --> A3[Line-level]
    root --> A4[Screen-level]
    root --> A5[File-level]
    root --> A6[Search-based]

    A1 --> A1a["h left"]
    A1 --> A1b["j down"]
    A1 --> A1c["k up"]
    A1 --> A1d["l right"]

    A2 --> A2a["w forward word"]
    A2 --> A2b["b back word"]
    A2 --> A2c["e end word"]
    A2 --> A2d["W B E WORD variants"]

    A3 --> A3a["0 col 1"]
    A3 --> A3b["^ first non-blank"]
    A3 --> A3c["$ end of line"]
    A3 --> A3d["g_ last non-blank"]

    A4 --> A4a["H top of screen"]
    A4 --> A4b["M middle"]
    A4 --> A4c["L bottom"]
    A4 --> A4d["Ctrl-F Ctrl-B full page"]
    A4 --> A4e["Ctrl-D Ctrl-U half page"]

    A5 --> A5a["gg top of file"]
    A5 --> A5b["G bottom of file"]
    A5 --> A5c["nG absolute line"]
    A5 --> A5d["% match bracket"]

    A6 --> A6a["f F find char"]
    A6 --> A6b["t T till char"]
    A6 --> A6c["/ forward search"]
    A6 --> A6d["? backward search"]
    A6 --> A6e["n N repeat search"]
```

## 4.3 Keyboard Layout — The `hjkl` Origin

The following Mermaid diagram reproduces the **physical layout of the ADM-3A terminal's "arrow cluster"** that dictated the choice of `hjkl`.

```mermaid
graph LR
    K1[" "] --> K2[" "]
    K2 --> K3["H left"]
    K3 --> K4["J down"]
    K4 --> K5["K up"]
    K5 --> K6["L right"]
    K6 --> K7["; "]
    K7 --> K8[" "]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Fact:** All four arrow letters (`h`, `j`, `k`, `l`) are clustered together and reachable **without looking at the keyboard**, which is why they were chosen over the physically more intuitive `<Up> <Down> <Left> <Right>` keys.

## 4.4 The Count-Prefix and Operator-Pending Pipeline

This Mermaid block diagram explains **how motion commands combine with operators** (delete `d`, yank `y`, change `c`) — a concept essential for the KTU practical exam.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Optional Count n] --> B[Operator d y c]
    B --> C[Motion w e b 0 $ G]
    C --> D[Action Span]
    A2[Count n] --> E[Direct Motion 3j 5G]
    E --> D2[Pure Movement]
    D --> Result[Operator applies to range]
    D2 --> Result2[Cursor moves]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** A command like `d3w` is parsed as **operator + count + motion**: delete 3 words forward. A bare motion like `5j` is parsed as **count + motion**: move 5 lines down. This dual grammar is one of the most heavily tested KTU viva points.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT412]**
> **Q1.** *Explain the two principal modes of the `vi` editor. How does the user switch between them?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Command Mode (Normal Mode)** is the default mode entered when `vi` starts. In this mode, every keystroke is interpreted as a command (movement, deletion, yank, search, etc.), and **no text is inserted** into the buffer. (1 mark)
- **Insert Mode** allows the user to type literal text directly into the file. The status line shows the indicator `-- INSERT --`. (1 mark)
- Switching is performed by:
    - Pressing one of the insert-mode entry keys (`i`, `a`, `o`, `c`, `s`, `I`, `A`, `O`) from Command Mode to *enter* Insert Mode.
    - Pressing the `Esc` key (or `Ctrl-[`) from any mode to *return* to Command Mode. (1 mark)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEEVT412]**
> **Q2.** *Differentiate between the `w` and `W` motion commands in `vi`.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The `w` command moves the cursor to the start of the **next word**, where a *word* is defined as a maximal contiguous run of **alphanumeric characters *or* punctuation** terminated by whitespace. (1.5 marks)
- The `W` command moves to the start of the next **WORD**, where a WORD is a maximal contiguous run of **non-blank characters** (whitespace is the only separator). (1.5 marks)
- Example: on `foo.bar_baz`, `w` stops at the `b` of `bar_baz` (skipping `foo.`), while `W` skips the entire identifier in one jump.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 5.2.1 Question A — Comprehensive Coverage `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **(a)** *List and explain the character-level and word-level cursor-movement commands in `vi` Command Mode with suitable examples.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Character-level commands** — These move the cursor by **one character** in a cardinal direction.

- `h` → moves cursor **one character to the left** on the current line. Example: on `The`, pressing `h` from `e` lands on `h`. (0.5 mark)
- `j` → moves cursor **one line down** (column preserved where possible). Example: from line 1, `j` moves to line 2 in the same column. (0.5 mark)
- `k` → moves cursor **one line up**. (0.5 mark)
- `l` → moves cursor **one character to the right**. (0.5 mark)

A numeric prefix **n** repeats the motion **n** times: `5j` moves down 5 lines, `20l` moves right 20 characters. (0.5 mark)

**(ii) Word-level commands** — These jump the cursor over linguistic units.

- `w` → forward to the **start of the next word**. (0.7 mark)
- `b` → backward to the **start of the current/previous word**. (0.7 mark)
- `e` → forward to the **end of the current/next word**. (0.7 mark)
- `W`, `B`, `E` → uppercase variants that treat **only whitespace as a delimiter**; useful for identifiers with punctuation. (0.7 mark)

**Example trace (1.2 marks):** On the buffer
```
The quick brown fox jumps
```
Starting at column 1, the keystrokes `w w w w` place the cursor on the `j` of `jumps` (column 21).

---

> **(b)** *With the help of a neat diagram, describe the line-level, screen-level, and file-level motion commands in `vi`.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Line-level motions (2 marks):**
- `0` — first column of the current line.
- `^` — first **non-blank** character of the current line.
- `$` — last character of the current line.
- `g_` — last **non-blank** character of the current line.
- `n|` — column `n` of the current line (e.g., `20|`).
- `+` / `<CR>` — first non-blank of the next line.
- `-` — first non-blank of the previous line.

**(ii) Screen-level motions (2 marks):**
- `H` — top of visible screen (1st row); takes count, e.g., `3H`.
- `M` — middle of screen.
- `L` — bottom of screen.
- `Ctrl-F` / `Ctrl-B` — full-screen forward / backward.
- `Ctrl-D` / `Ctrl-U` — half-screen down / up.

**(iii) File-level motions (1.5 mark):**
- `gg` — first line of the file.
- `G` — last line of the file.
- `nG` or `:n` — jump to absolute line `n`.
- `%` — jump to the matching bracket (parens, square, curly).

**Diagram (1.5 mark):** A simple block diagram grouping the motions, similar to the Mermaid block in §4.2, may be drawn in the answer script to score full marks.

---

### 5.2.2 Question B — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **(a)** *Explain the search-based and character-search motion commands in `vi` with examples.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Pattern-search motions (3.5 marks):**
- `/pattern<CR>` — searches **forward** from the cursor for a regex pattern.
