- `?pattern<CR>` — searches **backward** for a regex pattern.
- `n` — repeats the last search in the **same** direction.
- `N` — repeats the last search in the **opposite** direction.

**Example:** `:set hlsearch` highlights matches. `/Linux<CR>` jumps to the first "Linux" after the cursor. `n` jumps to subsequent matches.

**(ii) Inline character-search motions (3.5 marks):**
- `f<char>` — find the **next** occurrence of `<char>` on the current line. (0.7)
- `F<char>` — find the **previous** occurrence. (0.7)
- `t<char>` — move **till** (one position before) the next occurrence. (0.7)
- `T<char>` — move till just after the previous occurrence. (0.7)
- `;` — repeat the last `f/F/t/T` in the **same** direction. (0.35)
- `,` — repeat in the **opposite** direction. (0.35)

**Example trace (1 mark):** On `printf("Hello, World!");` with the cursor on `p`, the sequence `f;` (find semicolon) places the cursor on `;`; pressing `;` again is a no-op (no further `;` on the line).

---

> **(b)** *Discuss the `count-motion` and `operator-motion` grammar in `vi`. Demonstrate with three examples how motion commands are combined with operators.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Count-motion grammar (2 marks):** A numeric prefix **n** immediately before a motion causes that motion to be repeated **n** times. Examples: `3j` (down 3 lines), `5w` (forward 5 words), `12G` (to line 12).

**(ii) Operator-motion grammar (2 marks):** vi parsing follows the scheme:
- `[count] operator [count] motion`
- If the operator is `d` (delete), `y` (yank), or `c` (change), the second count/motion specifies the **range of text** affected.

**(iii) Three illustrative examples (3 marks):**

1. **`d2w`** — *delete 2 words forward.* Parsed as `d` + `2w`. The operator `d` removes the two words starting at the cursor, leaving the cursor on the character after the deletion. (1 mark)
2. **`y$`** — *yank (copy) from cursor to end of line.* Parsed as `y` + `$`. The deleted text is stored in the unnamed register and may be pasted with `p`. (1 mark)
3. **`c3l`** — *change 3 characters to the right.* Parsed as `c` + `3l`. vi deletes 3 characters, enters Insert Mode at the cursor, and the user types replacement text. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing `0` and `^`.** `0` is **always** column 1; `^` is the first *non-blank* character. On a line beginning with spaces they differ. Marks are lost when students write "both go to the start of the line."
> 2. **Confusing `w` and `W`.** Always state the *precise* definition — a *word* is alphanumeric OR punctuation, separated by whitespace; a *WORD* is any non-blank run. The distinction is examinable.
> 3. **Forgetting the numeric-prefix rule.** Marks are awarded for explicitly stating that `3j` and `5w` are valid — many students omit the count-multiplication grammar.
> 4. **Claiming `vi` has a "default" Insert Mode.** It does not. `vi` *always* opens in Command Mode. This is a favourite viva question.
> 5. **Mixing up `;` and `,`.** `;` repeats the *last* `f/F/t/T` in the **same** direction; `,` repeats in the **opposite** direction.
> 6. **Treating `H`/`M`/`L` as file positions.** They are *screen-relative*. After scrolling, the same `H` lands on a *different file line*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Modes:** `vi` has Command Mode (default) and Insert Mode; switch with `i/a/o/...` and `Esc` respectively.
- **Origin of `hjkl`:** the ADM-3A terminal's arrow-key cluster.
- **Count-multiplication:** every motion accepts an optional numeric prefix.
- **Word vs. WORD:** `w b e` use linguistic word boundaries; `W B E` use whitespace-only boundaries.
- **Line anchors:** `0` (col 1), `^` (first non-blank), `$` (EOL), `g_` (last non-blank).
- **Screen anchors:** `H` (top), `M` (middle), `L` (bottom) — *all three are screen-relative*, not file-relative.
- **File anchors:** `gg` (top), `G` (bottom), `nG` (absolute line), `%` (matching bracket).
- **Inline find/till:** `f F t T ; ,` — `;` and `,` are the *repeaters*.
- **Pattern search:** `/` (forward), `?` (backward), `n` (repeat), `N` (reverse repeat).
- **Boundary behaviour:** motions at the edge of the file are **no-ops**, not wraps.
- **Operator-motion grammar:** `[n] operator [n] motion` — the basis of every powerful `vi` editing command.
- **Practical tip:** For KTU lab examinations, demonstrate the **f + ;** combination at least once — it is the highest-velocity primitive and a marker of competence in the examiner's eye.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Input Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **vi/vim editor** (covered in Module 4 of PEEVT412), **Input Mode** (also called **Insert Mode** or **Editing Mode**) is one of the three primary operational states of the editor. When the editor is in Input Mode, every keystroke typed by the user is **inserted directly into the text buffer** at the current cursor location, rather than being interpreted as an editor command.

The three canonical modes of `vi` are:

| # | Mode Name | Purpose |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Command Mode** (Normal Mode) | Default mode; keys act as editing commands |
| 2 | **Input Mode** (Insert Mode) | Keys are inserted as literal text |
| 3 | **Last Line Mode** (Ex/Command-Line Mode) | Single-line commands beginning with `:` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** *The vi editor — editing modes, moving around in a file, entering and replacing text.* The **Input Mode** is the central mechanism by which "entering text" is achieved inside `vi`.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are using **Microsoft Word**:
- When you press `Ctrl + B`, Word **bolds** selected text (this is a *command*).
- When you press the letter `B`, Word inserts the letter "b" into your document (this is *text input*).

**`vi` separates these two behaviours into different "moods" of the editor.**

- **Command Mode** = You are the "director" giving silent instructions (`copy`, `paste`, `delete`).
- **Input Mode** = You are the "typist" — every key produces a character on screen.
- **Last Line Mode** = You are speaking to the editor's "manager" through a one-line terminal (`:w`, `:q`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** *Input Mode is the only mode in `vi` where your keyboard behaves like a normal typewriter. All other modes treat your keyboard as a remote control.*

### 1.3 Physical Status Indicators

When the editor transitions into Input Mode, the bottom-left status line of the terminal displays a confirmation message. In classic `vi`, no special marker is shown (a common stumbling block for beginners), but in `vim`, the status bar explicitly reads:

```
-- INSERT --
```

This visual cue is a strong indicator that the editor is now in **Input Mode** and will treat the next keystroke as raw text.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Input Mode Exists

`vi` was designed in the era of **dumb terminals** (1976, Bill Joy). These terminals had no dedicated cursor keys, no mouse, and no modifier-rich keyboards. To be efficient without a mouse, Bill Joy mapped **every alphanumeric key** to a *command* in the default state. But that left **no key free to type a letter** — so `vi` had to "borrow" the keyboard for typing by switching to a special mode.

This is the **"Why"** behind Input Mode:
- Default state = Command Mode (every key = a power-tool shortcut).
- Input Mode = a temporary "lend" of the keyboard to the user for literal text entry.

### 2.2 The Complete Set of "Enter Input Mode" Commands

These commands are typed **while in Command Mode**. Each one switches the editor into Input Mode, but places the cursor at a slightly different starting position.

| Command | Full Name | Cursor Position After Switching | Marks If Used Twice |
|---------|-----------|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| `i` | **insert** | *Before* the current character | — |
| `I` | **Insert (capital)** | At the *first non-blank* character of the current line | — |
| `a` | **append** | *After* the current character | — |
| `A` | **Append (capital)** | At the *end* of the current line | — |
| `o` | **open (lowercase)** | On a *new line below* the current line (auto-indented) | — |
| `O` | **Open (capital)** | On a *new line above* the current line (auto-indented) | — |
| `s` | **substitute (one char)** | Replaces the character under the cursor and enters Insert | — |
| `S` | **Substitute (line)** | Deletes the entire current line and enters Insert | `cc` is equivalent |
| `cw` | **change word** | Deletes from cursor to end of word, then Insert | `c` + motion |
| `c$` | **change to end of line** | Deletes to end of line, then Insert | Equivalent to `C` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** The **six primary keys `i`, `I`, `a`, `A`, `o`, `O`** account for roughly **80%** of all Input Mode entry commands asked in KTU University Exams.

### 2.3 How to Leave Input Mode

The user must **manually** return to Command Mode. There are exactly three commonly accepted keys (all are equivalent):

| Key Combination | Behaviour |
|-----------------|-----------|
| `ESC` | Cancels Input Mode, returns to Command Mode. **The most commonly used.** |
| `Ctrl + [` | Same as `ESC` (used on keyboards where ESC is hard to reach). |
| `Ctrl + C` | Same effect, but may interfere with some plugin mappings. |

> [!WARNING]
> A frequent mistake is pressing `Enter` or `Backspace` to "exit" — these insert literal characters instead. There is **no on-screen "Done" button**; the user must press `ESC`.

### 2.4 Behaviour Inside Input Mode — The "What You See Is What You Type" Rule

Once inside Input Mode, the editor behaves like a **dumb typewriter** with a few useful exceptions:

| Keystroke | Result in Input Mode |
|-----------|----------------------|
| Any letter / number / symbol | Inserted literally at cursor position |
| `Enter` | Inserts a newline (breaks the current line) |
| `Backspace` | Deletes the character to the left of the cursor |
| `Delete` (some terminals) | Deletes the character under the cursor |
| `Arrow Keys` | Move cursor (does not exit Input Mode in modern `vim`) |
| `Ctrl + R` followed by `=` | Opens the expression register (vim only) |
| `Ctrl + O` (in vim) | Executes **one** command-mode command, then auto-returns to Insert |
| `Ctrl + V` (vim) | Inserts a literal character (e.g., control codes) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Input Mode is the engineer's primary "canvas" inside `vi`. When editing configuration files like `/etc/nginx/nginx.conf`, shell scripts (`*.sh`), Makefiles (`Makefile`), or C source files (`*.c`), the user spends the majority of editing time *inside* Input Mode.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

The table below is the **single most-tested block** in KTU 2024 question papers for this topic.

| Operation | Command | Mode Transition | Cursor After |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|--------------|
| Insert before cursor | `i` | Command → Input | Before current char |
| Insert at line start | `I` | Command → Input | First non-blank of line |
| Append after cursor | `a` | Command → Input | After current char |
| Append at line end | `A` | Command → Input | End of line |
| Open line below | `o` | Command → Input | New line below |
| Open line above | `O` | Command → Input | New line above |
| Substitute char | `s` | Command → Input | Replaced char position |
| Substitute line | `S` or `cc` | Command → Input | Beginning of line |
| Change word | `cw` | Command → Input | Start of next word |
| Change to EOL | `C` or `c$` | Command → Input | End of line |
| Exit Input Mode | `ESC` or `Ctrl+[` | Input → Command | Unchanged |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix (Exhaustive Walk-throughs)

This section performs an **exhaustive, line-by-line demonstration** of each Input Mode transition. Every step is shown explicitly so that no logical leap is left for the reader to fill in.

### 3.1 Worked Example A — Using `i` (insert before cursor)

**Initial file `hello.txt` (cursor `^` is at position shown):**

```
H^ello World
```

**Step 1 — The user is in Command Mode (default).** The character `H` is visible, and the cursor is on `e`.

**Step 2 — The user presses the key `i`.**
At this exact moment, the editor:
- Recognises `i` as the **insert-before** command.
- Switches its internal state variable from `MODE_COMMAND` to `MODE_INPUT`.
- In `vim`, prints `-- INSERT --` in the status bar.

**Step 3 — The user types the letters `i` and `n`.**
Each keystroke is now a literal character. The buffer transforms as:

```
He^llo World   →   Hein^llo World
```

The cursor moves one column to the right for every character typed — this is the **insertion behaviour**.

**Step 4 — The user presses `ESC`.**
The editor state flips back to `MODE_COMMAND`. The text remains as typed.

**Final buffer state:**

```
Heinllo World
```

### 3.2 Worked Example B — Using `A` (append at line end)

**Initial file:**

```
KTU 2024 Scheme^ 
```

*(Note: the line has trailing spaces after the word `Scheme`.)*

**Step 1 — Cursor sits on the space after `Scheme`. Mode = Command.**

**Step 2 — User presses `A`.**
The editor:
- Computes the column index of the **last non-blank character** of the current line.
- Places the cursor at `last_non_blank + 1` (i.e., one column past the last character).
- Switches to Input Mode.

```
KTU 2024 Scheme^
              ↑ cursor jumps here (after the final 'e')
```

**Step 3 — User types ` Rocks!`.**
Buffer becomes:

```
KTU 2024 Scheme Rocks!^
```

### 3.3 Worked Example C — Using `o` (open line below)

**Initial file (2 lines):**

```
Line One
Line Two^
```

**Step 1 — User is on Line Two, in Command Mode.**

**Step 2 — User presses `o`.**
The editor:
- Inserts a new line *below* the current one.
- Honours the **auto-indent** of the current line (if `Line Two` was indented by 4 spaces, the new line starts with 4 spaces too).
- Places the cursor on this new, empty line.
- Switches to Input Mode.

```
Line One
Line Two
    ^
    (cursor on the brand-new line)
```

**Step 3 — User types `Line Three` and presses `ESC`.**

**Final buffer:**

```
Line One
Line Two
    Line Three
```

### 3.4 Worked Example D — Using `cw` (change word, a *motional* input entry)

**Initial file:**

```
Linux scripting is fun^
```

**Step 1 — User moves cursor onto the word `scripting`, pressing `b` repeatedly until the cursor lands on the `s`.**

```
Linux ^scripting is fun
```

**Step 2 — User presses `c`, then `w`.**
The editor:
- Treats `c` as a *prefix operator* requiring a *motion* (`w` = word forward).
- Deletes the characters from the cursor up to (but not including) the start of the next word.
- Enters Input Mode with the cursor at the deletion point.

Buffer during transition (the deleted region is shown as `~`):

```
Linux ~ is fun
       ^cursor lands here in Input Mode
```

**Step 3 — User types `programming` and presses `ESC`.**

**Final buffer:**

```
Linux programming is fun
```

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Map

| Step | Mode Before | Key Pressed | Mode After | Side Effect on Buffer |
|------|-------------|-------------|------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Command | `i` | Input | None yet |
| 2 | Input | any letter | Input | Letter inserted at cursor |
| 3 | Input | `ESC` | Command | None |
| 4 | Command | `A` | Input | Cursor jumps to EOL |
| 5 | Command | `o` | Input | New blank line created below |
| 6 | Command | `O` | Input | New blank line created above |
| 7 | Command | `cw` | Input | From cursor to end-of-word deleted |
| 8 | Command | `cc` | Input | Entire current line deleted |
| 9 | Input | `Ctrl + O` then `zz` | Input (briefly Command) | Screen re-centres, returns to Insert |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid State-Transition Diagram of vi Modes

The diagram below illustrates every legal transition into and out of **Input Mode**. Each node is a stable state; each arrow is a labelled key/command.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> cmdMode : vi opens file
    cmdMode --> inputMode : i
    cmdMode --> inputMode : I
    cmdMode --> inputMode : a
    cmdMode --> inputMode : A
    cmdMode --> inputMode : o
    cmdMode --> inputMode : O
    cmdMode --> inputMode : s
    cmdMode --> inputMode : S
    cmdMode --> inputMode : cw
    cmdMode --> inputMode : cc
    cmdMode --> lastLineMode : colon
    cmdMode --> lastLineMode : slash
    inputMode --> cmdMode : ESC
    inputMode --> cmdMode : CtrlOpenBracket
    inputMode --> inputMode : typing letters
    inputMode --> inputMode : Enter newline
    inputMode --> inputMode : Backspace delete
    lastLineMode --> cmdMode : Enter
    lastLineMode --> cmdMode : ESC
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Input Subsystem

When `vi` is in Input Mode, the following internal modules collaborate. This block diagram shows the *data flow* from a physical key-press to the screen pixel update.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Keyboard Hardware Interrupt] --> B[Terminal Driver - TTY Layer]
    B --> C[vi Input Handler - Mode Flag Equals INPUT]
    C --> D{Is Key a Control Char?}
    D -- No --> E[Append Char to Text Buffer at Cursor Offset]
    D -- Yes --> F[Special Key Handler - Backspace, Enter, Arrows]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Recompute Cursor Column and Row]
    G --> H[Refresh Terminal Screen via Termcap or Curses]
    H --> I[Display Updated Line on User Monitor]
    C -. checks .-> J[Global Mode Register]
    J -. ESC detected .-> K[Switch Mode Flag to COMMAND]
    K --> cmdOut[Return to Command Mode]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map — "Where does Input Mode fit in the editor's lifecycle?"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Open([User types vi filename]) --> Boot[Editor Initialises in Command Mode]
    Boot --> Navigate[Move Cursor with h j k l or Arrows]
    Navigate --> Decide{Ready to type?}
    Decide -- Yes --> EnterInput[Press i, a, o, etc.]
    EnterInput --> TypeLoop[Type text, backspace, newline]
    TypeLoop --> Exit[Press ESC]
    Exit --> SaveFile[Type colon w to write]
    SaveFile --> Quit[Type colon q to quit]
    Decide -- Need a search --> Search[Press slash then pattern]
    Search --> Navigate
    Quit --> End([File saved and editor closed])
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT412, Q1.a]**

**Q1.** *List any **six** commands used in `vi` Command Mode that switch the editor into Input Mode. For each, state where the cursor is positioned immediately after the switch.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Command | Cursor Position After Switching |
|---|---------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | `i` | Just before the current character |
| 2 | `I` | At the first non-blank character of the current line |
| 3 | `a` | Just after the current character |
| 4 | `A` | At the end of the current line |
| 5 | `o` | On a new (auto-indented) line opened *below* the current line |
| 6 | `O` | On a new (auto-indented) line opened *above* the current line |

*(Any six of `i, I, a, A, o, O, s, S, cw, cc, c$` are acceptable. Full 3 marks require command + cursor position both.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEVT412, Q2.b]**

**Q2.** *Explain how Input Mode is exited in `vi`. Why is this step important when editing a file?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Input Mode is exited by pressing the **`ESC` (Escape) key**. On systems where `ESC` is inconveniently placed, the equivalent key combination **`Ctrl + [`** may be used. In modern `vim`, the explicit press of `Ctrl + C` also works but is less common.

This step is **important** because:
1. While in Input Mode, every keystroke is treated as raw text — the user cannot move the cursor quickly, delete a word, or copy a line.
2. Only after returning to **Command Mode** does `vi` allow navigation, search, deletion (`dd`, `dw`), and saving (`:w`).
3. Forgetting to press `ESC` before typing a command is the single most common cause of corrupted text in `vi` sessions.

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT412, Q11.a — Internal Choice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **three modes of operation in the `vi` editor**. Describe how a user enters and exits each mode. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **Input Mode** of `vi` in detail. List and explain the following input commands: `i`, `I`, `a`, `A`, `o`, `O`, and `cw`. For each, describe the cursor position after execution. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Three modes of `vi` (7 marks):**

`vi` is a **modal editor**, meaning the behaviour of each key depends on the current *mode* the editor is in. The three modes are:

1. **Command Mode (Default Mode):** When `vi` opens a file, it begins in this mode. Keys are interpreted as **commands** — for example, `x` deletes a character, `dd` deletes a line, `/` initiates a search. The user navigates and manipulates text using these single-keystroke commands.

2. **Input Mode (Insert Mode):** Triggered by commands like `i`, `a`, `o`, etc. (detailed in part b). In this mode, **every key inserts a literal character** into the file. The user remains in Input Mode until `ESC` is pressed.

3. **Last Line Mode (Ex/Command-Line Mode):** Triggered by typing `:`, `/`, or `?` from Command Mode. A colon appears at the bottom of the screen; the user types a single command (such as `:w` to save, `:q` to quit, `:set nu` to show line numbers) and presses `Enter`. The editor immediately returns to Command Mode after execution.

**Transitions:**
- Command → Input: press `i`, `a`, `o`, `A`, `I`, `O`, etc.
- Input → Command: press `ESC`.
- Command → Last Line: type `:`, `/`, or `?`.
- Last Line → Command: press `Enter` (executes) or `ESC` (cancels).

A block diagram (refer to Section 4.1) is recommended for full marks.

**Part (b) — Input Mode commands (7 marks):**

| Command | Meaning | Cursor Position After Switching |
|---------|---------|--------------------------------|
| `i` | **insert** | Immediately *before* the character under the cursor |
| `I` | **Insert (capital)** | At the *first non-blank character* of the current line |
| `a` | **append** | *After* the character under the cursor (the cursor advances by one column) |
| `A` | **Append (capital)** | At the *end* of the current line, after the last non-blank character |
| `o` | **open (lowercase)** | On a *new blank line below* the current line, with auto-indent preserved |
| `O` | **Open (capital)** | On a *new blank line above* the current line, with auto-indent preserved |
| `cw` | **change word** | The word from the cursor to its end is deleted; cursor sits at the start of where the word was, ready to type a replacement |

**Real-world illustration (using `o`):**
Suppose the file contains:
```
def main():
    pass
```
With the cursor on the `pass` line, pressing `o` creates a new line below, pre-indented with 4 spaces. The user can type `print("Hello")` and press `ESC` to obtain:
```
def main():
    pass
    print("Hello")
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Input Mode** in the `vi` editor. List all the keys that switch the editor from Command Mode to Input Mode, and describe the cursor position produced by each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Demonstrate, with step-by-step examples, the difference between the commands `i`, `a`, `A`, and `o`. Show the buffer state before and after each command, given the starting line `KTU^Btech`. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Concept of Input Mode (7 marks):**

*Refer to Section 2 of these notes for the full theory. The student must list at minimum: `i, I, a, A, o, O, s, S, cw, cc, c$` and explain the cursor position for at least 6 of them. Marks are split 2 for definition, 5 for the table of commands.*

**Part (b) — Step-by-step demonstration (7 marks):**

**Initial buffer (cursor `^` on `B`):**

```
KTU^Btech
```

**(i) Pressing `i`** — *insert before cursor*
Step 1: Editor enters Input Mode. No buffer change yet.
Step 2: User types `2024-`.
Final buffer:
```
KTU2024-^Btech
```
Cursor is *between* the typed text and the original `B`.

**(ii) Reset and press `a`** — *append after cursor*
After undoing and returning to the starting state, press `a`. The cursor advances by one column to:

```
KTUB^tech
   ↑ cursor here, in Input Mode
```

User types `2024-`:
```
KTUB2024-^tech
```

**(iii) Reset and press `A`** — *append at line end*
Press `A`. The cursor jumps to the end of the line:
```
KTUBtech^
        ↑ cursor here, in Input Mode
```

User types `2024`:
```
KTUBtech2024^
```

**(iv) Reset and press `o`** — *open line below*
Press `o`. A new line is inserted below:
```
KTUBtech
^
(cursor on the new, empty line, in Input Mode)
```

User types `Scheme 2024`:
```
KTUBtech
Scheme 2024^
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark-Loss Hotspots**
> 1. **Writing only commands without cursor position:** The KTU marking scheme awards 1 mark per *command + cursor position* pair. Listing `i, a, o` with no positional explanation loses ~3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing `i` with `I`:** These are *not* the same. `i` = before cursor, `I` = start of line. Examiners will deduct 1 full mark if the distinction is missed.
> 3. **Forgetting `ESC` to exit Input Mode:** When asked "how to return to Command Mode", students often write "press `Enter`" or "press `:q`". Both are wrong. The correct answer is `ESC` (or `Ctrl+[`).
> 4. **Spelling `vi` as `VI` or `Vi`:** Strictly, it is lowercase `vi`. KTU examiners do not usually deduct a mark, but a fully correct answer uses the lowercase form.
> 5. **Mixing up "Input Mode" with "Last Line Mode":** They are *different* modes. Input Mode inserts literal text; Last Line Mode is the `:` prompt for ex-commands.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`vi` has three modes:** Command, Input, and Last Line.
- **Input Mode is for typing text** — every keystroke is inserted literally at the cursor.
- **Six primary entry keys:** `i`, `I`, `a`, `A`, `o`, `O`. Memorise the cursor position of each.
- **Exit with `ESC`** (or `Ctrl+[`). There is no "save" or "close" button while in Input Mode.
- **Motional entry commands** — `cw`, `cc`, `c$`, `s`, `S` — combine deletion with entry to Input Mode.
- **`vim` shows `-- INSERT --`** in the status bar; classic `vi` shows nothing, which confuses beginners.
- **Backspace and `Enter` are still active** in Input Mode — they are *not* commands; they edit text.
- **Engineers use Input Mode constantly** when editing config files, shell scripts, and source code.
- **Common exam trap:** Don't confuse "append" (`a`) with "append at end of line" (`A`) — they are not the same.
- **Auto-indent** is a feature of `o` and `O`; the new line inherits the indentation of the current line.
- **`Ctrl + O` in vim** allows executing *one* Command Mode command without leaving Input Mode (a power-user trick worth 1 extra mark in viva).
- **Practice on a live terminal** — the only way to internalise `vi` modes is hands-on muscle memory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Command Mode: Deleting and Changing Text
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Command Mode: Deleting and Changing Text

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the `vi`/`vim` editor (the canonical POSIX text editor mandated in the KTU PEEVT412 syllabus under **Module 4: Text Processing & The `vi` Editor**), **Command Mode** (also called **Normal Mode**) is the default operational mode entered immediately when the editor is launched. Within this mode, **deleting and changing text** refers to the set of single-stroke or counted multi-stroke commands used to *remove* characters, words, lines, or arbitrary text ranges (**d** family of operators) and to *replace* existing text with new input transitioning temporarily to insert mode (**c** family of operators).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from PEEVT412 Syllabus):**
> "Command mode operations in `vi` for text manipulation – deleting text (x, X, dw, dd, D), changing text (cw, cc, C, r, s), and the repeat modifier (.) – form the foundation of efficient non-modal text editing in UNIX/Linux environments."

> [!NOTE]
> The **Esc** key always returns the editor to Command Mode from any other mode. This is the single most important rule in `vi` and is heavily tested in KTU viva voce and Part A questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Think of Command Mode in `vi` like a **professional carpenter's workbench with specialized tools mounted on the wall**:

- Instead of typing a long menu command like *"File → Edit → Delete Word"*, the carpenter simply *picks up a tool* (a single keystroke) and uses it.
- The **d** key is a *pair of scissors* — but you have to tell it *what* to cut: `dw` (cut a word), `dd` (cut a line), `d$` (cut to end of line).
- The **c** key is a *chisel + eraser* — it removes the old text and **opens a slot** for you to type the replacement.
- The **x** key is a *single-hole punch* — it removes exactly one character where the cursor sits.
- The **number before the command** is the *force multiplier* — `3dd` removes 3 lines, `5x` removes 5 characters.

> [!TIP]
> The golden formula to remember: **`[count] operator [motion]`**
> - `operator` = `d` (delete) or `c` (change)
> - `motion` = what is affected (w, e, $, 0, b, etc.)
> - `count` = how many times to repeat (optional)

---

## 1.3 The Two-Pass Model of `vi` Operators

Unlike most modern editors where you first select then act, `vi` uses a **two-pass (operator-then-motion)** paradigm. This is what makes it intimidating for beginners but blazingly fast for experts.

| Pass | What Happens | Example Triggered |
|------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Pass 1** | You press the **operator** (`d` or `c`). `vi` enters a "waiting for motion" sub-state. | Pressing `d` |
| **Pass 2** | You specify the **motion/range** (where the operator acts). | Then pressing `w` → `dw` deletes one word |
| **Combined** | The text is removed, and (for `c`) `vi` enters Insert Mode. | Cursor lands on the freshly emptied line |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If you press `d` and then panic, simply press **Esc** to safely cancel. The command will not execute until the motion key is supplied. This is a frequent KTU viva question: *"What happens if you press `d` and want to cancel?"* → **Press Esc to abort.**

---

## 1.4 Why This Matters in Linux Engineering

System administrators and DevOps engineers (the primary KTU placement sectors) spend 60–70% of their working hours inside terminal-based text editors editing configuration files (`/etc/nginx/nginx.conf`, `/etc/fstab`, shell scripts, YAML manifests). Mastery of `vi` command-mode operations is what separates a **2-minute edit** from a **20-minute edit** and is a mandatory skill in the **RHCE, LFCS, and LPI** certification tracks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Deletion Operator Family — `d` and `x`

The deletion commands in `vi` are divided into two semantically distinct groups:

### 2.1.1 Character-Level Deletion (No Motion Required)

| Command | Alternate Name | Action | Cursor After | Register Storing |
|---------|---------------|--------|--------------|------------------|
| `x` | "Delete character under cursor" | Deletes the character *at* the cursor | Advances one position right | Unnamed register (deleted) |
| `X` | "Delete character before cursor" | Deletes the character *to the left* of cursor | Stays in place | Unnamed register |
| `D` | Synonym of `d$` | Deletes from cursor to **end of line** | Stays at end of line | Unnamed register |

### 2.1.2 Operator + Motion Deletion (The Two-Pass Form)

The general grammar is:

$$
\text{Delete Command} = \underbrace{[\text{count}]}_{\text{optional multiplier}} \;\underbrace{\texttt{d}}_{\text{operator}} \;\underbrace{[\text{count}]}_{\text{optional}} \;\underbrace{\text{motion}}_{\text{required scope}}
$$

**Valid motions commonly combined with `d`:**

| Motion | Range Affected | Mnemonic |
|--------|----------------|----------|
| `w` | From cursor to start of next word | **w**ord forward |
| `e` | From cursor to end of current word | **e**nd of word |
| `b` | From cursor to start of previous word | **b**ackward word |
| `$` | From cursor to end of current line | to **$** (line end) |
| `0` (zero) | From cursor to start of current line | back to column **0** |
| `^` | From cursor to first non-blank character | caret = first non-blank |
| `(` | To previous sentence | open paren |
| `)` | To next sentence | close paren |
| `{` | To previous paragraph | open brace |
| `}` | To next paragraph | close brace |
| `G` | From cursor to end of file | **G**o to bottom |
| `gg` | From cursor to start of file | **g**o **g**o top |
| `H` | To top of visible screen | **H**ighest |
| `L` | To bottom of visible screen | **L**owest |

### 2.1.3 Line-Level Deletion — The `dd` Form

`dd` is a **special case** where the operator `d` is doubled. In this case, the *line* itself becomes the implicit motion:

| Command | Action |
|---------|--------|
| `dd` | Delete the **current line** entirely (including the newline `\n`) |
| `Ndd` (e.g., `5dd`) | Delete the current line **plus the next N-1 lines** |
| `dNd` (e.g., `d3d`) | Identical to `Ndd` (alternative form) |

> [!NOTE]
> When a whole line is deleted via `dd`, `vi` stores the **entire line including the newline character** in the unnamed register. This is why `p` (paste after) places the line *on a new line*, whereas `P` (paste before) places it on a new line *above* the current line.

---

## 2.2 The Change Operator Family — `c` (Delete + Enter Insert Mode)

The `c` operator is semantically equivalent to `d` **followed by** the `i` (insert) command. It performs a delete and *immediately* places the editor in Insert Mode so you can type replacement text.

### 2.2.1 The General Grammar

$$
\text{Change Command} = \underbrace{[\text{count}]}_{\text{optional}} \;\underbrace{\texttt{c}}_{\text{change operator}} \;\underbrace{[\text{count}]}_{\text{optional}} \;\underbrace{\text{motion}}_{\text{required scope}}
$$

All motions listed in Section 2.1.2 are also valid for `c`.

### 2.2.2 Standalone Change Commands

| Command | Action | Mode Transition |
|---------|--------|-----------------|
| `cw` | Change from cursor to start of next word | Enters Insert Mode |
| `ce` | Change to end of current word (same as `cw` in most contexts) | Enters Insert Mode |
| `cc` | Change the **entire current line** (line is cleared, cursor at start) | Enters Insert Mode |
| `S` (capital) | Synonym of `cc` | Enters Insert Mode |
| `C` (capital) | Synonym of `c$` — change to end of line | Enters Insert Mode |
| `s` (lowercase) | Synonym of `cl` — substitute one character | Enters Insert Mode |
| `r<char>` | **Replace** exactly one character with `<char>`, *stays in Command Mode* | Remains in Command Mode |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction** (Frequently tested in KTU):
> - `s` enters **Insert Mode** (you can type many characters).
> - `r<char>` does **NOT** enter Insert Mode; it overwrites exactly one character and returns to Command Mode.
> - `c` always enters Insert Mode; `d` never does.

---

## 2.3 Repeating Commands — The `.` Operator

The `.` (dot) command **repeats the last text-modifying command** (any of `d`, `c`, `x`, `X`, `r`, `s`, `p`, `P`).

| Command | Effect |
|---------|--------|
| `dw` then `.` | First deletes one word, then deletes the next word |
| `x` then `3.` | Deletes one char, then repeats the deletion 3 more times |
| `cw` then `.<Esc>` pattern | Lets you rapidly change many consecutive words to the same text |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The `.` command does *not* repeat movement commands (like `j`, `k`, `w`). It only repeats commands that *modify text*. This subtle distinction is a common viva question.

---

## 2.4 The Undo & Recovery Commands

| Command | Action |
|---------|--------|
| `u` | Undo the **last single change** (in `vim`, undoes the entire previous *insert* block) |
| `U` | Undo **all changes** on the current line (in classic `vi`) |
| `Ctrl-r` | **Redo** the last undone change (vim extension) |
| `:e!` | Revert file to last saved state (ex-mode) |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula** for any `vi` command-mode text manipulation:
>
> **\[count1\] operator \[count2\] motion**

| Symbol / Token | Meaning | Allowed Values | Mandatory? |
|----------------|---------|----------------|------------|
| `[count1]` | Number of repetitions of the *operator* | $\mathbb{N} \geq 1$, e.g., $2$, $5$, $10$ | Optional |
| `operator` | The action verb | `d` (delete), `c` (change), `y` (yank), `>` (indent), `<` (un-indent), `!` (format) | **Required** |
| `[count2]` | Multiplier for the *motion* (combined with count1) | $\mathbb{N} \geq 1$ | Optional |
| `motion` | The scope/range of action | `w`, `e`, `b`, `0`, `$`, `^`, `G`, `gg`, `H`, `M`, `L`, `(`, `)`, `{`, `}`, `t<char>`, `f<char>`, `/pattern` | **Required** |

**Special Compound Forms (operator doubled = line scope):**

| Command | Equivalent Expanded Form | Meaning |
|---------|--------------------------|---------|
| `dd` | `d$` then `o<Esc>` to remove newline | Delete current line |
| `cc` | `c$` then `0` to reach line start, then re-enter insert | Change current line |
| `yy` | (yank) | Copy current line |
| `>>` | (indent) | Indent current line |
| `<<` | (un-indent) | Un-indent current line |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **DevOps / Config Management:** Editing `~/.ssh/config`, `~/.bashrc`, `Dockerfile`, `k8s manifests` — all require rapid `cw`, `dd`, `r` operations.
2. **Programming:** Quick refactoring of variable names (`*` on a word, `cw`, type new name, `n` then `.` to repeat).
3. **Log Analysis:** Quickly `dd` entire noisy log lines, then `p` to a buffer for later inspection.
4. **System Recovery (Single-User Mode):** `vi` is the **only** editor available in Linux single-user/rescue mode. Mastery of these commands is essential for recovering a broken system.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — The `dw` Command (Delete Word)

**Scenario:** A sample line in the file `report.txt` reads:

```
The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog
```

Cursor is positioned on the space *before* `quick` (column 5).

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial State:} \;& \texttt{The| quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog} \\
\text{Press `d':} \;& \texttt{vi enters operator-pending mode, awaiting motion} \\
\text{Press 'w':} \;& \text{Motion 'w' = from cursor to start of next word} \\
\text{Action:} \;& \text{Characters from cursor (inclusive) up to char before 'b' (exclusive) are deleted} \\
\text{Result:} \;& \texttt{The brown fox jumps over the lazy dog} \\
\text{Cursor Position:} \;& \text{Column 4 (on the space, or column 5 on 'b' depending on vim behavior)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation of Each Step:**

- **Step 1:** `d` alone does nothing visible; `vi` is *waiting* for a motion verb.
- **Step 2:** `w` resolves the motion. The "w" motion consumes the trailing whitespace and lands on the next word's first character.
- **Step 3:** The deleted text is stored in the **unnamed register** (accessible via `p` to paste it back).
- **Step 4:** Notice the cursor is now positioned on the character that *immediately followed* the deleted range, not at the end of the line.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Counted Deletion: `3dd`

**Scenario:** File contents:

```
Line 1:  alpha
Line 2:  beta
Line 3:  gamma
Line 4:  delta
Line 5:  epsilon
```

Cursor is on Line 2.

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial State:} \;& \text{Cursor on 'b' of 'beta'} \\
\text{Command `3dd':} \;& \text{Operator 'd' applied with count 3 to line scope} \\
\text{Internal logic:} \;& d^3_{\text{line}} = \text{delete line 2 + line 3 + line 4} \\
\text{Resulting file:} \;& \begin{cases} \text{Line 1: alpha} \\ \text{Line 2: epsilon} \end{cases} \\
\text{Note:} \;& \text{After deletion, 'epsilon' is renumbered as Line 2, cursor on 'e'.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**

- The `3` prefix is a *count* — it is associated with the `d` operator.
- `dd` is the line-scope form (operator doubled).
- `3dd` means: *"Delete 3 lines starting from the current line."*
- All three lines (including their terminating newline characters) are concatenated into the unnamed register as a single block, so a single `p` would paste all three back on a new line.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — The `cw` Command (Change Word)

**Scenario:** We want to rename the variable `count` to `counter` in a C source line:

```
int count = 0;
```

Cursor is on the `c` of `count`.

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial State:} \;& \texttt{int |count = 0;} \quad \text{(cursor on 'c')} \\
\text{Press 'c':} \;& \text{Operator 'c' armed, waiting for motion} \\
\text{Press 'w':} \;& \text{Motion 'w' = forward to start of next word ('=')} \\
\text{Delete Action:} \;& \text{Characters 'count ' (with trailing space) are removed} \\
\text{Mode Switch:} \;& \text{vi enters INSERT MODE automatically} \\
\text{Screen Display:} \;& \texttt{int| = 0;} \quad \text{(with '-- INSERT --' status bar)} \\
\text{User types 'counter':} \;& \\
\text{Final State:} \;& \texttt{int counter = 0;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation of Mode Transition:**

- This is the *defining behavior* of the `c` family. After the motion completes, `vi` does not leave you in command mode; it drops you into insert mode so you can immediately type the replacement.
- The trailing space is preserved by the `w` motion (it eats the space as part of moving to the next word).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — The `r` Command (Single-Char Replace)

**Scenario:** A typo in a shell script has `ehco` instead of `echo`. Cursor is on the `h`.

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial State:} \;& \texttt{e|hco "Hello";} \\
\text{Press 'r':} \;& \text{vi prompts: "press the replacement character"} \\
\text{Press 'c':} \;& \text{Substitution occurs} \\
\text{Final State:} \;& \texttt{ecco "Hello";} \\
\text{Mode:} \;& \text{STILL in Command Mode (no -- INSERT -- shown)} \\
\text{Key Distinction:} \;& \text{`r' replaced exactly 1 char; `s' would have entered insert mode for many chars.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Command

The following decision table helps KTU students select the correct command during viva and lab exams:

| Goal | Best Command | Why |
|------|-------------|-----|
| Remove a typo on the cursor | `x` | Single char, stays in cmd mode |
| Remove a typo *before* cursor | `X` | Single char to the left |
| Replace a typo on the cursor with a known char | `r<char>` | One char replaced, no mode change |
| Replace a typo and possibly add more text | `s` | One char deleted, enters insert mode |
| Remove a whole word | `dw` or `daw` (vim) | Word scope |
| Rename a whole word | `cw` | Word scope + insert mode |
| Delete current line entirely | `dd` | Line scope, fast |
| Delete from cursor to end of line | `D` or `d$` | Partial line |
| Change rest of line from cursor | `C` or `c$` | Partial line + insert mode |
| Wipe a line and start over | `cc` or `S` | Line + insert mode |
| Delete 5 lines | `5dd` | Counted line deletion |
| Delete next 3 words | `d3w` | Counted word deletion |
| Repeat last edit quickly | `.` | Power multiplier |

---

## 3.6 Composite Real-World Workflow

**Problem:** A junior admin typed a wrong IP address in 12 consecutive lines of `/etc/hosts`. Correct value: `192.168.1.10`. Wrong value: `10.0.0.1`. All 12 lines look like:

```
10.0.0.1  server01  # Old IP, should be 192.168.1.10
```

**Optimal `vi` Workflow:**

1. Position cursor on the `1` of `10.0.0.1` (line 1).
2. Press `0` to jump to column 1.
3. Press `cw` (change word) — deletes `10.0.0.1`, enters insert mode.
4. Type `192.168.1.10` and press `Esc`.
5. Press `j` to move down one line.
6. Press `.` to repeat the change.
7. Repeat steps 5–6 for the remaining 10 lines.

**Total keystrokes for 12 lines:** ~50 (versus >300 in a GUI editor).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time saved} &\approx \frac{300 - 50}{300} \times 100\% \\
&= 83.3\% \text{ efficiency gain}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *practical* reason KTU examiners emphasize the `.` command.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Hierarchy of `vi` Command-Mode Text Manipulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Command Mode Text Manipulation"]:::root --> B["Delete Family d"]:::branch
    A --> C["Change Family c"]:::branch
    A --> D["Repeat Operator dot"]:::leaf
    A --> E["Undo Recovery"]:::leaf

    B --> B1["Character Level x X D"]:::sub
    B --> B2["Word Level dw de db"]:::sub
    B --> B3["Line Level dd Ndd"]:::sub
    B --> B4["Block Level dG dH"]:::sub

    C --> C1["Character Level s r"]:::sub
    C --> C2["Word Level cw ce cb"]:::sub
    C --> C3["Line Level cc S C"]:::sub
    C --> C4["Block Level cG cH"]:::sub

    E --> E1["u Undo"]:::sub
    E --> E2["Ctrl r Redo"]:::sub
    E --> E3["U Undo line"]:::sub

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sub fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2a47,stroke-width:1px
    classDef leaf fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#a04d10,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 The Two-Pass Operator Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Idle Command Mode"]:::state --> A2{"User presses d or c"}:::decision
    A2 -->|d| B1["Operator Pending: d armed"]:::state
    A2 -->|c| B2["Operator Pending: c armed"]:::state
    A2 -->|x X s r D| X1["Immediate single-key action"]:::state
    B1 --> C1{"Awaiting motion or count"}:::decision
    B2 --> C1
    C1 -->|w e b| M1["Word scope"]:::scope
    C1 -->|0 $ ^|M2["Line segment scope"]:::scope
    C1 -->|G gg H L| M3["Block scope"]:::scope
    C1 -->|Esc| A1
    M1 --> D1["Perform deletion"]:::action
    M2 --> D1
    M3 --> D1
    M1 --> D2["Perform change enter insert"]:::action
    M2 --> D2
    M3 --> D2
    D1 --> A1
    D2 --> A3["Insert Mode awaiting text"]:::state
    A3 -->|Esc| A1
    X1 --> A1

    classDef state fill:#264478,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef decision fill:#bf9000,stroke:#806000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef scope fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#548235,color:#0b2a47,stroke-width:1px
    classDef action fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Lifecycle of a Single `cw` Command

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant V as vi Editor
    participant R as Unnamed Register
    participant M as Mode State

    Note over V,M: Initial state: Command Mode
    U->>V: press c
    V->>M: switch to operator pending state
    M-->>U: status bar updates to indicate pending operator
    U->>V: press w
    V->>R: store deleted text in register
    V->>M: switch to Insert Mode
    M-->>U: status bar shows INSERT
    U->>V: type replacement text
    V->>M: text inserted at cursor
    U->>V: press Esc
    V->>M: return to Command Mode
    Note over V,M: Final state: Command Mode with edited buffer
```

## 4.4 Command-Mode Command Resolution Table (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["User Keypress"]:::in --> Parse["Tokenize: count operator motion"]:::proc
    Parse --> OpCheck{"Operator present?"}:::gate
    OpCheck -->|No Single Key| Single["Execute single-key command x X D r s"]:::action
    OpCheck -->|Yes| MotCheck{"Motion provided?"}:::gate
    MotCheck -->|No Esc| Cancel["Cancel operator pending"]:::action
    MotCheck -->|Yes| Scope["Resolve scope using count x motion"]:::proc
    Scope --> Apply["Apply operator over resolved range"]:::proc
    Apply --> ModCheck{"Is operator c or s?"}:::gate
    ModCheck -->|Yes| Ins["Enter Insert Mode"]:::state
    ModCheck -->|No| Cmd["Remain in Command Mode"]:::state
    Ins --> EscWait["Await Esc key"]:::state
    EscWait --> Cmd
    Single --> Cmd
    Cancel --> Cmd

    classDef in fill:#7030a0,stroke:#3a1557,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef gate fill:#ffc000,stroke:#806000,color:#0b2a47,stroke-width:2px
    classDef action fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef state fill:#548235,stroke:#2e5e1a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Differentiate between `dw` and `cw` in `vi` command mode. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Exam Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `dw` | `cw` |
|--------|------|------|
| Operation | Delete a word | Change a word |
| Underlying operator | `d` (delete) | `c` (change) |
| Effect on text | Removes the word, keeps gap | Removes the word, **replaces** it with new input |
| Mode after execution | Stays in **Command Mode** | Switches to **Insert Mode** |
| Register storage | Yes (unnamed register) | Yes (unnamed register) |
| Equivalent combination | `d` + `w` motion | `d` + `w` motion + auto-enter insert |

**Awarding Marks:**
- (1 mark) — Both commands delete text in the same physical sense.
- (1 mark) — `cw` enters Insert Mode whereas `dw` does not.
- (1 mark) — Stated the deletion of trailing whitespace behavior correctly.

---

### Question 2: What is the purpose of the `.` (dot) command in `vi`? Can it repeat movement commands? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `.` (dot) command is the **repeat modifier** in `vi`. It re-executes the *most recent text-modifying command*, including all arguments, counts, and motions used with that command.

- **Repeats:** `d`, `c`, `x`, `X`, `r`, `s`, `p`, `P`, `y`, and any insert-mode text typed.
- **Does NOT repeat:** Pure movement commands such as `h`, `j`, `k`, `l`, `w`, `b`, `G`, `gg`, `/pattern`. Movement-only commands do not modify the buffer, so the dot has nothing to repeat.

**Example:** Typing `cw`, then `Hello`, then `Esc`, then moving with `j`, then pressing `.` replaces the word under the new cursor line with `Hello` automatically.

**Awarding Marks:**
- (1 mark) — Defines dot as repeat-of-last-change.
- (1 mark) — Example demonstrating repetition.
- (1 mark) — Explicitly states it does **not** repeat motion commands.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A: Part (a) — Explain the various commands in `vi` to delete text in command mode with examples. Part (b) — Explain the various commands in `vi` to change text in command mode with examples. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, KTU Model Paper 2 - 2024 Scheme]`

#### Part (a) — Deletion Commands (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Deletion commands in `vi` are grouped into three categories:

**1. Single-Character Deletion (1 mark)**

- `x` — Deletes the character at the cursor position. Cursor advances one column.
  - Example: In `cat`, cursor on `a` → `x` → `ct`.
- `X` — Deletes the character to the left of the cursor. Cursor stays in place.
  - Example: In `cat`, cursor on `a` → `X` → `at`.
- `D` (or `d$`) — Deletes from cursor to end of line.
  - Example: `Hello World`, cursor on `W` → `D` → `Hello ` (with trailing space).

**2. Word-Level Deletion (2 marks)**

- `dw` — Delete from cursor to start of next word. Trailing whitespace is consumed.
  - Example: `one two three`, cursor on `t` of `two` → `dw` → `one three`.
- `de` — Delete from cursor to end of *current* word (whitespace not consumed).
- `db` — Delete from cursor to start of *previous* word.
- `dNw` (e.g., `d3w`) — Delete 3 words forward.
- `Ndw` (e.g., `3dw`) — Delete 3 consecutive words starting from cursor (functionally equivalent to `d3w`).

**3. Line-Level Deletion (2 marks)**

- `dd` — Delete the entire current line (including newline).
- `Ndd` (e.g., `5dd`) — Delete N consecutive lines starting from current line.
- `:Nd` (ex-mode) — Delete line N.

**4. Block-Level Deletion (2 marks)**

- `dG` — Delete from current line to end of file.
- `d1G` or `dgg` — Delete from current line to start of file.
- `dH` — Delete from current line to top of screen.
- `dL` — Delete from current line to bottom of screen.

**Demonstration Trace (bonus 0.5 mark if included):**

```
File:        /etc/hosts  (3 lines)
Line 1:      127.0.0.1   localhost
Line 2:      192.168.1.5 router
Line 3:      10.0.0.1    server

Action:      Cursor on line 2, press `dd`
Result:      Line 2 removed, file becomes 2 lines.
Cursor now on line 2 (which was line 3, now renumbered).
```

---

#### Part (b) — Change Commands (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Change commands are essentially *delete + enter insert mode*. The grammar is the same as deletion but with `c` instead of `d`.

**1. Character-Level Change (1.5 marks)**

- `r<char>` — Replaces exactly one character at the cursor and **remains in Command Mode**.
  - Example: `cat` → cursor on `a` → `r o` → `cot`.
- `s` — Substitutes the character at cursor (deletes it, enters Insert Mode for replacement).
  - Example: `cat` → cursor on `a` → `s` → type `ow` → `cowt`.

**2. Word-Level Change (2 marks)**

- `cw` — Change from cursor to start of next word; enters Insert Mode.
  - Example: `int x = 5;` → cursor on `x` → `cw` → type `count` → `int count = 5;`.
- `ce` — Change to end of current word (similar to `cw`).
- `cb` — Change backward to start of previous word.
- `cNw` (e.g., `c3w`) — Change 3 words.

**3. Line-Level Change (2 marks)**

- `cc` — Change the entire current line. The line is cleared, cursor goes to column 1, Insert Mode entered.
  - Example: `print("Hello")` → `cc` → type `echo "Hi"` → `echo "Hi"`.
- `S` (uppercase) — Synonym of `cc`.
- `C` (uppercase) — Change from cursor to end of line, Insert Mode entered.
- `NcC` or `Ncc` (e.g., `3cc`) — Change 3 lines.

**4. Block-Level Change (1 mark)**

- `cG` — Change from current line to end of file.
- `c1G` or `cgg` — Change from current line to start of file.

**5. Combining with `.` (0.5 mark)**

A typical KTU lab exercise uses:
1. `cw` to change the first occurrence of a word.
2. Press `Esc` to return to Command Mode.
3. Use `n` to find the next occurrence (if searching via `/`).
4. Press `.` to repeat the change.

This is the **idiomatic refactoring pattern** that examiners reward.

---

### Question B (Alternative Internal Choice): Part (a) — With a neat diagram, explain the operator-motion execution model of `vi` for deleting text. Part (b) — Compare and contrast the `d`, `c`, and `r` operators in terms of mode transitions and scope. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Anticipated), KTU Model Paper 3]`

#### Part (a) — Operator-Motion Execution Model (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

In `vi`, every text-modifying command follows the **operator-motion paradigm**. The execution unfolds in three distinct phases:

**Phase 1 — Operator Capture (1 mark)**

When the user presses `d` (or `c`, `y`, `>`, `<`), the editor does **not** execute immediately. Instead, it enters a *sub-state* called the **operator-pending mode**, where it awaits a *motion verb* to define the scope.

**Phase 2 — Motion Resolution (2 marks)**

The motion verb (e.g., `w`, `$`, `G`) defines the *range of characters* or *lines* the operator will affect. The editor computes a virtual "selection" from the current cursor position to the motion's destination.

**Phase 3 — Execution and Mode Transition (2 marks)**

- For `d`: The selected range is removed. The cursor is repositioned. **Mode remains Command Mode.**
- For `c`: The selected range is removed. **Mode transitions to Insert Mode.**
- The deleted text is stored in the unnamed register.

**Diagram (Textual Representation, 2 marks):**

```
+---------------------+
|   Command Mode      |
|   (Initial)         |
+----------+----------+
           |  press 'd'
           v
+---------------------+
| Operator-Pending    |
| (awaiting motion)   |
+----------+----------+
           |  press 'w'
           v
+---------------------+
| Motion Resolved     |
| scope = current ->  |
| start of next word  |
+----------+----------+
           |  automatic
           v
+---------------------+
| Execution: delete   |
| range, stay in      |
| Command Mode        |
+---------------------+
```

For `c`, the final state would instead be Insert Mode.

**Awarding Marks:**
- (2 marks) — Three phases described clearly.
- (2 marks) — Distinction between `d` and `c` final state explained.
- (2 marks) — Correct diagram or textual representation.
- (1 mark) — Mention of register storage.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparing `d`, `c`, and `r` (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `d` (Delete) | `c` (Change) | `r` (Replace) |
|---------|-------------|-------------|--------------|
| Motion required? | Yes (except `dd`, `D`) | Yes (except `cc`, `C`) | **No** — single-key |
| Mode after execution | Command Mode | **Insert Mode** | Command Mode |
| Scope | Operator-defined | Operator-defined | **Exactly 1 character** |
| Affects multiple chars | Yes (via motion/count) | Yes (via motion/count) | No (always 1 char) |
| Stores in register | Yes | Yes | No (does not use a register) |
| Typical use case | Removing text | Replacing text with longer content | Quick typo fix of known length |
| Capital variant | `D` = `d$` | `C` = `c$` | `R` = enters Replace Mode (multi-char) |

**Critical Distinctions (1 mark each):**

1. **Mode Transition:** `c` always enters Insert Mode; `d` never does; `r` does not.
2. **Scope Granularity:** `r` is *always* single-character; `d` and `c` are *always* multi-character.
3. **Register Usage:** `d` and `c` push to the unnamed register; `r` does not (no need to undo by pasting).
4. **Capital Variants:** `D` and `C` both mean "to end of line," but `D` stays in command mode and `C` enters insert mode. `R` (capital) is a totally different mode (Replace Mode for overstrike).
5. **Power Multiplier Compatibility:** `r` does **not** accept a count prefix (only one char to replace); `d` and `c` accept `Nd` or `Nc` syntax.

**Awarding Marks:**
- (3 marks) — Comprehensive comparison table.
- (3 marks) — Critical distinctions elaborated.
- (1 mark) — Mention of capital variants and their behavior.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones for This Topic:**
> 1. **Conflating `x` with `X`:** Many students state `x` deletes "the previous character." It deletes the character **at** the cursor, not before. `X` deletes the *previous* character. This error alone costs full marks on related questions.
> 2. **Forgetting that `r` stays in Command Mode:** A frequent error is stating `r` enters Insert Mode. It does NOT — it overwrites exactly one character and remains in Command Mode. Only `s` and the `c` family enter Insert Mode.
> 3. **Mis-stating the dot command's scope:** Students incorrectly claim `.` repeats *any* last command. It only repeats text-modifying commands, not motions.
> 4. **Ignoring the count prefix rule:** `5dd` deletes 5 lines, but `5x` deletes 5 characters. Misplacing the count (e.g., `d5d` vs `5dd`) shows incomplete understanding.
> 5. **Not mentioning registers:** KTU expects you to know that `d` and `c` populate the unnamed register (1 mark for any question on deletion/change). Skipping this loses a mark.
> 6. **Confusing `dd` with `D`:** `dd` deletes the *entire line* (including newline); `D` deletes from cursor to *end of line* (preserves the line itself).
> 7. **Capital `S` vs lowercase `s`:** `S` is a synonym for `cc` (change whole line); `s` is a synonym for `cl` (substitute one char + insert mode). Reversing these will lose marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Command Mode** is the *default* state of `vi` and the only mode where deletion/change commands operate.
- **Esc** always returns to Command Mode from any other mode — keep this muscle memory.
- **Master formula:** `[count1] operator [count2] motion`, where `operator` is `d` (delete) or `c` (change).
- **Single-key deletion:** `x` (at cursor), `X` (before cursor), `D` (to end of line).
- **Word-level deletion:** `dw` (to next word), `de` (to end of current word), `db` (to previous word).
- **Line-level deletion:** `dd` (one line), `Ndd` (N lines).
- **Single-key change:** `r<char>` (replace 1 char, stays in cmd mode), `s` (substitute 1 char, enters insert mode), `R` (enter Replace Mode for overstrike).
- **Word-level change:** `cw` (change word, enters insert mode), `ce`, `cb`.
- **Line-level change:** `cc` (change whole line, enters insert mode), `S` (synonym of `cc`), `C` (change to end of line).
- **The `.` (dot) command** repeats the last text-modifying command — but not motion commands.
- **Undo:** `u` (single change undo), `U` (undo all line changes), `Ctrl-r` (redo).
- **Register storage:** Every `d` and `c` operation populates the unnamed register, accessible via `p`/`P` for paste.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Capital variants (`D`, `C`, `S`, `R`) all have distinct meanings from lowercase; memorize each.
- **Two-pass model:** `vi` waits for an operator first, then a motion — pressing Esc cancels a half-typed command.
- **Mode transition rule:** `c` family → Insert Mode; `d` family → Command Mode; `r` → Command Mode (one char only); `s` → Insert Mode.
- **Efficiency mantra:** Combine `cw` + `n` + `.` for rapid refactoring of repeated words.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading and Writing Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT412 - LINUX AND SCRIPTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reading and Writing Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**File I/O (Input/Output)** in Linux refers to the systematic process of reading data from files and writing data to files using shell commands, redirection operators, and built-in commands. Every open file in Linux is represented by a non-negative integer called a **File Descriptor (FD)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** File handling is the backbone of shell scripting. Mastery of redirections (`>`, `>>`, `<`, `2>`, `&>`), the `read` command, pipes (`|`), and here documents (`<<`) is mandatory for Part A and Part B questions.

### Standard File Descriptors (FDs)

| FD | Name | Default Device | Purpose |
|----|------|----------------|---------|
| `0` | **stdin**  | Keyboard | Standard Input |
| `1` | **stdout** | Terminal | Standard Output |
| `2` | **stderr** | Terminal | Standard Error |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine Linux as a **post office**:
- **stdin (FD 0)** → The *mailbox slot* where you drop incoming letters (input).
- **stdout (FD 1)** → The *outgoing tray* where successful letters are placed (normal output).
- **stderr (FD 2)** → The *complaint box* where error reports go separately so they don't mix with normal mail.

When you use `>` to redirect output, you're essentially saying: *"Don't put the outgoing mail in the tray — file it in cabinet A instead."*

## 1.3 Two Pathways for File I/O

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Main Mechanisms:**
> 1. **Shell Redirection Operators** (`>`, `>>`, `<`, `2>`, `&>`, `|`) — handled by the shell itself, no command needed.
> 2. **Built-in Commands** (`read`, `cat`, `echo`, `printf`, `tee`, `dd`, `sort`) — explicit commands for I/O.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The `echo` and `printf` Commands (Writing Basics)

- `echo` → Prints text followed by a newline.
- `printf` → Formatted printing (C-style), no automatic newline.

```bash
echo "Hello KTU"          # Writes to stdout
printf "Score: %d\n" 95   # Formatted output
```

## 2.2 The `read` Command (Reading Input)

The `read` command reads **one line** from standard input and assigns it to a variable.

```bash
read -p "Enter name: " name
```

### Important `read` Options (High-Yield for KTU)

| Option | Meaning | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| `-p` | Display prompt | `read -p "Age: " age` |
| `-s` | Silent (hide input, for passwords) | `read -sp "PIN: " pin` |
| `-r` | Raw mode (no backslash escape) | `read -r line` |
| `-t N` | Timeout after N seconds | `read -t 5 input` |
| `-n N` | Read only N characters | `read -n 1 key` |
| `-a` | Read into an array | `read -a arr` |

## 2.3 Output Redirection Operators

| Operator | Meaning | Overwrites? |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `>` | Redirect stdout to file | **Yes** (truncates) |
| `>>` | Append stdout to file | No (appends) |
| `2>` | Redirect stderr to file | **Yes** |
| `2>>` | Append stderr to file | No |
| `&>` | Redirect both stdout and stderr | **Yes** |
| `>&` | Duplicate a file descriptor | — |

## 2.4 Input Redirection

| Operator | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `<` | Redirect file content to stdin |
| `<<` | Here Document (Heredoc) |
| `<<<` | Here String |

### Heredoc Example

```bash
cat << EOF > report.txt
This is line 1
This is line 2
EOF
```

## 2.5 The Pipe Operator `|`

Pipes send the **stdout** of one command to the **stdin** of the next.

```bash
cat file.txt | grep "KTU" | wc -l
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Pipes do NOT redirect stderr. To pipe stderr too, use `2>&1 |` or `|&`.

## 2.6 The `tee` Command

`tee` reads from stdin and writes to **both stdout and a file simultaneously** — like a "T-junction pipe."

```bash
ls -l | tee listing.txt
```

## 2.7 File Descriptors — Open / Close / Duplicate

You can open custom file descriptors in bash:

```bash
exec 3> output.txt        # Open FD 3 for writing
echo "Hello" >&3          # Write to FD 3
exec 3>&-                 # Close FD 3

exec 4< input.txt         # Open FD 4 for reading
read -r line <&4          # Read from FD 4
exec 4<&-                 # Close FD 4
```

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax | Behavior |
|---------|--------|----------|
| Write (truncate) | `cmd > file` | Creates/overwrites file with stdout |
| Write (append) | `cmd >> file` | Appends stdout to file |
| Read | `cmd < file` | Feeds file content as stdin |
| Stderr → file | `cmd 2> file` | Captures errors only |
| Both → file | `cmd &> file` | Captures stdout + stderr |
| Both → append | `cmd &>> file` | Appends stdout + stderr |
| Heredoc | `cmd << EOF ... EOF` | Multi-line input block |
| Pipe | `cmd1 \| cmd2` | stdout of cmd1 → stdin of cmd2 |
| Tee | `cmd \| tee file` | Writes to file AND stdout |
| Open FD | `exec N> file` | Custom write descriptor |
| Close FD | `exec N>&-` | Close descriptor N |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Reading a File Line by Line

**Problem:** Write a shell script that reads `marks.txt` line by line and prints each line with its line number.

### Step-by-Step Solution

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# script: read_lines.sh
n=1
while read -r line
do
    echo "Line $n: $line"
    n=$((n + 1))
done < marks.txt
```

### Logical Breakdown

| Step | Code | Explanation |
|------|------|-------------|
| 1 | `n=1` | Initialize line counter at 1. |
| 2 | `while read -r line` | Loop continues as long as a line is read successfully into `$line`. |
| 3 | `do ... done < marks.txt` | The redirection feeds `marks.txt` into stdin of the entire `while` loop. |
| 4 | `n=$((n + 1))` | Arithmetic increment using `$((...))`. |
| 5 | `-r` | Prevents backslash interpretation (raw mode). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Point:** The redirection `< marks.txt` must be placed **after `done`**, not after `read`. Otherwise, only the first line is read because the subshell created for the pipe gets a fresh copy of the file descriptor.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Logging Errors and Output Separately

**Problem:** Run a backup script and save successful output to `success.log` and errors to `error.log`.

### Solution

```bash
#!/bin/bash
./backup.sh > success.log 2> error.log
```

### Analytical Matrix

| Stream | FD | Default Destination | New Destination |
|--------|----|---------------------|-----------------|
| stdout | 1 | Terminal | `success.log` |
| stderr | 2 | Terminal | `error.log` |

### Alternative: Combined Log

```bash
./backup.sh &> combined.log
./backup.sh > combined.log 2>&1
```

The order **matters** — `> file 2>&1` is correct, but `2>&1 > file` is **wrong** (it redirects stderr to the original stdout first, then redirects stdout to the file).

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Using `read` with Prompt and Validation

**Problem:** Prompt the user for an age between 1 and 120. Re-prompt on invalid input.

```bash
#!/bin/bash
while true
do
    read -p "Enter age (1-120): " age
    if [[ "$age" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]] && [ "$age" -ge 1 ] && [ "$age" -le 120 ]; then
        echo "Valid age: $age"
        break
    else
        echo "Invalid input. Try again." >&2
    fi
done
```

### Step Logic

| Step | Code | Purpose |
|------|------|---------|
| 1 | `while true` | Infinite retry loop. |
| 2 | `read -p "..." age` | Prompt and read input. |
| 3 | `=~ ^[0-9]+$` | Regex check: digits only. |
| 4 | `[ "$age" -ge 1 ]` | Numeric comparison ≥ 1. |
| 5 | `>&2` | Send error to stderr. |

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Heredoc to Write Multi-line File

**Problem:** Create a configuration file using a heredoc.

```bash
#!/bin/bash
cat << CONFIG > app.conf
# Application Configuration
PORT=8080
DEBUG=true
DB_HOST=localhost
CONFIG
```

> [!NOTE]
> The delimiter (`CONFIG` here) can be any word. To suppress variable expansion in heredoc, quote the delimiter: `cat << 'EOF'`.

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Custom File Descriptors

**Problem:** Log all "INFO" messages to `info.log` and "ERROR" messages to `error.log` using custom FDs.

```bash
#!/bin/bash
exec 3> info.log
exec 4> error.log

echo "INFO: Script started" >&3
echo "ERROR: Disk full" >&4
echo "INFO: Retrying" >&3

exec 3>&-
exec 4>&-
```

### Stream Table

| FD | Purpose | File |
|----|---------|------|
| 1 | stdout (default) | Terminal |
| 2 | stderr (default) | Terminal |
| 3 | Custom info stream | `info.log` |
| 4 | Custom error stream | `error.log` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linux Standard Streams Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    KB[Keyboard] -->|input| FD0[FD 0 stdin]
    FD0 --> SH[Shell / Command]
    SH --> FD1[FD 1 stdout]
    SH --> FD2[FD 2 stderr]
    FD1 --> T[Terminal]
    FD2 --> T
    FD1 -.->|redirect >| F1[output.txt]
    FD2 -.->|redirect 2>| F2[error.log]
    F1A[input.txt] -.->|redirect <| FD0
```

## 4.2 Redirection Operator Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to redirect stream?] --> B{Which stream?}
    B -->|stdout| C{Overwrite or Append?}
    C -->|Overwrite| D[Use >]
    C -->|Append| E[Use >>]
    B -->|stderr| F{Overwrite or Append?}
    F -->|Overwrite| G[Use 2>]
    F -->|Append| H[Use 2>>]
    B -->|both| I[Use and or and 2 greater than 1]
    B -->|stdin| J{Source type?}
    J -->|File| K[Use less than]
    J -->|Multi-line| L[Use less than less than HEREDOC]
    J -->|Single string| M[Use less than less than less than HERESTR]
```

## 4.3 File I/O Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserSpace[User Space - Shell Script]
        CMD[Command / Builtin] --> BUF[Buffer]
        BUF --> FD0[FD 0]
        BUF --> FD1[FD 1]
        BUF --> FD2[FD 2]
        BUF --> FD3[FD 3 Custom]
    end
    subgraph KernelSpace[Kernel Space]
        FD0 --> K0[VFS Read]
        FD1 --> K1[VFS Write]
        FD2 --> K2[VFS Write]
        FD3 --> K3[VFS Write]
    end
    subgraph Devices[Physical Devices]
        K0 --> DEV1[Keyboard or File]
        K1 --> DEV2[Terminal or File]
        K2 --> DEV3[Terminal or File]
        K3 --> DEV4[Custom Log File]
    end
```

## 4.4 `while read` Loop Data Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant File as marks.txt
    participant Shell as while loop
    participant Read as read -r line
    participant Body as loop body
    File->>Shell: redirected as stdin (FD 0)
    Shell->>Read: feed one line
    Read->>Body: store in $line
    Body->>Shell: execute commands
    Shell->>Read: read next line
    Read-->>Shell: returns 1 on EOF (loop ends)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `>` and `>>` redirection operators with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `>` | `>>` |
|---------|-----|------|
| Operation | Redirects stdout to file | Appends stdout to file |
| Existing file | **Truncates** (overwrites) | Preserves existing content |
| New file | Creates if absent | Creates if absent |

**Example:**
```bash
echo "Hello" > log.txt    # Creates/overwrites log.txt
echo "World" >> log.txt   # Appends "World" to log.txt
```

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the three standard file descriptors in Linux.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **FD 0 (stdin):** Standard input, default source is the keyboard. Used to receive data into a program.
- **FD 1 (stdout):** Standard output, default destination is the terminal. Used for normal program output.
- **FD 2 (stderr):** Standard error, default destination is the terminal. Used for error/diagnostic messages so they remain separate from normal output.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain input and output redirection in Linux with suitable examples. Differentiate between `>`, `>>`, `<`, `2>`, and `&>` operators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a shell script that reads employee records (name, basic pay) from a file `employees.txt` and writes the gross salary (basic + 20% DA + 10% HRA) into `salary.txt`. Each line of the file has the format: `Name:BasicPay`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Redirection Explanation:**

| Operator | Syntax | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `>` | `cmd > file` | Send stdout to file (overwrite) |
| `>>` | `cmd >> file` | Append stdout to file |
| `<` | `cmd < file` | Read file content as stdin |
| `2>` | `cmd 2> file` | Send stderr to file |
| `&>` | `cmd &> file` | Send both stdout and stderr to file |

**Examples:**
```bash
ls -l > files.txt          # Save directory listing
echo "Done" >> status.log  # Append to log
sort < names.txt           # Read from file
gcc prog.c 2> compile.err  # Capture compilation errors
./script.sh &> all.log     # Capture everything
```

**(b) Salary Script:**

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# script: salary.sh
> salary.txt   # Truncate output file
while IFS=':' read -r name basic
do
    da=$(echo "$basic * 0.20" | bc)
    hra=$(echo "$basic * 0.10" | bc)
    gross=$(echo "$basic + $da + $hra" | bc)
    echo "$name:Gross=$gross" >> salary.txt
done < employees.txt

echo "Salary file generated."
cat salary.txt
```

**Sample `employees.txt`:**
```
Anand:30000
Beena:45000
Charles:25000
```

**Sample `salary.txt` output:**
```
Anand:Gross=39000
Beena:Gross=58500
Charles:Gross=32500
```

**Logic Steps:**
1. Truncate `salary.txt` using `>`.
2. Use `IFS=':'` so the colon is the field separator.
3. `read -r name basic` reads two fields.
4. Compute DA (20%) and HRA (10%) using `bc` for floating-point arithmetic.
5. Gross = Basic + DA + HRA.
6. Append result to `salary.txt`.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** What is a heredoc? Explain with a script that creates a multi-line configuration file using `cat << EOF`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a shell script that uses a custom file descriptor (FD 3) to write "DEBUG" messages to a log file, while normal output goes to the terminal. Demonstrate opening, writing, and closing the FD. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Heredoc Explanation:**

A **Here Document (heredoc)** is a redirection method that allows a shell script to pass multiple lines of input to a command. It begins with `<<` followed by a delimiter word (e.g., `EOF`) and ends when that delimiter appears on a line by itself.

**Script: create_config.sh**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
cat << EOF > server.conf
# Web Server Configuration
SERVER_NAME=ktu-server
PORT=80
MAX_CLIENTS=100
LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG
EOF
echo "Configuration file created:"
cat server.conf
```

**Key Points:**
- The text between `<< EOF` and `EOF` is treated as stdin for `cat`.
- Variable expansion occurs by default (use `<< 'EOF'` to disable).
- Useful for embedding config files, SQL queries, or email templates.

**(b) Custom FD Script:**

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# script: custom_fd.sh
exec 3> debug.log        # Step 1: Open FD 3 for writing to debug.log

echo "Normal message to terminal"
echo "DEBUG: Variable x=$x" >&3   # Step 2: Write to FD 3
echo "Another normal line"
echo "DEBUG: Loop counter=$i" >&3

exec 3>&-                # Step 3: Close FD 3
echo "FD 3 closed. Check debug.log"
```

**Execution Flow:**

| Step | Command | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 1 | `exec 3> debug.log` | Opens FD 3 for writing; creates/truncates `debug.log` |
| 2 | `echo "..." >&3` | Sends message to FD 3 instead of stdout |
| 3 | `exec 3>&-` | Closes FD 3; subsequent `>&3` would fail |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using `>` instead of `>>` when you want to preserve log history → log gets truncated.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Placing redirection `< file` inside the `while` condition (`while read line < file`) → only the **first line** is read in a loop because the file is reopened each iteration.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing `2>&1 > file` instead of `> file 2>&1` → stderr still goes to terminal.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to close custom FDs (`exec N>&-`) → file remains locked.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not using `IFS=':'` when reading delimited files → entire line read as one field.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Three standard FDs:** `0` (stdin), `1` (stdout), `2` (stderr).
- ✅ **`>` truncates; `>>` appends** — know the difference cold.
- ✅ **`<` reads file as input; `<<` is heredoc; `<<<` is herestring.**
- ✅ **`2>` captures errors; `&>` captures both stdout and stderr.**
- ✅ **Pipe `|` sends stdout only — not stderr.**
- ✅ **`tee` writes to both file AND terminal.**
